topics.title: Scope and Hierarchy of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scope and Hierarchy of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Process Optimization** is the systematic, mathematical procedure of determining the best operating conditions, design parameters, or material specifications for a chemical process such that a defined objective (such as maximum profit, minimum energy consumption, or minimum environmental impact) is achieved while satisfying all physical, thermodynamic, safety, and economic constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> Optimization is the act of obtaining the **best result** under given circumstances. In chemical engineering, it can be defined as the *minimization or maximization* of a desired objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to equality constraints $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and inequality constraints $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.

Mathematically, the general optimization problem is expressed as:

$$\text{Find } \mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$$

that minimizes (or maximizes) the objective function:

$$f(\mathbf{x})$$

subject to:

$$h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

$$g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is the **vector of decision variables**, $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**, $h_j$ are the **equality constraints**, and $g_k$ are the **inequality constraints**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hiking to the Peak" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine you are hiking in dense fog on a mountain and want to reach the **highest peak** (your optimum) using minimum effort (your objective). You cannot see the full mountain, so you feel the slope of the ground under your feet. If the ground rises, you keep walking forward; if it falls, you change direction. This is exactly what an **optimization algorithm** does — it explores the *terrain* (search space) and climbs or descends the *gradient* until it finds the **best possible answer**.

- The mountain's elevation at any point = objective function value $f(\mathbf{x})$
- Your location (latitude, longitude) = decision variables $\mathbf{x}$
- Steepness of the slope = gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$
- The summit = global optimum
- A small nearby bump = local optimum

### 1.3 Scope of Optimization in Chemical Engineering

The **scope of optimization** refers to the breadth of engineering problems where mathematical optimization can be applied. It spans virtually every stage of a chemical process life cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Areas Where Optimization is Applied in Chemical Engineering:**
> - **Process Design:** Selecting optimal reactor type, column size, heat exchanger area.
> - **Process Operation:** Tuning flow rates, temperatures, pressures to maximize yield.
> - **Planning & Scheduling:** Sequencing batch operations in a multi-product plant.
> - **Process Control:** Real-time adjustment of manipulated variables (MPC).
> - **Supply Chain & Logistics:** Minimizing transportation cost of feedstocks/products.
> - **Energy Management:** Reducing specific energy consumption in distillation, drying, etc.
> - **Environmental Engineering:** Minimizing emissions, effluent load, and carbon footprint.
> - **Economics:** Maximizing Net Present Value (NPV) and Return on Investment (ROI).

### 1.4 Why Optimization is Indispensable

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Chemical Engineer Must Study Optimization:**
> In a competitive global market, even a **1% improvement** in yield, energy efficiency, or raw material utilization translates into **crores of rupees** per year for a large chemical plant. Optimization is therefore not an academic exercise — it is a **core economic and sustainability tool**.

- A typical refinery processing **10 million tonnes/year** of crude oil
- A **1% yield gain** = **100,000 tonnes/year** of extra product
- A **5% energy reduction** in a distillation column can save **millions of kWh/year**

These numbers make optimization a **boardroom-level priority**, not just a textbook chapter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Optimization Problems (KTU Perspective)

The scope of optimization is best understood by classifying problems along several dimensions. The KTU 2024 syllabus highlights the following taxonomy:

| # | Classification Criterion | Category A | Category B | Board Exam Relevance |
|---|--------------------------|------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Nature of Variables** | Continuous | Discrete / Integer | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | **Objective Function Nature** | Linear | Nonlinear | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Number of Objectives** | Single-objective | Multi-objective | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Existence of Constraints** | Unconstrained | Constrained | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Determinism** | Deterministic (LP, NLP) | Stochastic (chance-constrained) | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | **Variable Nature** | Static (steady-state) | Dynamic (time-dependent) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Optimization — The KTU Core Concept

The **hierarchy of optimization** is one of the most heavily-tested concepts in KTU board examinations. It classifies optimization problems according to the **level of decision-making** within a chemical process enterprise.

A chemical plant has three principal decision layers, each with its own objective, decision variables, and constraints:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Hierarchical Levels of Optimization (Edgar & Himmelblau Framework):**
> 1. **Level 1 — Overall Process (Enterprise / Superstructure Level)**
> 2. **Level 2 — Unit Operation (Process Unit Level)**
> 3. **Level 3 — Equipment / Mechanical Design (Hardware Level)**

This hierarchy is **top-down**: decisions made at the top level bound and define the problem space for the lower levels.

#### 2.2.1 Level 1 — Overall Process Optimization

- **Goal:** Decide *what* to make, *which* process route, *where* to locate, *how much* to produce.
- **Decision Variables:** Choice of technology, production rate, feed allocation among plants, utility network configuration.
- **Typical Objective:** Maximize annual profit or minimize total annualized cost across the **entire plant**.
- **Time Horizon:** Months to years.
- **Mathematical Form:** Large-scale Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP) or Linear Programming (LP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples (KTU 2024 Reference):**
> - Selecting between a distillation vs. membrane-based separation for ethanol-water.
> - Choosing the feedstock slate (crude oil mix) that maximizes refinery margin.
> - Network optimization of a multi-plant company with shared utilities.

#### 2.2.2 Level 2 — Unit Operation Optimization

- **Goal:** Given the process route and throughput from Level 1, determine the *optimal operating conditions* of each major unit.
- **Decision Variables:** Temperatures, pressures, flow rates, reflux ratios, conversion, recycles.
- **Typical Objective:** Maximize conversion, minimize energy per unit product, maximize selectivity.
- **Time Horizon:** Days to months (operational decisions).
- **Mathematical Form:** Nonlinear Programming (NLP) with equality constraints (mass/energy balances).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples (KTU 2024 Reference):**
> - Optimizing reflux ratio and feed stage in a distillation column to minimize reboiler duty.
> - Finding the optimal temperature profile in a CSTR for maximum selectivity in parallel reactions.
> - Determining the optimum solvent-to-feed ratio in an extraction column.

#### 2.2.3 Level 3 — Equipment / Mechanical Design Optimization

- **Goal:** Minimize equipment cost (capital cost) given the operating conditions fixed by Level 2.
- **Decision Variables:** Vessel diameter, length, number of tubes, baffle spacing, impeller diameter.
- **Typical Objective:** Minimize purchase cost (subject to mechanical codes) or maximize heat transfer area efficiency.
- **Time Horizon:** Once per equipment design phase (project execution).
- **Mathematical Form:** Often a constrained NLP where the cost function is empirical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples (KTU 2024 Reference):**
> - Optimum diameter-to-length ratio of a distillation column shell.
> - Minimum-cost heat exchanger design (number of tubes, tube length, baffle cut).
> - Optimum pipe diameter for minimum annualized cost of pumping + pipe purchase.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Hierarchy & Scope of Optimization

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Expression | Unit / Type | KTU Mark Weightage |
|---------|-----------------------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| General Optimization Problem | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $h_j(\mathbf{x})=0, \, g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ | Vector formulation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Decision Variable Vector | $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ | Dimensionless / SI | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Objective Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = $ Profit, Cost, Yield, Energy, NPV | Currency / kg / kWh | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Hierarchy Level 1 (Overall) | MINLP / LP — enterprise level | Strategic | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Hierarchy Level 2 (Unit) | NLP with mass/energy balance constraints | Operational | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Hierarchy Level 3 (Equipment) | Cost-minimization NLP with mechanical codes | Design | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Degrees of Freedom | $n - m$ (where $n$ = variables, $m$ = independent equations) | Scalar | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Stationary Point Condition (Unconstrained) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = 0$ | First-order necessary condition | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Second-Order Sufficient Condition | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star) \succ 0$ (for minimum) | Hessian positive definite | ⭐⭐⭐ |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hierarchy Matters in Practice:**
> You **cannot** find the optimal pipe diameter (Level 3) before knowing the flow rate (Level 2), and you **cannot** pick the flow rate before choosing the process route (Level 1). The hierarchy therefore enforces a **logical sequence of decisions**, prevents inconsistent design choices, and dramatically reduces the search-space size at each level.

This hierarchical decomposition is the foundation of modern **computer-aided process engineering (CAPE)** tools such as Aspen Plus, HYSYS, and PRO/II, which automatically pass optimized parameters from one level to the next.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why We Use Stationary Point Conditions

The **first-order necessary condition** for an unconstrained optimum is the starting point of every optimization problem. Its derivation is foundational and frequently asked in KTU examinations.

**Step 1: Taylor Series Expansion of the Objective Function**

For a twice-differentiable scalar function $f(\mathbf{x})$ around a candidate point $\mathbf{x}^\star$, the first-order Taylor expansion is:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}^\star) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^\star) + \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^\star)^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star)(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^\star) + \mathcal{O}(\vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^\star \vert\vert^3)
$$

where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ is the **gradient vector** and $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ is the **Hessian matrix**.

**Step 2: Take a Small Perturbation**

Let $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^\star + \epsilon \mathbf{d}$, where $\mathbf{d}$ is an arbitrary unit direction vector and $\epsilon$ is a small scalar. Substituting:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^\star + \epsilon \mathbf{d}) = f(\mathbf{x}^\star) + \epsilon \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^T \mathbf{d} + \frac{\epsilon^2}{2} \mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star) \mathbf{d} + \mathcal{O}(\epsilon^3)
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Definition of Optimum**

At a local minimum $\mathbf{x}^\star$, the function value must be **greater than or equal to** $f(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ for all small perturbations in any direction $\mathbf{d}$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^\star + \epsilon \mathbf{d}) - f(\mathbf{x}^\star) \geq 0 \quad \text{for sufficiently small } \epsilon
$$

Therefore:

$$
\epsilon \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^T \mathbf{d} + \frac{\epsilon^2}{2} \mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star) \mathbf{d} \geq 0
$$

**Step 4: First-Order Necessary Condition**

Since $\epsilon$ can be positive or negative, the only way the inequality holds for **all** directions $\mathbf{d}$ is if the first-order (linear) term vanishes:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)^T \mathbf{d} = 0 \quad \text{for all } \mathbf{d}
$$

This implies the gradient itself must be the **zero vector**:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = \cdots = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} = 0}
$$

**Step 5: Second-Order Sufficient Condition**

To distinguish between a **minimum**, **maximum**, or **saddle point**, we examine the Hessian matrix $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star)$:

$$
\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star) = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The classification is:

| Hessian Property | Conclusion |
|------------------|------------|
| $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star) \succ 0$ (positive definite) | Local **minimum** ✓ |
| $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star) \prec 0$ (negative definite) | Local **maximum** ✓ |
| $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ **indefinite** | **Saddle point** ✗ |
| $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ singular | Higher-order test required |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Hierarchy Comparison Table

This is a **board-exam-ready table** that students should memorize verbatim for any 7-mark or 14-mark question on the hierarchy of optimization:

| Feature | Level 1: Overall Process | Level 2: Unit Operation | Level 3: Equipment Design |
|---------|--------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Decision Scope** | Whole plant / enterprise | Single unit / section | Single equipment item |
| **Variables Decided** | Technology, capacity, location | Temperature, pressure, flow | Diameter, length, thickness |
| **Objective** | Max profit / Min total cost | Max yield / Min energy | Min equipment cost |
| **Constraints** | Market demand, raw material availability | Mass & energy balances | Mechanical codes, materials |
| **Time Horizon** | Months – Years | Days – Months | Once (project) |
| **Mathematical Type** | LP / MINLP | NLP (steady-state) | Constrained NLP |
| **Tools** | Superstructure optimization, MILP | Aspen, HYSYS, gPROMS | Mechanical design software |
| **KTU Example** | Choosing distillation vs. membrane for ethanol | Reflux ratio optimization in a column | Optimum column diameter |
| **Hierarchy Role** | Sets targets for Level 2 | Sets targets for Level 3 | Implements the design |
| **Decision Frequency** | Strategic, infrequent | Operational, periodic | Once per project |

### 3.3 Degrees of Freedom — A Key Board-Exam Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — KTU Definition:**
> The number of independent decision variables available for optimization is called the **degrees of freedom** of the system.

$$
\text{DOF} = n_{\text{variables}} - n_{\text{independent equations}}
$$

- If **DOF = 0** → system is fully specified, no optimization possible.
- If **DOF > 0** → optimization is possible; you have freedom to choose variables.
- If **DOF < 0** → system is over-specified (infeasible problem).

**Worked Example (KTU Style):**

A mixer mixes two streams of flow rates $F_1$ and $F_2$ to give outlet $F_3$. The material balance gives $F_3 = F_1 + F_2$. We want to fix $F_3$ and optimize $F_1$ (and $F_2$ follows).

- Total variables: $F_1, F_2, F_3$ → $n_{\text{variables}} = 3$
- Independent equations: 1 (mass balance)
- **DOF = 3 − 1 = 2**

If we fix $F_3$ (specification), then DOF = 1, meaning we can optimize $F_1$ to meet some objective (e.g., minimize cost).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Three-Level Hierarchy — Block-Level Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the **top-down flow of information and decisions** in the hierarchy of optimization. Each higher level *sets targets* and *passes constraints* to the level below it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LEVEL 1: Overall Process Optimization] --> B[Process Route Selection]
    A --> C[Production Capacity]
    A --> D[Plant Location and Feedstock]
    A --> E[Utility Network]

    B --> F[LEVEL 2: Unit Operation Optimization]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G[Reactor Operating Conditions]
    F --> H[Distillation Reflux and Feed Stage]
    F --> I[Heat Exchanger Flow Arrangement]
    F --> J[Separator Operating Pressure]

    G --> K[LEVEL 3: Equipment Design Optimization]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L[Reactor Volume and Catalyst]
    K --> M[Column Diameter and Height]
    K --> N[Heat Exchanger Area and Baffles]
    K --> O[Pump and Compressor Sizing]

    A -. Strategic Targets .-> F
    F -. Operating Targets .-> K
    K -. Final Hardware Spec .-> P[Constructed Plant]
```

### 4.2 Optimization Problem Classification Flowchart

This second diagram classifies the **scope of optimization** problems based on the nature of the objective function and constraints — a frequently asked KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Optimization Problem] --> B{Objective Function Type?}
    B -- Linear --> C{Constraints Type?}
    B -- Nonlinear --> D{Number of Objectives?}

    C -- All Linear --> E[Linear Programming LP]
    C -- Some Integer --> F[Mixed Integer LP MILP]

    D -- Single --> G{Number of Variables?}
    D -- Multiple --> H[Multi-Objective Optimization]

    G -- Continuous --> I[Nonlinear Programming NLP]
    G -- Mixed --> J[Mixed Integer NLP MINLP]

    E --> K[Solver: Simplex]
    F --> K
    I --> L[Solver: SQP or Gradient]
    J --> L
    H --> M[Solver: Pareto or Weighted Sum]
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Optimization Cycle (Process Operation)

The diagram below represents the **continuous optimization loop** used in real-time plant operation, where measurements are fed back to update the optimizer, which then sends new setpoints to the controllers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plant Operation Sensors] --> Q[Data Acquisition and Validation]
    Q --> R[Process Model and State Estimator]
    R --> S[Optimization Solver NLP]
    S --> T[Setpoints: Temperatures Flows Pressures]
    T --> U[Process Controllers DCS]
    U --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of the Loop:**
> - **Sensors → Model:** Real plant data is reconciled with the steady-state or dynamic process model.
> - **Model → Solver:** The model provides equality constraints (mass/energy balances) to the solver.
> - **Solver → Controllers:** The optimal setpoints are implemented by the Distributed Control System (DCS).
> - **Controllers → Plant:** The plant responds and the cycle repeats (typically every 1–60 minutes in real-time optimization, RTO).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Optimization" in the context of chemical engineering. List any two areas where it is applied.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** Optimization is the mathematical procedure of determining the values of decision variables that minimize or maximize an objective function while satisfying all equality and inequality constraints. Mathematically, it is the process of finding $\mathbf{x}^\star$ that optimizes $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.
- **Two application areas (1 Mark):**
  1. Optimal design of heat exchangers, columns, and reactors.
  2. Real-time optimization of operating conditions in refinery and petrochemical plants.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*State the three levels in the hierarchy of optimization with one example for each level.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Level 1 — Overall Process (Enterprise Level):** Decides the process route, production rate, and plant capacity. *Example: Choosing between a distillation and a membrane-based process for ethanol dehydration.*
- **Level 2 — Unit Operation (Process Unit Level):** Optimizes operating variables of a specific unit. *Example: Finding the optimal reflux ratio and feed stage in a distillation column.*
- **Level 3 — Equipment (Mechanical Design Level):** Minimizes equipment cost subject to design codes. *Example: Optimum diameter and length of a heat exchanger.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Hierarchy Questions:**
> - Students **often confuse the three levels** and write vague answers. Always mention *objective*, *decision variables*, and *one concrete chemical-engineering example* for each level to score full marks.
> - Do **not** write about a single level only — KTU expects a **comparative table** for full credit.
> - **Do not** forget to mention the **time horizon** (strategic vs. operational vs. design) — this is a 2-mark differentiator.
> - Avoid phrases like *"optimization is finding the best answer"* without quantifying the objective function. Always state **what** is being optimized.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Comprehensive]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **hierarchy of optimization** in a chemical process plant. Describe each level with a clear objective, decision variables, and one specific example from chemical engineering. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A chemical plant produces two products P1 and P2 from a shared raw material. The profit (in ₹ lakhs/year) is given by:

$$
f(x_1, x_2) = 50 x_1 + 60 x_2 - x_1^2 - x_2^2
$$

where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are the production levels (in thousand tonnes) of P1 and P2, subject to availability constraints $x_1 + x_2 \leq 10$ and $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$. Find the **unconstrained optimum** and check whether it satisfies the constraints. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchy of Optimization — Full-Marks Answer (7 Marks):**

The hierarchy of optimization classifies process decisions into three distinct levels, organized **top-down from strategic to detailed design**:

**Level 1: Overall Process Optimization (2 Marks)**
- **Scope:** Entire plant or enterprise.
- **Objective:** Maximize total annualized profit or minimize total cost.
- **Decision Variables:** Choice of process technology, plant capacity, location, feedstock mix, utility integration.
- **Example:** Deciding whether to use a cryogenic distillation or a pressure-swing adsorption route for air separation.

**Level 2: Unit Operation Optimization (2 Marks)**
- **Scope:** A single process unit or section.
- **Objective:** Optimize operating variables (T, P, flow) to maximize yield or minimize energy.
- **Decision Variables:** Reactor temperature, residence time, reflux ratio, solvent-to-feed ratio.
- **Example:** Optimizing the reflux ratio and feed stage of an ethanol-water distillation column to minimize reboiler duty while meeting product purity specs.

**Level 3: Equipment / Mechanical Design Optimization (2 Marks)**
- **Scope:** A single piece of hardware.
- **Objective:** Minimize purchase cost subject to mechanical codes (ASME, TEMA).
- **Decision Variables:** Vessel diameter, length, wall thickness, tube count, baffle spacing.
- **Example:** Optimum shell-and-tube heat exchanger design to minimize total annualized cost (capital + pumping).

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The three levels are interdependent: Level 1 sets operating targets, Level 2 translates them into operating conditions, and Level 3 designs the hardware. This hierarchical decomposition is the foundation of modern CAPE tools.

**(b) Unconstrained Optimum Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Set up the Lagrangian (Lagrange multiplier not needed since unconstrained) (1 Mark)**

For the unconstrained problem, set the partial derivatives to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 50 - 2 x_1 = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 60 - 2 x_2 = 0
$$

**Step 2: Solve the first-order conditions (2 Marks)**

$$
x_1^\star = \frac{50}{2} = 25
$$

$$
x_2^\star = \frac{60}{2} = 30
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Hessian matrix (2 Marks)**

$$
\mathbf{H} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} \\
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2}
\end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
-2 & 0 \\
0 & -2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The Hessian is **negative definite** (both eigenvalues = −2 < 0), so the stationary point is a **local maximum** of the profit function.

**Step 4: Check constraint feasibility (1 Mark)**

Constraint: $x_1 + x_2 \leq 10$. At the unconstrained optimum: $25 + 30 = 55 \not\leq 10$. **The unconstrained optimum is INFEASIBLE.**

**Step 5: Conclusion (1 Mark)**

Since the unconstrained optimum violates the resource constraint, the actual optimum must lie on the boundary $x_1 + x_2 = 10$. Substitute and re-solve (or use the KKT conditions):

$$
f(x_1, 10 - x_1) = 50 x_1 + 60(10 - x_1) - x_1^2 - (10 - x_1)^2
$$

$$
= 50 x_1 + 600 - 60 x_1 - x_1^2 - (100 - 20 x_1 + x_1^2) = 500 - 10 x_1 - 2 x_1^2 + 20 x_1
$$

$$
= 500 + 10 x_1 - 2 x_1^2
$$

Setting derivative to zero: $10 - 4 x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 2.5$, $x_2 = 7.5$. Maximum profit $= 500 + 25 - 12.5 = 512.5$ ₹ lakhs.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Comprehensive]**

**(a)** Discuss the **scope of optimization** in chemical engineering. Classify optimization problems based on the **nature of the objective function and constraints**, giving one example for each class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CSTR produces a product via a first-order reaction with rate constant $k = 0.1 \text{ min}^{-1}$. The outlet concentration $C_A$ (mol/L) is related to residence time $\tau$ (min) and feed concentration $C_{A0}$ (mol/L) by:

$$
C_A = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + k \tau}
$$

The profit (₹/min) from operating the reactor is:

$$
\Pi = 100 (C_{A0} - C_A) - 5 \tau
$$

Given $C_{A0} = 2$ mol/L, find the **optimum residence time** $\tau^\star$ that maximizes profit. Also verify if it is a maximum. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Scope & Classification of Optimization (7 Marks):**

**Scope of Optimization (3 Marks):**
Optimization finds application in every stage of chemical engineering:
- **Process Design:** Sizing equipment, selecting materials, choosing technology.
- **Process Operation:** Setting optimal T, P, flow, recycle ratios during day-to-day running.
- **Planning & Scheduling:** Sequencing batch jobs in multi-product plants.
- **Process Control:** Real-time setpoint optimization (Model Predictive Control).
- **Supply Chain:** Minimizing total logistics cost.
- **Energy & Environment:** Reducing $\text{CO}_2$ emissions, energy per ton of product.

**Classification (4 Marks):**

| Class | Objective | Constraints | Example |
|-------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | Linear in variables | Linear | Optimal blending of crude oils to meet product specs at minimum cost |
| **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** | Nonlinear in variables | Linear or nonlinear | Optimal reflux ratio in a distillation column |
| **Integer / Mixed-Integer (MILP / MINLP)** | Linear / Nonlinear | Includes integer vars | Selecting the number of trays (integer) in a column design |
| **Dynamic Optimization** | Time-dependent | Time-dependent ODEs | Optimal temperature trajectory in a batch reactor |
| **Multi-Objective** | Multiple conflicting | Inequality | Maximize yield AND minimize energy simultaneously |

**(b) Optimum Residence Time Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Substitute the CSTR relation into the profit function (2 Marks)**

Given $C_{A0} = 2$ mol/L and $k = 0.1$ min$^{-1}$:

$$
C_A = \frac{2}{1 + 0.1 \tau}
$$

$$
\Pi(\tau) = 100 \left(2 - \frac{2}{1 + 0.1 \tau}\right) - 5 \tau
$$

$$
\Pi(\tau) = \frac{200 \cdot 0.1 \tau}{1 + 0.1 \tau} - 5 \tau = \frac{20 \tau}{1 + 0.1 \tau} - 5 \tau
$$

**Step 2: Differentiate and set to zero (2 Marks)**

$$
\frac{d\Pi}{d\tau} = \frac{20 (1 + 0.1 \tau) - 20 \tau (0.1)}{(1 + 0.1 \tau)^2} - 5 = \frac{20}{(1 + 0.1 \tau)^2} - 5
$$

Set $\dfrac{d\Pi}{d\tau} = 0$:

$$
\frac{20}{(1 + 0.1 \tau)^2} = 5
$$

$$
(1 + 0.1 \tau)^2 = 4
$$

$$
1 + 0.1 \tau = 2 \quad (\text{taking positive root since } \tau \geq 0)
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau^\star = 10 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**Step 3: Verify it is a maximum using the second derivative (2 Marks)**

Differentiate again:

$$
\frac{d^2\Pi}{d\tau^2} = -20 \cdot \frac{2 (0.1)}{(1 + 0.1 \tau)^3} = \frac{-4}{(1 + 0.1 \tau)^3}
$$

At $\tau^\star = 10$: $\dfrac{d^2\Pi}{d\tau^2} = \dfrac{-4}{(1 + 1)^3} = \dfrac{-4}{8} = -0.5 < 0$.

Since the second derivative is **negative**, the stationary point is a **local maximum**. ✓

**Step 4: Compute the maximum profit (1 Mark)**

$$
\Pi^\star = \frac{20 \cdot 10}{1 + 1} - 5 \cdot 10 = 100 - 50 = \text{₹ } 50 \text{ /min}
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Every Exam**

- **Optimization** = minimize or maximize an objective $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.
- The general problem has **three components**: objective function, decision variables, and constraints.
- **First-order necessary condition (unconstrained):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Second-order sufficient condition (minimum):** Hessian $\mathbf{H} \succ 0$; (maximum): $\mathbf{H} \prec 0$.
- **Hierarchy of Optimization has exactly 3 levels** — remember the mnemonic **"P-U-E" (Process-Unit-Equipment)**.
  - **Level 1 (Process):** Strategic, enterprise-wide, LP/MINLP, sets production targets.
  - **Level 2 (Unit):** Operational, NLP, optimizes T, P, flow for individual units.
  - **Level 3 (Equipment):** Design, cost-minimization NLP, sizes the hardware.
- The hierarchy is **top-down**: Level 1 passes targets to Level 2, which passes operating conditions to Level 3.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** = number of variables − number of independent equations. DOF must be $\geq 1$ for optimization.
- **Scope of optimization** includes: design, operation, planning, scheduling, control, energy, environment, supply chain.
- **Types of problems:** LP, NLP, MILP, MINLP, dynamic, multi-objective, stochastic.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Confusing "process optimization" (Level 1) with "process control" (Level 2/3). They are **different** — control regulates around a setpoint; optimization finds the best setpoint.
- Always state the **objective, variables, constraints, and method** when describing any optimization problem to score full marks.
- **Real-time optimization (RTO)** in refineries typically runs every 1–60 minutes using steady-state NLP models.
- A **1% improvement** in a large plant = **crores of rupees/year** — optimization is a financial, not academic, priority.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Chemical Engineering Applications of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Chemical Engineering Applications of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Typical Chemical Engineering Applications of Optimization"?

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (course **PECHT755 – Process Optimization**, Module 1), *Typical Chemical Engineering Applications of Optimization* refers to the **translation of real chemical engineering decision-making problems** — equipment sizing, operating condition selection, production planning, and resource allocation — into the **standard mathematical structure of an optimization problem**:

$$\text{Find } \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \text{ that minimizes (or maximizes) } f(\mathbf{x})$$

subject to a set of **equality constraints** $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, **inequality constraints** $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, and **bounds** $x_i^{L} \le x_i \le x_i^{U}$.

In plain words, it is the practice of **replacing "engineering intuition" and rule-of-thumb heuristics with mathematically proven best choices** for design, operating, or scheduling decisions in chemical processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT755 – Module 1):**
> Optimization in chemical engineering deals with selecting the **best** values of decision variables among all feasible alternatives that satisfy material/energy balances, equipment constraints, product specifications, and safety limits — to extremize a chosen performance criterion such as **cost, profit, energy, conversion, yield, or emissions**.

---

## 1.2 The Three Building Blocks of Any Chemical Engineering Optimization Problem

Every application, no matter how complex, contains three structural components:

| Block | Mathematical Symbol | Engineering Meaning | Typical Examples |
|-------|---------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Decision Variables** | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$ | Knobs the engineer can control | Pipe diameter, reflux ratio, temperature, feed flow |
| **Objective Function** | $f(\mathbf{x})$ | The single number we want to optimize | Total annualized cost, profit, energy use |
| **Constraints** | $h(\mathbf{x})=0, \, g(\mathbf{x})\le 0$ | Physical/operational/quality limits | Volume must be ≥ 50 m³, purity must be ≥ 95% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition):**
> Imagine you are packing a school bag. Your **decision variables** are *which books to put in and how to arrange them*. Your **objective** might be *minimizing weight while covering all subjects*. Your **constraints** are *the bag's zipper must close and the bag must not tear*. Optimization in chemical engineering is exactly this — packing the best plant operation, with constraints like material balances and safety limits, while minimizing cost or maximizing profit.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Exams

Module 1 lays the foundation for the entire course. The KTU examiner typically expects the student to:

1. Recognize a real chemical engineering problem and **formulate** it as an optimization problem.
2. Identify whether it is a **design optimization** (capital + operating trade-off) or an **operating optimization** (set-point selection).
3. Identify whether the resulting formulation is **linear (LP)**, **nonlinear (NLP)**, or **unconstrained**.
4. Solve small textbook problems analytically using calculus (set $\frac{df}{dx}=0$).

> [!TIP]
> A very common **valuation trap**: students write the objective function but forget to verify the *boundary* or *inequality constraint* is **active** at the optimum. Always check **Kuhn–Tucker conditions** mentally — they are the backbone of KTU Module 1–2 questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Chemical Engineering Optimization Applications

Chemical engineering optimization problems can be classified along several axes. The KTU examiner commonly asks this classification as a **short-answer question (3 marks)**.

### A. By the Type of Decision Being Made

| Category | Meaning | Classic Example |
|----------|---------|-----------------|
| **Equipment Design Optimization** | Choosing geometry/size at the design stage | Minimum-cost cylindrical tank of fixed volume |
| **Operating Optimization** | Choosing operating set-points | Optimal reflux ratio in distillation |
| **Process Synthesis Optimization** | Choosing the flowsheet/structure | Heat exchanger network synthesis |
| **Planning & Scheduling** | Allocating resources over time | Multi-product plant production planning |
| **Parameter Estimation** | Fitting model parameters from data | Regression in kinetics |

### B. By the Mathematical Structure

| Structure | Defining Feature | Solver Used |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Unconstrained** | Only objective function exists | Set $\nabla f = 0$ (calculus) |
| **Equality-constrained** | Only $h(\mathbf{x})=0$ | Lagrange multipliers |
| **Inequality-constrained** | $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ | Kuhn–Tucker conditions |
| **Linear Program (LP)** | $f$ and constraints are linear | Simplex method |
| **Nonlinear Program (NLP)** | Any nonlinearity present | Gradient methods, KKT, penalty methods |

---

## 2.2 Canonical Examples Studied Across KTU Universities

The following canonical problems appear repeatedly in KTU university exams (Module 1):

### Example Type 1 — Minimum Cost of a Cylindrical Tank (Design Optimization)

**Statement (Typical):** A chemical storage tank of **fixed volume** $V$ must be built with cylindrical body and flat ends using material costing $C_s$ per unit area. Find the **most economical dimensions** (radius $R$ and height $H$).

**Engineering Meaning:** Capital cost is dominated by surface area of metal used; making the tank very tall and thin or very short and fat wastes material.

### Example Type 2 — Optimal Pipe Diameter (Flow Optimization)

**Statement:** A fluid of density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$ is to be pumped at flow rate $Q$ through a pipe of length $L$. Pipe cost $\propto D$ and pumping cost $\propto 1/D^5$. Find the **most economic diameter**.

**Engineering Meaning:** Smaller pipes → cheaper capital but more pumping power; the optimum balances the two.

### Example Type 3 — Optimum Reflux Ratio in Distillation

**Statement:** A binary distillation column separates a feed of given composition. Find the reflux ratio $R$ that minimizes total cost (column + reboiler + condenser + utilities).

**Engineering Meaning:** As $R$ rises, fewer stages are needed (cheaper column) but more reboiling is needed (expensive utilities). An optimum exists.

### Example Type 4 — Optimal Operating Temperature of a CSTR

**Statement:** A first-order exothermic reaction $A \to B$ takes place in a CSTR. Find the temperature $T$ that maximizes conversion or profit.

**Engineering Meaning:** Higher $T$ increases rate but decreases equilibrium constant for exothermic reactions — a maximum exists.

### Example Type 5 — Allocation of Utilities in a Refinery

**Statement:** A refinery has limited crude and must allocate it among several products to maximize profit subject to demand, quality, and capacity constraints.

**Engineering Meaning:** Linear Programming (LP) problem with 10s–100s of variables.

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every mathematical expression you should have at your fingertips for a Module 1 question on this topic. Memorize it.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Use |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | General optimization problem | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $h_j(\mathbf{x})=0$, $g_k(\mathbf{x})\le 0$ | Problem statement |
| 2 | Necessary condition (unconstrained) | $\dfrac{df}{dx_i} = 0$ for all $i$ | Find stationary point |
| 3 | Second-order sufficient condition | $\dfrac{d^2 f}{dx_i^2} > 0$ (min) or $< 0$ (max) | Classify optimum |
| 4 | Lagrange multiplier (1 equality) | $\mathcal{L} = f(x) - \lambda \, h(x)$; set $\nabla \mathcal{L} = 0$ | Equality constraints |
| 5 | KKT conditions (inequality) | $\nabla f - \mu \nabla g = 0$, $\mu \ge 0$, $\mu \cdot g = 0$ | Inequality constraints |
| 6 | Cylindrical tank volume constraint | $V = \pi R^2 H$ | Design problem |
| 7 | Surface area of cylinder (closed) | $A = 2\pi R^2 + 2\pi R H$ | Objective function |
| 8 | Pipe friction factor (turbulent) | $\Delta P \propto \dfrac{L \rho v^2}{D}$ (or $\propto \dfrac{1}{D^5}$ via $v \propto Q/D^2$) | Pumping cost |
| 9 | Pumping power | $P = Q \cdot \Delta P$ | Operating cost |
| 10 | Fanning equation | $\Delta P_f = 4f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Head loss |
| 11 | Conversion in PFR (1st order) | $X = 1 - \exp(-k\tau)$ | Reactor optimization |
| 12 | Conversion in CSTR (1st order) | $X = \dfrac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$ | Reactor optimization |
| 13 | Annualized capital cost | $C_{\text{cap}} = \text{PWF} \cdot C_{\text{purchase}}$ | Total cost |
| 14 | Present worth factor | $\text{PWF} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Economic life |
| 15 | Arrhenius rate | $k = k_0 \exp(-E/RT)$ | Temperature optimum |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units & Conversion Pitfall (KTU Examiner's note):** When you write $\Delta P \propto 1/D^5$, ensure $D$ is raised to the **fifth power, not squared**. Mixing the power leads to a wrong optimum and loss of 4–6 marks.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

The applications above are not textbook curiosities — they drive multi-crore decisions in:

- **Refineries & petrochemical plants** — every crude-distillation column is sized using optimization.
- **Pharmaceutical batch plants** — scheduling problems are LP/MILP formulations.
- **Piping networks** — sizing every cross-country pipeline (e.g., gas pipelines) uses the optimum diameter rule.
- **Polymer reactors** — operating temperature is set at the optimum of rate-vs-property trade-off.
- **Utilities & energy systems** — heat integration minimizes fuel usage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Big Picture:** Modules 2–5 of PECHT755 teach you *how to solve* these problems (calculus, Lagrange, KKT, LP, NLP). Module 1 teaches you *how to recognize and formulate* them. Without strong formulation skills, no solver can help you.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops **two classic KTU-style problems completely from scratch** — every algebraic step is shown explicitly. These two derivations cover roughly **80% of the marks** awarded on this topic in Module 1 university exams.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Optimal Dimensions of a Cylindrical Storage Tank

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> A chemical plant needs a cylindrical storage tank with **two flat ends** to hold a **fixed liquid volume** of $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$. The cost of the tank material is proportional to its **surface area**. Find the **radius $R$ and height $H$** that minimize the material cost.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Formulation

**Step 1 — Identify decision variables.**
The variables we can choose are $R$ (radius) and $H$ (height).

$$\mathbf{x} = (R, H)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the objective function.**
Since cost is proportional to surface area $A$, we write

$$f(R,H) = A = 2\pi R^2 + 2\pi R H$$

The factor of proportionality to actual currency is irrelevant for optimization, so we minimize $A$ directly.

**Step 3 — Identify the constraint.**
The volume is fixed at $V$:

$$h(R,H) = \pi R^2 H - V = 0$$

**Step 4 — Identify bounds.**
Both dimensions must be positive (physical realizability):

$$R > 0, \qquad H > 0$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the optimization problem.**

$$
\min_{R,H} \; 2\pi R^2 + 2\pi R H
$$

subject to

$$
\pi R^2 H = V, \qquad R > 0, \, H > 0
$$

### 3.1.3 Analytical Solution via Lagrange Multipliers

**Step 6 — Build the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L}(R, H, \lambda) = 2\pi R^2 + 2\pi R H - \lambda(\pi R^2 H - V)$$

**Step 7 — Take partial derivatives and set to zero.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial R} &= 4\pi R + 2\pi H - \lambda (2\pi R H) = 0 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H} &= 2\pi R - \lambda (\pi R^2) = 0 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} &= -(\pi R^2 H - V) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Simplify the second equation.**

From the second equation:

$$2\pi R = \lambda \pi R^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = \dfrac{2}{R}$$

**Step 9 — Substitute $\lambda$ back into the first equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4\pi R + 2\pi H - \dfrac{2}{R} \cdot 2\pi R H &= 0 \\
4\pi R + 2\pi H - 4\pi H &= 0 \\
4\pi R - 2\pi H &= 0 \\
4R &= 2H \\
H &= 2R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Substitute $H = 2R$ into the volume constraint.**

$$
\pi R^2 (2R) = V \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2\pi R^3 = V
$$

$$
R = \left(\dfrac{V}{2\pi}\right)^{1/3}, \qquad H = 2R = 2\left(\dfrac{V}{2\pi}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 11 — Compute the numerical answer for $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \left(\dfrac{50}{2\pi}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\dfrac{50}{6.2832}\right)^{1/3} = (7.9577)^{1/3} \\
R &\approx 1.996 \text{ m} \approx 2.0 \text{ m} \\
H &= 2R \approx 4.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 12 — Verify it is a minimum (second-order check).**

Substitute $H = V/(\pi R^2)$ into $f$:

$$f(R) = 2\pi R^2 + \dfrac{2V}{R}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{df}{dR} &= 4\pi R - \dfrac{2V}{R^2} \\
\dfrac{d^2 f}{dR^2} &= 4\pi + \dfrac{4V}{R^3} > 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ minimum}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Final Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The optimal cylindrical tank with two flat ends has $H = 2R$ (height equals diameter).** This holds **regardless of volume**, and is one of the most widely-quoted results in chemical engineering design.

| Quantity | Value for $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$ |
|----------|-------------------------------|
| Optimum radius $R^*$ | **≈ 2.0 m** |
| Optimum height $H^*$ | **≈ 4.0 m** |
| Minimum surface area $A^*$ | $2\pi R^{*2} + 2\pi R^* H^* = 6\pi R^{*2} \approx 75.4 \text{ m}^2$ |

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Optimum Pipe Diameter for Pumping a Fluid

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> An incompressible fluid of density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and viscosity $\mu$ is to be pumped at volumetric flow rate $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ through a horizontal straight pipe of length $L = 1000 \text{ m}$. The cost of the pipe is $C_p = a \cdot D$ per unit length (where $a$ is a constant) and the cost of electrical pumping is $C_e = b \cdot (\Delta P_f \cdot Q)$ per unit time (where $b$ is a constant and $\Delta P_f$ is the frictional pressure drop). The flow is fully turbulent. Find the **most economical pipe diameter $D$**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Formulation

**Step 1 — Define decision variable.**

$$x = D \quad (\text{pipe inner diameter})$$

**Step 2 — Express velocity in terms of diameter.**

For circular pipe:

$$v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{pipe}}} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D^2/4} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 3 — Express the frictional pressure drop (Fanning equation).**

For fully turbulent flow in a smooth pipe, the friction factor $f \approx \text{const}$ (independent of $D$):

$$\Delta P_f = 4f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

Substituting $v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_f &= 4f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right)^2 \\
&= 4f \cdot \dfrac{L \rho}{2 D} \cdot \dfrac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4} \\
&= \dfrac{32 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\Delta P_f \propto 1/D^5$. This is the **classic fifth-power rule**.

**Step 4 — Express the total annualized cost.**

For a project life of $N$ years at interest rate $i$, the Present Worth Factor $\beta$ converts initial pipe cost to equivalent annual cost:

$$C_{\text{total}}(D) = \beta \cdot (a \cdot D \cdot L) + 8760 \cdot b \cdot (\Delta P_f \cdot Q)$$

where $8760$ is hours/year. Dropping the constants that don't depend on $D$, the **shape** of the objective function is:

$$C_{\text{total}}(D) \propto A_1 \cdot D + \dfrac{A_2}{D^5}$$

where

$$A_1 = \beta \, a \, L, \qquad A_2 = 8760 \, b \, \dfrac{32 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the unconstrained optimization problem.**

$$
\min_{D > 0} \; C_{\text{total}}(D) = A_1 D + \dfrac{A_2}{D^5}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the unconstrained necessary condition $\dfrac{dC_{\text{total}}}{dD} = 0$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dC_{\text{total}}}{dD} &= A_1 - \dfrac{5 A_2}{D^6} = 0 \\
A_1 &= \dfrac{5 A_2}{D^{*6}} \\
D^{*6} &= \dfrac{5 A_2}{A_1} \\
D^* &= \left(\dfrac{5 A_2}{A_1}\right)^{1/6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify it is a minimum (second derivative test).**

$$
\dfrac{d^2 C_{\text{total}}}{dD^2} = \dfrac{30 A_2}{D^8} > 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ minimum}
$$

### 3.2.3 Final Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The optimum diameter satisfies $D^* = \left(5 A_2 / A_1\right)^{1/6}$.** Equivalently, at the optimum the **pumping cost is exactly 5 times the pipe capital cost** (because $C_e/D^5$ term contributes a derivative $5\times$ bigger than the linear term). This $5:1$ ratio is a famous KTU examiner-favourite.

| Quantity | Value (using $A_1 = 1$, $A_2 = 1$ for illustration) |
|----------|------------------------------------------------------|
| Optimum $D^*$ | $5^{1/6} \approx 1.308$ |
| Capital share | $A_1 D^* = 1.308$ |
| Pumping share | $A_2/D^{*5} = 1/1.308^5 = 0.262$ |
| Ratio (Pumping / Capital) | $\mathbf{0.262 / 1.308 = 1/5 = 0.20}$ ✓ |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Identification Guide

Use this matrix during the exam to identify which type of problem you have been given:

| If you see … | Then it is a … | Decision Variables | Constraints | Solver |
|--------------|----------------|--------------------|-------------|--------|
| Tank / vessel / drum sizing | Design optimization | $R, H, D, L$ | Fixed volume | Lagrange / $H=2R$ |
| Pipe / duct sizing | Design optimization | $D$ | None (capital vs pumping) | $\dfrac{dC}{dD}=0$ |
| Reflux ratio, feed stage | Operating optimization | $R$, $N$ | Product purity | KKT or numerical |
| Reactor temperature / residence time | Operating optimization | $T, \tau$ | Conversion or selectivity | $\dfrac{df}{dT}=0$ |
| Multi-product allocation | Planning | $x_i$ (production rates) | Demand, capacity, quality | Simplex / LP |
| Heat exchanger network | Synthesis | $Q_{ij}$ (heat duties) | Energy balance | LP / NLP |
| Batch scheduling | Scheduling | $t_{\text{start},i}$ | Time, equipment | MILP |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Block Diagram — How a Chemical Engineering Problem Becomes an Optimization Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Chemical Engineering Problem] --> B[Step 1: Identify Decision Variables x]
    B --> C[Step 2: Define Objective Function f of x]
    C --> D[Step 3: Enumerate Constraints h of x equals 0]
    D --> E[Step 4: Enumerate Inequality Constraints g of x less or equal 0]
    E --> F[Step 5: Identify Bounds on x]
    F --> G[Mathematical Optimization Model]
    G --> H{Is f Linear?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Linear Programming LP - Simplex]
    H -- No --> J[Nonlinear Programming NLP]
    J --> K{Constraints?}
    K -- None --> L[Set gradient f equals 0]
    K -- Equality Only --> M[Lagrange Multipliers]
    K -- Inequality --> N[Karush Kuhn Tucker KKT]
    L --> O[Optimal Solution x star and f x star]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    I --> O
    O --> P[Translate Back to Engineering Decision]
    P --> Q[Implement in Plant or Equipment]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Mapping Typical Applications to Their Problem Class

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DO[Design Optimization]
        A1[Tank Sizing]
        A2[Pipe Diameter]
        A3[Heat Exchanger Area]
    end
    subgraph OO[Operating Optimization]
        B1[Reflux Ratio]
        B2[Reactor Temperature]
        B3[Catalyst Loading]
    end
    subgraph PS[Process Synthesis]
        C1[HEN Synthesis]
        C2[Distillation Sequence]
        C3[Reactor Network]
    end
    subgraph PL[Planning and Scheduling]
        D1[Crude Allocation]
        D2[Batch Sequencing]
        D3[Utility Distribution]
    end
    DO --> R[Objective: Minimize Cost or Maximize Profit]
    OO --> R
    PS --> R
    PL --> R
    R --> S[Subject to: Mass Balance, Energy Balance, Equipment Capacity, Product Spec, Safety]
    S --> T[Mathematical Formulation]
    T --> U[Numerical Solver]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Workflow Inside a Typical Plant Optimization Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plant Operation Data] --> P2[Build Steady State Model]
    P2 --> P3[Identify Degrees of Freedom]
    P3 --> P4[Define Objective and Constraints]
    P4 --> P5[Solve using NLP Solver]
    P5 --> P6[Optimal Set Points]
    P6 --> P7{Validation in Pilot or Simulation}
    P7 -- Pass --> P8[Implement in DCS or Plant]
    P7 -- Fail --> P2
    P8 --> P9[Monitor and Re optimize]
    P9 --> P1
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Optimum Reflux Ratio in Distillation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Reflux Ratio R] --> B[Many Theoretical Stages N]
    B --> C[Tall Column: High Capital Cost]
    C --> D[Small Reboiler Duty: Low Utility Cost]
    D --> E[Total Cost: HIGH Capital dominates]
    E --> F[Optimum R Star]
    F --> G[High Reflux Ratio R]
    G --> H[Few Theoretical Stages N]
    H --> I[Short Column: Low Capital Cost]
    I --> J[Large Reboiler Duty: High Utility Cost]
    J --> K[Total Cost: HIGH Utility dominates]
    K --> F
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**State any three components that constitute a typical chemical engineering optimization problem. Illustrate with one example.**

**Model Answer (to be written in exam):**

> A typical chemical engineering optimization problem consists of:
>
> 1. **Decision variables** — quantities the engineer can control (e.g., pipe diameter $D$, reflux ratio $R$, reactor temperature $T$).
> 2. **Objective function** — the single performance criterion to be extremized (e.g., total annualized cost, profit, conversion).
> 3. **Constraints** — restrictions imposed by physics, equipment, or product specification (e.g., volume $= V$, purity $\ge 95\%$, material balance).
>
> **Example:** Minimization of the surface area of a cylindrical storage tank of fixed volume $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$.
> - Decision variables: $R, H$
> - Objective: $f(R,H) = 2\pi R^2 + 2\pi RH$
> - Constraint: $\pi R^2 H = V$
>
> (3 marks: 1 mark for listing components, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for example.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]`

**Explain the difference between design optimization and operating optimization in chemical engineering, with one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | Design Optimization | Operating Optimization |
> |--------|---------------------|------------------------|
> | Stage of decision | Before plant is built | During plant operation |
> | Nature of variables | Geometric / sizing variables | Set-point / operating variables |
> | Time horizon | One-time (project) | Recurrent / real-time |
> | Example | Choosing $D$ of a pipe | Choosing reflux ratio $R$ of a distillation column |
>
> Design optimization trades **capital cost vs operating cost** at the design stage, while operating optimization seeks the **best set-point** for an existing plant.
>
> (3 marks: 2 marks comparison, 1 mark example.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1(a)/(b)]`

**A (14 marks):**

> **(a)** **[7 marks]** Formulate the problem of finding the **optimal dimensions of a cylindrical storage tank** with two flat ends and fixed volume $V$ as a mathematical optimization problem. Identify the decision variables, objective function, and constraints clearly.
>
> **(b)** **[7 marks]** Solve the formulated problem using the **method of Lagrange multipliers** to obtain the optimum $R$ and $H$ in terms of $V$. Verify that the stationary point is a **minimum** and compute the numerical values for $V = 100 \text{ m}^3$.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Formulation

**Step 1 — Decision variables:** $\mathbf{x} = (R, H)$.

**Step 2 — Objective function** (cost proportional to surface area):

$$f(R,H) = 2\pi R^2 + 2\pi R H$$

**Step 3 — Constraints:**

- Equality (volume fixed): $h(R,H) = \pi R^2 H - V = 0$
- Bounds: $R > 0$, $H > 0$

**Step 4 — Mathematical form:**

$$
\min_{R,H} \; 2\pi R^2 + 2\pi R H \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \pi R^2 H = V, \; R>0, H>0
$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Solution using Lagrange Multipliers

**Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L} = 2\pi R^2 + 2\pi R H - \lambda(\pi R^2 H - V)$$

**First-order conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial R} &= 4\pi R + 2\pi H - 2\lambda \pi R H = 0 \\
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H} &= 2\pi R - \lambda \pi R^2 = 0 \\
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} &= -\pi R^2 H + V = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

From the second equation: $\lambda = 2/R$. Substituting in the first:

$$4\pi R + 2\pi H - \dfrac{2}{R}(2\pi R H) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 4R + 2H - 4H = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad H = 2R$$

From the third equation:

$$\pi R^2 (2R) = V \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R^* = \left(\dfrac{V}{2\pi}\right)^{1/3}, \quad H^* = 2R^*$$

**Numerical answer for $V = 100 \text{ m}^3$:**

$$R^* = \left(\dfrac{100}{2\pi}\right)^{1/3} = (15.915)^{1/3} \approx 2.516 \text{ m}$$

$$H^* = 5.032 \text{ m}$$

**Verification of minimum** (eliminate $H$ and differentiate $f$ twice):

$$f(R) = 2\pi R^2 + \dfrac{2V}{R}$$

$$\dfrac{d^2 f}{dR^2} = 4\pi + \dfrac{4V}{R^3} > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{confirmed minimum}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls in Question A:**
> 1. **Forgetting the volume constraint** while writing the objective: −2 marks.
> 2. **Sign error in Lagrangian**: many students write $\mathcal{L} = f + \lambda h$ instead of $f - \lambda h$. This flips the sign of $\lambda$ but the final answer is the same; however, presenting both equations inconsistently loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not verifying second-order conditions**: −1 mark.
> 4. **Unit mistakes**: writing $R = (V/(2\pi))^{1/2}$ instead of $1/3$: −3 marks (entire algebra collapses).
> 5. **Missing the final numerical substitution**: −1 mark.

---

### Question B (Alternative 14-mark choice) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]`

> **(a)** **[7 marks]** Derive the **optimum pipe diameter** $D^*$ for pumping an incompressible fluid at flow rate $Q$ through a horizontal pipe of length $L$, given that pipe cost is proportional to $D$ and pumping cost is proportional to $\Delta P_f \cdot Q$. Use the Fanning equation with constant friction factor $f$.
>
> **(b)** **[7 marks]** Show that at the optimum, the **annual pumping cost is exactly 1/5 of the annual pipe capital cost**. Compute $D^*$ numerically for the data:
> $L = 500 \text{ m}$, $Q = 0.02 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $f = 0.005$, pipe cost coefficient $a = 1500 \text{ Rs/m of diameter per m of length}$, electricity cost coefficient $b = 0.05 \text{ Rs per W}$, interest rate $i = 0.1$, plant life $N = 10$ yr. Take $8760$ operating hours/year.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Derivation

(See Section 3.2 above for the full derivation.) Summary:

**Step 1:** $v = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$

**Step 2:** Fanning: $\Delta P_f = 32 f L \rho Q^2 / (\pi^2 D^5)$

**Step 3:** Total cost:

$$C(D) = \beta \cdot a L \cdot D + 8760 \cdot b \cdot \dfrac{32 f L \rho Q^3}{\pi^2 D^5} \equiv A_1 D + \dfrac{A_2}{D^5}$$

**Step 4:** $\dfrac{dC}{dD} = A_1 - 5A_2/D^6 = 0 \Rightarrow D^* = (5A_2/A_1)^{1/6}$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Capital vs Pumping Ratio

At $D = D^*$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{cap}}(D^*) &= A_1 D^* \\
C_{\text{pump}}(D^*) &= \dfrac{A_2}{D^{*5}} = \dfrac{A_1 D^*}{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\dfrac{C_{\text{pump}}}{C_{\text{cap}}} = \dfrac{1}{5}$.

**Numerical evaluation:**

**Step 1 — Compute $A_1$:** Present Worth Factor at $i=0.1$, $N=10$:

$$\beta = \dfrac{0.1 (1.1)^{10}}{(1.1)^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{0.1 \cdot 2.5937}{1.5937} = 0.16275$$

$$A_1 = \beta \cdot a \cdot L = 0.16275 \cdot 1500 \cdot 500 = 1.22 \times 10^{5} \text{ Rs/m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A_2$:**

$$A_2 = 8760 \cdot b \cdot \dfrac{32 f L \rho Q^3}{\pi^2}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{32 f L \rho Q^3}{\pi^2} &= \dfrac{32 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 500 \cdot 1000 \cdot (0.02)^3}{\pi^2} \\
&= \dfrac{32 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 500 \cdot 1000 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696} \\
&= \dfrac{640}{9.8696} = 64.84 \text{ W}\cdot\text{m}^5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$A_2 = 8760 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 64.84 = 28402 \text{ Rs}\cdot\text{m}^5$$

**Step 3 — Compute $D^*$:**

$$D^* = \left(\dfrac{5 \cdot 28402}{1.22 \times 10^5}\right)^{1/6} = (1.1639)^{1/6}$$

Using $1.1639^{1/6} \approx \exp(\ln 1.1639 / 6) = \exp(0.1519/6) = \exp(0.02531) \approx 1.0256$

$$\boxed{D^* \approx 0.0256 \text{ m} = 25.6 \text{ mm}}$$

This is the **economic pipe diameter**; the nearest commercial size (1 inch ≈ 25.4 mm) would be selected in practice.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls in Question B:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Present Worth Factor $\beta$** when computing annualized capital cost: −2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units**: $Q$ in m³/s but hours in hours/yr → must multiply by $8760$: −2 marks.
> 3. **Raising to the wrong power**: students write $(5A_2/A_1)^{1/5}$ instead of $1/6$: −4 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the $1:5$ ratio explicitly**: −1 mark.
> 5. **Picking an off-the-shelf pipe without comment** (real engineers round up): acceptable, but acknowledge it.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1, Typical Applications of Optimization**

- **Every** chemical engineering optimization problem has **3 components**: decision variables $\mathbf{x}$, objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$, constraints (equality, inequality, bounds).
- **Formulation** is the most important skill tested in Module 1 — not the solver.
- The **optimal cylindrical tank with flat ends has $H = 2R$** regardless of volume. This is the most-quoted result.
- The **fifth-power rule** $\Delta P_f \propto 1/D^5$ leads to the **1:5 ratio** between pumping cost and capital cost at the optimum pipe diameter.
- **Unconstrained optimum** → set $\nabla f = 0$.
- **Equality-constrained optimum** → use **Lagrange multipliers**, set $\nabla \mathcal{L} = 0$.
- **Inequality-constrained optimum** → use **KKT conditions** (introduced formally in Module 2 but already expected conceptually in Module 1).
- Always **verify the second-order condition** ($\dfrac{d^2 f}{dx^2} > 0$ for min, $<0$ for max).
- Always check whether the **optimum is at a boundary** rather than interior — Kuhn–Tucker conditions are needed.
- **Linear Programming (LP)** problems in chemical engineering include: blending, allocation, multi-product planning, refinery scheduling.
- **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** problems include: tank design, pipe sizing, reactor optimization, distillation operating point.
- **Cost functions** typically contain a **capital term** (scaling with size) and an **operating term** (scaling inversely with size); the optimum is where these two contributions balance.
- **Present Worth Factor (PWF)** must be used to annualize capital cost when comparing with annual operating cost.
- **Numerical values to memorize** (for $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$): $R^* \approx 2$ m, $H^* \approx 4$ m.
- The KTU examiner **loves to ask** for: (i) formulation, (ii) Lagrange solution of a 1-constraint problem, (iii) verification that the result is a minimum, (iv) numerical answer for a specific data set.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Typical Chemical Engineering Applications of Optimization.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statement of an Optimization Problem and Its Essential Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Statement of an Optimization Problem and Its Essential Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Optimization Problem** is a mathematical statement whose objective is to determine the values of decision variables from a permissible (feasible) set that either minimizes or maximizes a defined criterion function, while satisfying a set of stated constraints.

In the language of the KTU 2024 Scheme for Process Optimization (PECHT755), the **Statement of an Optimization Problem** is the formal translation of a real-world engineering decision-making scenario into a precise mathematical model consisting of three essential pillars:

1. **Decision Variables (x):** The parameters the engineer can control.
2. **Objective Function (f(x)):** A scalar performance index to be extremized (minimized or maximized).
3. **Constraints (g(x) and h(x)):** The physical, economic, safety, and regulatory limitations that bound the search space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Canonical Formulation:**
> $$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x)$$
> subject to:
> $$g_i(x) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$
> $$h_j(x) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p$$
> $$x \in \mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$$
> where $\mathcal{S}$ is the **feasible region**, the set of all admissible design vectors. A point $x^*$ is said to be the **optimal solution** if $f(x^*) \le f(x)$ for all $x \in \mathcal{S}$ (for a minimization problem).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are planning a road trip from Kochi to Delhi. You have a few real choices to make:
- **The route** (which highways to take),
- **The fuel stops** (where to refuel),
- **The time of departure** (leaving now or after a meal).

You want to **minimize** total travel time (your *objective*). But you are constrained by:
- Your car can only hold **40 litres** of fuel at a time (*constraint*),
- You must be home within **36 hours** to attend a function (*constraint*),
- Your budget is capped at **₹5,000** (*constraint*).

The set of *every* possible combination of route, stops, and timings that satisfies all three rules is the **feasible region**. The *single best combination* among them is the **optimum**. That is, in essence, what every optimization problem—whether it is sizing a heat exchanger, tuning a distillation column, or planning a refinery—asks the engineer to do.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Optimization is not about finding *a* workable answer; it is about finding the *best possible* answer within a clearly defined set of permissible answers. The "statement" of the problem is the engineering blueprint that separates wishful thinking from rigorous mathematical problem-solving.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

In Process Optimization (PECHT755), the decision variables and objective functions carry physical units. Common metrics include:
- **Cost** (₹, USD, or ₹/kg of product).
- **Profit** (₹/year or % yield).
- **Conversion** (dimensionless, typically $\le 1$).
- **Selectivity** (dimensionless, typically $\le 1$).
- **Temperature** (K), **Pressure** (Pa), **Flow rate** (kg/s or m³/h), **Composition** (mole fraction).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** When the objective is to maximize a quantity (e.g., profit), the canonical form requires you to convert it into a minimization by negating the function. For example, $\max P(x) \equiv \min -P(x)$. Failing to do this is a frequent deduction point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Essential Features of an Optimization Problem

Any genuine engineering optimization problem must be reducible to the following structural skeleton. KTU examiners typically award marks for explicitly listing and defining these features.

#### 2.1.1 Decision Variables (Design Variables)

These are the independent quantities that the optimizer is permitted to manipulate. In a chemical process, they might be reactor temperature, feed flow rate, or reflux ratio. Mathematically, they form a vector:

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

**The 'Why':** Without decision variables, there is nothing to "decide." The entire problem hinges on isolating which knobs you can actually turn in a real plant.

**The 'How':** Variables are bounded by either *physical limits* (e.g., $T > 0$ K) or *inequality constraints* imposed by equipment ratings.

#### 2.1.2 Objective Function (Criterion / Performance Function)

A scalar-valued function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that maps a decision vector to a single real number representing performance. Common objectives in chemical engineering include:
- Minimize **total annualized cost** ($/yr).
- Maximize **conversion** or **yield**.
- Minimize **energy consumption** (kW).
- Minimize **environmental impact** (kg CO₂/hr).

**The 'Why':** Without an objective, every feasible solution is equally good—the problem has no meaning. The objective is the *yardstick* by which "best" is defined.

**The 'How':** It is derived from first principles, thermodynamic correlations, cost data, or experimental regression models.

#### 2.1.3 Constraints (Equality and Inequality)

Mathematical expressions defining the boundaries of the feasible region.

- **Equality Constraints** $h_j(x) = 0$: Represent physical laws, mass/energy balances, or specifications that *must* be exactly satisfied.
  * *Example:* Stoichiometric relation: $x_A + x_B = 1$ (mole fractions summing to unity).
- **Inequality Constraints** $g_i(x) \le 0$: Represent capacity limits, safety margins, equipment restrictions, or quality specifications.
  * *Example:* Reactor wall temperature must not exceed design: $T_{wall} \le 600\,°C$.

**The 'Why':** Real engineering always operates under restrictions. An unbounded problem has either a trivial answer (go to infinity) or no answer at all.

**The 'How':** Constraints are typically written in the canonical form $g_i(x) \le 0$ (less than or equal) and $h_j(x) = 0$ by convention. Greater-than constraints are multiplied by $-1$ to convert them.

### 2.2 The Feasible Region (Search Space)

The **feasible region** $\mathcal{S}$ is the subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ that satisfies *all* constraints simultaneously.

$$\mathcal{S} = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_i(x) \le 0 \, \forall i, \; h_j(x) = 0 \, \forall j \}$$

A point inside $\mathcal{S}$ is a **feasible point**. The set of all $x^*$ that minimize $f$ over $\mathcal{S}$ is the **optimal set**, and the corresponding $f(x^*)$ is the **optimal value**.

### 2.3 Classification of Optimization Problems (KTU 2024)

| Property | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Variables | **Unconstrained** vs **Constrained** | Find $T$ to minimize cost with no rules vs. with pipe-rating limits |
| Objective | **Linear** vs **Nonlinear** | $f(x) = ax + b$ vs. $f(x) = x^2 + \sin(x)$ |
| Variables | **Continuous** vs **Discrete / Integer** | Reflux ratio $R \in [0.5, 5]$ vs. Number of stages $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| Solution nature | **Single-objective** vs **Multi-objective** | One profit target vs. Pareto front (cost vs. emissions) |
| Randomness | **Deterministic** vs **Stochastic** | Fixed feed vs. noisy feed composition |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Element | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning | Units (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decision Vector | $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$ | Set of controllable parameters | Mixed |
| Objective Function | $f(x) \rightarrow \min$ or $\max$ | Performance criterion | ₹/yr, %, K |
| Inequality Constraint | $g_i(x) \le 0, \; i = 1, \dots, m$ | Capacity, safety, or quality limits | Mixed |
| Equality Constraint | $h_j(x) = 0, \; j = 1, \dots, p$ | Material/energy balances, specs | Mixed |
| Feasible Region | $\mathcal{S} = \{ x \mid g_i \le 0, h_j = 0\}$ | All admissible design vectors | Subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| Optimal Point | $f(x^*) \le f(x), \; \forall x \in \mathcal{S}$ | The best feasible solution | — |
| Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda, \mu) = f(x) + \sum \lambda_j h_j + \sum \mu_i g_i$ | Combines objective + constraints | Mixed |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The statement of an optimization problem is the universal language used in:
- **Process Design:** Finding the optimal diameter and length of a heat exchanger to minimize cost subject to heat duty.
- **Refinery Scheduling:** Maximizing profit from crude oil blending given inventory and demand constraints.
- **Supply Chain:** Minimizing transportation cost across a network with capacity limits.
- **Reactor Engineering:** Maximizing conversion in a CSTR subject to residence time and temperature constraints.
- **Pollution Control:** Minimizing flue gas emissions subject to production rate targets.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "state the optimization problem" for any given engineering scenario, always write the explicit minimization (or maximization) form with all variables, the objective, and the constraints clearly separated. A well-structured statement is worth at least 2 marks in Part A.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Classical CSTR Optimization Statement

**Problem Setup:** A continuously stirred tank reactor (CSTR) carries out a first-order liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow B$. The objective is to **maximize the steady-state conversion $X_A$** by selecting the operating temperature $T$ and residence time $\tau$, subject to:
1. A maximum allowable temperature: $T \le 350\,K$ (safety).
2. A maximum allowable residence time: $\tau \le 3000\,s$ (plant capacity).
3. The CSTR design equation (material balance): $X_A = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau}$, where $k = k_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E}{RT}\right)$.

Let us *formally state* this problem step by step.

#### Step 1: Identify the Decision Variables

The variables we can control are temperature $T$ and residence time $\tau$. We assemble them into the decision vector:

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} T \\ \tau \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 2: Identify the Objective Function

The goal is to maximize $X_A$. Since KTU canonical form is *minimization*, we multiply by $-1$:

$$f(x) = -X_A = -\dfrac{k(T) \cdot \tau}{1 + k(T) \cdot \tau}$$

where $k(T) = k_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E}{RT}\right)$ with $k_0$ and $E$ being kinetic parameters and $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$ the universal gas constant.

#### Step 3: Identify the Constraints

**Inequality constraints** (converted to $\le 0$ form):

$$g_1(x) = T - 350 \le 0$$
$$g_2(x) = \tau - 3000 \le 0$$

**Implicit lower bounds** (physical positivity):

$$g_3(x) = -T \le 0, \quad g_4(x) = -\tau \le 0$$

**Equality constraints:** None in this case (the conversion equation is absorbed into the objective via the model).

#### Step 4: Assemble the Complete Mathematical Statement

We can now write the complete optimization problem in canonical form:

$$\min_{T, \tau} \quad -\dfrac{k_0 \exp(-E/RT) \cdot \tau}{1 + k_0 \exp(-E/RT) \cdot \tau}$$

subject to:

$$T - 350 \le 0$$
$$\tau - 3000 \le 0$$
$$-T \le 0$$
$$-\tau \le 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The CSTR model is nonlinear in $T$ (exponential Arrhenius term) and nonlinear in $\tau$ (rational function). Thus this is a **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** problem. KTU Module 2 and 3 will focus on the numerical techniques to solve such problems.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: From Real Scenario to Math

| Stage | Engineering Question | Mathematical Translation |
|---|---|---|
| 1. *Goal Identification* | What does "best" mean? (cost, profit, conversion?) | Choose $f(x)$ to minimize or maximize |
| 2. *Variable Selection* | What can I control? | Define $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 3. *Model Derivation* | How do the variables affect performance? | Establish $f(x)$ from physics/data |
| 4. *Constraint Listing* | What limits exist in the real plant? | Convert to $g_i(x) \le 0, h_j(x) = 0$ |
| 5. *Statement Assembly* | Combine all above | Canonical form with $\min$ and $\le 0$ |
| 6. *Feasibility Check* | Is the search space non-empty? | Verify $\mathcal{S} \neq \emptyset$ |

### 3.3 Derivation of the Lagrangian Form (Foundational for KTU)

When the problem is constrained, KTU Module 1 also requires expressing the **Lagrangian function**, which combines the objective and constraints using multipliers. The Lagrangian is defined as:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda, \mu) = f(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j h_j(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mu_i g_i(x)$$

where $\lambda_j$ are the **equality multipliers** and $\mu_i \ge 0$ are the **inequality multipliers** (Karush–Kuhn–Tucker multipliers, to be covered in depth in Module 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Although Lagrangian derivation is formally Module 2 (KKT conditions), the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus explicitly states that students must "state an optimization problem with essential features," which includes recognizing that constraints must be merged with the objective for solution. Always write the Lagrangian skeleton in your answer if asked for a "complete statement."

### 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Example: A 2-Variable Linear Case

Consider a simple linear optimization problem (Linear Programming, LP):

$$\max_{x_1, x_2} \quad Z = 3x_1 + 5x_2$$
subject to:
$$x_1 \le 4$$
$$2x_2 \le 12$$
$$3x_1 + 2x_2 \le 18$$
$$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

To convert to canonical minimization form, we multiply the objective by $-1$:

$$\min_{x_1, x_2} \quad f(x) = -3x_1 - 5x_2$$

Inequality constraints in canonical $\le 0$ form:

$$g_1(x) = x_1 - 4 \le 0$$
$$g_2(x) = 2x_2 - 12 \le 0$$
$$g_3(x) = 3x_1 + 2x_2 - 18 \le 0$$
$$g_4(x) = -x_1 \le 0$$
$$g_5(x) = -x_2 \le 0$$

The feasible region is the convex polygon with corner points obtained by intersecting the binding constraints. The optimal point (for the maximization) is found at the vertex where $3x_1 + 2x_2 = 18$ and $2x_2 = 12$ intersect, giving:

$$x_2 = 6, \quad x_1 = 2, \quad Z = 3(2) + 5(6) = 36$$

This is the **complete problem statement** for a simple LP that KTU may ask you to formulate in Part A.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Anatomy of an Optimization Problem

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete conceptual structure of an optimization problem statement, showing how the three essential features interact to define the optimum.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real-World Engineering Scenario] --> B[Identify Decision Variables x1, x2 ... xn]
    B --> C{Identify Performance Criterion}
    C --> D[Define Objective Function f of x]
    D --> E{Identify Plant Limitations}
    E --> F[List Inequality Constraints g_i of x is less or equal 0]
    E --> G[List Equality Constraints h_j of x is equal 0]
    F --> H[Assemble Canonical Statement]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Feasible Region S contains all admissible x]
    I --> J[Apply Numerical Solver or Analytical Method]
    J --> K[Optimal Solution x star]
    K --> L[Optimal Objective Value f of x star]
    L --> M[Engineering Implementation in Real Plant]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram maps the flow of information in the optimization process, treating it as a feedback control system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer
        V[Decision Variables x]
        O[Objective Function f of x]
```
