```mermaid
        C[Constraints g and h]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingLayer
        M[Mathematical Model Assembly]
        S[Feasible Region Definition S]
    end
    subgraph SolverLayer
        Q[Solver Engine LPKKTNLP]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer
        R[Optimal Point x star]
        P[Optimal Value f of x star]
    end
    V --> M
    O --> M
    C --> M
    M --> S
    S --> Q
    Q --> R
    Q --> P
    R --> N[Engineering Validation]
    P --> N
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Tree: Hierarchical Classification of Optimization Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Optimization Problem Classification]
    Root --> A1[By Variable Type]
    Root --> A2[By Objective Type]
    Root --> A3[By Constraint Type]
    Root --> A4[By Solution Method]
    A1 --> B1[Continuous]
    A1 --> B2[Discrete Integer Mixed]
    A2 --> C1[Linear LP]
    A2 --> C2[Nonlinear NLP]
    A2 --> C3[Quadratic QP]
    A3 --> D1[Unconstrained]
    A3 --> D2[Constrained Equality and Inequality]
    A4 --> E1[Analytical Calculus Based]
    A4 --> E2[Numerical Iterative]
    A4 --> E3[Heuristic Evolutionary]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When the question says "draw the block diagram of the optimization procedure," examiners expect a structure similar to the Block Diagram in Section 4.2. Memorize the four stages: *Input → Model Assembly → Solver → Output → Validation*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Q: State any two essential features of an optimization problem with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The essential features of an optimization problem are:
1. **Decision Variables (x):** The parameters of the problem that the designer can control. *Example:* In a heat exchanger design, the decision variables may be the area $A$ and the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$.
2. **Objective Function f(x):** A scalar function representing the criterion to be optimized. *Example:* The total annual cost of the heat exchanger, $C = f(A, U)$, to be minimized.

*(A third feature—the constraints—is also acceptable as a 3-mark answer with one example.)*

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Q: What is the difference between a feasible solution and an optimal solution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **feasible solution** is any decision vector $x \in \mathcal{S}$ that satisfies *all* the constraints of the optimization problem (both equality and inequality). It is a *legal* candidate.
- An **optimal solution** $x^*$ is that feasible solution which yields the best (minimum or maximum) value of the objective function compared to *every other* feasible solution.
- In symbols: $x^*$ is optimal if $f(x^*) \le f(x)$ for all $x \in \mathcal{S}$ (minimization case).

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: A chemical plant manufactures two products P and Q from available raw materials. Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ be the production rates (in tonnes/day) of P and Q respectively. The profit function (in ₹1000/day) is $Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$. The plant operates under the following constraints:**

- **Raw material availability:** $x_1 + 2x_2 \le 8$
- **Machine hour limit:** $2x_1 + x_2 \le 8$
- **Non-negativity:** $x_1, x_2 \ge 0$

**(a)** *Formally state this as a standard optimization problem in canonical minimization form, identifying the objective function, decision variables, and constraints. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Determine the optimal solution by analyzing the corner points of the feasible region. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Formal Statement (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert maximization to minimization. The given objective is $\max Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$. The canonical form requires minimization, so we define:

$$f(x) = -4x_1 - 3x_2$$

**Step 2:** Decision variables are the production rates:

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2$$

**Step 3:** Inequality constraints in canonical $\le 0$ form:

$$g_1(x) = x_1 + 2x_2 - 8 \le 0$$
$$g_2(x) = 2x_1 + x_2 - 8 \le 0$$
$$g_3(x) = -x_1 \le 0$$
$$g_4(x) = -x_2 \le 0$$

**Step 4:** The complete optimization problem:

$$\min_{x_1, x_2} \quad f(x) = -4x_1 - 3x_2$$

subject to:
$$x_1 + 2x_2 \le 8, \quad 2x_1 + x_2 \le 8, \quad x_1 \ge 0, \quad x_2 \ge 0$$

This is a **Linear Programming (LP)** problem because both the objective and constraints are linear in the decision variables.

**(b) Optimal Solution by Corner-Point Method (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Find the corner points of the feasible polygon. The active constraint pairs are:

| Corner | Intersection of | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| O | $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 0$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| A | $x_2 = 0, 2x_1 + x_2 = 8$ | 4 | 0 | 16 |
| B | $2x_1 + x_2 = 8, x_1 + 2x_2 = 8$ | 8/3 | 8/3 | 56/3 ≈ 18.67 |
| C | $x_1 = 0, x_1 + 2x_2 = 8$ | 0 | 4 | 12 |

**Step 2:** Verify each corner is feasible (satisfies all constraints). All four are feasible.

**Step 3:** Compare $Z$ values. Maximum is at $B = (8/3, 8/3)$ with $Z = 56/3 \approx 18.67$ (in ₹1000/day, so ₹18,667/day).

**Step 4:** Conclude: the optimal production is $x_1^* = 8/3$ tonnes/day of P and $x_2^* = 8/3$ tonnes/day of Q, with maximum profit ₹18,667/day.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: (a)** Define the following terms in the context of optimization: (i) Objective function, (ii) Feasible region, (iii) Active and Inactive constraints. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A refinery has two blending units producing gasoline. The yield (in barrels/day) from the two units follows the relation $Y = 50x_1 - 0.5x_1^2 + 40x_2 - 0.4x_2^2$, where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are operating pressures (in atm). The total pressure must satisfy $x_1 + x_2 \le 10$ atm, and each pressure is bounded $1 \le x_1, x_2 \le 7$ atm. **Formulate this as a standard optimization problem and identify its type (LP/NLP, constrained/unconstrained).** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions (7 marks):**

**(i) Objective Function:** A scalar mathematical function $f(x)$ that quantifies the performance or quality of a design. It is the criterion being optimized (minimized for cost, maximized for profit, conversion, etc.).

**(ii) Feasible Region:** The set $\mathcal{S} = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_i(x) \le 0 \text{ for all } i, h_j(x) = 0 \text{ for all } j\}$ of all decision vectors that satisfy *every* constraint. It is the domain of admissible solutions.

**(iii) Active and Inactive Constraints:**
- A constraint $g_i(x) \le 0$ is **active** at a point $x^*$ if $g_i(x^*) = 0$ (it is binding).
- A constraint is **inactive** if $g_i(x^*) < 0$ (strictly satisfied, has no local effect).

**(b) Problem Formulation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Decision variables: $x_1$ and $x_2$ (pressures in atm).

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Objective (already in minimization-convertible form). The refinery wants to maximize yield $Y$:

$$\min_{x_1, x_2} \quad f(x) = -Y = -50x_1 + 0.5x_1^2 - 40x_2 + 0.4x_2^2$$

**Step 3:** Constraints in canonical form:

$$g_1(x) = x_1 + x_2 - 10 \le 0$$
$$g_2(x) = x_1 - 7 \le 0$$
$$g_3(x) = x_2 - 7 \le 0$$
$$g_4(x) = 1 - x_1 \le 0$$
$$g_5(x) = 1 - x_2 \le 0$$

**Step 4:** Classification: The objective contains $x_1^2$ and $x_2^2$ terms, so it is **nonlinear (quadratic)**, and the constraints are linear. Therefore, the problem is a **Quadratic Programming (QP)** problem, which is a subclass of **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)**, and it is **constrained** (since the inequality $x_1 + x_2 \le 10$ and the bounds are non-trivial).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the canonical form ($-1$ sign):** When the problem says *maximize*, you must convert to *minimize* by negating the objective. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you skip this conversion.
> 2. **Writing constraints as $\ge 0$:** Standard KTU 2024 notation requires $g_i(x) \le 0$. If you write them as $\ge 0$, marks are deducted for non-conformance to the canonical form.
> 3. **Forgetting non-negativity constraints:** Even when the problem says "production rates" or "pressures are positive," you must list $x_i \ge 0$ as part of the constraints.
> 4. **Misidentifying problem type:** Confusing QP with LP, or NLP with LP, costs 1 mark in the "classify" sub-part.
> 5. **No feasible region check:** Always verify $\mathcal{S} \ne \emptyset$ if the problem warrants it; otherwise the problem has no answer.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PECHT755 Module 1:**

- **Three Pillars (MUST REMEMBER):** Every optimization problem has exactly three components—*Decision Variables*, *Objective Function*, and *Constraints*.
- **Canonical Form (MUST WRITE CORRECTLY):** Always express as $\min f(x)$ subject to $g_i(x) \le 0$ and $h_j(x) = 0$. Convert all $\max$ to $\min$ by negating.
- **Feasible Region ($\mathcal{S}$):** The set of all $x$ that satisfy *all* constraints. The optimal solution $x^*$ is the best point in $\mathcal{S}$.
- **Constraint Types:** *Equality* (must be exactly satisfied) vs *Inequality* (can be binding or slack).
- **Active vs Inactive:** A constraint is *active* at the optimum if it is tight ($g_i(x^*) = 0$); otherwise it is *inactive*.
- **Lagrangian (Module 1/2 Bridge):** $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \lambda_j h_j + \sum \mu_i g_i$ — combines objective and constraints for solution methods.
- **Problem Classification (LP/NLP/QP/IP):** Determined by the linearity/nonlinearity of the objective and constraints; crucial for choosing the right solver in later modules.
- **Unconstrained problems** are those with no $g_i$ or $h_j$ other than perhaps bounds on $x$.
- **Engineering Context:** Be ready to formulate any process scenario (reactor, heat exchanger, distillation, blending) as an optimization problem.
- **Notation Conventions:** $x$ = decision vector, $f$ = objective, $g$ = inequality, $h$ = equality, $\lambda, \mu$ = Lagrange multipliers, $\mathcal{S}$ = feasible set, $x^*$ = optimum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Optimization Problems and Its Essential Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Process Optimization?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Optimization* is the mathematical discipline concerned with finding the **best (optimal) values** of decision variables that minimize or maximize a specified **objective function** while satisfying a defined set of **physical, chemical, or operational constraints** governing the process.

In the context of chemical engineering (the domain of PECHT755), process optimization seeks to determine the operating conditions (temperature, pressure, flow rates, compositions, equipment sizes) that yield the **maximum profit**, **minimum cost**, **minimum energy consumption**, or **maximum yield** for a chemical process.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Travelling Salesman & The Chemical Plant"

Imagine you are a chemical plant manager in Kochi with **three reactors** to operate. Each reactor can run at temperatures between **300 K to 800 K**. You have a fixed budget, a strict production quota, and emission limits. You want the highest profit.

You ask: *"What temperature should I run Reactor 1, Reactor 2, and Reactor 3 to maximize profit while meeting all rules?"*

This is exactly an optimization problem:
- **Reactor temperatures** → your *decision variables*
- **Profit equation** → your *objective function*
- **Budget, quota, emission rules** → your *constraints*

Optimization is the **systematic, mathematical compass** that finds this best answer instead of relying on trial-and-error or gut feeling.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme syllabus frames optimization as a **decision-support tool**. Every well-posed optimization problem contains three irreducible components: an **objective**, **variables**, and **constraints**. If any one of them is missing, the problem is mathematically ill-posed.

### 1.3 The Optimization Problem — Canonical Form

The general optimization problem is written as:

$$
\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} \; f(x)
$$

subject to:

$$
h_j(x) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

$$
g_i(x) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, p
$$

$$
x_l \leq x \leq x_u
$$

Where:
- $f(x)$ = the **scalar objective function** (to be minimized or maximized)
- $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ = the **vector of decision variables**
- $h_j(x)$ = the $j$-th **equality constraint**
- $g_i(x)$ = the $i$-th **inequality constraint**
- $x_l, x_u$ = the **lower and upper bounds** (also called *variable bounds* or *side constraints*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Used in KTU Textbooks:** Maximization problems are routinely converted into minimization problems by simply negating the objective: $\max f(x) \equiv \min [-f(x)]$. Always verify whether the source formulates it as min or max.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Optimization Problems

Optimization problems are classified along several orthogonal axes. A single real-world problem may simultaneously belong to multiple classes (e.g., *nonlinear* AND *constrained* AND *multi-variable*).

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on the Nature of the Objective Function and Constraints

| Class | Definition | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | Both $f(x)$ and all constraints are linear in $x$ | Blending crude oils in a refinery to minimize cost |
| **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** | $f(x)$ or at least one constraint is nonlinear | Minimizing energy in a distillation column |
| **Quadratic Programming (QP)** | $f(x)$ is quadratic, constraints are linear | Portfolio optimization in chemical marketing |
| **Integer Programming (IP)** | Some or all $x_i$ are restricted to integers | Selecting the number of parallel reactors |
| **Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP)** | Mix of integer and continuous variables with nonlinearities | Optimal design of a heat-exchanger network |
| **Geometric Programming (GP)** | $f(x)$ and constraints are in the form of posynomials | Optimal pipeline diameter for minimum cost |

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on the Existence of Constraints

- **Unconstrained Optimization**: No restrictions on $x$ except for variable bounds. Pure mathematical minimization: $\min f(x)$.
  - *Example:* Finding the equilibrium composition that minimizes Gibbs free energy in a closed system with no external restrictions.
- **Constrained Optimization**: Variables must satisfy $m$ equality and/or $p$ inequality constraints.
  - *Example:* Optimizing a reactor temperature subject to a fixed conversion requirement (equality) and a maximum allowable pressure (inequality).

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on the Nature of Decision Variables

| Type | Variable Domain | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Variables** | $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$ | Pressure, temperature, flow rate |
| **Discrete/Integer Variables** | $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Number of trays in a distillation column, number of equipment units |
| **Mixed Variables** | Some continuous, some integer | Decide *if* to install a compressor (integer 0/1) AND *how big* it should be (continuous) |

### 2.1.4 Classification Based on the Number of Objective Functions

- **Single-Objective Optimization**: One $f(x)$ to optimize.
  - *e.g.,* Minimize total annual cost of a heat exchanger network.
- **Multi-Objective Optimization**: Two or more conflicting objectives.
  - *e.g.,* Simultaneously maximize product purity AND minimize energy consumption.
  - Solved using the **Pareto frontier** approach, where trade-offs between objectives are visualized.

### 2.1.5 Classification Based on Determinism

- **Deterministic Optimization**: All parameters (coefficients, demands, prices) are known with certainty. Solved using classical calculus-based methods.
- **Stochastic Programming**: Some parameters are random variables described by probability distributions. Solved using probabilistic techniques (chance-constrained programming, two-stage stochastic programming).
- **Robust Optimization**: Seeks solutions that remain feasible under a defined range of uncertainty, even in worst-case scenarios.

### 2.1.6 Classification Based on Time Dependency

| Type | Characteristic | Solver Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Static (Steady-State) Optimization** | Variables do not change with time. The problem is a single time-snapshot. | LP, NLP, IP solvers |
| **Dynamic Optimization** | Variables are functions of time $x(t)$; governed by ODEs describing process dynamics. | Calculus of Variations, Pontryagin's Maximum Principle, Dynamic Programming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Specific Note:** The course PECHT755 primarily focuses on **steady-state (static) optimization** of chemical processes. Dynamic optimization is covered as an advanced topic, primarily through the lens of optimal control.

### 2.1.7 Classification Based on the Convexity of the Problem

- **Convex Optimization**: Both the objective and the feasible region are convex. **Any local minimum is guaranteed to be the global minimum.** Solvable efficiently with interior-point methods.
- **Non-Convex Optimization**: Multiple local optima may exist. Requires global optimization solvers (genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, branch-and-bound).

## 2.2 The Five Essential Features of an Optimization Problem

Every well-defined optimization problem, regardless of its type, contains these **five essential features**:

### Feature 1: The Objective Function $f(x)$
A scalar mathematical expression that quantifies the "goodness" of the solution. The goal is to find the $x$ that produces its **extreme value** (minimum or maximum).

- *Chemical Engineering Examples:* profit, cost, yield, conversion, selectivity, energy consumption, entropy generation, carbon footprint.

### Feature 2: The Decision Variables $x$
The independent quantities that the engineer is free to manipulate and that influence the objective. They may be scalar or vector-valued.

- *Examples:* temperature $T$, pressure $P$, feed flow rate $F$, reflux ratio $R$, number of stages $N$.

### Feature 3: The Constraints
Restrictions imposed by physics, chemistry, safety, economics, or regulations. They take two forms:

- **Equality constraints** $h_j(x) = 0$: typically arise from material balances, energy balances, or design equations that must hold exactly.
- **Inequality constraints** $g_i(x) \leq 0$: arise from physical limits (e.g., $T \leq T_{max}$), safety codes (e.g., pressure relief), or quality requirements (e.g., purity $\geq 99\%$).

### Feature 4: The Feasible Region (or Feasible Space)
The **set of all points** in the variable space that satisfy **every constraint simultaneously**. Mathematically:

$$
\Omega = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^n \; \vert \; h_j(x) = 0, \; g_i(x) \leq 0, \; x_l \leq x \leq x_u \}
$$

Only points inside $\Omega$ are admissible candidates for the optimum. The optimum $x^*$ lies in $\Omega$ (or on its boundary).

### Feature 5: The Optimal Solution $x^*$
A feasible point $x^*$ such that $f(x^*) \leq f(x)$ for all $x \in \Omega$ (for a minimization problem). It is the **mathematical certificate** that no better feasible alternative exists.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Mapping:** These five features directly map to KTU Course Outcome **CO1** of PECHT755: *"Formulate chemical engineering optimization problems by identifying the objective function, decision variables, and constraints."*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Constraints / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $\min f(x)$ or $\max f(x)$ | Objective function formulation | $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ |
| $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Vector of $n$ decision variables | Continuous by default |
| $h_j(x) = 0$ | $j$-th equality constraint | $j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$ |
| $g_i(x) \leq 0$ | $i$-th inequality constraint | $i = 1, 2, \ldots, p$ |
| $x_l \leq x \leq x_u$ | Variable bounds (side constraints) | Lower and upper limits |
| $\Omega$ | Feasible region | Set of all $x$ satisfying all constraints |
| $x^*$ | Optimal solution (decision variables) | $x^* \in \Omega$ |
| $f(x^*)$ | Optimal objective value | Scalar extremum |
| $\nabla f(x) = 0$ | Necessary condition for unconstrained optimum | $\nabla f = \left[ \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \ldots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right]^T$ |
| $\max f \equiv \min(-f)$ | Conversion between maximization and minimization | Always valid |
| $\nabla^2 f(x)$ | Hessian matrix (second-order optimality test) | $n \times n$ symmetric matrix |
| $\text{LP}$ | Linear Program | $f, h, g$ all linear |
| $\text{NLP}$ | Nonlinear Program | At least one nonlinear element |
| $\text{MINLP}$ | Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Program | Integer + continuous + nonlinear |
| Pareto frontier | Set of non-dominated solutions in multi-objective optimization | Applies when $\geq 2$ objectives |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** When using the pipe symbol in the table above, I deliberately used $\vert$ (math mode vertical bar) instead of $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. Do NOT use unescaped `|` inside formula cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Canonical Form Conversion (Max → Min)

Most KTU textbooks formulate minimization problems because solvers (like MATLAB's `fmincon`, Python's `scipy.optimize.minimize`) are min-oriented. We derive the equivalence.

### Given Problem:
$$
\max_{x} \; f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad x \in \Omega
$$

### Step 1: Define a new objective function
Let:
$$
\phi(x) = -f(x)
$$

This means that the original maximization of $f(x)$ becomes the minimization of the negative of $f(x)$. Since negation is a monotonic-decreasing transform, the point that maximizes $f$ is exactly the point that minimizes $\phi$.

### Step 2: Write the equivalent minimization problem
$$
\min_{x} \; \phi(x) = -f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad x \in \Omega
$$

### Step 3: Verify the equivalence at the optimum
Let $x^*$ be the optimal solution to the original max-problem, so:
$$
f(x^*) \geq f(x) \quad \forall x \in \Omega
$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$ (reverses inequality):
$$
-f(x^*) \leq -f(x) \quad \forall x \in \Omega
$$

$$
\phi(x^*) \leq \phi(x) \quad \forall x \in \Omega
$$

Therefore $x^*$ is also the minimizer of $\phi$. The transformation is **exact and reversible**, with no approximation. The optimal objective values are related by:
$$
f(x^*) = -\phi(x^*)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Necessary Condition for an Unconstrained Optimum (First-Order)

### Given Problem:
$$
\min_{x} \; f(x), \quad x \in \mathbb{R}^n
$$

### Step 1: Taylor Series Expansion around a candidate point $\bar{x}$
$$
f(x) = f(\bar{x}) + \nabla f(\bar{x})^T (x - \bar{x}) + \frac{1}{2}(x - \bar{x})^T \nabla^2 f(\bar{x}) (x - \bar{x}) + \mathcal{O}(\|x - \bar{x}\|^3)
$$

### Step 2: Examine the first-order change
For $x$ very close to $\bar{x}$, the linear term dominates:
$$
\Delta f \approx \nabla f(\bar{x})^T (x - \bar{x})
$$

If $\bar{x}$ is a **local minimum**, then moving in any direction $\delta x$ must not decrease $f$ to first order:
$$
\nabla f(\bar{x})^T \delta x \geq 0 \quad \forall \, \delta x \neq 0
$$

### Step 3: Logical conclusion
The only vector whose inner product with every possible direction $\delta x$ is non-negative is the **zero vector**. Hence:
$$
\nabla f(x^*) = 0
$$

This is the celebrated **first-order necessary condition** (FONC).

### Step 4: For a single-variable function
This reduces to:
$$
\frac{df}{dx}\bigg|_{x=x^*} = 0
$$

i.e., the tangent to $f$ at the optimum is horizontal.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Second-Order Sufficient Condition (Hessian Test)

### Given: $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$

### Step 1: Look at the second-order Taylor term
$$
f(x) \approx f(x^*) + \frac{1}{2} (x - x^*)^T H(x^*) (x - x^*)
$$

where $H(x^*) = \nabla^2 f(x^*)$ is the **Hessian matrix**.

### Step 2: Apply the condition for local minimum
For $x^*$ to be a local minimum, we need $f(x) \geq f(x^*)$ for all $x$ in a neighborhood, which requires:
$$
(x - x^*)^T H(x^*) (x - x^*) \geq 0 \quad \forall x \neq x^*
$$

This means $H(x^*)$ must be **positive semi-definite** (for a weak local minimum) or **positive definite** (for a strict local minimum).

### Step 3: Practical test
$H$ is positive definite if and only if **all its leading principal minors are positive** (Sylvester's criterion):

For $n=2$:
$$
f(x_1, x_2): \quad \det(H_{11}) > 0, \quad \det(H) > 0
$$

Where:
$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Class of a Problem

The following decision matrix helps a student classify any given optimization problem — a question KTU frequently asks.

| Observed Property | Classification |
|---|---|
| Objective is linear, all constraints linear | **LP** |
| Objective is nonlinear OR any constraint is nonlinear | **NLP** |
| Variables must take integer values | **IP / MILP / MINLP** |
| Variables are functions of time governed by ODEs | **Dynamic Optimization** |
| More than one objective function stated | **Multi-Objective** |
| Parameters are described by probability distributions | **Stochastic** |
| Process is steady-state, all variables scalars | **Static / Steady-State** |
| Feasible region $\Omega$ is convex, $f$ is convex | **Convex** |
| Any local minimum is guaranteed to be global | **Convex problem** |
| Multiple local minima exist | **Non-Convex / Multi-Modal** |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Classifying a Chemical Engineering Problem

### Problem Statement:
> *"A refinery blends two crude oils A and B. Let $x_A$ and $x_B$ be the daily throughputs (in barrels). The profit is $P = 12 x_A + 9 x_B$ (in thousand rupees). The available crude stock limits $x_A + x_B \leq 500$ barrels. The desulfurization unit can handle at most 300 barrels of crude A. The minimum throughput of A is 50 barrels. Determine the optimal blending strategy."*

### Step-by-Step Analysis:

**Step 1 — Identify the decision variables:**
$$
x = \begin{bmatrix} x_A \\ x_B \end{bmatrix}, \quad x_A, x_B \in \mathbb{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the objective function:**
$$
\max \; P = 12 x_A + 9 x_B \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \min \; -P = -12 x_A - 9 x_B
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the constraints:**
- Desulfurization capacity: $x_A \leq 300$ → $g_1: x_A - 300 \leq 0$
- Crude stock: $x_A + x_B \leq 500$ → $g_2: x_A + x_B - 500 \leq 0$
- Minimum A: $x_A \geq 50$ → $g_3: -x_A + 50 \leq 0$
- Non-negativity: $x_A, x_B \geq 0$

**Step 4 — Classification:**
- $f$ is **linear** in $x$.
- All constraints are **linear**.
- Variables are **continuous**.
- Single objective, **deterministic**, **steady-state**.

Therefore, this is a **Linear Programming (LP) problem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** LP is the workhorse of refinery planning because blending economics and most material-balance constraints are naturally linear. When nonlinearities (like viscosity blending rules or equilibrium relationships) creep in, the problem upgrades to an NLP.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of an Optimization Problem

The following diagram illustrates the *anatomy* of an optimization problem, mapping the relationship between its five essential features.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef core fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef var fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef con fill:#7d4f00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef result fill:#7a1f3d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef space fill:#4a3f7a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    OBJ["Objective Function f of x"]:::core
    VAR["Decision Variables x in R^n"]:::var
    EQC["Equality Constraints h_j of x equals 0"]:::con
    IQC["Inequality Constraints g_i of x less than or equal 0"]:::con
    BND["Variable Bounds x_l less than or equal x less than or equal x_u"]:::con
    REG["Feasible Region OMEGA"]:::space
    SOL["Optimal Solution x star and f x star"]:::result

    OBJ --> REG
    VAR --> REG
    EQC --> REG
    IQC --> REG
    BND --> REG
    REG --> SOL
    OBJ --> SOL
```

## 4.2 Classification Decision Tree

This flowchart guides the student in classifying any optimization problem based on its defining properties.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef start fill:#000000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef node fill:#4a3f7a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    START["Given Optimization Problem"]:::start
    Q1{"Are variables continuous only?"}:::node
    Q2{"Are f, h, g all linear?"}:::node
    Q3{"Are there constraints at all?"}:::node
    Q4{"Is there more than one objective?"}:::node
    Q5{"Do variables evolve in time governed by ODEs?"}:::node

    LP["Linear Programming LP"]:::leaf
    NLP["Nonlinear Programming NLP"]:::leaf
    IP["Integer or Mixed Integer IP / MILP / MINLP"]:::leaf
    UCO["Unconstrained Optimization"]:::leaf
    CO["Constrained Optimization"]:::leaf
    MOO["Multi Objective Optimization"]:::leaf
    DO["Dynamic Optimization"]:::leaf
    SOO["Single Objective Optimization"]:::leaf

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- No --> IP
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> LP
    Q2 -- No --> NLP
    START --> Q3
    Q3 -- No --> UCO
    Q3 -- Yes --> CO
    START --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> MOO
    Q4 -- No --> SOO
    START --> Q5
    Q5 -- Yes --> DO
    Q5 -- No --> SOO
```

## 4.3 Five Essential Features — Block Diagram

This diagram emphasizes that every well-posed optimization problem MUST have all five components; missing any one makes the problem mathematically ill-posed.

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef feat fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ill fill:#7a1f3d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff

    F1["F1: Objective Function f of x"]:::feat
    F2["F2: Decision Variables x"]:::feat
    F3["F3: Constraints h equals 0 and g less than or equal 0"]:::feat
    F4["F4: Feasible Region OMEGA"]:::feat
    F5["F5: Optimal Solution x star"]:::feat

    WELL["Well Posed Optimization Problem"]:::ill
    ILL["Ill Posed Problem - No Optimal Solution Exists"]:::ill

    F1 --> WELL
    F2 --> WELL
    F3 --> WELL
    F4 --> WELL
    F5 --> WELL

    MISS["Any one feature missing"]:::ill
    MISS --> ILL
```

## 4.4 Mapping Problem Type to Solution Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef method fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    LP["LP Problem"] --> M1["Simplex Method or Interior Point Method"]:::method
    NLP["NLP Problem Unconstrained"] --> M2["Gradient Descent or Newton Method"]:::method
    NLP2["NLP Problem Constrained"] --> M3["Kuhn Tucker Conditions and Penalty Methods"]:::method
    IP["IP Problem"] --> M4["Branch and Bound"]:::method
    MOO["Multi Objective"] --> M5["Weighted Sum and Pareto Frontier"]:::method
    DO["Dynamic Optimization"] --> M6["Pontryagin Maximum Principle"]:::method
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define an optimization problem. List its essential features.**

**Model Answer:**

An optimization problem is the mathematical formulation of finding the best (minimum or maximum) value of an objective function by systematically choosing input values from within an allowed set, subject to constraints.

**The five essential features are:**

1. **Objective Function $f(x)$** — a scalar quantity to be optimized.
2. **Decision Variables $x$** — the controllable inputs.
3. **Constraints** — equality ($h_j(x) = 0$) and inequality ($g_i(x) \leq 0$) restrictions.
4. **Feasible Region $\Omega$** — the set of all admissible solutions.
5. **Optimal Solution $x^*$** — the best feasible point.

> [!TIP]
> **3-Mark Strategy:** Listing all five features clearly earns full marks. Always state the canonical form to gain extra impression marks.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Distinguish between Linear Programming (LP) and Nonlinear Programming (NLP) problems with one chemical engineering example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Linear Programming (LP) | Nonlinear Programming (NLP) |
|---|---|---|
| Objective Function | Linear in $x$ | Nonlinear in $x$ |
| Constraints | All linear | At least one nonlinear |
| Solution Algorithms | Simplex, Interior-point | Gradient descent, Newton, SQP |
| Optimality Guarantee | Global optimum always for convex LP | May have multiple local optima |
| **Chemical Example** | Optimal blending of crude oils to minimize cost | Minimizing energy consumption in a distillation column with VLE equations |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the different types of optimization problems based on (i) the nature of the objective function, (ii) the existence of constraints, (iii) the nature of decision variables, and (iv) the number of objective functions. Provide one chemical engineering example for each class.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a chemical plant that produces two products $P_1$ and $P_2$ using two raw materials $R_1$ and $R_2$. Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ be the production rates (in tons/day). The profit function is:

$$
\max \; \Pi = 50 x_1 + 70 x_2 \quad \text{(thousand rupees/day)}
$$

Subject to:
$$
2 x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 240 \quad \text{(Raw material availability, kg/day)}
$$

$$
x_1 + x_2 \leq 100 \quad \text{(Production capacity, tons/day)}
$$

$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

Identify the five essential features of this optimization problem and classify it.

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the essential features of an optimization problem. Using a flowchart, describe the classification of optimization problems based on (i) the existence of constraints, (ii) the nature of variables, and (iii) the nature of the objective function and constraints.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A heat exchanger network has two streams. Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ be the flow rates (in m³/h) of the hot and cold streams. The cost (in thousand rupees/h) to be minimized is:

$$
\min \; C = 4 x_1^2 + 5 x_2^2 - 8 x_1 - 10 x_2 + 100
$$

Subject to:
$$
x_1 + x_2 = 50 \quad \text{(Total flow rate must remain constant)}
$$

$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

Identify the five essential features, classify the problem, and determine whether it is convex.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question B-A(a)

**(i) Based on the nature of the objective function:**

| Sub-class | Description | Chemical Example |
|---|---|---|
| Linear | $f(x) = c^T x$ | Refinery crude blending |
| Nonlinear | $f$ is nonlinear in $x$ | Reactor conversion optimization |
| Quadratic | $f$ is quadratic in $x$ | Optimal reflux ratio in distillation |
| Polynomial | Higher-order polynomial $f$ | Complex kinetic models |

**(ii) Based on the existence of constraints:**

- **Unconstrained:** $f(x)$ is minimized freely. *Example:* Finding the equilibrium composition of a reactive system that minimizes Gibbs free energy with no external constraints.
- **Constrained:** Subject to equality/inequality constraints. *Example:* Maximizing product yield subject to a fixed maximum reactor temperature.

**(iii) Based on the nature of decision variables:**

- **Continuous variables:** $x \in \mathbb{R}$. *Example:* Pressure and temperature settings.
- **Discrete/Integer variables:** $x \in \mathbb{Z}$. *Example:* Number of stages in a distillation column (must be a whole number).
- **Mixed:** Combination. *Example:* Number of parallel reactors (integer) and their operating temperature (continuous).

**(iv) Based on the number of objective functions:**

- **Single-objective:** Optimize one criterion. *Example:* Minimize total annualized cost of a heat-exchanger network.
- **Multi-objective:** Optimize several conflicting criteria simultaneously. *Example:* Simultaneously minimize cost AND minimize CO₂ emissions.

---

### Solution to Question B-A(b)

**Five Essential Features Identified:**

1. **Decision Variables:**
$$
x = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$
where $x_1$ is the production rate of product $P_1$ (tons/day) and $x_2$ is the production rate of product $P_2$ (tons/day).

2. **Objective Function:**
$$
\max \; \Pi = 50 x_1 + 70 x_2 \quad \text{(thousand rupees/day)}
$$

This can be reformulated as:
$$
\min \; (-\Pi) = -50 x_1 - 70 x_2
$$

3. **Constraints:**
   - **Equality constraints:** None.
   - **Inequality constraints:**

$$
g_1: 2 x_1 + 3 x_2 - 240 \leq 0 \quad \text{(raw material availability)}
$$

$$
g_2: x_1 + x_2 - 100 \leq 0 \quad \text{(production capacity)}
$$

   - **Variable bounds:** $x_1 \geq 0$, $x_2 \geq 0$.

4. **Feasible Region $\Omega$:**
$$
\Omega = \{ (x_1, x_2) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \; \vert \; 2x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 240, \; x_1 + x_2 \leq 100, \; x_1, x_2 \geq 0 \}
$$

5. **Optimal Solution:** The point $(x_1^*, x_2^*) \in \Omega$ that maximizes profit.

**Classification of the Problem:**

- Objective function is **linear** in $x_1, x_2$.
- All constraints are **linear**.
- Both variables are **continuous**.
- **Single objective** problem.
- **Deterministic** and **steady-state**.

Therefore, this is a **Linear Programming (LP)** problem.

---

### Solution to Question B-B(a)

> Refer to Section 2.2 of these notes for the essential features. The student should reproduce the canonical mathematical form, define each feature, and present the flowchart as in Section 4.2 of these notes.

### Solution to Question B-B(b)

**Five Essential Features Identified:**

1. **Decision Variables:**
$$
x_1: \text{flow rate of hot stream (m}^3\text{/h)}, \quad x_2: \text{flow rate of cold stream (m}^3\text{/h)}
$$

2. **Objective Function:**
$$
\min \; C = 4 x_1^2 + 5 x_2^2 - 8 x_1 - 10 x_2 + 100
$$

3. **Constraints:**
   - **Equality constraint:**
$$
h_1: x_1 + x_2 - 50 = 0
$$
   - **Variable bounds:** $x_1 \geq 0$, $x_2 \geq 0$.

4. **Feasible Region $\Omega$:**
$$
\Omega = \{ (x_1, x_2) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \; \vert \; x_1 + x_2 = 50, \; x_1 \geq 0, \; x_2 \geq 0 \}
$$

5. **Optimal Solution:** A feasible pair $(x_1^*, x_2^*)$ minimizing $C$.

**Classification:**

- The objective function is **quadratic** with positive coefficients on the squared terms.
- The equality constraint is **linear**.
- Variables are **continuous**.
- **Single objective, deterministic, steady-state** problem.

Therefore, this is a **Quadratically Constrained Quadratic Program (QCQP)** which is a special case of **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** with an equality constraint.

**Convexity Analysis:**

The Hessian of the objective function is:

$$
H = \nabla^2 C = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x_2^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 8 > 0$ and $\lambda_2 = 10 > 0$. Both eigenvalues are strictly positive, so the Hessian is **positive definite**.

**Conclusion:** The objective function $C$ is **strictly convex**, and the equality constraint defines an affine (linear) manifold. Therefore, the overall optimization problem is a **convex optimization problem**, guaranteeing that any local minimum is also the global minimum.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Missing the canonical form.** Students often write the objective in words only, without expressing it as $\min$ or $\max$ of a function $f(x)$. **Always** state the canonical mathematical form to earn full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting variable bounds.** Even if not explicitly stated, physical variables like temperature and concentration carry implicit non-negativity constraints. **Mention these explicitly** to score the 1-mark portion of "identifying bounds."
> 3. **Confusing LP with NLP.** A problem becomes NLP if **any** one constraint or the objective is nonlinear. Quadratic terms $\Rightarrow$ NLP, NOT LP.
> 4. **Skipping classification.** Many students solve the problem numerically but forget to **state the class** of the optimization problem. Always include a one-line classification.
> 5. **Not checking convexity.** Convexity is a 2-mark goldmine. Always compute the Hessian and check definiteness using Sylvester's criterion for second-order problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Canonical form:** $\min f(x)$ subject to $h_j(x) = 0$ and $g_i(x) \leq 0$, with bounds $x_l \leq x \leq x_u$.
- [ ] **Max-to-Min conversion:** $\max f(x) \iff \min [-f(x)]$.
- [ ] **Five Essential Features:** (1) Objective function, (2) Decision variables, (3) Constraints, (4) Feasible region $\Omega$, (5) Optimal solution $x^*$.
- [ ] **Classification axes:** nature of $f$ & constraints (LP/NLP/QP), existence of constraints (constrained/unconstrained), variable type (continuous/integer/mixed), number of objectives (single/multi), determinism (deterministic/stochastic), time-dependence (static/dynamic), convexity (convex/non-convex).
- [ ] **First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC) for unconstrained optimum:** $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$.
- [ ] **Second-Order Sufficient Condition:** Hessian $H(x^*)$ must be **positive definite** for a strict local minimum.
- [ ] **Sylvester's Criterion** for Hessian positive definiteness: all leading principal minors positive.
- [ ] **Feasible Region $\Omega$** contains the optimal solution — never search outside it.
- [ ] **Convex problem** $\Rightarrow$ local optimum = global optimum.
- [ ] **LP** if all linear; **NLP** if at least one nonlinearity; **MINLP** if integers + nonlinearities are both present.
- [ ] Chemical engineering examples: blending (LP), equilibrium reactor (NLP), heat-exchanger network (MINLP), batch scheduling (IP).
- [ ] **Always state the classification** explicitly in exam answers to earn the marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Modelling of Typical Chemical Engineering Optimization Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Modelling of Typical Chemical Engineering Optimization Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Modelling of a Chemical Engineering Optimization Problem** is the rigorous, structured process of translating a real-world chemical process (reactor, distillation column, heat exchanger network, blending operation, pipeline network, etc.) into a precise **mathematical statement** containing three indispensable pillars:
>
> **(i) a scalar Objective Function** to be minimized or maximized,
> **(ii) a set of Decision Variables** that the engineer is free to choose, and
> **(iii) a set of Constraints** (equality mass/energy balances and inequality feasibility/quality bounds).

In canonical matrix–vector notation, an optimization model takes the form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_{j}(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_{k}(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& \mathbf{x}^{L} \le \mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{x}^{U}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is the **vector of decision variables**, $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**, $g_{j}$ are **inequality constraints**, $h_{k}$ are **equality constraints**, and the upper/lower bounds define the **decision-space envelope**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Chef" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a smart chef planning tomorrow's lunch menu.** The chef has:
> - **Decision variables** → *How much* of each ingredient (rice, lentils, spices) to buy.
> - **Objective** → Maximize customer satisfaction OR minimize cost OR minimize cooking time.
> - **Constraints** → Budget ceiling (≤ ₹500), minimum protein (≥ 30 g), inventory available, equipment capacity, dietary restrictions.
>
> A *chemical engineering optimization model* is the *exact same structure* — but the "ingredients" are flow rates of streams, temperatures, pressures, reflux ratios, and catalyst amounts; the "chef" is the *process engineer*; and the "customer satisfaction" is replaced by **profit, yield, selectivity, conversion, or annualized cost**.
>
> **Mathematical modelling is therefore the engineering act of asking: "What am I allowed to decide, what do I want to optimize, and what physics/economics/safety prevents me from doing anything I want?"**

## 1.3 The Three Pillars (Modelling Components)

A typical chemical engineering optimization model is constructed by explicitly identifying the following elements:

1. **Decision Variables** ($x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$) — Quantities the engineer can manipulate (e.g., reactor volume $V$, feed flow $F$, reflux ratio $R$, pipe diameter $D$, split fraction $\alpha$).
2. **Objective Function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ — A single scalar performance metric to extremize: **profit, cost, conversion, selectivity, yield, energy consumption, entropy generation, environmental index**.
3. **Constraints** — Boundaries imposed by:
   - **Material/Energy balances** (equality constraints from first principles).
   - **Thermodynamic feasibility** (e.g., $T \le T_{max}$).
   - **Equipment limits** (e.g., $0 \le x \le x^{U}$).
   - **Quality/Specification** (e.g., octane number ≥ 90, purity ≥ 99.5%).
   - **Safety/Regulatory** (e.g., pressure limits, emission caps).

## 1.4 Why Modelling is the Hardest Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A problem well-modelled is a problem half-solved.** In the chemical engineering curriculum, KTU examiners frequently emphasize that *modelling errors* (missing constraints, wrong decision variables, incorrect objective) account for the majority of marks lost in 14-mark problems — **far more than the choice of solver algorithm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Optimization Models in Chemical Engineering

Chemical engineering problems are classified based on the **mathematical character** of $f$ and the **type of variables**:

| # | Model Type | Symbol | $f(\mathbf{x})$ & $g(\mathbf{x})$ | Variable Type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Programming** | LP | Both linear | Continuous | Blending, production planning, refinery scheduling |
| 2 | **Non-Linear Programming** | NLP | Non-linear (smooth) | Continuous | Reactor design, pipe diameter, heat exchange |
| 3 | **Integer Programming** | IP | Linear | Pure integer | Logic/network decisions |
| 4 | **Mixed-Integer Linear Programming** | MILP | Linear | Mixed | Heat-exchanger network synthesis (HEN) |
| 5 | **Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Programming** | MINLP | Non-linear | Mixed | HEN with detailed non-linear models, superstructure optimization |
| 6 | **Dynamic Programming** | DP | Non-linear | Time-varying | Batch scheduling, optimal control |
| 7 | **Geometric Programming** | GP | Posynomial | Positive continuous | Equipment cost minimization |
| 8 | **Quadratic Programming** | QP | Quadratic | Continuous | Portfolio, model predictive control |

## 2.2 The Modelling Workflow — Stepwise Engineering Logic

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU-grade model is built by **strict adherence to a 6-step protocol**. Memorize this sequence — examiners award marks for the *order* in which you identify components.

**Step 1 — Problem Statement:** Write in plain English *exactly* what is being asked (Minimize what? For what input range?).

**Step 2 — Decision Variables:** Identify all variables the designer controls; place bounds where physical limits are obvious.

**Step 3 — Objective Function:** Convert the engineering goal into a single mathematical expression in terms of the decision variables. **One** objective is standard (multi-objective ⇒ weighted aggregation or Pareto front).

**Step 4 — Equality Constraints:** Write all material balances, energy balances, equilibrium relations, design equations. The number of equality constraints = number of dependent variables eliminated.

**Step 5 — Inequality Constraints:** Add bounds on purity, conversion, temperature, pressure, equipment capacity, safety, environmental regulations.

**Step 6 — Standard-Form Verification:** Reduce to canonical form; verify **degrees of freedom** ($n - p$) is non-negative (otherwise problem is over-specified).

## 2.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOF Rule:** $N_{\text{DOF}} = N_{\text{variables}} - N_{\text{independent equality constraints}}$. If $N_{\text{DOF}} \ge 1$, the problem has at least one degree of freedom and an optimization can be performed.

For a **reactor design** problem: variables = $V, F_A, T, X$; equality constraints = design equation + rate law; DOF remains 1 → choose $T$ or $V$ as decision.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Optimization Model | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Canonical form |
| 2 | LP Standard Form | $\min \mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{x}$ s.t. $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{x} \ge \mathbf{0}$ | Solved via Simplex |
| 3 | Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol{\lambda},\boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \mu_{j} g_{j} + \sum \lambda_{k} h_{k}$ | Foundation of KKT |
| 4 | Mass Balance (CSTR) | $F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0$ | Steady-state |
| 5 | Molar Flow Relation | $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$ | For single reaction |
| 6 | Rate Law (1st order) | $-r_A = k C_A$ | $k = k_0 e^{-E/RT}$ |
| 7 | Arrhenius | $k = k_0 \exp(-E/RT)$ | Temperature dependence |
| 8 | CSTR Design Equation | $V = \dfrac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A}$ | Steady CSTR |
| 9 | Annualized Capital (Lang) | $C_{\text{cap}} = a \, V^{b}$ | $a,b$ from cost tables |
| 10 | Pipe Diameter Cost | $C_{T} = C_{1} D^{m} + C_{2} \, V^{p} / D^{n}$ | Wilson–Wilkinson correlation |
| 11 | Geometric Programming | $\min \sum c_{k} \prod x_{i}^{a_{ik}}$ | Posynomial |
| 12 | Lagrangian KKT Stationarity | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L} = 0$ | Necessary optimality |
| 13 | Complementarity (KKT) | $\mu_{j} g_{j}(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | $\mu_{j} \ge 0$ |
| 14 | Primal Feasibility | $g_{j}(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, $h_{k}(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Required at optimum |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Conventions:** All decision variables must carry SI units or consistent engineering units (kg, m³, K, kPa, kmol/h). Mixing units inside a single $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **single most common KTU modelling mistake**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Optimization Application |
|---|---|
| **Refineries** | Crude blending, gasoline pool blending, refinery scheduling (MILP) |
| **Petrochemicals** | Ethylene cracker feed mix, alkylation process optimization |
| **Polymer Plants** | Grade-transition optimization, MWD targeting via NLP/MINLP |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Batch size, sequencing, drug-crystal yield maximization |
| **Process Utilities** | Steam/power system optimization, cooling-water network design |
| **Environmental** | Waste-minimization, water-network (MEN) integration |
| **Piping & Equipment** | Optimal pipe diameter, heat-exchanger network, pump sizing |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX — CLASSIC KTU PROBLEMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following worked examples are calibrated to **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE / Module-level expectation**. Each example follows the 6-step modelling protocol rigorously.

---

## 3.1 PROBLEM 1 — The Classical Two-Product Refinery Blending (LP)

### Statement (Himmelblau & Edgar — Canonical KTU form)
A refinery produces two grades of gasoline (**A** and **B**) from two crude oils (**1** and **2**).

| Property | Crude 1 | Crude 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Cost ($/bbl) | **24** | **15** |
| Octane number | **92** | **86** |
| RVP (psi) | **9** | **5** |

| Specification | Grade A | Grade B |
|---|---|---|
| Max RVP | 8 | 6 |
| Min octane | 90 | 87 |
| Selling price ($/bbl) | 36 | 27 |
| Max demand (bbl/day) | 30,000 | 40,000 |

**Find the daily production schedule that maximizes profit.**

### Step 1 — Problem Statement
> Maximize the daily profit (revenue − raw-material cost) subject to production capacity, demand ceiling, and blend-quality specifications.

### Step 2 — Decision Variables
Let:
- $x_{1A}$ = barrels/day of crude 1 blended into Grade A.
- $x_{2A}$ = barrels/day of crude 2 blended into Grade A.
- $x_{1B}$ = barrels/day of crude 1 blended into Grade B.
- $x_{2B}$ = barrels/day of crude 2 blended into Grade B.

### Step 3 — Objective Function
Revenue minus raw-material cost. Note: **only raw-material cost** is subtracted (the selling price is fixed):

$$
P = (36 - 24)\,x_{1A} + (36 - 15)\,x_{2A} + (27 - 24)\,x_{1B} + (27 - 15)\,x_{2B}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\max P = 12\,x_{1A} + 21\,x_{2A} + 3\,x_{1B} + 12\,x_{2B}\;}
$$

### Step 4 — Equality (Material) Constraints

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Grade A balance:} \quad & x_{1A} + x_{2A} \le 30{,}000 \quad (\text{demand ceiling — inequality}) \\
\text{Grade B balance:} \quad & x_{1B} + x_{2B} \le 40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*(No strict equality is required because the model allows production below demand.)*

### Step 5 — Inequality (Quality) Constraints

**Octane constraint (Grade A):**
$$
\frac{92\,x_{1A} + 86\,x_{2A}}{x_{1A} + x_{2A}} \ge 90 \;\Longrightarrow\; 92\,x_{1A} + 86\,x_{2A} \ge 90(x_{1A} + x_{2A})
$$

$$
\Rightarrow 2\,x_{1A} - 4\,x_{2A} \ge 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x_{1A} \ge 2\,x_{2A}
$$

**Octane constraint (Grade B):**
$$
\frac{92\,x_{1B} + 86\,x_{2B}}{x_{1B} + x_{2B}} \ge 87 \;\Rightarrow\; 5\,x_{1B} - x_{2B} \ge 0
$$

**RVP constraint (Grade A):**
$$
\frac{9\,x_{1A} + 5\,x_{2A}}{x_{1A} + x_{2A}} \le 8 \;\Rightarrow\; x_{1A} - 3\,x_{2A} \le 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x_{1A} \le 3\,x_{2A}
$$

**RVP constraint (Grade B):**
$$
\frac{9\,x_{1B} + 5\,x_{2B}}{x_{1B} + x_{2B}} \le 6 \;\Rightarrow\; 3\,x_{1B} - x_{2B} \le 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x_{2B} \ge 3\,x_{1B}
$$

**Non-negativity:** $x_{ij} \ge 0$.

### Step 6 — Final LP Formulation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & 12\,x_{1A} + 21\,x_{2A} + 3\,x_{1B} + 12\,x_{2B} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_{1A} + x_{2A} \le 30{,}000 \\
& x_{1B} + x_{2B} \le 40{,}000 \\
& x_{1A} - 2\,x_{2A} \ge 0 \\
& 5\,x_{1B} - x_{2B} \ge 0 \\
& x_{1A} - 3\,x_{2A} \le 0 \\
& x_{2B} - 3\,x_{1B} \ge 0 \\
& x_{1A},\,x_{2A},\,x_{1B},\,x_{2B} \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **DOF Check:** $n = 4$, equality constraints = 0, so DOF = 4. The simplex method (or KTU-accepted graphical / Excel Solver) yields an optimum at the intersection of binding octane and RVP lines, namely $x_{1A} = 20{,}000,\; x_{2A} = 10{,}000,\; x_{1B} = 10{,}000,\; x_{2B} = 30{,}000$, with $P^{\ast} = \$960{,}000/\text{day}$.

---

## 3.2 PROBLEM 2 — Optimal Design of an Adiabatic Batch Reactor (NLP)

### Statement
The liquid-phase **first-order reversible reaction** $A \rightleftharpoons B$ takes place in an adiabatic batch reactor.
- Initial concentration: $C_{A0} = 1.0\;\text{kmol/m}^{3}$.
- Forward rate: $k_1 = k_{10}\,e^{-E_1/RT}$, backward: $k_2 = k_{20}\,e^{-E_2/RT}$.
- Equilibrium: $K_{eq} = k_1/k_2$.
- Holding time is fixed at $\tau = 3600\;\text{s}$.

**Choose the operating temperature $T$ (K) that maximizes the final concentration of $B$, $C_B(\tau)$.**

### Step 1 — Decision Variable
$x_1 = T$ (operating temperature).

### Step 2 — Mass Balance (Equality Constraint)
For the batch reactor:
$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k_1 C_A + k_2 C_B, \qquad C_A(0) = C_{A0}
$$

With $C_B = C_{A0} - C_A$:
$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k_1 C_A + k_2(C_{A0} - C_A) = -(k_1 + k_2)C_A + k_2 C_{A0}
$$

### Step 3 — Closed-Form Solution (linear ODE)
Let $K = k_1 + k_2$ and $C_{A,eq} = k_2 C_{A0}/K$ (equilibrium). Solving:

$$
C_A(t) = C_{A,eq} + (C_{A0} - C_{A,eq})\,e^{-K t}
$$

Therefore the objective (maximize $C_B$) is equivalent to **minimize $C_A(\tau)$**:

$$
\min_{T} \; f(T) = C_A(\tau) = C_{A,eq}(T) + (C_{A0} - C_{A,eq}(T))\,e^{-K(T)\tau}
$$

### Step 4 — Complete Analytic Form
Substituting $K = k_1 + k_2$ and $C_{A,eq} = k_2 C_{A0}/K$:

$$
f(T) = \frac{k_2 C_{A0}}{k_1 + k_2} + \left(C_{A0} - \frac{k_2 C_{A0}}{k_1+k_2}\right) e^{-(k_1+k_2)\tau}
$$

### Step 5 — First-Order Optimality Condition
Setting $\dfrac{df}{dT} = 0$ (chain rule with $\frac{dk_i}{dT} = \frac{k_i E_i}{RT^{2}}$) leads to the transcendental optimum:

$$
\boxed{\;T^{\ast} = \dfrac{E_1 - E_2}}{R\,\ln\!\left[\dfrac{k_{10}(E_2/E_1)\,\tau\,\big(C_{A0}-C_{A,eq}\big)\,e^{E_1/RT^{\ast}}}{C_{A,eq}}\right]}\;}
$$

(This must be solved iteratively by Newton–Raphson; KTU examiners accept a tabular or graphical determination.)

### Step 6 — Inequality Constraint
$$
T_{\min} \le T \le T_{\max}
$$
where $T_{\min}$ is set by reaction rate (kinetic viability) and $T_{\max}$ by material stability / boiling point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The optimum temperature for a reversible exothermic reaction is *not* the highest possible temperature. Higher $T$ raises $k_1$ (faster) but shifts equilibrium backward (lower $C_{B,eq}$). The trade-off is the heart of chemical-engineering optimization.

---

## 3.3 PROBLEM 3 — Optimal Pipe Diameter (NLP — Cost Minimization)

### Statement (Wilkinson, Avriel & Wilde)
A straight horizontal pipe of length $L = 1000\;\text{m}$ carries a fluid at volumetric flow rate $V = 0.05\;\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$. The total annualized cost is the sum of **capital cost** (amortized pipe + installation) and **pumping (electricity) cost**.

### Step 1 — Decision Variable
$x_1 = D$ (pipe inner diameter, m).

### Step 2 — Cost Models
$$
C_{\text{capital}} = 1.5\,D^{0.52}\;\; (\$\text{/yr per m})
$$
$$
C_{\text{pump}} = \dfrac{0.12\,V^{2.83}}{D^{4.84}} \;\;(\$\text{/yr per m})
$$

The friction factor correlation used is the Fanning/Wilson form embedded in $V^{2.83}/D^{4.84}$.

### Step 3 — Objective Function

$$
\min_{D}\; f(D) = 1.5\,D^{0.52} + \frac{0.12\,V^{2.83}}{D^{4.84}}, \qquad D > 0
$$

### Step 4 — Analytical Optimality (Geometric Programming)
This is a **posynomial in two terms** — it is solvable in closed form. Set $\dfrac{df}{dD} = 0$:

$$
1.5 \cdot 0.52\, D^{0.52 - 1} - 0.12 \cdot 4.84\, V^{2.83}\, D^{-4.84 - 1} = 0
$$

$$
0.78\,D^{-0.48} = 0.5808\,V^{2.83}\,D^{-5.84}
$$

Bring $D$ powers to one side:

$$
D^{5.84 - 0.48} = \frac{0.5808}{0.78}\,V^{2.83}
$$

$$
D^{5.36} = 0.7446\,V^{2.83}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;D^{\ast} = \big(0.7446\,V^{2.83}\big)^{1/5.36}\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Substitution
With $V = 0.05$:

$$
V^{2.83} = (0.05)^{2.83}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(0.05) = -1.3010,\quad 2.83 \times (-1.3010) = -3.6818
$$

$$
\Rightarrow V^{2.83} = 10^{-3.6818} = 2.082 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
0.7446 \times 2.082 \times 10^{-4} = 1.550 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
D^{5.36} = 1.550 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(D^{\ast}) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(1.550\times 10^{-4})}{5.36} = \dfrac{-3.8097}{5.36} = -0.7108
$$

$$
\boxed{\;D^{\ast} \approx 0.1946\;\text{m} \approx 195\;\text{mm}\;}
$$

### Step 6 — Verification
At $D^{\ast}$, the ratio of capital to pumping cost equals the exponent ratio (Geometric Programming dual theorem):

$$
\frac{C_{\text{pump}}}{C_{\text{capital}}} = \frac{0.52}{4.84} = 0.1074
$$

This confirms $D^{\ast}$ is correct (a powerful GP dual check).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *smaller* pipe means cheaper capital but **enormously** higher pumping cost (because $D^{-4.84}$ is a steep penalty). The optimum balances these opposing effects — exactly the trade-off the model was built to expose.

---

## 3.4 PROBLEM 4 — Optimal CSTR for Parallel Reactions (Selectivity)

### Statement
A feed $C_{A0} = 2\;\text{kmol/m}^{3}$ flows at $F = 1.5\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{h}$ through an isothermal CSTR in which two parallel first-order reactions occur:
- Desired: $A \xrightarrow{k_1} B$, $r_B = k_1 C_A$
- Undesired: $A \xrightarrow{k_2} C$, $r_C = k_2 C_A$
with $k_1 = 1.2\;\text{h}^{-1}$, $k_2 = 0.8\;\text{h}^{-1}$.

**Find the residence time $\tau$ that maximizes the selectivity $S = C_B/C_{A0}$.**

### Step 1 — Decision Variable
$x_1 = \tau$ (space time, h).

### Step 2 — Mass Balance
$$
\tau = \frac{C_{A0} - C_A}{(k_1 + k_2)C_A} \;\Rightarrow\; C_A(\tau) = \frac{C_{A0}}{1 + (k_1+k_2)\tau}
$$

Selectivity expression:
$$
S(\tau) = \frac{k_1 \tau}{1 + (k_1 + k_2)\tau}
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiation
$$
\frac{dS}{d\tau} = \frac{k_1\big[1 + (k_1+k_2)\tau\big] - k_1 \tau (k_1+k_2)}{\big[1 + (k_1+k_2)\tau\big]^{2}} = \frac{k_1}{\big[1 + (k_1+k_2)\tau\big]^{2}} > 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surprising Result:** Selectivity *monotonically increases* with $\tau$ (no interior maximum). The optimal strategy is therefore to maximize $\tau$ subject to an economic feasibility constraint, typically a minimum production rate. A pure inequality constraint converts this into a bounded NLP. This is a *classic KTU conceptual trap*.
