# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unimodal Function (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A function $f(x)$ defined on a closed interval $[a, b]$ is said to be **unimodal** if there exists a point $x^* \in [a, b]$ such that $f(x)$ is **strictly monotonically decreasing** on the interval $[a, x^*]$ and **strictly monotonically increasing** on the interval $[x^*, b]$ (for a minimization problem). Equivalently, for maximization, the function is strictly increasing on $[a, x^*]$ and strictly decreasing on $[x^*, b]$.

Mathematically, a unimodal function on $[a, b]$ satisfies the following condition for some unique interior or boundary point $x^*$:

$$\forall \, x_1, x_2 \in [a, b], \quad x_1 < x_2 \le x^* \implies f(x_1) > f(x_2)$$

$$\forall \, x_1, x_2 \in [a, b], \quad x^* \le x_1 < x_2 \implies f(x_1) < f(x_2)$$

Where $x^*$ is the location of the **global minimum** (or maximum, depending on problem type) on the interval $[a, b]$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Valley Analogy:**
> Imagine you are standing on a mountain ridge at dawn and looking at a valley below. The valley has exactly **one lowest point** (the river bed). As you walk down one slope, the elevation strictly decreases. Once you cross the river, the elevation strictly increases up the other slope. The terrain on either side of the river is **monotonic** — it never goes up, then down, then up again. This is precisely what a unimodal function looks like when plotted: **one trough, no multiple bumps, no plateaus with dips.**

If, however, the terrain had two valleys separated by a small hillock, then the function would be **multimodal** — containing multiple local minima — and direct one-dimensional search methods would fail to guarantee finding the deepest point without additional global search strategies.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Unimodal vs. Multimodal Functions

| Property | Unimodal Function | Multimodal Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Optima** | Exactly **one** global optimum (max or min) | **Multiple** local optima |
| **Monotonicity** | Strictly monotonic on either side of $x^*$ | Oscillates; non-monotonic regions exist |
| **Search Reliability** | Guaranteed convergence to global optimum within interval | Convergence depends on starting point |
| **KTU Relevance** | Basis of Fibonacci, Golden Section, Bisection | Requires stochastic / global methods (GA, Simulated Annealing) |

## 1.4 Physical / Engineering Context

In **process optimization**, many engineering problems (reactor temperature profiles, distillation reflux optimization, pipe diameter vs. cost trade-off) naturally produce unimodal response surfaces over a sensible bounded region. This is why classical direct search methods — which assume unimodality — are the **workhorse of single-variable optimization** in chemical engineering design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:**
> The **interval reduction ratio** ($L_n / L_0$) measures how much the original uncertainty interval $[a, b]$ is shrunk after $n$ function evaluations. A smaller ratio implies faster convergence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Unimodal Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinal Property (Basis of All 1-D Search):**
> If $f(x)$ is unimodal on $[a, b]$ and $a < x_1 < x_2 < b$, then **only one** of the following conditions can hold:
> 1. If $f(x_1) \le f(x_2)$ → the minimum lies in $[a, x_2]$, so $[x_2, b]$ can be **eliminated**.
> 2. If $f(x_1) > f(x_2)$ → the minimum lies in $[x_1, b]$, so $[a, x_1]$ can be **eliminated**.

This is the foundational logic behind interval elimination methods.

## 2.2 Logic of Interval Reduction

- **Step 1:** Start with the bounded interval $[a, b]$ where the optimum is known to exist.
- **Step 2:** Place two interior test points $x_1$ and $x_2$ such that $a < x_1 < x_2 < b$.
- **Step 3:** Evaluate $f(x_1)$ and $f(x_2)$.
- **Step 4:** Compare the function values and **discard** the sub-interval that **cannot contain** the optimum.
- **Step 5:** Repeat until the interval length $(b - a)$ is smaller than a pre-specified tolerance $\varepsilon$.

## 2.3 Why Unimodality Matters in Process Optimization

- **Chemical Reactor Design:** Optimum temperature for maximum yield over a wide range is often unimodal.
- **Heat Exchanger Network:** Optimum approach temperature showing a single minimum.
- **Economic Trade-offs:** Cost functions typically show a single U-shape when plotted against a design variable.
- **Distillation Column:** Reflux ratio vs. total cost exhibits a clear unimodal behavior.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unimodal Condition (min) | $f(x_1) \le f(x_2) \Rightarrow x^* \in [a, x_2]$ | Interval elimination rule | — |
| Unimodal Condition (min) | $f(x_1) > f(x_2) \Rightarrow x^* \in [x_1, b]$ | Interval elimination rule | — |
| Interval Reduction Ratio | $r_n = L_n / L_0$ | Ratio of final to initial interval length | dimensionless |
| Final Interval Length | $L_n = L_0 \cdot r_n$ | After $n$ evaluations | same as $x$ |
| Stopping Criterion | $\vert b_n - a_n \vert \le \varepsilon$ | Convergence tolerance | same as $x$ |
| Symmetric Test Points | $x_1 = a + (1 - \tau)L$, $x_2 = a + \tau L$ | $\tau \in (0, 1)$ placement ratio | same as $x$ |
| Symmetry Constraint | $x_1 + x_2 = a + b$ | To reuse one evaluation per iteration | same as $x$ |
| Elimination Length Fraction | $L_{k+1} = L_k - (x_2 - x_1)$ | After discarding one segment | same as $x$ |
| Cumulative Reduction | $L_n = L_0 \cdot \tau^{n-1}$ | When both points reusable (Golden Section) | same as $x$ |
| Function Evaluation Count | $n = \lceil \log_\tau(\varepsilon / L_0) \rceil + 1$ | Iterations to converge | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Unimodal function analysis underpins **all direct search algorithms** in single-variable process optimization. It is a mandatory pre-requisite for understanding Fibonacci Search, Golden Section Search, Quadratic Interpolation, and Cubic Interpolation methods that appear in the KTU Process Optimization syllabus (Module 1 & 2).

## 2.5 Placement of Test Points (Why Symmetric?)

If we place two test points at $\tau L$ and $(1-\tau)L$ from the left boundary, and ensure the **elimination fraction** is identical on both sides, then one of the two previously evaluated points lies inside the new (reduced) interval. This permits **re-using one function evaluation** at each iteration, reducing the total computational cost. The optimal placement ratio $\tau$ is what distinguishes Fibonacci ($\tau = F_{n-1}/F_n$) from Golden Section ($\tau = (\sqrt{5}-1)/2 \approx 0.618$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Symmetric Test Point Placement for Maximum Reuse

### Problem Setup

We have a unimodal function $f(x)$ on interval $[a, b]$ of length $L_0 = b - a$. We wish to place two test points $x_1$ and $x_2$ such that:

- $a < x_1 < x_2 < b$
- Regardless of which sub-interval is eliminated, the **remaining interval contains exactly one of the two test points** (so it can be reused).
- The reduction ratio is the **same** whether we eliminate the left or right sub-interval.

### Derivation

Let $L_0 = b - a$ be the original interval length. Place the two test points symmetrically:

$$x_1 = a + (1 - \tau) L_0$$

$$x_2 = a + \tau L_0$$

Here $\tau$ is the placement ratio, with $0 < \tau < 0.5$ to ensure $x_1 < x_2$.

The distance between the test points is:

$$x_2 - x_1 = \tau L_0 - (1 - \tau) L_0 = (2\tau - 1) L_0$$

For the right-elimination case, if $f(x_1) \le f(x_2)$, we discard $[x_2, b]$. The new interval becomes $[a, x_2]$ with length:

$$L_{\text{new, right}} = x_2 - a = \tau L_0$$

For the left-elimination case, if $f(x_1) > f(x_2)$, we discard $[a, x_1]$. The new interval becomes $[x_1, b]$ with length:

$$L_{\text{new, left}} = b - x_1 = L_0 - (1 - \tau) L_0 = \tau L_0$$

Both cases yield the **same new interval length** $\tau L_0$. Therefore, the reduction ratio is:

$$r = \frac{L_{\text{new}}}{L_0} = \tau$$

After $n$ function evaluations (with one reused per iteration), the cumulative reduction is:

$$L_n = \tau \cdot L_{n-1} = \tau^2 \cdot L_{n-2} = \cdots = \tau^{n-1} \cdot L_0$$

Hence, the general formula for interval length after $n$ evaluations is:

$$\boxed{L_n = L_0 \cdot \tau^{n-1}}$$

### Solving for the Number of Iterations

Given a tolerance $\varepsilon$, we require $L_n \le \varepsilon$:

$$\tau^{n-1} \cdot L_0 \le \varepsilon$$

$$\tau^{n-1} \le \frac{\varepsilon}{L_0}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$(n-1) \ln \tau \le \ln \left( \frac{\varepsilon}{L_0} \right)$$

Since $\tau < 1$, $\ln \tau$ is negative, so inequality reverses:

$$(n-1) \ge \frac{\ln(\varepsilon / L_0)}{\ln \tau}$$

$$\boxed{n \ge 1 + \frac{\ln(\varepsilon / L_0)}{\ln \tau}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$n = \left\lceil \, 1 + \frac{\ln(\varepsilon / L_0)}{\ln \tau} \, \right\rceil$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Interval Reduction Using Unimodal Property

### Problem Statement

A chemical engineer is minimizing the cost function $f(x) = (x - 4)^2 + 2$ on the interval $[a, b] = [0, 8]$. Apply the unimodal interval elimination principle with $\tau = 0.3826$ (Fibonacci placement for a fixed number of evaluations).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Iteration 1:**
- $L_0 = 8 - 0 = 8$
- $x_1 = 0 + (1 - 0.3826)(8) = 0.6174 \times 8 = 4.9392$
- $x_2 = 0 + 0.3826 \times 8 = 3.0608$

Wait — this gives $x_1 > x_2$. To maintain $x_1 < x_2$, the convention is that $x_1$ is the **right-side** test point and $x_2$ is the **left-side** test point in some textbooks. Let us re-assign using the Fibonacci convention where $\tau > 0.5$:

- $\tau_F = 0.618$ (Golden ratio complement, common in KTU textbooks)
- $x_1 = 0 + (1 - 0.618)(8) = 3.056$
- $x_2 = 0 + 0.618 \times 8 = 4.944$

Evaluate:
- $f(x_1) = f(3.056) = (3.056 - 4)^2 + 2 = 0.892 + 2 = 2.892$
- $f(x_2) = f(4.944) = (4.944 - 4)^2 + 2 = 0.892 + 2 = 2.892$

For this symmetric quadratic, both evaluations are equal — special case. Conventionally, we discard the right segment, keeping $[0, 4.944]$ with new $L_1 = 4.944$.

**Iteration 2 (using reused $x_1 = 3.056$):**
- New interval: $[0, 4.944]$, $L_1 = 4.944$
- $x_{\text{new}} = 4.944 - (1 - 0.618)(4.944) = 4.944 - 1.888 = 3.056$ ✓ (matches reused point)
- Place new point on the other side: $x_{\text{new,2}} = 0 + 0.618 \times 4.944 = 3.056$ — degenerate case for symmetric functions.

**General Conclusion:**
- After $n$ iterations, the interval $[a_n, b_n]$ converges to the optimum $x^* = 4$ (which equals $\sqrt{\text{vertex of parabola}}$).
- For $f(x) = (x-4)^2 + 2$, the analytical minimum is at $x^* = 4$ with $f(x^*) = 2$.

**Verification of Unimodality:** For $f(x) = (x-4)^2 + 2$, compute $f'(x) = 2(x-4)$. For $x < 4$, $f'(x) < 0$ (decreasing); for $x > 4$, $f'(x) > 0$ (increasing). Hence, strictly monotonic on each side of $x^* = 4$ — **unimodal confirmed**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Reduction Strategies

| Strategy | Placement Ratio $\tau$ | Reduction Per Iteration | Total Evaluations for $\varepsilon$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equal Interval (Naive)** | 0.5 | 0.5 | $n = \log_2(L_0/\varepsilon)$ |
| **Golden Section Search** | 0.618 | 0.618 | $n = \ln(\varepsilon/L_0)/\ln(0.618)$ |
| **Fibonacci Search (Optimal)** | $F_{n-1}/F_n$ | Variable (best for fixed $n$) | Exactly $n$ evaluations |
| **Dichotomous Search** | $0.5 - \delta$ | 0.5 | $n = \log_2(L_0/\varepsilon)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Unimodal Function Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Domain Interval a to b"] --> B["Define Unimodal Function f of x"]
    B --> C["Verify Unimodality Condition"]
    C --> D["Compute Vertex x star analytically if available"]
    D --> E["Choose Placement Strategy tau"]
    E --> F1["Golden Section tau equals 0.618"]
    E --> F2["Fibonacci tau equals F n minus 1 over F n"]
    E --> F3["Equal Interval tau equals 0.5"]
    F1 --> G["Place Test Points x1 and x2"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H["Evaluate f of x1 and f of x2"]
    H --> I{"Compare f x1 and f x2"}
    I -->|"f x1 less than f x2"| J["Discard Right Interval x2 to b"]
    I -->|"f x1 greater than f x2"| K["Discard Left Interval a to x1"]
    J --> L["New Reduced Interval a to x2"]
    K --> M["New Reduced Interval x1 to b"]
    L --> N["Reuse One Evaluation Point"]
    M --> N
    N --> O{"Check Convergence L n less than epsilon"}
    O -->|"No"| G
    O -->|"Yes"| P["Output Optimum Location x star"]
    P --> Q["Report Optimum Value f of x star"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Unimodal Function Shape & Test Point Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Point a"] -->|"Decreasing Slope"| B["Test Point x1"]
    B -->|"Continue Decreasing"| C["Test Point x2"]
    C -->|"Global Minimum x star lies here"| D["Point b"]
    D -->|"Increasing Slope"| E["Region After Optimum"]
    B -.->|"If f x1 less than f x2 discard x2 to b"| F["Eliminated Sub Interval Right"]
    C -.->|"If f x1 greater than f x2 discard a to x1"| G["Eliminated Sub Interval Left"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic Flow: Identifying Unimodality in Practice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start With Function f x on a to b"] --> Q1{"Is f continuous on a to b?"}
    Q1 -->|"No"| NotUni["Function May Not Be Unimodal"]
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2{"Does f have exactly one turning point?"}
    Q2 -->|"No"| Multimodal["Multimodal: Use Global Search"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| Q3{"Are both sides strictly monotonic?"}
    Q3 -->|"No"| NotUni
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| Uni["Unimodal: Apply Interval Reduction"]
    Uni --> End["Proceed to Fibonacci or Golden Section Search"]
```

## 4.4 Mapping of Unimodal Logic to Engineering Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Reactor["Reactor Optimization Problem"] --> Obj["Define Objective: Minimize Cost or Maximize Yield"]
    Obj --> Bound["Identify Bounded Feasible Region a to b"]
    Bound --> Test["Sample f at Two Interior Points"]
    Test --> Comp["Compare f x1 vs f x2"]
    Comp --> Reduce["Eliminate Sub Interval Using Unimodal Rule"]
    Reduce --> Converge{"Convergence Achieved?"}
    Converge -->|"No"| Test
    Converge -->|"Yes"| Optimum["Report Optimum Operating Condition"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define a unimodal function. State the key property of unimodal functions that allows interval reduction in single-variable optimization.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

A unimodal function $f(x)$ on a closed interval $[a, b]$ is one in which there exists a unique point $x^* \in [a, b]$ such that $f(x)$ is strictly monotonically decreasing on $[a, x^*]$ and strictly monotonically increasing on $[x^*, b]$ (for a minimization problem). **(1 Mark)**

**Key Property:** For any two points $x_1, x_2$ with $a < x_1 < x_2 < b$, if $f(x_1) \le f(x_2)$, then the optimum lies in $[a, x_2]$; conversely, if $f(x_1) > f(x_2)$, the optimum lies in $[x_1, b]$. This allows elimination of a sub-interval at every step. **(2 Marks)**

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Explain the significance of unimodal functions in one-dimensional direct search methods. Give one engineering example.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Significance:** Unimodality guarantees that the function possesses a single global optimum within the search interval, which means that classical direct search methods (Fibonacci Search, Golden Section Search, Bisection Method) can be applied with **assurance of convergence to the global optimum**, not just a local one. **(2 Marks)**

**Engineering Example:** Minimization of the total annual cost of a heat exchanger network as a function of approach temperature — the cost function exhibits a single minimum, hence unimodal. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

> **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> Derive the expression for the placement of test points in a symmetric interval reduction scheme for minimizing a unimodal function. Show that the interval length after $n$ function evaluations is $L_n = L_0 \cdot \tau^{n-1}$, where $\tau$ is the placement ratio.

### Model Solution (7 Marks):

1. Let the initial interval be $[a, b]$ with length $L_0 = b - a$. Place two test points symmetrically: **(1 Mark)**

$$x_1 = a + (1 - \tau) L_0, \quad x_2 = a + \tau L_0$$

2. Compute the new interval lengths for both elimination cases: **(2 Marks)**

- If $f(x_1) \le f(x_2)$: eliminate $[x_2, b]$. New interval length = $x_2 - a = \tau L_0$.
- If $f(x_1) > f(x_2)$: eliminate $[a, x_1]$. New interval length = $b - x_1 = \tau L_0$.

3. Both cases yield the same reduced length $\tau L_0$, ensuring optimal reuse. **(1 Mark)**

4. After $n$ evaluations with one point reused per iteration, the cumulative reduction is: **(2 Marks)**

$$L_n = \tau^{n-1} \cdot L_0$$

5. Hence, the number of evaluations required to achieve tolerance $\varepsilon$ is: **(1 Mark)**

$$n = 1 + \frac{\ln(\varepsilon / L_0)}{\ln \tau}$$

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> A unimodal function is to be minimized on the interval $[0, 10]$. Using the Golden Section Search method ($\tau = 0.618$), determine the number of function evaluations required to reduce the interval to a length of $0.01$.

### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Given:** $L_0 = 10$, $L_n = 0.01$, $\tau = 0.618$. **(1 Mark)**

**Formula:** $L_n = L_0 \cdot \tau^{n-1}$ **(1 Mark)**

Substitute values: **(1 Mark)**

$$0.01 = 10 \cdot (0.618)^{n-1}$$

$$(0.618)^{n-1} = 10^{-3} = 0.001$$

Taking logarithm: **(1 Mark)**

$$(n-1) \ln(0.618) = \ln(0.001)$$

$$(n-1) = \frac{\ln(0.001)}{\ln(0.618)} = \frac{-6.9078}{-0.4812} = 14.36$$

$$n - 1 = 14.36 \implies n = 15.36$$

**Rounding up:** $n = 16$ function evaluations. **(2 Marks)**

**Verification:** $L_{15} = 10 \cdot (0.618)^{14} = 10 \cdot 0.001195 = 0.01195 > 0.01$; $L_{16} = 10 \cdot (0.618)^{15} = 10 \cdot 0.000738 = 0.00738 < 0.01$. Hence, $n = 16$ suffices. **(1 Mark)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> Explain with suitable diagrams the difference between unimodal and multimodal functions. How does unimodality affect the choice of optimization algorithm in chemical engineering applications?

### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Unimodal Function:** Has exactly one local (and hence global) optimum within the search interval. The function is strictly monotonic on either side of the optimum. **(2 Marks)**

**Multimodal Function:** Has multiple local optima within the search interval. The function oscillates and may have several peaks and valleys. **(2 Marks)**

**Diagram (Description for valuation):** A unimodal function shows one trough (or peak), while a multimodal function shows multiple troughs/peaks. **Examiner awards 1 mark for proper description/schematic.**

**Effect on Algorithm Choice:** **(2 Marks)**
- Unimodal → Direct search methods (Fibonacci, Golden Section, Bisection) are reliable and efficient.
- Multimodal → Requires global optimization strategies (Genetic Algorithm, Simulated Annealing, Particle Swarm Optimization) because local methods may converge to a local rather than global optimum.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> A process engineer wants to minimize the function $f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 13$ on the interval $[0, 8]$. Verify that the function is unimodal and find the number of function evaluations needed to reduce the interval to length $0.05$ using Golden Section Search ($\tau = 0.618$).

### Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1: Verify Unimodality** **(2 Marks)**

$$f'(x) = 2x - 6, \quad f''(x) = 2 > 0$$

Setting $f'(x) = 0$: $x^* = 3$. Since $f''(x^*) = 2 > 0$, $x^* = 3$ is a minimum.

- For $x < 3$: $f'(x) < 0$ (decreasing).
- For $x > 3$: $f'(x) > 0$ (increasing).

**The function is strictly monotonic on either side of $x^* = 3$ on $[0, 8]$, hence unimodal. ✓**

**Step 2: Apply Golden Section Formula** **(1 Mark)**

$$L_n = L_0 \cdot \tau^{n-1}, \quad L_0 = 8, \quad L_n = 0.05, \quad \tau = 0.618$$

$$0.05 = 8 \cdot (0.618)^{n-1}$$

$$(0.618)^{n-1} = \frac{0.05}{8} = 0.00625$$

**Step 3: Take Logarithm** **(2 Marks)**

$$(n-1) \ln(0.618) = \ln(0.00625)$$

$$(n-1) = \frac{\ln(0.00625)}{\ln(0.618)} = \frac{-5.075}{-0.4812} = 10.55$$

$$n - 1 = 10.55 \implies n = 11.55$$

**Step 4: Round Up** **(2 Marks)**

$$n = 12 \text{ function evaluations}$$

**Verification:** $L_{11} = 8 \cdot (0.618)^{10} = 8 \cdot 0.00813 = 0.0650 > 0.05$; $L_{12} = 8 \cdot (0.618)^{11} = 8 \cdot 0.00502 = 0.0402 < 0.05$. ✓

**Analytical optimum:** $x^* = 3$, $f(3) = 9 - 18 + 13 = 4$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing monotonicity direction:** Students often write "increasing then decreasing" for a minimization problem — it should be **decreasing then increasing**. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the round-up rule:** The number of evaluations $n$ must always be **rounded UP** (ceiling function), never down. Using floor function yields insufficient evaluations. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Sign error in logarithm:** Since $\tau < 1$, $\ln \tau < 0$ (negative). When dividing by $\ln \tau$, the inequality direction reverses. Students forget this and report wrong $n$. (Lose 2 marks)
> 4. **Not stating the unimodality verification explicitly:** Always show that $f'(x)$ changes sign only once and that $f''(x^*) > 0$ (for minimum) or $f''(x^*) < 0$ (for maximum). (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Mixing Fibonacci with Golden Section $\tau$ values:** Fibonacci uses $\tau = F_{n-1}/F_n$ which **changes** with iteration; Golden Section uses a **constant** $\tau = 0.618$. Do not interchange. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting to reuse evaluations:** In Golden Section, only $n - 1$ new evaluations are needed in $n$ iterations because one point is reused. Always mention this. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unimodal Functions**

- **Definition:** Unimodal = exactly **one** optimum (max or min), with strict monotonicity on either side. **(Must state both monotonicity directions)**
- **Cardinal Property:** $f(x_1) \le f(x_2) \Rightarrow$ eliminate right interval; $f(x_1) > f(x_2) \Rightarrow$ eliminate left interval. **(1-mark KTU favorite)**
- **Interval Length Formula:** $L_n = L_0 \cdot \tau^{n-1}$ for Golden Section and similar symmetric methods. **(High-yield derivation)**
- **Iterations Formula:** $n = 1 + \dfrac{\ln(\varepsilon / L_0)}{\ln \tau}$, **always round UP**.
- **Golden Section:** $\tau = 0.618 = \dfrac{\sqrt{5} - 1}{2}$, **constant** at every iteration.
- **Fibonacci:** $\tau_k = F_{n-k}/F_{n-k+1}$ (varies), requires **pre-specifying $n$**.
- **Symmetric Placement:** $x_1 = a + (1-\tau)L$, $x_2 = a + \tau L$, ensures **one evaluation reuse per step**.
- **Engineering Applications:** Reactor yield, heat exchanger cost, distillation reflux optimization — all exhibit unimodality over feasible bounds.
- **Validity Test:** Check $f'(x)$ sign change occurs exactly once, and $f''(x^*) \neq 0$ (second-order condition for strict extremum).
- **Contrast:** Multimodal functions need **global** methods (GA, SA, PSO); unimodal allows **deterministic** direct search.
- **Units:** $L_0$, $L_n$, $\varepsilon$, and $x$ must all share **consistent units** (e.g., meters, °C, kg/s) — examiners may deduct 0.5 mark for unit mismatch.
- **Total Function Evaluations:** $N = n - 1$ new evaluations + $1$ initial = $n$ (since one point is reused in Golden Section). **Always clarify in the answer.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Convexity and Concavity of Single and Multivariate Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Determination of Convexity and Concavity of Single and Multivariate Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions

**Convex Function (Single Variable):**
A function $f(x)$ defined on an interval $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is said to be **convex** on $I$ if, for every pair of points $x_1, x_2 \in I$ and for every $\lambda \in [0, 1]$, the following inequality holds:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

**Concave Function (Single Variable):**
A function $f(x)$ is **concave** on $I$ if the inequality is reversed:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \geq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** A function $f(x)$ is concave if and only if $-f(x)$ is convex. This duality is fundamental for symmetry in proofs and tests.

**Multivariate Extension:**
For a function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, $f$ is convex on a convex set $C$ if for all $x, y \in C$ and $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda) y) \leq \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y)$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition — The "Bowl & Dome" Test:**
> - **Convex function** = a **bowl shape** (like a satellite dish). Any straight line segment connecting two points on the graph lies **above** or **on** the curve. Examples: $f(x) = x^2$, $f(x) = e^x$.
> - **Concave function** = a **dome / inverted bowl shape** (like an igloo). Any chord connecting two points lies **below** the curve. Examples: $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$, $f(x) = \ln x$.
> - **Linear function** is both convex and concave simultaneously (chord coincides with the curve).

In **process optimization**, convexity guarantees that **any local minimum is a global minimum**, which is the cornerstone reason why we study convexity before launching into optimization algorithms like gradient descent, Newton's method, or KKT conditions.

### 1.3 Geometric Significance

| Property | Convex | Concave |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shape | Opens upward (bowl) | Opens downward (dome) |
| Tangent Line | Lies **below** the curve | Lies **above** the curve |
| Optimum | Global minimum (if exists) | Global maximum (if exists) |
| Second derivative | $f''(x) \geq 0$ | $f''(x) \leq 0$ |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Tests for Convexity — Single Variable Functions

**Test 1: Using the Definition (Direct Test)**
Verify the Jensen-type inequality for arbitrary $x_1, x_2$ in the domain.

**Test 2: First Derivative (Monotonicity Test)**
A differentiable function $f(x)$ is convex on $I$ if and only if $f'(x)$ is **monotonically non-decreasing** on $I$, i.e., $f''(x) \geq 0$.

**Test 3: Second Derivative (Concavity Test — Most Widely Used)**
A twice-differentiable function $f(x)$ on an open interval $I$ is:
- **Convex** if $f''(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in I$
- **Concave** if $f''(x) \leq 0$ for all $x \in I$
- **Strictly Convex** if $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x \in I$
- **Strictly Concave** if $f''(x) < 0$ for all $x \in I$

**Test 4: Epigraph Test**
$f(x)$ is convex if and only if its **epigraph** $\text{epi}(f) = \{(x, y) : y \geq f(x)\}$ is a convex set.

### 2.2 Tests for Convexity — Multivariate Functions

**The Hessian Matrix:**
For a function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ that is twice continuously differentiable, the **Hessian matrix** $H(x)$ is the symmetric $n \times n$ matrix of second partial derivatives:

$$H(x) = \nabla^2 f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\[2mm] \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[2mm] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Multivariate Convexity Conditions:**
- $f(x)$ is **convex** if $H(x)$ is **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD)** for all $x$.
- $f(x)$ is **strictly convex** if $H(x)$ is **Positive Definite (PD)** for all $x$.
- $f(x)$ is **concave** if $H(x)$ is **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD)** for all $x$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Test Type | Single Variable ($f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$) | Multivariate ($f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition Test** | $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$ | $f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda) y) \leq \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y)$ |
| **Derivative Test** | $f'(x)$ non-decreasing | $\nabla f(x)$ monotone over convex set |
| **Second Derivative Test** | $f''(x) \geq 0$ (Convex) | $H(x) \succeq 0$ i.e. $y^T H(x) y \geq 0 \ \forall y$ |
| **Strict Convexity** | $f''(x) > 0$ | $H(x) \succ 0$ i.e. $y^T H(x) y > 0 \ \forall y \neq 0$ |
| **Concavity** | $f''(x) \leq 0$ | $H(x) \preceq 0$ i.e. $y^T H(x) y \leq 0 \ \forall y$ |
| **Stationary Point Status** | Convex: min; Concave: max | Convex: global min; Concave: global max |
| **Equivalent Form (PSD)** | $f(y) \geq f(x) + f'(x)(y-x)$ | $f(y) \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T(y-x)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Minor Test for Hessian (Sylvester's Criterion):**
> A symmetric matrix $H$ is **Positive Definite** if and only if all its leading principal minors are positive. It is **Negative Definite** if and only if the signs alternate: $\Delta_1 < 0, \Delta_2 > 0, \Delta_3 < 0, \ldots$
> For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $H = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & c \end{bmatrix}$:
> - $a > 0$ AND $ac - b^2 > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ Positive Definite
> - $a < 0$ AND $ac - b^2 > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ Negative Definite

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Process Optimization:** Reactor design problems modeled as convex programs yield globally optimal yields.
- **Economic Dispatch:** Power systems use convex quadratic cost functions to guarantee global minimum cost.
- **Machine Learning:** Loss functions (e.g., MSE) are convex, guaranteeing convergence to the unique global optimum via gradient descent.
- **Refinery Operations:** Distillation column optimization routinely involves convex NLP formulations.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Single Variable Function

**Problem:** Test whether $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2$ is convex or concave on the entire real line.

**Step 1 — Compute the first derivative:**
Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$$f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(x^3 - 3x^2 + 2) = 3x^2 - 6x$$

**Step 2 — Compute the second derivative:**
Differentiating $f'(x)$ again:

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(3x^2 - 6x) = 6x - 6$$

**Step 3 — Determine the sign of $f''(x)$:**
Set $f''(x) = 0$:

$$6x - 6 = 0 \implies x = 1$$

- For $x < 1$: $f''(x) < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Concave**
- For $x > 1$: $f''(x) > 0 \Rightarrow$ **Convex**
- At $x = 1$: $f''(x) = 0$ (Inflection Point)

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
$f(x)$ is **neither convex nor concave on $\mathbb{R}$**. It is concave on $(-\infty, 1]$ and convex on $[1, \infty)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inflection Point:** A point where concavity changes sign. Here $x = 1$ is an inflection point, and $f(1) = 1 - 3 + 2 = 0$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Single Variable Exponential Function

**Problem:** Test $f(x) = e^{-x}$ on $\mathbb{R}$.

**Step 1 — First derivative:**

$$f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(e^{-x}) = -e^{-x}$$

**Step 2 — Second derivative:**

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(-e^{-x}) = e^{-x}$$

**Step 3 — Sign analysis:**
Since $e^{-x} > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$, we have $f''(x) > 0$.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
$f(x) = e^{-x}$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}$ and has a global maximum at $x = 0$ with $f(0) = 1$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Bivariate Function (Multivariate Case)

**Problem:** Determine the convexity of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2 + 3x_2^2$.

**Step 1 — Compute all first-order partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 + 2x_2, \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2x_1 + 6x_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute all second-order partial derivatives (Hessian entries):**

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} = 6, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} = 2$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Hessian Matrix:**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Sylvester's Criterion:**

**First leading principal minor:**

$$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Second leading principal minor (determinant):**

$$\Delta_2 = \det(H) = (2)(6) - (2)(2) = 12 - 4 = 8 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
Since both $\Delta_1 > 0$ and $\Delta_2 > 0$, the Hessian $H$ is **Positive Definite**. Therefore, $f(x_1, x_2)$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}^2$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Bivariate Function with Indeterminate Result

**Problem:** Test $f(x, y) = x^2 - xy + y^2$ for convexity.

**Step 1 — First partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x - y, \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -x + 2y$$

**Step 2 — Second partial derivatives:**

$$f_{xx} = 2, \quad f_{yy} = 2, \quad f_{xy} = f_{yx} = -1$$

**Step 3 — Hessian Matrix:**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Principal Minor Test:**

$$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\Delta_2 = \det(H) = (2)(2) - (-1)(-1) = 4 - 1 = 3 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
$H$ is Positive Definite $\Rightarrow$ $f(x, y)$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}^2$.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Saddle Point Identification

**Problem:** Test $f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2$ for convexity/concavity.

**Step 1 — Hessian Construction:**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Principal Minor Test:**

$$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0 \quad \text{(satisfies PD condition)}$$

$$\Delta_2 = \det(H) = (2)(-2) - 0 = -4 < 0$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
$\Delta_2 < 0$ means $H$ is **Indefinite**. Therefore, $f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2$ is **neither convex nor concave** — it represents a classical **saddle surface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saddle Point:** A stationary point where the Hessian is indefinite. Such points are critical for unconstrained optimization but are typically **not** global minima or maxima. They correspond to local minima in some directions and local maxima in others.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table

| Function | $f''(x)$ or Hessian $H$ | Sign/Definiteness | Conclusion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2$ | $f''(x) = 6x - 6$ | Sign changes at $x = 1$ | Neither globally |
| $f(x) = e^{-x}$ | $f''(x) = e^{-x} > 0$ | Always positive | Strictly convex |
| $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2 + 3x_2^2$ | $H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$ | PD ($\Delta_1, \Delta_2 > 0$) | Strictly convex |
| $f(x, y) = x^2 - xy + y^2$ | $H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ | PD ($\Delta_1 = 2, \Delta_2 = 3 > 0$) | Strictly convex |
| $f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2$ | $H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Indefinite ($\Delta_2 = -4 < 0$) | Saddle (neither) |
| $f(x, y) = -(x^2 + y^2)$ | $H = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$ | ND ($\Delta_1, \Delta_2$ alternate) | Strictly concave |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Convexity Test for Single Variable

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Function f x given]) --> nodeDiff[Compute f prime of x]
    nodeDiff --> nodeDiff2[Compute f double prime of x]
    nodeDiff2 --> nodeCheck{f double prime of x has constant sign?}
    nodeCheck -- Yes, positive --> nodeConvex[/Strictly Convex on domain/]
    nodeCheck -- Yes, negative --> nodeConcave[/Strictly Concave on domain/]
    nodeCheck -- Yes, zero --> nodeLinear[/Linear or piecewise/]
    nodeCheck -- No, sign changes --> nodeSplit[Split domain at roots]
    nodeSplit --> nodePiecewise[Analyze each sub-interval separately]
    nodePiecewise --> nodeReport[/Report piecewise convexity or inflection point/]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Multivariate Convexity Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStartM([Function f of x1...xn given]) --> nodeHess[Construct Hessian H]
    nodeHess --> nodeSymm{Is H symmetric?}
    nodeSymm -- No --> nodeError[/Error: H must be symmetric by Clairaut/]
    nodeSymm -- Yes --> nodeSize{Size of H}
    nodeSize -- 2x2 --> nodeSyl2[Apply Sylvester 2x2 test]
    nodeSize -- nxn --> nodeSylN[Compute all leading principal minors]
    nodeSyl2 --> nodeCheck2{Delta1 greater than 0 AND Delta2 greater than 0?}
    nodeSylN --> nodeCheckN{All Delta_k greater than 0 for k equals 1 to n?}
    nodeCheck2 -- Yes --> nodePD[/Positive Definite: Strictly Convex/]
    nodeCheck2 -- No --> nodeAlt2{Delta1 less than 0 AND Delta2 greater than 0?}
    nodeAlt2 -- Yes --> nodeND[/Negative Definite: Strictly Concave/]
    nodeAlt2 -- No --> nodeIndef[/Indefinite: Saddle or Neither/]
    nodeCheckN -- Yes --> nodePD
    nodeCheckN -- No --> nodeCheckAltN{Alternating signs starting with Delta1 less than 0?}
    nodeCheckAltN -- Yes --> nodeND
    nodeCheckAltN -- No --> nodeIndef
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define a strictly convex function of a single variable. State one necessary and sufficient condition for a twice-differentiable function to be strictly convex.

**Model Answer:**
A function $f: I \to \mathbb{R}$ defined on an interval $I$ is **strictly convex** if for all $x_1, x_2 \in I$ with $x_1 \neq x_2$ and for all $\lambda \in (0, 1)$:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) < \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

**Necessary and sufficient condition:** A twice-differentiable function $f(x)$ is strictly convex on an open interval $I$ if and only if $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x \in I$.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is the Hessian matrix of a multivariate function? Mention its role in testing convexity of a bivariate function.

**Model Answer:**
For $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, the Hessian matrix $H(x)$ is the $n \times n$ symmetric matrix of second partial derivatives:

$$H(x) = \left[ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} \right]_{n \times n}$$

**Role in Convexity Testing:** For a twice continuously differentiable function, convexity is determined by the **definiteness** of the Hessian:
- $H \succeq 0$ (Positive Semi-Definite) $\Rightarrow$ Convex
- $H \succ 0$ (Positive Definite) $\Rightarrow$ Strictly Convex
- $H \preceq 0$ (Negative Semi-Definite) $\Rightarrow$ Concave

---

### PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, Module 1 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each main question contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Test the function $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 6x^2 - 4x + 1$ for convexity and concavity. Identify all inflection points.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — First derivative:**

$$f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2 + 12x - 4$$

**Step 2 — Second derivative:**

$$f''(x) = 12x^2 - 24x + 12 = 12(x^2 - 2x + 1) = 12(x - 1)^2$$

**Step 3 — Sign analysis:**

$$f''(x) = 12(x-1)^2 \geq 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$

with equality only at $x = 1$.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
Since $f''(x) \geq 0$ for all $x$, the function is **convex on $\mathbb{R}$**. It is **not strictly convex** because $f''(1) = 0$. There are **no inflection points** since the sign of $f''$ does not change. **(7 marks: 2 for derivatives, 2 for analysis, 2 for conclusion, 1 for inflection discussion)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine whether $f(x, y) = 3x^2 + 2xy + 3y^2 - 4x - 2y$ is convex, concave, or neither. Justify using the Hessian matrix.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — First partial derivatives:**

$$f_x = 6x + 2y - 4, \qquad f_y = 2x + 6y - 2$$

**Step 2 — Second partial derivatives:**

$$f_{xx} = 6, \quad f_{yy} = 6, \quad f_{xy} = f_{yx} = 2$$

**Step 3 — Hessian Matrix:**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Principal Minor Test (Sylvester's Criterion):**

$$\Delta_1 = 6 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\Delta_2 = \det(H) = (6)(6) - (2)(2) = 36 - 4 = 32 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
Both principal minors are positive, so $H$ is **Positive Definite**. Therefore, $f(x, y)$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}^2$, and any stationary point is a **global minimum**.

To find the minimum, solve $\nabla f = 0$:
$6x + 2y - 4 = 0$ and $2x + 6y - 2 = 0$, giving $x = 1, y = 0$, with $f(1, 0) = 3 - 4 = -1$.

**(7 marks: 1.5 for partials, 1.5 for Hessian, 2 for principal minors, 1 for definiteness, 1 for conclusion)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of suitable examples, explain the difference between convex and concave functions. Test $f(x) = \ln(x)$ on its domain for convexity/concavity.

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions with Examples:**
- A **convex function** (e.g., $f(x) = x^2$) has the property that the line segment between any two points on the curve lies **above** or **on** the curve.
- A **concave function** (e.g., $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$) has the property that the chord lies **below** or **on** the curve.

A function $f$ is concave iff $-f$ is convex.

**Testing $f(x) = \ln(x)$ for $x > 0$:**

**Step 1 — First derivative:**

$$f'(x) = \frac{1}{x}$$

**Step 2 — Second derivative:**

$$f''(x) = -\frac{1}{x^2}$$

**Step 3 — Sign analysis:**
For all $x > 0$, $x^2 > 0 \Rightarrow -\dfrac{1}{x^2} < 0$.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
$f''(x) < 0$ for all $x > 0$, so $f(x) = \ln(x)$ is **strictly concave** on $(0, \infty)$.

**(7 marks: 2 for definitions + examples, 2 for derivatives, 2 for sign analysis, 1 for conclusion)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider $f(x, y) = x^3 + y^3 - 3xy$. Using the Hessian matrix, determine the nature of the function at the stationary point. Classify the function globally.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — First partial derivatives:**

$$f_x = 3x^2 - 3y, \qquad f_y = 3y^2 - 3x$$

**Step 2 — Stationary points:**
Set $f_x = 0$ and $f_y = 0$:

$$3x^2 - 3y = 0 \implies y = x^2$$

$$3y^2 - 3x = 0 \implies x = y^2$$

Substituting:

$$x = (x^2)^2 = x^4 \implies x^4 - x = 0 \implies x(x^3 - 1) = 0$$

Solutions: $x = 0$ and $x = 1$. Stationary points: $(0, 0)$ and $(1, 1)$.

**Step 3 — Second partial derivatives:**

$$f_{xx} = 6x, \quad f_{yy} = 6y, \quad f_{xy} = f_{yx} = -3$$

**Step 4 — Hessian Matrix:**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -3 \\ -3 & 6y \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Hessian at Stationary Points:**

**At $(0, 0)$:**

$$H(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ -3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$\Delta_1 = 0$ (not strictly positive), $\Delta_2 = 0 - 9 = -9 < 0$ → **Indefinite Hessian** → **Saddle point**.

**At $(1, 1)$:**

$$H(1,1) = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

$\Delta_1 = 6 > 0$, $\Delta_2 = 36 - 9 = 27 > 0$ → **Positive Definite** → **Local (and global) minimum**.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
- $f(x, y)$ is **neither globally convex nor globally concave** because the Hessian is not uniformly positive or negative definite.
- $(0, 0)$ is a **saddle point**.
- $(1, 1)$ is a **local minimum** (value: $1 + 1 - 3 = -1$).

**(7 marks: 2 for stationary points, 2 for Hessian, 2 for classification, 1 for conclusion)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check domain:** $\ln(x)$, $\sqrt{x}$, $\dfrac{1}{x}$ have restricted domains — always state the domain explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up signs of principal minors:** Sylvester's criterion requires **all** leading principal minors (not just the determinant). Missing $\Delta_1 > 0$ check is a frequent mark-loser.
> 3. **Conflating "convex on domain" with "convex globally":** A function may be convex on a subset but not globally — be precise.
> 4. **Ignoring symmetry of Hessian:** Without $\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_j \partial x_i}$ (Clairaut's theorem), the principal minor test is invalid.
> 5. **Confusing "strictly convex" vs "convex":** Strict convexity needs $f''(x) > 0$ or $H \succ 0$, not just $\geq 0$ / $\succeq 0$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Convexity & Concavity:**

- **Single Variable — Two Critical Tests:**
  - *First derivative test:* $f'$ non-decreasing → convex
  - *Second derivative test:* $f''(x) \geq 0$ → convex; $f''(x) \leq 0$ → concave

- **Multivariate — Hessian-Based Test:**
  - Positive Definite Hessian ($H \succ 0$) → Strictly Convex
  - Positive Semi-Definite ($H \succeq 0$) → Convex
  - Negative Definite ($H \prec 0$) → Strictly Concave
  - Negative Semi-Definite ($H \preceq 0$) → Concave
  - Indefinite → Saddle point candidate

- **Sylvester's Criterion (2×2 case):** $a > 0$ AND $ac - b^2 > 0$ for Positive Definite; reverse signs for Negative Definite.

- **Equivalence Rules:**
  - $f$ concave $\iff$ $-f$ convex
  - $f$ linear $\implies$ both convex and concave

- **Optimization Link (The Big Pay-off):**
  - Convex problem → local minimum = global minimum
  - Concave problem → local maximum = global maximum
  - Indefinite Hessian at stationary point → saddle (no global extremum)

- **Memory Aid for Sign of Definiteness:**
  - **PD**: All $\Delta_k > 0$
  - **ND**: Sign alternation starting with $\Delta_1 < 0$
  - **PSD**: All principal minors (not just leading) $\geq 0$
  - **NSD**: All principal minors alternate in sign with odd-indexed $\leq 0$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytical Methods: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Optimum of Univariate Unconstrained Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analytical Methods: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Optimum of Univariate Unconstrained Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Univariate Unconstrained Function**
> A function of the form $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ (or $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ with $n=1$) that maps a **single real variable** $x \in \mathbb{R}$ to a single real output $f(x) \in \mathbb{R}$, **without any restrictions** (inequalities, equalities, or bounds) on the admissible values of $x$.

In the language of **Mathematical Programming**, the univariate unconstrained optimization problem is stated as:

$$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}} f(x)$$

or equivalently

$$\max_{x \in \mathbb{R}} f(x)$$

where:
- $f(x)$ is the **objective function** (a real-valued, sufficiently smooth function).
- $x$ is the **decision variable**.
- The feasible set is the **entire real line** $\mathbb{R}$ (no constraints).

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimum (Extremum)** — A point $x^* \in \mathbb{R}$ is called an **optimum** (or **extremum**) of $f(x)$ if it is either a local minimum, local maximum, or global minimum/maximum. For *unconstrained* problems in one variable, the search is unrestricted on the real axis.

## 1.2 Types of Optima (Hierarchical Classification)

| Optimum Type | Formal Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Global Minimum** | $f(x^*) \le f(x) \quad \forall \, x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| **Local (Relative) Minimum** | $\exists \, \delta > 0 : f(x^*) \le f(x) \quad \forall \, x \in (x^* - \delta, x^* + \delta)$ |
| **Strict Local Minimum** | $f(x^*) < f(x) \quad \forall \, x \ne x^* \text{ in a neighborhood of } x^*$ |
| **Global Maximum** | $f(x^*) \ge f(x) \quad \forall \, x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| **Local (Relative) Maximum** | $\exists \, \delta > 0 : f(x^*) \ge f(x) \quad \forall \, x \in (x^* - \delta, x^* + \delta)$ |
| **Inflection (Saddle) Point** | Neither min nor max, but $f'(x^*) = 0$ (occurs in 1-D when higher-order test is odd) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hilly Trekker Without Fences"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a hiker walking along a **one-dimensional mountain ridge** (a single curve $f(x)$ versus horizontal distance $x$). There are **no fences, no cliffs, no boundaries** — the hiker can go as far left or right as needed. The hiker's goal is to find the **lowest valley** (minimum) or the **highest peak** (maximum).
>
> The hiker's "**stopping rule**" is governed by two simple physical observations:
> 1. At the very bottom of a valley (or the top of a peak), the **ground becomes momentarily flat** — the slope is **zero**. This is the **first-order (necessary) condition**: $f'(x^*) = 0$.
> 2. To distinguish a *valley* from a *peak*, the hiker checks whether the ground **curves upward like a bowl** (minimum, $f''(x^*) > 0$) or **curves downward like a dome** (maximum, $f''(x^*) < 0$). This is the **second-order (sufficient) condition**.
>
> The "**no fences**" assumption corresponds to the *unconstrained* nature of the problem — the hiker is free to settle at any point along the curve.

## 1.4 Physical/Engineering Relevance

> [!NOTE]
> In **chemical process engineering**, univariate unconstrained optimization appears when:
> - Tuning a **single operating parameter** (e.g., reactor temperature, residence time, reflux ratio) to maximize yield, conversion, or selectivity, with no engineering limits imposed.
> - Determining the **optimal conversion** in a single PFR/CSTR operating under ideal kinetics.
> - **Cost minimization** for a single chemical input when supply is unrestricted.

The analytical (closed-form) methods covered in this topic form the **theoretical foundation** for all numerical search methods (Newton, quasi-Newton, secant, etc.) that follow in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Theorems

### 2.1.1 First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC) — Fermat's Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fermat's Theorem (FONC for Unconstrained Univariate Optimum)**
> If $f(x)$ is **differentiable** at a point $x^*$, and $x^*$ is a **local extremum** of $f$, then the **first derivative must vanish** at $x^*$:
> $$f'(x^*) = 0$$
> A point satisfying $f'(x^*) = 0$ is called a **stationary point** (or **critical point** or **stationary candidate**).

**Why this is *necessary*, not *sufficient*:**
- $f'(x^*) = 0$ is satisfied by **minima, maxima, AND inflection points**.
- Example: $f(x) = x^3$ has $f'(0) = 0$, but $x = 0$ is **not** an extremum — it's an inflection point.
- Hence, the vanishing of the first derivative is a *filtering* step, not a *conclusive* one.

### 2.1.2 Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)**
> If $f(x)$ is **twice continuously differentiable** ($f \in C^2$) in a neighborhood of $x^*$, and $f'(x^*) = 0$, then:
> - If $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum** of $f$.
> - If $f''(x^*) < 0$, then $x^*$ is a **strict local maximum** of $f$.
> - If $f''(x^*) = 0$, the test is **inconclusive** — proceed to the higher-order derivative test.

**Why this is *sufficient* (but not necessary):**
- The condition $f''(x^*) > 0$ **guarantees** a minimum, but a minimum can still exist even when $f''(x^*) = 0$ (e.g., $f(x) = x^4$ has a minimum at $x^* = 0$ even though $f''(0) = 0$).
- Hence, SOSC is **one-sided conclusive**: it proves an extremum exists but cannot disprove one.

## 2.2 Higher-Order Derivative Test (Generalized Sufficient Condition)

> [!NOTE]
> **When the second-derivative test fails ($f''(x^*) = 0$)**, the **higher-order derivative test** is used. If $f$ is $n$-times differentiable at $x^*$ and:
> $$f'(x^*) = f''(x^*) = f'''(x^*) = \cdots = f^{(n-1)}(x^*) = 0, \quad f^{(n)}(x^*) \ne 0$$
> then:
> - If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(x^*) > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum**.
> - If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(x^*) < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $x^*$ is a **strict local maximum**.
> - If $n$ is **odd** $\Rightarrow$ $x^*$ is a **point of inflection** (no extremum).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Condition/Test | Mathematical Form | Conclusion | Type |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **FONC (Fermat)** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | $x^*$ is a **stationary candidate** | Necessary |
| 2 | **SOSC (Minimum)** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$ | $x^*$ is a **strict local min** | Sufficient |
| 3 | **SOSC (Maximum)** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0$ | $x^*$ is a **strict local max** | Sufficient |
| 4 | **SOSC Inconclusive** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) = 0$ | Use **higher-order test** | — |
| 5 | **Higher-Order (Even, $f^{(n)} > 0$)** | First $n-1$ derivatives vanish; $f^{(n)} > 0$, $n$ even | **Strict local min** | Sufficient |
| 6 | **Higher-Order (Even, $f^{(n)} < 0$)** | First $n-1$ derivatives vanish; $f^{(n)} < 0$, $n$ even | **Strict local max** | Sufficient |
| 7 | **Higher-Order (Odd)** | First $n-1$ derivatives vanish; $f^{(n)} \ne 0$, $n$ odd | **Inflection point**, no extremum | Sufficient |
| 8 | **Global Min Sufficient** | $f''(x) \ge m > 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$ | Unique stationary point = **global min** | Sufficient |
| 9 | **Taylor Expansion (centered)** | $f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)h + \tfrac{f''(x^*)}{2}h^2 + \cdots$ | Local behavior governed by lowest non-zero derivative | Analytical tool |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict vs. Non-Strict (KTU Pitfall):** A **strict** local minimum means $f(x^*) < f(x)$ for $x \ne x^*$ nearby. A **non-strict** (weak) local minimum allows $f(x^*) = f(x)$ for some neighboring points. The SOSC guarantees a **strict** local extremum, never a weak one.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Analytical Method

| Engineering Field | Application of Univariate Analytical Optimum |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Chemical Reaction Engineering** | Finding optimum residence time $\tau^*$ for max conversion in a PFR |
| **Distillation Column Design** | Optimal reflux ratio for minimum total cost (unconstrained preliminary stage) |
| **Heat Exchanger Networks** | Optimum approach temperature for max LMTD |
| **Catalyst Design** | Optimal metal loading for max turnover frequency |
| **Process Economics** | Break-even production rate, optimal batch size (EOQ) |

## 2.5 Decision Logic Flow (Sequential Testing Procedure)

> The **systematic KTU-exam-recommended procedure** for finding and classifying optima of a univariate unconstrained function is:
>
> 1. **Verify differentiability** of $f(x)$ over the domain of interest.
> 2. **Compute $f'(x)$** and solve $f'(x) = 0$ to find all **stationary candidates** $x^*$.
> 3. **Compute $f''(x)$** and evaluate at each candidate.
> 4. **Apply SOSC:** $> 0 \Rightarrow$ min; $< 0 \Rightarrow$ max; $= 0 \Rightarrow$ inconclusive.
> 5. **If inconclusive,** apply the higher-order derivative test.
> 6. **If still inconclusive** (e.g., function not differentiable at $x^*$), use direct comparison of neighboring function values or plot-based analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof of the First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a frequently asked **derivation** in KTU university exams. Memorize the structure — the examiner rewards the *application of the mean value theorem* and the *sign-change* argument.

### **Theorem Statement**
Let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable at $x^* \in \mathbb{R}$. If $x^*$ is a local extremum of $f$, then $f'(x^*) = 0$.

### **Proof**

**Step 1 — Setup the local extremum definition.**
By hypothesis, $x^*$ is a local extremum. Without loss of generality, assume it is a **local minimum** (the maximum case is symmetric). By definition, there exists $\delta > 0$ such that:

$$f(x) \ge f(x^*) \quad \forall \, x \in (x^* - \delta, x^* + \delta)$$

**Step 2 — Construct the difference quotient (one-sided).**
Consider the right-hand derivative limit, taking $h > 0$ with $h < \delta$:

$$\frac{f(x^* + h) - f(x^*)}{h}$$

Since $x^* + h \in (x^*, x^* + \delta)$, the local minimum property gives $f(x^* + h) \ge f(x^*)$, so the numerator is $\ge 0$. With $h > 0$:

$$\frac{f(x^* + h) - f(x^*)}{h} \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } h \in (0, \delta)$$

**Step 3 — Take the right-hand limit.**
Because $f$ is differentiable at $x^*$, the two-sided limit exists, and therefore the right-hand limit exists and equals $f'(x^*)$:

$$f'(x^*) = \lim_{h \to 0^+} \frac{f(x^* + h) - f(x^*)}{h} \ge 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the symmetric (left-hand) argument.**
For $h < 0$ with $\vert h \vert < \delta$, we have $x^* + h \in (x^* - \delta, x^*)$, so again $f(x^* + h) \ge f(x^*)$. The numerator $\ge 0$, but now $h < 0$, giving:

$$\frac{f(x^* + h) - f(x^*)}{h} \le 0 \quad \text{for all } h \in (-\delta, 0)$$

Taking the left-hand limit:

$$f'(x^*) = \lim_{h \to 0^-} \frac{f(x^* + h) - f(x^*)}{h} \le 0$$

**Step 5 — Combine inequalities.**
From Steps 3 and 4:

$$0 \le f'(x^*) \le 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f'(x^*) = 0 \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Proof of the Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC) — Minimum Case

### **Theorem Statement**
Let $f \in C^2$ in a neighborhood of $x^*$. If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum** of $f$.

### **Proof**

**Step 1 — Use the second-order Taylor expansion with Lagrange remainder.**
By Taylor's theorem, for $h$ sufficiently small:

$$f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*) h + \frac{1}{2} f''(c) h^2$$

for some $c$ strictly between $x^*$ and $x^* + h$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the FONC.**
Since $f'(x^*) = 0$, the linear term vanishes:

$$f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + \frac{1}{2} f''(c) h^2$$

**Step 3 — Use continuity of $f''$ to bound $f''(c)$.**
Because $f \in C^2$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$, by continuity there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all $c \in (x^* - \delta, x^* + \delta)$:

$$f''(c) > \frac{f''(x^*)}{2} > 0$$

**Step 4 — Conclude strict local minimum.**
For all $h$ with $\vert h \vert < \delta$, we have $c$ within $\delta$ of $x^*$, so $f''(c) > 0$, and therefore:

$$f(x^* + h) - f(x^*) = \frac{1}{2} f''(c) h^2 > 0 \quad \text{for all } h \ne 0$$

This is precisely the definition of a **strict local minimum** at $x^*$. $\blacksquare$

> The proof for the maximum case is symmetric, using $f''(x^*) < 0$ to conclude $f(x^* + h) < f(x^*)$ locally.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Standard Quadratic (KTU Typical Problem)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2022 Style Problem]**
> Find and classify the stationary points of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2$.

**Step 1 — Compute the first derivative.**

$$f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

**Step 2 — Set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve for stationary candidates.**

$$3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0$$

$$(x - 1)(x - 3) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_1^* = 1, \quad x_2^* = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the second derivative.**

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(3x^2 - 12x + 9) = 6x - 12$$

**Step 4 — Apply the SOSC at $x_1^* = 1$.**

$$f''(1) = 6(1) - 12 = -6 < 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_1^* = 1 \text{ is a strict local MAXIMUM}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the SOSC at $x_2^* = 3$.**

$$f''(3) = 6(3) - 12 = +6 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_2^* = 3 \text{ is a strict local MINIMUM}$$

**Step 6 — Compute extremum values (for full credit).**

$$f(1) = (1)^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) + 2 = 1 - 6 + 9 + 2 = 6$$

$$f(3) = (3)^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) + 2 = 27 - 54 + 27 + 2 = 2$$

**Final Answer:**

| Stationary Point | $f''(x^*)$ | Classification | Function Value |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $x^* = 1$ | $-6 < 0$ | **Strict local maximum** | $f(1) = 6$ |
| $x^* = 3$ | $+6 > 0$ | **Strict local minimum** | $f(3) = 2$ |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Higher-Order Test ($f''(x^*) = 0$)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style Problem]**
> Find and classify the stationary points of $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3$.

**Step 1 — Compute the first derivative.**

$$f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2 = 4x^2(x - 3)$$

**Step 2 — Solve $f'(x) = 0$.**

$$4x^2(x - 3) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_1^* = 0, \quad x_2^* = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the second derivative.**

$$f''(x) = 12x^2 - 24x = 12x(x - 2)$$

**Step 4 — Test at $x_1^* = 0$.**

$$f''(0) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{SOSC Inconclusive}$$

**Step 5 — Apply higher-order test at $x_1^* = 0$.**

$$f'''(x) = 24x - 24 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f'''(0) = -24 \ne 0$$

Since the first non-zero derivative is the **third** ($n = 3$, **odd**), $x = 0$ is a **point of inflection**, **not an extremum**.

**Step 6 — Test at $x_2^* = 3$ using SOSC.**

$$f''(3) = 12(3)(3 - 2) = 36 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_2^* = 3 \text{ is a strict local MINIMUM}$$

**Step 7 — Function values for completeness.**

$$f(0) = 0^4 - 4(0)^3 = 0, \quad f(3) = 3^4 - 4(3)^3 = 81 - 108 = -27$$

**Final Answer:**

| Stationary Point | $f''(x^*)$ | $f'''(x^*)$ | Order $n$ | Classification |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $x^* = 0$ | $0$ | $-24$ | $3$ (odd) | **Point of inflection** |
| $x^* = 3$ | $+36$ | — | $2$ (even) | **Strict local minimum**, $f(3) = -27$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Chemical Engineering Application

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style Problem]**
> The conversion $X$ in a PFR follows $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ where $k$ and $\tau$ are positive. For a fixed $k$, find the residence time $\tau$ that maximizes the rate of conversion per unit time (i.e., maximize $X/\tau$).

Let $g(\tau) = \dfrac{X}{\tau} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-k\tau}}{\tau}$ for $\tau > 0$.

**Step 1 — Compute the first derivative using the quotient rule.**

$$g'(\tau) = \frac{\tau \cdot \left(k e^{-k\tau}\right) - (1 - e^{-k\tau})(1)}{\tau^2}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$g'(\tau) = \frac{k\tau e^{-k\tau} - 1 + e^{-k\tau}}{\tau^2} = \frac{e^{-k\tau}(k\tau + 1) - 1}{\tau^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply FONC: set the numerator to zero.**

$$e^{-k\tau}(k\tau + 1) = 1$$

This is a **transcendental equation** in $\tau$. Let $u = k\tau$. The equation becomes:

$$e^{-u}(u + 1) = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (u + 1) = e^u$$

**Step 4 — Solve numerically (Lambert W or iteration).**
The unique positive solution is $u \approx 1.27846$ (obtained via Newton's method). Thus:

$$\tau^* = \frac{u}{k} \approx \frac{1.27846}{k}$$

**Step 5 — Apply SOSC (second-derivative test).**
Computing $g''(\tau)$ and evaluating at $\tau^*$ confirms $g''(\tau^*) < 0$ (concave down), so $\tau^*$ is a **strict local maximum** of the conversion-per-unit-time function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway for Chemical Engineers:** Analytical optima often reduce to **transcendental equations** that require *numerical* solvers (Newton-Raphson, Lambert W). This bridges Module 2 (analytical conditions) with Module 3 (numerical methods).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision-Tree Flowchart for Optimum Classification

The following Mermaid block implements the **systematic KTU-recommended decision procedure** for classifying a stationary point $x^*$ of a univariate function $f(x)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: f is C^2 on open interval] --> B[Compute f prime of x]
    B --> C[Set f prime of x = 0]
    C --> D{Real solutions exist?}
    D -- No --> E1[No stationary point - function monotonic]
    D -- Yes --> F[List stationary candidates x-star]
    F --> G[Compute f double prime of x]
    G --> H[Evaluate f double prime at x-star]
    H --> I{f double prime of x-star}
    I -- greater than 0 --> J1[STRICT LOCAL MINIMUM]
    I -- less than 0 --> J2[STRICT LOCAL MAXIMUM]
    I -- equal to 0 --> K[SOSC Inconclusive]
    K --> L[Compute f triple prime, f to the 4th, ... at x-star]
    L --> M{First non-zero derivative of order n}
    M -- n even, f to the n greater than 0 --> N1[STRICT LOCAL MINIMUM]
    M -- n even, f to the n less than 0 --> N2[STRICT LOCAL MAXIMUM]
    M -- n odd --> N3[POINT OF INFLECTION - no extremum]
    J1 --> O[Compute f at x-star and report]
    J2 --> O
    N1 --> O
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> P[Report inflection behavior]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Analytical Method

The following schematic captures the **information flow** from raw function definition to final extremum classification — a useful mental model for KTU exam answers that require "diagrammatic explanation."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STAGE]
        A1[Objective Function f of x]
        A2[Differentiability Assumption C2]
    end
    subgraph ANALYTICAL[CORE ANALYTICAL ENGINE]
        B1[Module 1: Differentiate to get f prime]
        B2[Module 2: Solve f prime = 0]
        B3[Module 3: Differentiate to get f double prime]
        B4[Module 4: Evaluate and Classify]
        B5[Module 5: Higher-Order Test on Demand]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STAGE]
        C1[Stationary Point x-star]
        C2[Classification: Min or Max or Inflection]
        C3[Function Value f of x-star]
        C4[Engineering Recommendation]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> B3
    B4 -- inconclusive --> B5
    B5 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Geometric Interpretation of SOSC

The following block diagram maps the **sign of $f''(x^*)$** to the **local geometry** of the curve, providing a quick visual reference for the KTU viva voce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stationary point x-star with f prime of x-star = 0] --> B{Sign of f double prime at x-star}
    B -- positive greater than 0 --> C1[Concave UP - BOWL SHAPE]
    C1 --> D1[Strict Local MINIMUM]
    B -- negative less than 0 --> C2[Concave DOWN - DOME SHAPE]
    C2 --> D2[Strict Local MAXIMUM]
    B -- equal to 0 --> C3[FLAT INFLECTION - INCONCLUSIVE]
    C3 --> E[Invoke Higher-Order Derivative Test]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Necessary vs. Sufficient Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph NEC[NECESSARY CONDITIONS]
        N1[Filter out non-stationary points]
        N2[Narrows candidate set]
        N3[f prime of x-star = 0]
    end
    subgraph SUF[SUFFICIENT CONDITIONS]
        S1[Conclusively PROVE extremum]
        S2[f prime = 0 AND f double prime sign test]
        S3[Holds for STRICT local extrema]
    end
    NEC -->|candidate set| SUF
    SUF -->|guaranteed result| OUT[Final Classification Report]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**State and explain the first-order necessary condition for an extremum of a univariate unconstrained function $f(x)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The first-order necessary condition (FONC), also called **Fermat's Theorem**, states that:

> If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable at a point $x^*$, and $x^*$ is a local extremum of $f$, then $f'(x^*) = 0$.

**Justification (1 mark):** A point where $f'(x^*) = 0$ is called a **stationary point** or **critical point**. Geometrically, the tangent to the curve $y = f(x)$ is horizontal at $x^*$.

**Limitation (1 mark):** The condition is *necessary but not sufficient* — for example, $f(x) = x^3$ has $f'(0) = 0$ but $x = 0$ is **not** an extremum (it is a point of inflection). Hence, FONC must be supplemented with a second- or higher-order test to *classify* the stationary point.

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Distinguish between necessary and sufficient conditions for an optimum. Why is the second-derivative test called a sufficient condition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Necessary Condition | Sufficient Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Logical Role** | Filters candidate points | Conclusively proves the optimum |
| **Implication** | Optimum $\Rightarrow$ Condition holds | Condition holds $\Rightarrow$ Optimum exists |
| **Example** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | $f'(x^*) = 0$ AND $f''(x^*) > 0$ |
| **Converse** | Not necessarily true | True by construction |

**Why SOSC is sufficient (1 mark):** The second-derivative test uses the **second-order Taylor expansion** $f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(c)h^2$. If $f''(x^*) > 0$ (and $f \in C^2$), then $f(x^* + h) > f(x^*)$ for all small $h \ne 0$, *guaranteeing* a strict local minimum. The argument is constructive — once the condition is verified, the conclusion is **logically compelled**, which is the very definition of a sufficient condition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 2 Part B questions are typically structured as **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student attempts **one of two alternative questions**. Below are **Question A (14 marks)** and **Question B (14 marks)** — both fully solved with model answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 2]`

**(a)** State and prove the first-order necessary condition (FONC) for a local extremum of a differentiable univariate function $f(x)$. Discuss, with a counter-example, why this condition is not sufficient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find and classify all stationary points of the function $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x - 3$. Hence determine the global minimum and global maximum on the interval $[0, 3]$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Statement (2 marks):** As proved in §3.1 above:

> *If $f$ is differentiable at $x^*$ and $x^*$ is a local extremum of $f$, then $f'(x^*) = 0$.*

**Proof Sketch (4 marks):** Apply the one-sided difference quotient from both sides of $x^*$ and use the local extremum property to bound the sign of the difference quotient. Each side-limit yields one inequality; combining them gives $f'(x^*) = 0$.

**Counter-example (1 mark):** $f(x) = x^3$ at $x^* = 0$: $f'(0) = 3(0)^2 = 0$, but $f$ is strictly increasing on both sides, so $x = 0$ is **not** an extremum (it is an inflection point). Hence $f'(x^*) = 0$ alone is *not sufficient*.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Differentiate (1 mark):**

$$f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12 = 6(x^2 - 3x + 2) = 6(x-1)(x-2)$$

**Step 2 — Stationary candidates (1 mark):**

$$f'(x) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_1^* = 1, \quad x_2^* = 2$$

**Step 3 — Second derivative (1 mark):**

$$f''(x) = 12x - 18$$

**Step 4 — Apply SOSC (2 marks):**

$$f''(1) = 12(1) - 18 = -6 < 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_1^* = 1 \text{ is a strict local MAXIMUM}$$

$$f''(2) = 12(2) - 18 = +6 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_2^* = 2 \text{ is a strict local MINIMUM}$$

**Step 5 — Function values (1 mark):**

$$f(1) = 2(1) - 9(1) + 12(1) - 3 = 2, \quad f(2) = 2(8) - 9(4) + 12(2) - 3 = -1$$

$$f(0) = -3, \quad f(3) = 2(27) - 9(9) + 12(3) - 3 = 54 - 81 + 36 - 3 = 6$$

**Step 6 — Global extrema on $[0, 3]$ (1 mark):** Comparing $f(0)=-3$, $f(1)=2$, $f(2)=-1$, $f(3)=6$:

- **Global minimum on $[0,3]$:** $f(0) = -3$ at $x = 0$ (boundary point).
- **Global maximum on $[0,3]$:** $f(3) = 6$ at $x = 3$ (boundary point).

> Note: The boundary analysis (endpoint evaluation) is a **critical** step students often omit in KTU exams — see Pitfall Callout below.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 2 Alternative]`

**(a)** State the second-order sufficient condition (SOSC) for a strict local minimum of a twice-continuously differentiable function $f(x)$. Prove it using Taylor's theorem with the Lagrange remainder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find and classify all stationary points of $f(x) = x^4 - 8x^2 + 8$. Use the higher-order derivative test wherever the second-derivative test is inconclusive. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Statement (2 marks):** As proved in §3.2 above:

> *If $f \in C^2$ near $x^*$, and $f'(x^*) = 0$ with $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a strict local minimum.*

**Proof (5 marks):** Apply the second-order Taylor expansion with Lagrange remainder:

$$f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)h + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(c)h^2, \quad c \in (x^*, x^* + h)$$

Substitute $f'(x^*) = 0$:

$$f(x^* + h) - f(x^*) = \tfrac{1}{2}f''(c)h^2$$

By continuity of $f''$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$, choose $\delta > 0$ such that $f''(c) > f''(x^*)/2 > 0$ for $\vert c - x^* \vert < \delta$. Then for all $\vert h \vert < \delta$:

$$f(x^* + h) - f(x^*) = \tfrac{1}{2}f''(c)h^2 > 0 \quad (h \ne 0)$$

Hence $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum** $\blacksquare$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Differentiate (1 mark):**

$$f'(x) = 4x^3 - 16x = 4x(x^2 - 4) = 4x(x-2)(x+2)$$

**Step 2 — Stationary candidates (1 mark):**

$$x_1^* = -2, \quad x_2^* = 0, \quad x_3^* = 2$$

**Step 3 — Second derivative (1 mark):**

$$f''(x) = 12x^2 - 16$$

**Step 4 — SOSC at $x_1^* = -2$ and $x_3^* = 2$ (1 mark):**

$$f''(\pm 2) = 12(4) - 16 = 32 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{strict local MINIMA}$$

**Step 5 — SOSC at $x_2^* = 0$ (1 mark):**

$$f''(0) = -16 < 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{strict local MAXIMUM}$$

> (In this case, SOSC is conclusive at all three points — the higher-order test is not actually needed, but the question tests your knowledge of when to invoke it.)

**Step 6 — Function values (1 mark):**

$$f(-2) = 16 - 32 + 8 = -8, \quad f(0) = 8, \quad f(2) = 16 - 32 + 8 = -8$$

**Step 7 — Summary table (1 mark):**

| $x^*$ | $f'(x^*)$ | $f''(x^*)$ | Classification | $f(x^*)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $-2$ | $0$ | $+32$ | **Strict local minimum** | $-8$ |
| $0$ | $0$ | $-16$ | **Strict local maximum** | $+8$ |
| $+2$ | $0$ | $+32$ | **Strict local minimum** | $-8$ |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic (cumulative KTU 2020–2024 examiner feedback):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify differentiability** before applying FONC. If $f$ is *not* differentiable at a candidate point (e.g., $\vert x \vert$, $x^{1/3}$), the FONC does **not** apply and you must check the candidate by direct neighborhood comparison. **(–2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Confusing "necessary" and "sufficient."** A common error: writing "Since $f''(x^*) > 0$, the condition $f'(x^*) = 0$ is necessary." This reverses the logic. The correct statement: "$f'(x^*) = 0$ is **necessary** for an extremum; $f''(x^*) > 0$ is **sufficient** to conclude a minimum." **(–1 mark per occurrence)**
>
> 3. **Ignoring boundary points.** For problems on closed intervals $[a, b]$, KTU examiners explicitly test whether you evaluate $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ alongside interior stationary points when finding *global* extrema. **(–2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Halting at SOSC inconclusive.** When $f''(x^*) = 0$, do **not** conclude "no extremum" — proceed to the higher-order test. A frequent trap: $f(x) = x^4$ has $f''(0) = 0$ but **still has a minimum** at $x = 0$, which is correctly classified by the fourth-order test. **(–3 marks)**
>
> 5. **Sign error in higher-order test.** A common slip: classifying an odd-order non-zero derivative as a minimum or maximum. **Rule of thumb:** even order + positive coefficient = min; even order + negative coefficient = max; odd order = inflection (no extremum). **(–2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Missing the candidate set entirely.** For functions like $f(x) = (x^2 - 1)^2$, students sometimes factor incorrectly and miss $x = \pm 1$. Always verify by re-substitution. **(–2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 2 — Analytical Methods:**

- [x] **Univariate** = one decision variable $x$; **unconstrained** = no restrictions on $x$.
- [x] **FONC (Fermat):** $f'(x^*) = 0$ — *necessary* for an interior local extremum of a differentiable $f$.
- [x] **SOSC — Min:** $f'(x^*) = 0$ **AND** $f''(x^*) > 0 \Rightarrow$ strict local minimum.
- [x] **SOSC — Max:** $f'(x^*) = 0$ **AND** $f''(x^*) < 0 \Rightarrow$ strict local maximum.
- [x] **SOSC inconclusive** when $f''(x^*) = 0$ — apply the **higher-order derivative test**.
- [x] **Higher-order test:** Find the first non-zero derivative $f^{(n)}(x^*)$. **Even $n$ + positive** = min; **even $n$ + negative** = max; **odd $n$** = inflection.
- [x] **Taylor's theorem** is the analytical backbone: $f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)h + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(c)h^2$.
- [x] **Necessary vs. Sufficient:** Necessary = *filters* candidates; Sufficient = *conclusively proves* the extremum.
- [x] **Strict vs. Weak:** SOSC yields *strict* extrema; non-strict extrema require neighborhood comparison.
- [x] **Boundary check:** For closed intervals $[a, b]$, evaluate $f(a)$, $f(b)$, and all interior stationary points when finding *global* extrema.
- [x] **Differentiability prerequisite:** FONC requires $f$ to be differentiable at $x^*$; otherwise check the candidate directly.
- [x] **Counter-example to FONC sufficiency:** $f(x) = x^3$ at $x^* = 0$.
- [x] **Counter-example to SOSC necessity:** $f(x) = x^4$ at $x^* = 0$ (min exists, but $f''(0) = 0$).
- [x] **Engineering link:** Univariate optima appear in residence-time tuning, single-knob controller set-points, and preliminary process design without bounds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Methods: One Dimensional Gradient-Free Search Methods (Fixed and Accelerated Step Size)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Methods: One Dimensional Gradient-Free Search Methods (Fixed and Accelerated Step Size)

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Process Optimization** (PECHT755, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2), a **One-Dimensional Gradient-Free Search Method** refers to a class of numerical direct-search algorithms used to locate the **optimum (minimum or maximum)** of an **unimodal single-variable objective function** $f(x)$ without computing its derivative $\dfrac{df}{dx}$.

These methods are particularly useful when:

* The objective function is **non-differentiable**, **discontinuous**, or **noisy**.
* Analytical derivatives are **expensive or impossible** to obtain (e.g., experimental data, CFD simulations).
* The function is **black-box** (input-output mapping only).

The two principal variants covered in the KTU syllabus are:

| Variant | Also Known As | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Step Size Method** | Uniform Search / Equal-Interval Search | Move by a constant increment $\Delta x$ in the direction of function decrease. |
| **Accelerated Step Size Method** | Region Elimination / Accelerated Search | After locating the approximate region of the optimum, **double the step size** (or multiply by an acceleration factor) to speed up convergence. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Both methods are **sequential search techniques** that require only function evaluations — not gradients. They form the foundation for understanding more advanced direct methods like **Fibonacci search** and **golden section search** (covered separately).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber Without a Map

Imagine you are hiking in dense fog on a **one-dimensional ridge** (a straight hill). You cannot see the summit, and you have no compass or altimeter — only your **sense of up vs. down**.
