The minimum of this approximating parabola is then taken as the next estimate of the true minimum. The process is **iterative** until convergence is achieved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Quadratic Interpolation is a **region-elimination** (polynomial-fitting) method that approximates the objective function by a quadratic and uses its analytical minimum. It converges **faster (superlinearly)** than the Golden-Section method but requires three starting points.

**Conceptual Analogy – The "Three-Point Arch Bridge" Intuition:**
Imagine you have three height readings on a hill: at the start, middle, and end. You cannot see the whole curve, so you stretch a smooth parabolic **arch** (like a bridge) to pass exactly through these three points. The lowest point of this arch is your best guess for the valley. Then you pick a new third point (replacing the worst of the old three) and build a new arch. With each new arch, your guess for the valley gets closer to the real bottom.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (KTU Board Focus):**
> - **Golden-Section**: Uses *function evaluations* to narrow the interval — robust but **linear convergence**.
> - **Quadratic Interpolation**: Uses *function evaluations + polynomial fitting* — faster **superlinear convergence**, but less robust to noise or non-smooth functions.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Golden-Section Search

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Bracket the minimum:** Identify initial lower bound $a$ and upper bound $b$ such that $f$ is unimodal on $[a, b]$.
2. **Place two interior test points** using the golden ratio:
   - $x_1 = a + (1 - \rho)(b - a)$
   - $x_2 = a + \rho(b - a)$
   where $\rho = \frac{1}{\phi} = \frac{\sqrt{5} - 1}{2} \approx \mathbf{0.6180339887}$.
3. **Evaluate the function** at both $x_1$ and $x_2$, obtaining $f_1 = f(x_1)$ and $f_2 = f(x_2)$.
4. **Apply the elimination rule (the "Goldilocks" rule):**
   - If $f_1 < f_2$ (for minimization) → the minimum lies in $[a, x_2]$. Set $b = x_2$, **and reuse** $x_1$ as the new $x_2$.
   - If $f_1 > f_2$ → the minimum lies in $[x_1, b]$. Set $a = x_1$, **and reuse** $x_2$ as the new $x_1$.
5. **Check convergence:** If $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$ (tolerance), stop. Otherwise, return to Step 2.
6. **Final estimate:** $x^* \approx \frac{a + b}{2}$.

**Why It Works (The "Why" Behind Each Step):**
- The two interior points are positioned so that the **new** subinterval shares one of the old test points. This is the **key to efficiency**: only **one new function evaluation** is needed per iteration, not two.
- The golden ratio is the **unique positive number** that satisfies $\rho^2 + \rho - 1 = 0$, which is exactly the condition for self-similar interval reduction.

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Ratio Equation (KTU Board Favorite):**
> $$\phi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$$
> $$\rho = \frac{1}{\phi} = \frac{\sqrt{5} - 1}{2} \approx 0.618$$
> The defining property: $\rho^2 = 1 - \rho$, i.e., $\rho^2 + \rho = 1$.

---

### 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of Quadratic Interpolation

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Choose three initial points** $x_0 < x_1 < x_2$ that bracket the minimum. Evaluate the function to obtain $f_0, f_1, f_2$.
2. **Fit a quadratic** $q(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$ passing through the three points. This gives a system of three linear equations.
3. **Find the analytical minimum** of $q(x)$ by setting $q'(x) = 2ax + b = 0$:
   $$x_3 = -\frac{b}{2a}$$
4. **Use the explicit Lagrange-form formula** (avoids solving the system):
   $$x_3 = \frac{x_0^2(f_1 - f_2) + x_1^2(f_2 - f_0) + x_2^2(f_0 - f_1)}{2\bigl[x_0(f_1 - f_2) + x_1(f_2 - f_0) + x_2(f_0 - f_1)\bigr]}$$
5. **Evaluate** $f_3 = f(x_3)$ and check convergence: if $\vert x_3 - x_{\text{prev}} \vert < \varepsilon$, stop.
6. **Update the three points:** Discard the point with the **highest** function value among $\{x_0, x_1, x_2, x_3\}$, keeping the three lowest-valued points. Re-label them and repeat from Step 2.

**Why It Works (The "Why" Behind Each Step):**
- A parabola is the **simplest nonlinear function** that has a unique minimum. If the true $f(x)$ is "nearly parabolic" near the optimum, the approximation is excellent.
- Unlike Golden-Section, the bracket is **not preserved** as a hard interval; instead, the **point set** is updated based on function values.
- Convergence is **superlinear** (order $\approx 1.324$) for smooth functions, but it can fail if the points are not well chosen (e.g., all three points lie on one side of the minimum, or $f$ is non-quadratic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's Method vs. Quadratic Interpolation (KTU Comparison):**
> - Newton's method uses the **first and second derivatives** $f'(x)$ and $f''(x)$ and achieves **quadratic** convergence.
> - Quadratic Interpolation uses **only function values** (no derivatives) and achieves **superlinear** convergence — slower than Newton but derivative-free.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Method** | **Key Equation** | **Notation / Constants** | **Convergence Order** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Golden-Section | $\rho = \frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \approx 0.618$ | Golden ratio reciprocal | — |
| 2 | Golden-Section | $x_1 = a + (1-\rho)(b-a)$ | Left interior test point | — |
| 3 | Golden-Section | $x_2 = a + \rho(b-a)$ | Right interior test point | — |
| 4 | Golden-Section | $L_{n} = \rho^{n} L_0$ | Interval length after $n$ iterations | **Linear** (rate $=\rho$) |
| 5 | Golden-Section | $n = \frac{\ln(\varepsilon / L_0)}{\ln(\rho)}$ | Iterations to reach tolerance $\varepsilon$ | — |
| 6 | Quadratic Interp. | $x_3 = \frac{x_0^2(f_1-f_2) + x_1^2(f_2-f_0) + x_2^2(f_0-f_1)}{2[x_0(f_1-f_2) + x_1(f_2-f_0) + x_2(f_0-f_1)]}$ | Min of fitted parabola | **Superlinear** ($\approx 1.324$) |
| 7 | Quadratic Interp. | $q'(x) = 2ax + b = 0 \Rightarrow x_3 = -b/(2a)$ | From $q(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$ | — |
| 8 | Quadratic Interp. | $a = \frac{(f_0-f_1)}{(x_0-x_1)} - \frac{(f_1-f_2)}{(x_1-x_2)}$ divided by $(x_0-x_2)$ | Coefficient of parabola | — |
| 9 | Both | Termination: $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$ **or** $\vert f(x_{k}) - f(x_{k-1}) \vert < \varepsilon$ | Convergence tolerance | — |
| 10 | Both | **Unimodal requirement:** $f$ strictly decreases then strictly increases (minimization) | Precondition | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility (KTU Industrial Context):**
> - **Golden-Section** is used in **reactor design** for finding optimal residence time, in **heat exchanger** area optimization, and in **pipeline diameter** cost minimization — whenever derivatives are unavailable or unreliable.
> - **Quadratic Interpolation** is preferred in **experimental optimization** (e.g., response surface methodology in chemical kinetics) where three trial experiments are conducted and a smooth model is desired.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Golden Ratio

We seek a positive number $\rho \in (0, 1)$ such that, when two points $x_1$ and $x_2$ are placed in interval $[a, b]$ at distances $(1-\rho)L$ and $\rho L$ from $a$ (where $L = b - a$), then **one of the old points coincides with a new point** in the reduced interval.

After eliminating the left sub-interval (so the new interval is $[x_1, b]$ of length $\rho L$), the new right interior point is placed at distance $\rho \cdot (\rho L) = \rho^2 L$ from $x_1$. For this to coincide with the old $x_2$ (which was at distance $(1-\rho)L$ from $a$ and thus at distance $L - \rho L = (1-\rho)L$ from $x_1$), we require:

$$
\rho^2 L = (1 - \rho) L
$$

Dividing by $L$:

$$
\rho^2 + \rho - 1 = 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula and choosing the positive root:

$$
\rho = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{5} - 1}{2}
$$

$$
\phi = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{\sqrt{5} + 1}{2} \approx 1.6180339887
$$

$$
\rho = 1 - \frac{1}{\phi} \approx 0.6180339887
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Quadratic Interpolation Minimum Formula

Let $q(x) = a x^2 + b x + c$ pass through three points:

$$
q(x_0) = a x_0^2 + b x_0 + c = f_0
$$
$$
q(x_1) = a x_1^2 + b x_1 + c = f_1
$$
$$
q(x_2) = a x_2^2 + b x_2 + c = f_2
$$

Subtracting the first equation from the second and third:

$$
a(x_1^2 - x_0^2) + b(x_1 - x_0) = f_1 - f_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a(x_1 + x_0) + b = \frac{f_1 - f_0}{x_1 - x_0}
$$

$$
a(x_2^2 - x_0^2) + b(x_2 - x_0) = f_2 - f_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a(x_2 + x_0) + b = \frac{f_2 - f_0}{x_2 - x_0}
$$

Subtracting these two:

$$
a(x_2 - x_1) = \frac{f_2 - f_0}{x_2 - x_0} - \frac{f_1 - f_0}{x_1 - x_0}
$$

Solving for $a$:

$$
a = \frac{1}{x_2 - x_1} \left[ \frac{f_2 - f_0}{x_2 - x_0} - \frac{f_1 - f_0}{x_1 - x_0} \right]
$$

Solving for $b$ by substitution back:

$$
b = \frac{f_1 - f_0}{x_1 - x_0} - a(x_1 + x_0)
$$

The minimum of the parabola is at $q'(x) = 2ax + b = 0$:

$$
x_3 = -\frac{b}{2a}
$$

After algebraic simplification (combining the two expressions for $a$ and $b$ over the common denominator $x_2 - x_1$), we obtain the closed-form **Powell's formula**:

$$
x_3 = \frac{x_0^2(f_1 - f_2) + x_1^2(f_2 - f_0) + x_2^2(f_0 - f_1)}{2 \bigl[ x_0(f_1 - f_2) + x_1(f_2 - f_0) + x_2(f_0 - f_1) \bigr]}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem Statement (Golden-Section):**
Minimize $f(x) = (x - 3)^2 + 2$ on the interval $[0, 6]$ using Golden-Section Search. Take tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.5$. Perform **3 iterations** and report the final estimate.

**Step 1 — Initial Setup:**
$L_0 = b - a = 6 - 0 = 6$
$\rho = 0.6180339887$,  $1 - \rho = 0.3819660113$

$$
x_1 = a + (1-\rho)(b-a) = 0 + 0.3819660113 \times 6 = 2.291796068
$$
$$
x_2 = a + \rho(b-a) = 0 + 0.6180339887 \times 6 = 3.708203932
$$

Evaluate $f$:
$f(x_1) = (2.291796068 - 3)^2 + 2 = 0.501796^2 + 2 = 0.25180 + 2 = 2.25180$
$f(x_2) = (3.708203932 - 3)^2 + 2 = 0.70820^2 + 2 = 0.50155 + 2 = 2.50155$

**Step 2 — Iteration 1 Decision:**
$f_1 < f_2$ ($2.25180 < 2.50155$), so the minimum lies in $[a, x_2] = [0, 3.708203932]$.
- New bounds: $a_1 = 0$, $b_1 = 3.708203932$.
- Reuse old $x_1$ as the new $x_2$: $x_2^{(\text{new})} = 2.291796068$.
- Compute new $x_1$:
  $L_1 = 3.708203932$
  $x_1^{(\text{new})} = a_1 + (1-\rho) L_1 = 0 + 0.3819660113 \times 3.708203932 = 1.416407932$

Evaluate $f(x_1^{(\text{new})}) = (1.416407932 - 3)^2 + 2 = 2.50612 + 2 = 4.50612$.
We already have $f(x_2^{(\text{new})}) = 2.25180$.

**Step 3 — Iteration 2 Decision:**
$f(x_1^{(\text{new})}) = 4.50612 > f(x_2^{(\text{new})}) = 2.25180$, so the minimum lies in $[x_1^{(\text{new})}, b_1] = [1.416407932, 3.708203932]$.
- New bounds: $a_2 = 1.416407932$, $b_2 = 3.708203932$.
- Reuse $x_2^{(\text{new})} = 2.291796068$ as the new $x_1$.
- Compute new $x_2$:
  $L_2 = 3.708203932 - 1.416407932 = 2.291796000$
  $x_2^{(\text{new})} = a_2 + \rho L_2 = 1.416407932 + 0.6180339887 \times 2.291796000 = 2.833796000$

Evaluate $f(x_2^{(\text{new})}) = (2.833796000 - 3)^2 + 2 = 0.02762 + 2 = 2.02762$.

**Step 4 — Iteration 3 Decision:**
$f(x_1) = 2.25180 > f(x_2) = 2.02762$, so the minimum lies in $[x_1, b_2] = [2.291796068, 3.708203932]$.
- Final interval length $L_3 = 3.708203932 - 2.291796068 = 1.416407864$.

**Step 5 — Convergence Check:**
$L_3 = 1.4164 > \varepsilon = 0.5$ → Not yet converged.
- Best estimate: $x^* \approx \frac{a_3 + b_3}{2} = \frac{2.291796068 + 3.708203932}{2} = 3.000000000$
- (Note: the exact minimum is $x = 3$, $f^* = 2$.)

| **Iter** | $a$ | $b$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $f(x_1)$ | $f(x_2)$ | $L$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 6.0000 | 2.2918 | 3.7082 | 2.2518 | 2.5015 | 6.0000 |
| 1 | 0.0000 | 3.7082 | 1.4164 | 2.2918 | 4.5061 | 2.2518 | 3.7082 |
| 2 | 1.4164 | 3.7082 | 2.2918 | 2.8338 | 2.2518 | 2.0276 | 2.2918 |
| 3 | 2.2918 | 3.7082 | — | — | — | — | 1.4164 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:**
> Notice the bracket length at each step: $6 \to 3.7082 \to 2.2918 \to 1.4164$. Each step multiplies by $\rho \approx 0.618$, confirming the linear convergence of the Golden-Section method.

---

**Problem Statement (Quadratic Interpolation):**
Minimize $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 7$ using three initial points $x_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 3$. Take tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.1$. Perform **2 iterations**.

**Step 1 — Initial Evaluations:**
$f_0 = 0 - 0 + 7 = 7$
$f_1 = 1 - 4 + 7 = 4$
$f_2 = 9 - 12 + 7 = 4$

**Step 2 — First Parabolic Minimum:**
Using Powell's formula:

$$
x_3 = \frac{0^2(4-4) + 1^2(4-7) + 3^2(7-4)}{2[0(4-4) + 1(4-7) + 3(7-4)]}
$$

$$
x_3 = \frac{0 + 1 \times (-3) + 9 \times 3}{2[0 + (-3) + 9]} = \frac{0 - 3 + 27}{2 \times 6} = \frac{24}{12} = 2.000
$$

$f_3 = f(2) = 4 - 8 + 7 = 3$.

**Step 3 — Update Point Set:**
The four candidate points have $f$-values $\{f(0)=7, f(1)=4, f(2)=3, f(3)=4\}$. The highest is $f(0) = 7$, so discard $x = 0$.
New set: $x_0 = 1$, $x_1 = 2$, $x_2 = 3$ with $f_0 = 4$, $f_1 = 3$, $f_2 = 4$.

**Step 4 — Second Parabolic Minimum:**

$$
x_3 = \frac{1^2(3-4) + 2^2(4-4) + 3^2(4-3)}{2[1(3-4) + 2(4-4) + 3(4-3)]}
$$

$$
x_3 = \frac{1 \times (-1) + 4 \times 0 + 9 \times 1}{2[(-1) + 0 + 3]} = \frac{-1 + 0 + 9}{2 \times 2} = \frac{8}{4} = 2.000
$$

**Step 5 — Convergence:**
$\vert x_3^{\text{new}} - x_3^{\text{old}} \vert = \vert 2.000 - 2.000 \vert = 0 < \varepsilon$. **Converged.**
Exact minimum: $x^* = 2$, $f^* = 3$ (matches $d/dx(x^2 - 4x + 7) = 2x - 4 = 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on Convergence Rate:**
> The true function is exactly quadratic, so the method converges in **one iteration** to the exact answer. In general, the convergence is superlinear with order approximately **1.324**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Golden-Section Search — Interval Reduction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define f x, bounds a, b, tolerance eps] --> B[Compute rho = 0.6180339887]
    B --> C[Set x1 = a + 1-rho times b-a]
    C --> D[Set x2 = a + rho times b-a]
    D --> E[Evaluate f1 = f x1, f2 = f x2]
    E --> F{Check convergence: b - a less than eps?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Report x_star = a+b / 2 as minimum]
    F -- No --> H{f1 less than f2?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Set b = x2, Reuse old x1 as new x2]
    I --> J[Compute new x1 using new bounds]
    H -- No --> K[Set a = x1, Reuse old x2 as new x1]
    K --> L[Compute new x2 using new bounds]
    J --> F
    L --> F
    G --> M[End]
```

### 4.2 Quadratic Interpolation — Three-Point Bracketed Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose x0 less than x1 less than x2] --> B[Evaluate f0 = f x0, f1 = f x1, f2 = f x2]
    B --> C[Compute parabolic minimum x3 using Powell formula]
    C --> D[Evaluate f3 = f x3]
    D --> E{Convergence: x3 - x_prev less than eps?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Report x3 as minimum estimate]
    E -- No --> G[Identify point with highest f-value among x0, x1, x2, x3]
    G --> H[Discard that point, keep three best points]
    H --> I[Re-label and sort as new x0, x1, x2]
    I --> B
    F --> J[End]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Method Selection Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Problem: Minimize f x on a, b] --> B{Available: derivatives?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Newton or Secant Method]
    B -- No --> D{Function behavior known?}
    D -- Noisy or non-smooth --> E[Use Golden-Section Search - Robust]
    D -- Smooth and nearly quadratic --> F{How many starting points?}
    F -- Two points --> G[Use Parabolic Interpolation]
    F -- Three points --> H[Use Quadratic Interpolation - Faster]
    E --> I[Output: x_star, f x_star]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    C --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart (KTU Board Pattern):**
> The first decision node asks about derivative availability. In process optimization of **black-box systems** (e.g., CFD-coupled reactor simulations), derivatives are unavailable — thus Golden-Section and Quadratic Interpolation are the workhorses. Quadratic Interpolation is preferred when the response is "smooth enough" that a parabolic fit is meaningful.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**Define the Golden-Section Search method. State the value of the golden ratio and its significance in interval reduction.**

**Model Answer:**
The Golden-Section Search method is a sequential, derivative-free numerical technique for finding the minimum of a unimodal function $f(x)$ on a closed interval $[a, b]$. It uses two interior test points placed at fractions $\rho$ and $1-\rho$ of the interval, where $\rho = \frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \approx 0.6180339887$ is the reciprocal of the golden ratio $\phi = \frac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2}$. Its significance is that each iteration reduces the interval length by the **same factor** $\rho$, and only **one new function evaluation** is needed per iteration because the new interval reuses one of the old test points. This makes it the most efficient **fixed-fraction reduction** method.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
**List any three differences between the Golden-Section Search method and the Quadratic Interpolation method.**

**Model Answer:**

| **S.No.** | **Golden-Section Search** | **Quadratic Interpolation** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses the constant reduction factor $\rho \approx 0.618$. | Fits a variable quadratic $q(x) = ax^2+bx+c$ at each iteration. |
| 2 | **Linear convergence** (rate = $\rho \approx 0.618$). | **Superlinear convergence** (order $\approx 1.324$). |
| 3 | Requires only **two** starting points (interval bounds). | Requires **three** starting points. |
| 4 | Interval $[a, b]$ is preserved at each step. | Bracket is updated by **point replacement**, not interval reduction. |
| 5 | Robust to noise and non-smoothness. | Less robust; sensitive to point selection. |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Golden-Section Method

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2 — 7 Marks]**
**Find the minimum of $f(x) = 2x^2 + \frac{16}{x}$ on the interval $[1, 5]$ using the Golden-Section Search method. Take tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.5$ and perform iterations until the interval length is less than 0.5.**

**Model Solution:**

We are given $f(x) = 2x^2 + 16/x$ on $[1, 5]$. The exact minimum is at $f'(x) = 4x - 16/x^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x^3 = 4 \Rightarrow x^* = 4^{1/3} \approx 1.587$, $f^* = 2(1.587)^2 + 16/1.587 = 5.038 + 10.082 = 15.12$.

**Iteration 0:** $a = 1$, $b = 5$, $L_0 = 4$.
$\rho = 0.6180339887$, $1 - \rho = 0.3819660113$.

$$
x_1 = 1 + 0.3819660113 \times 4 = 1 + 1.527864045 = 2.527864045
$$
$$
x_2 = 1 + 0.6180339887 \times 4 = 1 + 2.472135955 = 3.472135955
$$

$f(x_1) = 2(2.527864)^2 + 16/2.527864 = 2(6.390) + 6.330 = 12.780 + 6.330 = 19.110$
$f(x_2) = 2(3.472136)^2 + 16/3.472136 = 2(12.055) + 4.609 = 24.110 + 4.609 = 28.719$

**Decision:** $f(x_1) = 19.11 < f(x_2) = 28.72$, so minimum is in $[1, x_2] = [1, 3.472136]$.
New bounds: $a_1 = 1$, $b_1 = 3.472136$. Reuse $x_1 = 2.527864$ as new $x_2$.

**Iteration 1:** $L_1 = 2.472136$.
$x_1^{\text{new}} = 1 + 0.381966 \times 2.472136 = 1 + 0.944272 = 1.944272$

$f(1.944272) = 2(3.780) + 16/1.944 = 7.560 + 8.230 = 15.790$
We retain $f(2.527864) = 19.110$.

**Decision:** $f(x_1^{\text{new}}) = 15.79 < f(x_2^{\text{new}}) = 19.11$, so minimum is in $[1, 2.527864]$.
New bounds: $a_2 = 1$, $b_2 = 2.527864$. Reuse $x_1^{\text{new}} = 1.944272$ as new $x_2$.

**Iteration 2:** $L_2 = 1.527864$.
$x_1^{\text{new}} = 1 + 0.381966 \times 1.527864 = 1 + 0.583592 = 1.583592$

$f(1.583592) = 2(2.508) + 16/1.5836 = 5.016 + 10.104 = 15.120$
We retain $f(1.944272) = 15.790$.

**Decision:** $f(x_1^{\text{new}}) = 15.120 < f(x_2^{\text{new}}) = 15.790$, so minimum is in $[1, 1.944272]$.
New bounds: $a_3 = 1$, $b_3 = 1.944272$. $L_3 = 0.944272$.

**Iteration 3:** $L_3 = 0.944 > 0.5$. Continue.
$x_1^{\text{new}} = 1 + 0.381966 \times 0.944272 = 1 + 0.360678 = 1.360678$
$f(1.360678) = 2(1.852) + 16/1.3607 = 3.704 + 11.760 = 15.464$
We retain $f(1.583592) = 15.120$.

**Decision:** $f(x_1^{\text{new}}) = 15.464 > f(x_2^{\text{new}}) = 15.120$, so minimum is in $[1.360678, 1.944272]$.
New bounds: $a_4 = 1.360678$, $b_4 = 1.944272$. $L_4 = 0.583594$.

**Iteration 4:** $L_4 = 0.5836 > 0.5$. Continue.
$x_1^{\text{new}} = 1.360678 + 0.381966 \times 0.583594 = 1.360678 + 0.222928 = 1.583606$
Reuse $x_2^{\text{new}} = 1.583592$.

**Decision:** $f(1.583606) \approx 15.120 \approx f(1.583592) = 15.120$, so minimum is in $[1.583606, 1.944272]$.
New bounds: $a_5 = 1.583606$, $b_5 = 1.944272$. $L_5 = 0.360666 < 0.5$. **Converged.**

**Final Answer:** $x^* \approx \frac{1.583606 + 1.944272}{2} = 1.763939$, $f^* \approx 15.30$ (approximate; exact is $x^* = 1.587$, $f^* = 15.12$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common 2-Mark Deduction):**
> Students often forget to **reuse** the old interior point in the new interval. Each iteration needs **only ONE new function evaluation**, not two. Marking schemes deduct 1–2 marks for showing two new evaluations per step. Also, students confuse the elimination rule direction (i.e., whether to keep the lower or higher $f$-value sub-interval for minimization).

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2 — 7 Marks]**
**Compare the convergence of the Golden-Section and Quadratic Interpolation methods. State two advantages and two limitations of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Convergence Comparison:**

| **Aspect** | **Golden-Section Search** | **Quadratic Interpolation** |
|---|---|---|
| Order of Convergence | **Linear** (rate $\rho \approx 0.618$) | **Superlinear** (order $\approx 1.324$) |
| Iterations to halve the interval | Constant factor (each iter $\times 0.618$) | Variable; depends on local curvature |
| Function evaluations per iter | **1** (one new, one reused) | 1 new + 3 retained = 4 total in the set |
| Starting points | 2 (interval bounds) | 3 (three bracketing points) |
| Robustness | **Very high** (always converges for unimodal $f$) | **Moderate** (can fail if $f$ non-quadratic or points collinear) |

**Golden-Section — Advantages:**
1. **Guaranteed convergence** for any unimodal continuous function — no assumptions on smoothness or differentiability.
2. **No derivative required** and **only one new function evaluation per iteration** — extremely efficient for expensive black-box functions (e.g., CFD simulations, pilot plant experiments).

**Golden-Section — Limitations:**
1. **Slow (linear) convergence** — requires many iterations for tight tolerance.
2. **Cannot exploit information** about the curvature or shape of the function — treats all functions as if they were the worst case (parabola opening in the "wrong" direction).

**Quadratic Interpolation — Advantages:**
1. **Superlinear convergence** — much fewer iterations than Golden-Section for smooth functions.
2. **Uses curvature information** implicitly (the fitted parabola encodes $f'$, $f''$ at the points), giving a higher-quality estimate per step.

**Quadratic Interpolation — Limitations:**
1. **Requires three starting points** and can **fail to converge** if the function is not locally quadratic (e.g., asymmetric, multi-modal near the bracket).
2. **Sensitive to numerical round-off** and the relative spacing of the three points — if two points coincide, the formula is singular (division by zero).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> A common student error is to state that Quadratic Interpolation has "quadratic convergence." This is **incorrect** — it has **superlinear** convergence with order $\approx 1.324$. Quadratic convergence is the order of **Newton's method**, which uses derivatives. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this conflation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Quadratic Interpolation Alternative

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2 — 7 Marks]**
**Minimize $f(x) = x^4 - 14x^3 + 60x^2 - 70x$ using the Quadratic Interpolation method. Use initial points $x_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 3$. Take $\varepsilon = 0.01$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Initial Evaluations:**
$f_0 = f(0) = 0 - 0 + 0 - 0 = 0$
$f_1 = f(1) = 1 - 14 + 60 - 70 = -23$
$f_2 = f(3) = 81 - 378 + 540 - 210 = 33$

**First Parabolic Minimum (Powell's formula):**

$$
x_3 = \frac{0^2(-23-33) + 1^2(33-0) + 3^2(0-(-23))}{2[0(-23-33) + 1(33-0) + 3(0-(-23))]}
$$

$$
x_3 = \frac{0 + 33 + 207}{2[0 + 33 + 69]} = \frac{240}{2 \times 102} = \frac{240}{204} = 1.1765
$$

$f(1.1765) = (1.1765)^4 - 14(1.1765)^3 + 60(1.1765)^2 - 70(1.1765)$
$\approx 1.916 - 22.820 + 83.058 - 82.355 = -20.201$

**Update Point Set:**
The four $f$-values are: $f(0) = 0$, $f(1) = -23$, $f(1.1765) = -20.20$, $f(3) = 33$.
The **highest** is $f(3) = 33$ → discard $x = 3$.
New set: $x_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 1.1765$ with $f_0 = 0$, $f_1 = -23$, $f_2 = -20.20$.

**Second Parabolic Minimum:**

$$
x_3 = \frac{0^2(-23-(-20.20)) + 1^2(-20.20-0) + (1.1765)^2(0-(-23))}{2[0(-23-(-20.20)) + 1(-20.20-0) + 1.1765(0-(-23))]}
$$

$$
x_3 = \frac{0 + (-20.20) + 1.3841 \times 23}{2[0 + (-20.20) + 27.060]} = \frac{-20.20 + 31.834}{2 \times 6.860} = \frac{11.634}{13.720} = 0.8478
$$

**Update:** $f(0.8478) \approx 0.516 - 8.534 + 43.130 - 59.346 = -24.234$.
The four $f$-values: $f(0) = 0$, $f(0.8478) = -24.23$, $f(1) = -23$, $f(1.1765) = -20.20$.
Highest is $f(0) = 0$ → discard $x = 0$.
New set: $x_0 = 0.8478$, $x_1 = 1.0000$, $x_2 = 1.1765$ with $f_0 = -24.23$, $f_1 = -23$, $f_2 = -20.20$.

**Convergence Check:** $x_3^{\text{new}} = 0.8478$ vs $x_3^{\text{old}} = 1.1765$. Difference $= 0.329 > 0.01$. Continue.

**Third Parabolic Minimum:**

$$
x_3 = \frac{(0.8478)^2(-23-(-20.20)) + 1^2(-20.20-(-24.23)) + (1.1765)^2(-24.23-(-23))}{2[0.8478(-23-(-20.20)) + 1(-20.20-(-24.23)) + 1.1765(-24.23-(-23))]}
$$

$$
x_3 = \frac{0.7188 \times (-2.80) + 4.03 + 1.3841 \times (-1.23)}{2[0.8478 \times (-2.80) + 4.03 + 1.1765 \times (-1.23)]}
$$

$$
x_3 = \frac{-2.013 + 4.03 - 1.702}{2[-2.374 + 4.03 - 1.447]} = \frac{0.315}{2 \times 0.209} = \frac{0.315}{0.418} = 0.7536
$$

**Update:** $f(0.7536) \approx 0.3228 - 5.985 + 34.083 - 52.752 = -24.331$.
Now $f_0 = -24.33$ (at $x = 0.7536$) is the new minimum; discard $x = 1.0000$ (next highest).
**Best estimate:** $x^* \approx 0.7536$, $f^* \approx -24.33$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> A common mistake is in the **sign of the function values** in Powell's formula. Students often mix up $f_1 - f_2$ with $f_2 - f_1$ or misplace them in the numerator. **Always double-check** the formula before substituting: $x_3 = \frac{x_0^2(f_1-f_2) + x_1^2(f_2-f_0) + x_2^2(f_0-f_1)}{2[x_0(f_1-f_2) + x_1(f_2-f_0) + x_2(f_0-f_1)]}$. Also, never forget to **re-sort** the three points in increasing order before the next iteration.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2 — 7 Marks]**
**Derive the closed-form expression for the minimum of the parabola passing through three points $(x_0, f_0)$, $(x_1, f_1)$, $(x_2, f_2)$. Hence obtain Powell's Quadratic Interpolation formula.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Let the parabola be $q(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$. It satisfies:

$$
q(x_i) = a x_i^2 + b x_i + c = f_i, \quad i = 0, 1, 2.
$$

**Step 2:** Take differences to eliminate $c$:

$$
q(x_1) - q(x_0) = a(x_1^2 - x_0^2) + b(x_1 - x_0) = f_1 - f_0
$$
$$
\Rightarrow (x_1 - x_0)[a(x_1 + x_0) + b] = f_1 - f_0
$$
$$
\Rightarrow a(x_1 + x_0) + b = \frac{f_1 - f_0}{x_1 - x_0} \quad \text{...(I)}
$$

Similarly:

$$
a(x_2 + x_0) + b = \frac{f_2 - f_0}{x_2 - x_0} \quad \text{...(II)}
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract (I) from (II):

$$
a(x_2 - x_1) = \frac{f_2 - f_0}{x_2 - x_0} - \frac{f_1 - f_0}{x_1 - x_0}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back and using $x_3 = -b/(2a)$, and simplifying (after multiplying numerator and denominator by $(x_2-x_0)(x_1-x_0)$):

$$
x_3 = \frac{x_0^2(f_1-f_2) + x_1^2(f_2-f_0) + x_2^2(f_0-f_1)}{2[x_0(f_1-f_2) + x_1(f_2-f_0) + x_2(f_0-f_1)]}
$$

This is **Powell's Quadratic Interpolation formula**. (The detailed algebraic reduction is given in Section 3.2 above.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> The most frequent error is forgetting the **factor of 2** in the denominator. Some textbooks write the formula without the 2; KTU 2024 Scheme **does include the 2** (this is the correct normalization that yields $x_3 = -b/(2a)$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Golden-Section Search** is a **derivative-free, sequential, interval-reduction** method for **unimodal** single-variable optimization.
- [x] **Golden ratio** $\phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$; **reduction factor** $\rho = \frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \approx 0.618$.
- [x] **Interval positions:** $x_1 = a + (1-\rho)(b-a)$ and $x_2 = a + \rho(b-a)$.
- [x] **Convergence:** **Linear** at rate $\rho$; interval length after $n$ iterations: $L_n = \rho^n L_0$.
- [x] **Efficiency:** Only **one new** function evaluation per iteration (one old point is reused).
- [x] **Quadratic Interpolation** uses **three points** to fit a parabola and find its analytical minimum.
- [x] **Powell's Formula:** $x_3 = \frac{x_0^2(f_1-f_2) + x_1^2(f_2-f_0) + x_2^2(f_0-f_1)}{2[x_0(f_1-f_2) + x_1(f_2-f_0) + x_2(f_0-f_1)]}$.
- [x] **Convergence:** **Superlinear** with order $\approx 1.324$ (NOT quadratic — that's Newton's).
- [x] **Update rule:** Discard the point with the **highest** $f$-value among the four candidates; re-label and re-sort.
- [x] **Pre-condition:** Both methods require a **unimodal** objective function on the search domain.
- [x] **Termination criteria:** (i) Interval length $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$, OR (ii) successive $x$-estimates differ by less than $\varepsilon$, OR (iii) function-value change $\vert f(x_k) - f(x_{k-1}) \vert < \varepsilon$.
- [x] **Golden-Section is robust but slow; Quadratic Interpolation is fast but less robust** — choose based on smoothness of $f$.
- [x] **KTU 2024 exam tip:** Always state the **reused point** in your Golden-Section iteration table — this is where the "efficiency" credit is given.
- [x] **Avoid the trap:** Quadratic Interpolation is **not** the same as Newton's method — Newton uses derivatives, quadratic interpolation does not.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: One Dimensional Gradient Search Methods (Newton's Method and Quasi-Newton Method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** One Dimensional Gradient Search Methods (Newton's Method and Quasi-Newton Method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Newton's Method — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Method (Definition as per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Newton's method is a second-order **gradient-based descent technique** used to find the stationary point (minimum or maximum) of a univariate objective function $f(x)$ by iteratively computing the root of its first derivative $f'(x)$, using the local quadratic approximation obtained from the **second-order Taylor series expansion**.

Mathematically, the iterative formula is:

$$x_{k+1} \;=\; x_{k} \;-\; \frac{f'(x_{k})}{f''(x_{k})}$$

provided that $f''(x_{k}) \neq 0$ and $f''(x_{k}) > 0$ for a **minimum**.

The method requires only **one function evaluation per iteration** beyond the gradient and Hessian information, making it a cornerstone of single-variable optimization in **process engineering** — e.g., minimizing reactor volume, finding optimal reflux ratio, or determining break-even conversion in a CSTR.

---

## 1.2 Quasi-Newton Method — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quasi-Newton Method (Definition):**
> A class of variable-metric algorithms that **approximate the inverse Hessian** $H_{k} \approx \left[f''(x_{k})\right]^{-1}$ using only first-derivative (gradient) information, while maintaining the **positive-definiteness** and **symmetry** required for guaranteed descent. They satisfy the **secant condition** $H_{k+1} \, y_{k} = s_{k}$ where $s_{k} = x_{k+1} - x_{k}$ and $y_{k} = f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_{k})$.

In the one-dimensional (1-D) case, the quasi-Newton update reduces dramatically to a **scalar secant approximation**:

$$H_{k+1} \;=\; \frac{s_{k}}{y_{k}} \;=\; \frac{x_{k+1} - x_{k}}{f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_{k})}$$

This is mathematically identical to the classical **secant method** of root-finding, applied to $f'(x) = 0$.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Walking Down a Foggy Mountain:**
>
> Imagine you are standing on a hillside in dense fog and you want to reach the **lowest valley** as fast as possible. You cannot see the bottom, but you have two instruments:
> - A **slope meter** (gives you the gradient $f'(x)$) — tells you *which way is down*.
> - A **curvature meter** (gives you $f''(x)$) — tells you *how steep the slope is changing*.
>
> **Newton's Method** = Use *both* instruments each step. The slope tells you direction; the curvature tells you *how big a leap* to take. On a sharply curved hill, leap big; on a gentle slope, step small.
>
> **Quasi-Newton Method** = The curvature meter is expensive (it requires extra experiments/calculations). So you *remember* the curvature from your last two positions and **estimate** it from the change in slope — like a hiker who learns the terrain by feel, not by map.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Interpretation |
|------|--------|---------|----------------------------|
| Objective Function | $f(x)$ | Scalar to be minimized | Cost, energy, time, error |
| First Derivative | $f'(x)$ | Slope / gradient | Rate of change of process variable |
| Second Derivative | $f''(x)$ | Curvature | Sensitivity of the rate to change |
| Hessian Approximation | $H_k$ | Approximate $1/f''(x_k)$ | Memory of recent curvature |
| Step Size | $\lambda_k$ | Length of move along search direction | Magnitude of operating-point update |
| Step Direction | $d_k$ | Direction of descent | Sign of $f'(x_k)$ is reversed |
| Secant Vector | $s_k$ | $x_{k+1} - x_k$ | Displacement of operating point |
| Gradient Difference | $y_k$ | $f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)$ | Change in process sensitivity |

> [!NOTE]
> The parameter **step size** $\lambda_k$ takes the value **1.0** for *pure* (full-step) Newton's method. **Modified Newton's method** introduces $0 < \lambda_k \leq 1$ to ensure global convergence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Newton's Method

The algorithm is built on a **second-order Taylor expansion** of $f(x)$ around the current iterate $x_k$:

$$f(x) \;\approx\; \phi(x) \;=\; f(x_k) \;+\; f'(x_k)\,(x - x_k) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,f''(x_k)\,(x - x_k)^2$$

Since $\phi(x)$ is a **parabola**, its stationary point is found by setting $\phi'(x) = 0$:

$$0 \;=\; f'(x_k) \;+\; f''(x_k)\,(x - x_k) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x \;=\; x_k - \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

This $x$ becomes the **next iterate** $x_{k+1}$.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initialize**: Choose a starting point $x_0$ and a tolerance $\varepsilon > 0$ (typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$ in KTU numericals).
2. **Evaluate**: Compute $f'(x_k)$ and $f''(x_k)$ at the current point.
3. **Check Convergence**: Stop if $\vert f'(x_k) \vert < \varepsilon$ — the first-order optimality condition is satisfied.
4. **Update**: Set $x_{k+1} = x_k - f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$.
5. **Iterate**: Set $k \leftarrow k+1$ and return to Step 2.

### Why It Works (The "Why")

- **Local quadratic model**: The Taylor expansion is *exact* up to second-order, so near the optimum, the parabola matches $f(x)$ to $O(\Delta x^3)$ — the error is of *third order*.
- **Convergence rate**: Newton's method exhibits **quadratic convergence** near the minimum, meaning the number of correct digits roughly **doubles** each iteration.
- **Curvature-aware step**: When $f''(x_k)$ is large (sharp curvature), the step is *small*; when $f''(x_k)$ is small (flat), the step is *large* — automatically self-scaling.

### When It Fails (The "How NOT To")

- **Divergence at inflection points**: If $f''(x_k) = 0$ (e.g., at an inflection point like $x = 0$ in $f(x) = x^3$), the iteration is undefined.
- **Wrong direction**: If $f''(x_k) < 0$ (concave region), the method may *ascend* toward a maximum.
- **Far from optimum**: The quadratic model is only a *local* approximation; if $x_0$ is far from the minimum, divergence is possible.

---

## 2.2 Modified Newton's Method

To guarantee *global* convergence, a **step-length parameter** $\lambda_k$ is introduced:

$$x_{k+1} \;=\; x_k \;-\; \lambda_k \,\frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}, \quad 0 < \lambda_k \le 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> Common strategies to choose $\lambda_k$:
> - **Armijo line search** (most common): $\lambda_k = \max \lambda$ such that $f(x_{k+1}) \le f(x_k) + c_1 \lambda_k f'(x_k)\,d_k$ with $c_1 = 10^{-4}$.
> - **Minimum bracketing** (KTU standard): try $\lambda = 1, 0.5, 0.25, \dots$ until $f(x_{k+1}) < f(x_k)$.

---

## 2.3 Quasi-Newton Method — The Core Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bottleneck of Newton's Method in Multi-Dimensions**: Computing the full Hessian matrix $\nabla^2 f$ costs $O(n^2)$ function evaluations and $O(n^3)$ operations to invert. For a chemical process with $n = 50$ design variables, this is prohibitive.

The Quasi-Newton idea: **build up an approximation** $H_k \approx [\nabla^2 f(x_k)]^{-1}$ from successive gradient evaluations only.

### The Secant Condition (Newton's Equation)

To ensure $H_{k+1}$ acts like a true inverse Hessian on the most recent change in gradient:

$$H_{k+1} \, y_k \;=\; s_k$$

where in 1-D:

$$s_k = x_{k+1} - x_k, \quad y_k = f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)$$

In 1-D, the secant condition has a **unique scalar solution**:

$$H_{k+1} \;=\; \frac{s_k}{y_k} \;=\; \frac{x_{k+1} - x_k}{f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)}$$

This scalar $H_{k+1}$ replaces $1/f''(x_{k+1})$ in the next Newton-like update:

$$x_{k+2} \;=\; x_{k+1} \;-\; H_{k+1}\,f'(x_{k+1})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interesting fact (often tested in KTU):** In *one dimension*, all Quasi-Newton variants (DFP, BFGS, SR1, etc.) reduce to the *same* scalar update as the secant method. The differences between DFP and BFGS appear only in **n-dimensions**, where the under-determined secant equation admits infinitely many solutions.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-exam essential. Memorize the table before any numerical.**

| # | Formula | Name | Condition / Note |
|---|---------|------|------------------|
| 1 | $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$ | Newton's Update | $f''(x_k) > 0$ for minimum |
| 2 | $x_{k+1} = x_k - \lambda_k \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$ | Modified Newton | $0 < \lambda_k \le 1$ |
| 3 | $f(x) \approx f(x_k) + f'(x_k)\Delta x + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(x_k)\Delta x^2$ | Taylor Approx. | Local quadratic model |
| 4 | $s_k = x_{k+1} - x_k$ | Secant Vector | Displacement |
| 5 | $y_k = f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)$ | Gradient Diff. | Change in slope |
| 6 | $H_{k+1} = \dfrac{s_k}{y_k}$ | Quasi-Newton (1D) | Secant approximation |
| 7 | $H_{k+1}\,y_k = s_k$ | Secant Condition | Inverse Hessian property |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \vert f'(x_k) \vert$ | Convergence Test | First-order necessary condition |
| 9 | $H_0 = 1$ (or $\dfrac{1}{f''(x_0)}$) | Initial Hessian | Identity or curvature scaling |
| 10 | $x_{k+1} = x_k - H_k f'(x_k)$ | Generic Quasi-Newton | Vector form (1D = scalar) |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Newton's Method** is used in **Newton-Raphson** algorithms for solving nonlinear systems arising in **flash calculations, bubble/dew point, and chemical equilibrium**.
- **Quasi-Newton Methods** underpin modern **process simulators** (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, gPROMS) for **dynamic optimization** and **real-time model predictive control (MPC)** of chemical plants.
- In **parameter estimation**, the Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm (a damped Quasi-Newton variant) is the *de facto* industry standard for fitting kinetic models to reactor data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Newton's Method from Taylor's Theorem

We start with the **second-order Taylor series** expansion of $f(x)$ about the current iterate $x_k$:

$$f(x) \;=\; f(x_k) \;+\; f'(x_k)\,(x - x_k) \;+\; \frac{1}{2!}\,f''(x_k)\,(x - x_k)^2 \;+\; \frac{1}{3!}\,f'''(\xi)\,(x - x_k)^3$$

where $\xi$ lies between $x$ and $x_k$. The truncated quadratic model is:

$$\phi(x) \;=\; f(x_k) \;+\; f'(x_k)\,(x - x_k) \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,f''(x_k)\,(x - x_k)^2$$

To find the *minimum* of $\phi(x)$, differentiate with respect to $x$ and set the result to zero:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} \;=\; f'(x_k) \;+\; f''(x_k)\,(x - x_k) \;=\; 0$$

Solving for $x$:

$$f''(x_k)\,(x - x_k) \;=\; -f'(x_k)$$

$$x \;=\; x_k \;-\; \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

By definition, this $x$ is the next iterate $x_{k+1}$:

$$\boxed{\,x_{k+1} \;=\; x_k \;-\; \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}\,}$$

### Analytical Verification of Descent Direction

We require $x_{k+1} < x_k$ if $x_k$ is to the *right* of the minimum (where $f'(x_k) > 0$). Since $f''(x_k) > 0$ at a minimum:

$$x_{k+1} - x_k \;=\; -\frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)} \;<\; 0 \quad \text{when } f'(x_k) > 0$$

Similarly, if $f'(x_k) < 0$ (left of minimum), the step is positive. Hence Newton's method **always descends** in a convex region.

---

## 3.2 Convergence Rate Analysis (Quadratic Convergence)

Let $x^*$ be the true minimum, so $f'(x^*) = 0$. Define the error $e_k = x_{k+1} - x^*$. From the Taylor expansion of $f'$ about $x^*$:

$$f'(x_{k+1}) \;=\; f'(x^*) \;+\; f''(x^*)\,e_k \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,f'''(x^*)\,e_k^2 \;+\; \mathcal{O}(e_k^3)$$

Substituting $f'(x^*) = 0$:

$$f'(x_{k+1}) \;=\; f''(x^*)\,e_k \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,f'''(x^*)\,e_k^2 \;+\; \cdots$$

From the Newton update:

$$e_k \;=\; x_{k+1} - x^* \;=\; \left[x_k - \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}\right] - x^*$$

Differentiate $f'$ once more around $x_k$:

$$f'(x^*) \;=\; f'(x_k) \;+\; f''(x_k)\,(x^* - x_k) \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,f'''(\eta)\,(x^* - x_k)^2$$

Using $f'(x^*) = 0$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)} \;=\; (x_k - x^*) \;+\; \frac{f'''(\eta)}{2\,f''(x_k)}\,(x_k - x^*)^2$$

Therefore:

$$e_{k-1} \;=\; x_k - x^* \;=\; \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)} \;-\; \frac{f'''(\eta)}{2\,f''(x_k)}\,(x_k - x^*)^2$$

$$e_{k-1} \;=\; \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)} \;-\; C\,e_{k-1}^2$$

From the Newton update, $e_{k-1} = f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$, which gives:

$$e_k \;=\; C\,e_{k-1}^2$$

This is the **quadratic convergence law**: the new error is proportional to the *square* of the previous error. The constant is $C = f'''(x^*) / [2\,f''(x^*)]$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the 1-D Quasi-Newton (Secant) Update

Start with the **secant condition** which enforces the inverse-Hessian property on the latest information:

$$H_{k+1}\,y_k \;=\; s_k$$

In 1-D, $H_{k+1}$, $y_k$, and $s_k$ are all **scalars**, so the equation has the unique solution:

$$H_{k+1} \;=\; \frac{s_k}{y_k} \;=\; \frac{x_{k+1} - x_k}{f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)}$$

This scalar plays the role of $1/f''(x_{k+1})$ in the *modified* Newton update. The iteration becomes:

$$x_{k+2} \;=\; x_{k+1} \;-\; H_{k+1}\,f'(x_{k+1})$$

$$\boxed{\,x_{k+2} \;=\; x_{k+1} \;-\; \frac{x_{k+1} - x_k}{f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)}\;f'(x_{k+1})\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of convergence of 1-D secant method**: The secant/quasi-Newton update has a **superlinear** convergence rate with order $\phi = (1 + \sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618$ (the **golden ratio**) — slower than pure Newton ($\phi = 2$) but requiring only *one* function evaluation per step (no second derivative needed).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Newton's Method, KTU Style)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6$. Start at $x_0 = 0$. Use $\varepsilon = 0.01$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Compute derivatives:
$$f'(x) \;=\; 3x^2 - 12x + 11, \quad f''(x) \;=\; 6x - 12$$

**Iteration 1** ($k = 0$, $x_0 = 0$):
$$f'(0) \;=\; 11, \quad f''(0) \;=\; -12$$
$$x_1 \;=\; 0 - \frac{11}{-12} \;=\; 0.9167$$
$$\vert f'(0.9167) \vert \;=\; \vert 3(0.8402) - 12(0.9167) + 11 \vert \;=\; \vert 2.521 \vert \;>\; 0.01$$

**Iteration 2** ($k = 1$, $x_1 = 0.9167$):
$$f'(0.9167) \;\approx\; 2.521, \quad f''(0.9167) \;=\; 6(0.9167) - 12 \;=\; -6.5$$
$$x_2 \;=\; 0.9167 - \frac{2.521}{-6.5} \;=\; 0.9167 + 0.3878 \;=\; 1.3045$$
$$\vert f'(1.3045) \vert \;=\; \vert 3(1.7017) - 12(1.3045) + 11 \vert \;=\; \vert 0.449 \vert \;>\; 0.01$$

**Iteration 3** ($k = 2$, $x_2 = 1.3045$):
$$f'(1.3045) \;\approx\; 0.449, \quad f''(1.3045) \;=\; 6(1.3045) - 12 \;=\; -4.173$$
$$x_3 \;=\; 1.3045 - \frac{0.449}{-4.173} \;=\; 1.3045 + 0.1076 \;=\; 1.4121$$
$$\vert f'(1.4121) \vert \;=\; \vert 3(1.994) - 12(1.4121) + 11 \vert \;\approx\; 0.094 \;>\; 0.01$$

**Iteration 4** ($k = 3$, $x_3 = 1.4121$):
$$f'(1.4121) \;\approx\; 0.094, \quad f''(1.4121) \;=\; 6(1.4121) - 12 \;=\; -3.5274$$
$$x_4 \;=\; 1.4121 - \frac{0.094}{-3.5274} \;=\; 1.4121 + 0.0266 \;=\; 1.4387$$

**Analytical Check:**
$$f'(x) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 3x^2 - 12x + 11 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 2 \pm \frac{\sqrt{12}}{6} \;=\; 2 \pm 0.5774$$

Two roots: $x \approx 1.4226$ (local **minimum**) and $x \approx 2.5774$ (local **maximum**). The algorithm correctly converges to $x^* \approx 1.4226$ in 4 iterations.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Quasi-Newton / Secant, 1-D)

Using the same $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6$, start with **two points** $x_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 0.5$ (no second derivative needed):

**Initial setup:**
$$f'(0) \;=\; 11, \quad f'(0.5) \;=\; 3(0.25) - 12(0.5) + 11 \;=\; 0.75 - 6 + 11 \;=\; 5.75$$

**Iteration 1:** Compute secant slope:
$$H_1 \;=\; \frac{x_1 - x_0}{f'(x_1) - f'(x_0)} \;=\; \frac{0.5 - 0}{5.75 - 11} \;=\; \frac{0.5}{-5.25} \;=\; -0.09524$$

$$x_2 \;=\; x_1 - H_1\,f'(x_1) \;=\; 0.5 - (-0.09524)(5.75) \;=\; 0.5 + 0.5476 \;=\; 1.0476$$

**Iteration 2:** 
$$f'(1.0476) \;=\; 3(1.0975) - 12(1.0476) + 11 \;=\; 3.293 - 12.571 + 11 \;=\; 1.722$$
$$H_2 \;=\; \frac{1.0476 - 0.5}{1.722 - 5.75} \;=\; \frac{0.5476}{-4.028} \;=\; -0.1360$$
$$x_3 \;=\; 1.0476 - (-0.1360)(1.722) \;=\; 1.0476 + 0.2342 \;=\; 1.2818$$

**Iteration 3:**
$$f'(1.2818) \;=\; 3(1.643) - 12(1.2818) + 11 \;=\; 4.929 - 15.382 + 11 \;=\; 0.547$$
$$H_3 \;=\; \frac{1.2818 - 1.0476}{0.547 - 1.722} \;=\; \frac{0.2342}{-1.175} \;=\; -0.1993$$
$$x_4 \;=\; 1.2818 - (-0.1993)(0.547) \;=\; 1.2818 + 0.1090 \;=\; 1.3908$$

The convergence is **slower** than Newton (superlinear, not quadratic), but each iteration requires *one* function evaluation vs. *two* — a worthwhile trade-off when $f''(x)$ is costly to compute.

---

## 3.6 Dimensional and Vector Form (For Multi-Variable Context, 1-D Reduction)

For the multi-dimensional case (n-D), the Quasi-Newton updates are matrix equations. The KTU syllabus mentions them for context; here is the **1-D scalar reduction** of the two most important families:

### DFP (Davidon–Fletcher–Powell) — 1-D Reduction

The n-D DFP formula is:
$$H_{k+1} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \frac{s_k s_k^T}{s_k^T y_k} \;-\; \frac{H_k y_k y_k^T H_k}{y_k^T H_k y_k}$$

In 1-D, all inner products reduce to products of scalars. Letting $H_k$, $s_k$, $y_k$ be scalars:
$$H_{k+1} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \frac{s_k^2}{s_k y_k} \;-\; \frac{H_k^2 y_k^2}{H_k y_k^2}$$
$$H_{k+1} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \frac{s_k}{y_k} \;-\; H_k \;=\; \frac{s_k}{y_k}$$

### BFGS (Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno) — 1-D Reduction

The n-D BFGS formula is:
$$H_{k+1} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \left(1 + \frac{y_k^T H_k y_k}{s_k^T y_k}\right)\frac{s_k s_k^T}{s_k^T y_k} \;-\; \frac{s_k y_k^T H_k + H_k y_k s_k^T}{s_k^T y_k}$$

In 1-D:
$$H_{k+1} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \left(1 + \frac{H_k y_k^2}{s_k y_k}\right)\frac{s_k^2}{s_k y_k} \;-\; \frac{2\,H_k y_k s_k}{s_k y_k}$$
$$H_{k+1} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \left(\frac{s_k y_k + H_k y_k^2}{s_k y_k}\right)\frac{s_k}{y_k} \;-\; 2 H_k$$
$$H_{k+1} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \frac{s_k}{y_k} + \frac{H_k y_k}{s_k} \;-\; 2 H_k \;=\; \frac{s_k}{y_k} - H_k + \frac{H_k y_k}{s_k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Wait — the algebra is cleaner if we recognize that the DFP and BFGS formulas are **equivalent** in 1-D *only* when $H_k$ happens to equal $s_k/y_k$ from the previous step. In general, the 1-D form is the **secant update** $H_{k+1} = s_k / y_k$, which is the **unique solution** of the scalar secant equation $H_{k+1} y_k = s_k$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow — Newton's Method (1-D)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose x0 and tolerance eps] --> B[Initialize k = 0]
    B --> C[Evaluate f prime xk and f double prime xk]
    C --> D{Is f double prime xk greater than 0?}
    D -- No --> E[Error: Not a minimum region, abort or use modified Newton]
    D -- Yes --> F{Is absolute f prime xk less than eps?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Converged: x star = xk, STOP]
    F -- No --> H[Compute new iterate: xk1 = xk minus f prime xk over f double prime xk]
    H --> I[Increment k = k plus 1]
    I --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Node ID Convention:** All Mermaid node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`...) to comply with Mermaid v10+ parser rules. Labels contain only clean uppercase text.

---

## 4.2 Algorithm Flow — Quasi-Newton (Secant) Method (1-D)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Choose x0 and x1, tolerance eps] --> B1[Compute f prime at x0 and x1]
    B1 --> C1[Set s0 = x1 minus x0, y0 = f prime x1 minus f prime x0]
    C1 --> D1[Set H1 = s0 over y0]
    D1 --> E1{Is absolute f prime x1 less than eps?}
    E1 -- Yes --> F1[Converged: x star = x1, STOP]
    E1 -- No --> G1[Update: x2 = x1 minus H1 times f prime x1]
    G1 --> H1[Shift: s1 = x2 minus x1, y1 = f prime x2 minus f prime x1]
    H1 --> I1[Update Hessian: H2 = s1 over y1]
    I1 --> J1[Set x1 = x2 and f prime x1 = f prime x2]
    J1 --> E1
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Newton vs Quasi-Newton Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NEWTON["NEWTON METHOD BLOCK"]
        N1[Input: x_k] --> N2[Compute f prime x_k]
        N1 --> N3[Compute f double prime x_k]
        N2 --> N4[Division Module]
        N3 --> N4
        N4 --> N5[x_k plus 1 equals x_k minus f prime over f double prime]
        N5 --> N6[Output: x_k plus 1]
    end

    subgraph QUASINEWTON["QUASI NEWTON SECANT BLOCK"]
        Q1[Input: x_k and x_k minus 1] --> Q2[Compute f prime at both points]
        Q2 --> Q3[Form s_k and y_k vectors]
        Q3 --> Q4[Compute H_k plus 1 equals s_k over y_k]
        Q4 --> Q5[x_k plus 1 equals x_k minus H_k plus 1 times f prime x_k]
        Q5 --> Q6[Output: x_k plus 1]
    end

    NEWTON --> COMPARE[PERFORMANCE COMPARISON METRICS]
    QUASINEWTON --> COMPARE

    COMPARE --> M1[Convergence: Quadratic vs Superlinear golden ratio]
    COMPARE --> M2[Cost per Iteration: Two function evals vs One]
    COMPARE --> M3[Robustness: Needs f double prime vs Not needed]
    COMPARE --> M4[Use Case: Smooth small problems vs Large scale or noisy]
```

---

## 4.4 Convergence Behavior — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT[Initial Guess x0] --> CHK1{Convex Region?}
    CHK1 -- No --> MOD[Use Modified Newton with step size lambda]
    CHK1 -- Yes --> ITER1[Newton Iteration 1]
    MOD --> ITER1
    ITER1 --> Q1{Quadratic Convergence Active?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> QSP[Rapid Doubling of Correct Digits]
    Q1 -- No --> SUP[Superlinear Secant Convergence]
    QSP --> END[Optimum Found]
    SUP --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The "Quadratic Convergence Active?" branch depends on whether the iterate has entered the *basin of attraction* of the minimum. Outside this basin, even pure Newton's method may behave erratically — hence the practical advice to use **Modified Newton** for global robustness.

---

## 4.5 Relationship Map — 1-D Search Methods Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[One Dimensional Gradient Search Methods] --> A[Use First Derivative Only]
    ROOT --> B[Use First and Second Derivatives]

    A --> A1[Secant Method]
    A --> A2[Quasi Newton in 1D equals Secant]

    B --> B1[Newton Raphson Method]
    B --> B2[Modified Newton with Step Size]

    B1 --> B1A[Full Step: lambda equals 1]
    B1 --> B1B[With Line Search: lambda varies]

    A1 --> A1A[Two Point Initialization]
    A1 --> A1B[Order phi equals 1.618]

    B1 --> B1C[Order 2 Quadratic]
```

This family tree clarifies the **taxonomy** that KTU examiners often test in 3-mark questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
> **Differentiate between Newton's method and the Quasi-Newton method in the context of one-dimensional optimization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate 1 mark per distinct point):**

| # | Newton's Method | Quasi-Newton Method |
|---|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Uses **both** $f'(x)$ and $f''(x)$ at every iteration. | Uses only $f'(x)$; **approximates** $f''(x)$ or its inverse. |
| 2 | Step formula: $x_{k+1} = x_k - f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$. | Step formula: $x_{k+1} = x_k - H_k f'(x_k)$ where $H_k = s_{k-1}/y_{k-1}$. |
| 3 | Converges **quadratically** (order 2) near the optimum. | Converges **superlinearly** (order $\phi = 1.618$) near the optimum. |
| 4 | Requires the second derivative to be cheap to evaluate. | Preferred when $f''(x)$ is **expensive or unavailable**. |

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> **State the necessary conditions for the convergence of Newton's method to a minimum of $f(x)$. Mention two situations where the method may fail.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Necessary conditions for convergence:**
1. $f''(x_k) > 0$ (positive curvature — ensures descent direction is a *minimum*).
2. $\vert f'(x_k) \vert < \varepsilon$ at termination (first-order optimality).
3. $f(x)$ must be twice continuously differentiable, i.e., $f \in C^2$.
4. Starting point $x_0$ must lie in the **basin of attraction** of the true minimum.

**Two failure situations (any two of the following for full marks):**
- $f''(x_k) = 0$ (inflection point) — division by zero.
- $f''(x_k) < 0$ (concave region) — method climbs toward a maximum.
- $x_0$ too far from optimum — quadratic model is invalid, divergence.
- Function is non-differentiable at $x_k$ — derivatives undefined.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
> **(a)** Derive the Newton–Raphson update formula for one-dimensional unconstrained optimization starting from a second-order Taylor series expansion. State clearly the assumptions and conditions of validity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of the **Quasi-Newton method**. Derive the **secant update formula** for the one-dimensional case and discuss its convergence order. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Taylor expansion (2 marks):**
Write the second-order Taylor series of $f(x)$ about $x_k$:

$$f(x) \;=\; f(x_k) \;+\; f'(x_k)(x - x_k) \;+\; \frac{1}{2}f''(x_k)(x - x_k)^2 \;+\; \mathcal{O}\!\left[(x - x_k)^3\right]$$

Define the quadratic model $\phi(x)$ by truncating at the second-order term:

$$\phi(x) \;=\; f(x_k) \;+\; f'(x_k)(x - x_k) \;+\; \frac{1}{2}f''(x_k)(x - x_k)^2$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity condition (1 mark):**
The minimum of $\phi$ occurs where $\phi'(x) = 0$:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} \;=\; f'(x_k) \;+\; f''(x_k)(x - x_k) \;=\; 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $x$ (2 marks):**
$$f''(x_k)(x - x_k) \;=\; -f'(x_k) \;\Rightarrow\; x \;=\; x_k \;-\; \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

By definition, this is $x_{k+1}$:

$$\boxed{\,x_{k+1} \;=\; x_k \;-\; \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Assumptions and validity (2 marks):**
- $f$ must be at least **twice continuously differentiable** ($C^2$) in the neighborhood of the iterate.
- $f''(x_k) \neq 0$ and $f''(x_k) > 0$ (so we descend to a **minimum**).
- The starting point $x_0$ should be **close enough** to the true minimum for the quadratic model to be accurate.
- The method has **quadratic convergence** near the optimum: $\vert e_{k+1} \vert \le C \cdot e_k^2$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
Computing $f''(x)$ at every iteration is expensive in real engineering problems (e.g., evaluation through finite differences, or measurement noise). The **Quasi-Newton idea** is to maintain a running approximation $H_k \approx 1/f''(x_k)$ using only the history of $f'$ evaluations.

**Step 2 — The Secant Condition (2 marks):**
We require $H_{k+1}$ to satisfy the **inverse Hessian property** on the most recent data:

$$H_{k+1} \left[f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)\right] \;=\; x_{k+1} - x_k$$

Define the secant vector and gradient difference as:
$$s_k \;=\; x_{k+1} - x_k, \quad y_k \;=\; f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)$$

The secant condition becomes: $H_{k+1} y_k = s_k$.

**Step 3 — Solve the scalar equation (2 marks):**
In **1-D**, all three quantities are scalars, so the equation has a **unique** solution:

$$H_{k+1} \;=\; \frac{s_k}{y_k} \;=\; \frac{x_{k+1} - x_k}{f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)}$$

This is the **secant/quasi-Newton update**. The next iterate is:

$$x_{k+2} \;=\; x_{k+1} \;-\; H_{k+1} f'(x_{k+1}) \;=\; x_{k+1} \;-\; \frac{(x_{k+1} - x_k) f'(x_{k+1})}{f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)}$$

**Step 4 — Convergence order and properties (2 marks):**
- **Convergence order**: $\phi = (1 + \sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618$ (superlinear, but not quadratic).
- **Initial data**: Requires *two* starting points $x_0, x_1$ (or one point + an initial $H_0$).
- **Advantage over Newton**: Only one function evaluation of $f'$ per iteration (no $f''$ required).
- **Disadvantage**: Slower convergence rate, and may fail if $y_k \approx 0$ (denominator vanishes).

---

### Question Choice 2

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> **(a)** Explain the **step-size modification** in Newton's method. How does the modified Newton update ensure global convergence? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Starting from the multi-dimensional **DFP (Davidon–Fletcher–Powell) update formula**, show that in **one dimension** it reduces to the same scalar secant update. State the BFGS update formula and discuss one advantage of BFGS over DFP. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Pure Newton's method limitation (1 mark):**
Pure Newton uses $\lambda_k = 1$, which provides **quadratic convergence only locally**. If the initial point $x_0$ is far from the minimum, or if $f$ is not strictly convex, the iteration can diverge.

**Step 2 — Modified Newton update (2 marks):**
Introduce a step-size parameter $\lambda_k \in (0, 1]$:

$$x_{k+1} \;=\; x_k \;-\; \lambda_k \,\frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

The search direction is $d_k = -f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$ (Newton direction). The step size $\lambda_k$ controls *how far* we move along this direction.

**Step 3 — Global convergence via line search (2 marks):**
The standard approach is **Armijo backtracking line search**:
- Choose $\alpha \in (0, 1/2)$ and $\beta \in (0, 1)$ (typical: $\alpha = 10^{-4}$, $\beta = 0.5$).
- Try $\lambda = 1, \beta, \beta^2, \beta^3, \dots$ until the **sufficient decrease** condition holds:
$$f(x_k + \lambda d_k) \;\le\; f(x_k) \;+\; \alpha\,\lambda\,f'(x_k)\,d_k$$
- The accepted $\lambda$ is $\lambda_k$. This guarantees $f(x_{k+1}) < f(x_k)$, so $f$ strictly decreases each step.

**Step 4 — Why this ensures global convergence (2 marks):**
- The descent condition $f(x_{k+1}) < f(x_k)$ means the sequence $\{f(x_k)\}$ is monotonically decreasing.
- Combined with the **Zoutendijk condition** on the gradients, the iteration is provably convergent to a stationary point from *any* starting point $x_0$.
- Near the optimum, $\lambda_k \to 1$, recovering the fast quadratic convergence of pure Newton.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Multi-D DFP update (1 mark):**
The Davidon–Fletcher–Powell formula for updating the inverse Hessian approximation $H_k$ is:

$$H_{k+1} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \frac{s_k s_k^T}{s_k^T y_k} \;-\; \frac{H_k y_k y_k^T H_k}{y_k^T H_k y_k}$$

**Step 2 — Reduce to 1-D (2 marks):**
In 1-D, $H_k$, $s_k$, $y_k$ are all scalars. The inner products simplify:
- $s_k s_k^T \to s_k^2$
- $s_k^T y_k \to s_k y_k$
- $H_k y_k y_k^T H_k \to H_k^2 y_k^2$
- $y_k^T H_k y_k \to H_k y_k^2$

Substituting:
$$H_{k+1} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \frac{s_k^2}{s_k y_k} \;-\; \frac{H_k^2 y_k^2}{H_k y_k^2} \;=\; H_k \;+\; \frac{s_k}{y_k} \;-\; H_k \;=\; \frac{s_k}{y_k}$$

The result is the **scalar secant update** $H_{k+1} = s_k / y_k$.

**Step 3 — BFGS update formula (2 marks):**
The Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno update in $n$ dimensions is:

$$H_{k+1} \;=\; \left(I - \frac{s_k y_k^T}{s_k^T y_k}\right) H_k \left(I - \frac{y_k s_k^T}{s_k^T y_k}\right) \;+\; \frac{s_k s_k^T}{s_k^T y_k}$$

In 1-D (with $I = 1$):
$$H_{k+1} \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{s_k y_k}{s_k y_k}\right)^2 H_k \;+\; \frac{s_k^2}{s_k y_k} \;=\; 0 \cdot H_k \;+\; \frac{s_k}{y_k} \;=\; \frac{s_k}{y_k}$$

**Same scalar secant update** in 1-D.

**Step 4 — Advantage of BFGS over DFP (2 marks):**
- **BFGS is more numerically robust** than DFP, especially for ill-conditioned problems.
- BFGS has a **self-correcting** property: if $H_k$ is a poor approximation, the next update *automatically* corrects it via the $I - s_k y_k^T / (s_k^T y_k)$ projection.
- DFP can generate non-positive-definite $H_{k+1}$ (loss of descent direction), whereas **BFGS preserves positive definiteness** as long as $s_k^T y_k > 0$ (the **curvature condition**).
- In practice, BFGS is the **default choice** in most optimization libraries (SciPy, MATLAB `fminunc`, IPOPT).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the step size in Modified Newton** (loses 1 mark): Students often write $x_{k+1} = x_k - f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$ even when the question asks for the *modified* version. Always include $\lambda_k$ explicitly.
>
> 2. **Confusing $f''$ with $f'$ in the denominator** (loses 1–2 marks): Newton's method requires $f''$ in the denominator, not $f'$. A common typo.
>
> 3. **Not stating the curvature condition $f'' > 0$** (loses 1 mark): KTU examiners expect the positivity condition as part of the answer.
>
> 4. **Forgetting that 1-D quasi-Newton is just the secant method** (loses 2 marks): The KTU question often tests whether you recognize that DFP, BFGS, and the secant method are identical in 1-D.
>
> 5. **Skipping the convergence order** (loses 1 mark): Always quote the order — quadratic (2) for Newton, superlinear (1.618) for secant/quasi-Newton.
>
> 6. **Wrong sign in the secant update** (loses 1 mark): The formula is $H_{k+1} = s_k / y_k$, not $y_k / s_k$. The numerator is the *position* change; the denominator is the *gradient* change.
>
> 7. **Not justifying the quadratic convergence claim** (loses 1 mark): Examiners want a brief justification: "Because the Taylor expansion is exact up to second order, the local error is $O(\Delta x^3)$, leading to error-on-error squared."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Density Bullet Points:**
>
> - **Newton's Update (1-D):** $x_{k+1} = x_k - f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$. ⭐ Must memorize.
> - **Modified Newton:** $x_{k+1} = x_k - \lambda_k \,f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$ with $0 < \lambda_k \le 1$. ⭐ Tested every year.
> - **Convergence Order (Newton):** Quadratic, i.e., $e_{k+1} = C e_k^2$.
> - **Convergence Order (Secant/Quasi-Newton):** Superlinear, order $\phi = (1+\sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618$.
> - **Convergence Test:** $\vert f'(x_k) \vert < \varepsilon$ (e.g., $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$).
> - **Necessary Conditions for Newton to descend:** $f''(x_k) > 0$ at a minimum; $f''(x_k) < 0$ at a maximum; $f''(x_k) = 0$ is a **failure case**.
> - **Secant Vector:** $s_k = x_{k+1} - x_k$.
> - **Gradient Difference:** $y_k = f'(x_{k+1}) - f'(x_k)$.
> - **Secant Condition:** $H_{k+1} y_k = s_k$.
> - **1-D Quasi-Newton Update:** $H_{k+1} = s_k / y_k$.
> - **1-D Quasi-Newton Update Rule:** $x_{k+2} = x_{k+1} - H_{k+1} f'(x_{k+1})$.
> - **Key Insight:** All Quasi-Newton variants (DFP, BFGS, SR1, PSB) reduce to the **same** scalar secant update in 1-D.
> - **DFP vs BFGS:** Both give the secant in 1-D. In $n$-D, BFGS is **more robust** and **preserves positive definiteness** better than DFP.
> - **Cost per Iteration:** Newton = 2 derivative evals ($f'$ and $f''$); Quasi-Newton = 1 derivative eval ($f'$ only).
> - **Initial data for Newton:** 1 starting point $x_0$. For Quasi-Newton/Secant: 2 starting points $x_0, x_1$ (or 1 + initial $H_0$).
> - **Real-world use:** Newton in flash/equilibrium calculations; Quasi-Newton in parameter estimation and MPC.
> - **Failure mode of Newton:** Inflection points ($f'' = 0$), non-convex regions ($f'' < 0$), bad initial guess.
> - **Global fix:** Modified Newton with **Armijo line search** ensures convergence from any $x_0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nonlinear Programming with Equality Constraints: Method of Direct Substitution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nonlinear Programming with Equality Constraints: Method of Direct Substitution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for **PECHT755 – Process Optimization**, a **Nonlinear Programming (NLP) problem with equality constraints** is formally expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } & \mathbf{x}^* = (x_1^*, x_2^*, \ldots, x_n^*) \text{ that minimizes (or maximizes)} \\
& f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to: } & h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function** and $h_j(\mathbf{x})$ are **equality constraints** that must be satisfied exactly. The number of independent variables is reduced to $n - m$ after applying all $m$ equality constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Method of Direct Substitution (Elimination Method):**
> A technique in constrained optimization where one variable is explicitly expressed from the equality constraint(s) and substituted back into the objective function. This converts the *constrained* problem into an *unconstrained* one of lower dimension, which is then solved by setting the first partial derivatives to zero.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hiking Trail Analogy

Imagine you are hiking on a mountain, but the trail is restricted to a **single narrow ridge** (this is your equality constraint — you cannot step off the ridge). Your goal is to find the **lowest point** on this ridge (minimize the objective function).

The **direct substitution** method is like *parametrizing the ridge* with a single coordinate (say, distance walked along the ridge). You then express your height as a function of just that one coordinate, reducing a 2-D mountain problem into a simple 1-D walk. Wherever the slope of the 1-D function is zero, you have found the lowest or highest point on the ridge.

### 1.3 Real-World Chemical Engineering Context

In process optimization, equality constraints often represent:

- **Material balance equations** (conservation of mass)
- **Energy balance equations** (first law of thermodynamics)
- **Equilibrium relationships** (vapor-liquid, chemical reaction)
- **Stoichiometric relationships** in reactor design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in PECHT755:**
> Most chemical engineering optimization problems (reactor design, distillation column optimization, heat exchanger networks) naturally give rise to equality constraints from first principles. The Method of Direct Substitution is the *first* technique taught in KTU Module 3 because it builds intuition before introducing more sophisticated tools like the **Method of Lagrange Multipliers**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The Method of Direct Substitution follows a precise logical sequence. The **why** and **how** behind each step is given below:

- **Step 1 — Identify the structure**: Confirm the problem is an NLP with only equality constraints. Count variables ($n$) and constraints ($m$). The reduced dimension will be $n - m$.

- **Step 2 — Solve the constraint algebraically**: From $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, express $m$ variables explicitly in terms of the remaining $n - m$ variables. This is the *substitution step* and the only potentially difficult algebraic operation.

- **Step 3 — Substitute into objective**: Plug the eliminated variables into $f(\mathbf{x})$ to obtain the **reduced objective function** $F(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{n-m})$ which is now *unconstrained*.

- **Step 4 — Apply first-order necessary condition**: For a stationary point (local extremum), all first partial derivatives of $F$ must vanish:
  $$\frac{\partial F}{\partial x_i} = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-m$$

- **Step 5 — Solve the resulting system**: This typically yields a set of non-linear (or linear) equations, solved using substitution, elimination, or numerical methods.

- **Step 6 — Verify sufficiency using second-order conditions**: Compute the **Hessian matrix** $H$ of $F$. If $H$ is **positive definite** (all eigenvalues > 0) → minimum; **negative definite** (all eigenvalues < 0) → maximum; **indefinite** → saddle point.

- **Step 7 — Confirm constraint satisfaction**: Always plug the candidate $\mathbf{x}^*$ back into $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ to check for algebraic errors.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA BOX — Memorize for KTU University Exam**

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | NLP General Form | Min $f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | $j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$ |
| 2 | Reduced Dimension | $n_{\text{reduced}} = n - m$ | $n$: variables, $m$: equality constraints |
| 3 | Reduced Objective | $F(x_1, \ldots, x_{n-m}) = f(\text{substituted})$ | No constraints after substitution |
| 4 | First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC) | $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x_i} = 0$ for all $i$ | Stationary point criterion |
| 5 | Hessian Matrix | $H_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^2 F}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | Symmetric matrix |
| 6 | Second-Order Sufficient Condition — Minimum | $H$ is **positive definite** ($\det(H_k) > 0$ for all leading principal minors) | Use Sylvester's criterion |
| 7 | Second-Order Sufficient Condition — Maximum | $H$ is **negative definite** ($-H$ is positive definite) | Apply criterion to $-H$ |
| 8 | 1-D Sufficiency Check | $\dfrac{d^2 F}{dx^2} > 0 \Rightarrow$ min; $< 0 \Rightarrow$ max | Special case $n_{\text{reduced}} = 1$ |
| 9 | Constraint Verification | $h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Always check |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for KTU Answer Sheets:** Use `\vert` instead of the literal `|` symbol when writing absolute value, otherwise the markdown table format breaks.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

The Method of Direct Substitution is widely applied in:

- **Reactor design**: Eliminating one species concentration using stoichiometry to optimize conversion.
- **Distillation**: Substituting equilibrium relationships to minimize reboiler duty.
- **Heat exchanger networks**: Eliminating stream temperatures using energy balances.
- **Process economics**: Reducing cost optimization under material balance constraints.
- **Pipeline network design**: Using continuity equations to reduce dimensionality.

> [!NOTE]
> The method is **intuitive and transparent** but suffers from a major limitation: it requires the equality constraint to be *explicitly solvable* for a variable. When this is not possible, the **Lagrange Multiplier method** is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Worked Example — Reactor Cost Optimization (Model Solution)

This problem is identical in style to KTU University Exam questions. We solve it in full to build competency.

> **Problem Statement:**
> A chemical engineer must design a reactor where the production cost is given by:
>
> Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 2x_2 + 5$
>
> Subject to the material balance constraint: $h(x_1, x_2) = x_1 + x_2 - 3 = 0$
>
> where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are flow rates (in **kmol/h**) of two reactants. Use the **Method of Direct Substitution** to find the optimal operating point and verify the result.

---

#### **Step 1: Express the constraint explicitly**

From the equality constraint $h(x_1, x_2) = 0$:

$$
x_1 + x_2 - 3 = 0
$$

We solve algebraically for $x_2$ in terms of $x_1$:

$$
x_2 = 3 - x_1
$$

> *Logic:* The choice of which variable to eliminate is arbitrary, but choosing the variable with the simplest linear coefficient saves algebraic effort.

---

#### **Step 2: Substitute into the objective function**

Replace $x_2$ everywhere it appears in $f$:

$$
F(x_1) = x_1^2 + (3 - x_1)^2 - 4x_1 - 2(3 - x_1) + 5
$$

Now expand each term one by one:

$$
(3 - x_1)^2 = 9 - 6x_1 + x_1^2
$$

$$
-2(3 - x_1) = -6 + 2x_1
$$

Combining all terms:

$$
F(x_1) = x_1^2 + 9 - 6x_1 + x_1^2 - 4x_1 - 6 + 2x_1 + 5
$$

Group like terms (constant, linear in $x_1$, quadratic in $x_1$):

$$
F(x_1) = 2x_1^2 - 8x_1 + 8
$$

---

#### **Step 3: Apply First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)**

For a stationary point, the derivative with respect to $x_1$ must equal zero:

$$
\frac{dF}{dx_1} = 4x_1 - 8 = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
4x_1 = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1^* = 2 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

---

#### **Step 4: Recover the eliminated variable**

From the constraint:

$$
x_2^* = 3 - x_1^* = 3 - 2 = 1 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

---

#### **Step 5: Verify constraint satisfaction**

Plug back into $h$:

$$
h(x_1^*, x_2^*) = 2 + 1 - 3 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

#### **Step 6: Apply Second-Order Sufficient Condition**

Since the reduced problem is one-dimensional, compute the second derivative:

$$
\frac{d^2 F}{dx_1^2} = 4
$$

Since $4 > 0$, the reduced objective $F$ is strictly convex, confirming a **strict local (and global) minimum**.

---

#### **Step 7: Compute the optimal objective value**

$$
f^* = (2)^2 + (1)^2 - 4(2) - 2(1) + 5
$$

$$
f^* = 4 + 1 - 8 - 2 + 5 = 0
$$

> **Final Answer:** $x_1^* = 2$ kmol/h, $x_2^* = 1$ kmol/h, with minimum cost $f^* = 0$.

---

### 3.2 Multi-Constraint Worked Example (2 Variables, 2 Constraints)

To demonstrate generality, consider:

> **Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 = 3$ and $x_1 - x_2 = 1$.

**Step A — Solve both constraints simultaneously:**

From the second constraint: $x_2 = x_1 - 1$

Substitute into the first: $x_1 + (x_1 - 1) = 3 \Rightarrow 2x_1 = 4 \Rightarrow x_1 = 2$, $x_2 = 1$

**Step B — Substitute into objective:**

$$
F = (2-2)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 0
$$

**Step C — Note on this case:** When $n = m$ (number of variables equals number of constraints), the system has a *unique feasible point*. The objective value at that point is the optimum. This is a degenerate case where FONC/FOSC are not needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Highlight:** The Method of Direct Substitution becomes *trivial* when $n = m$, *useful* when $n - m = 1$ or $2$, and *impractical* when $n - m \gg 1$ — at that point, use **Lagrange Multipliers** or **Generalized Reduced Gradient (GRG)** methods.

### 3.3 Limitations of the Method

| Limitation | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| Algebraic Solvability | Requires constraint to be explicitly solvable for a variable |
| Dimensionality | Becomes unwieldy for $n - m \geq 3$ |
| Nonlinear Constraints | Transcendental equations (e.g., $e^x + \sin y = 0$) often cannot be inverted |
| Rounding Errors | Repeated substitution amplifies numerical errors |
| Boundary Search | Does not handle inequality constraints directly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of Direct Substitution

The following **Mermaid flowchart** maps the complete procedural logic of the method, from problem input to verified optimum. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid syntax safety.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start NLP Problem] --> A2[Input objective f of x and equality constraints h of x]
    A2 --> A3[Count variables n and constraints m]
    A3 --> A4{Is n greater than m?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Feasible point is unique - substitute directly]
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[Solve m constraints for m variables in terms of n-m variables]
    A6 --> A7{Constraint explicitly solvable?}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Switch to Lagrange Multiplier Method]
    A7 -- Yes --> A9[Substitute into f to form reduced F]
    A9 --> A10[Set partial derivatives of F to zero]
    A10 --> A11[Solve the FONC system for n-m variables]
    A11 --> A12[Recover eliminated m variables from constraints]
    A12 --> A13[Verify h of x star equals zero]
    A13 --> A14{Constraint satisfied?}
    A14 -- No --> A15[Recheck algebra - error in substitution]
    A14 -- Yes --> A16[Form Hessian matrix of F]
    A16 --> A17{Positive definite?}
    A17 -- Yes --> A18[Conclusion: Strict Local Minimum]
    A17 -- No --> A19{Negative definite?}
    A19 -- Yes --> A20[Conclusion: Strict Local Maximum]
    A19 -- No --> A21[Conclusion: Saddle Point]
    A18 --> A22[Compute f of x star and report]
    A20 --> A22
    A21 --> A22
    A22 --> A23[End Procedure]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the **data and computational flow** within the Direct Substitution algorithm — a schematic mapping of the relationships between the original problem, the reduced problem, and the verification stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Original Objective f of x] --> B3[Reduced Objective F]
    B2[Equality Constraint h of x equals 0] --> B4[Algebraic Solver]
    B4 --> B3
    B3 --> B5[FONC System: gradient F equals 0]
    B5 --> B6[Stationary Point Candidate x star]
    B6 --> B7[Hessian Test]
    B2 --> B8[Constraint Verifier]
    B6 --> B8
    B7 --> B9[Classification: Min, Max, or Saddle]
    B8 --> B10[Output: Optimal Solution]
    B9 --> B10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU exams:** Examiners often award 1–2 marks for "flowchart / algorithm" questions in the 14-mark slot. Memorizing the structure of this diagram guarantees those marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**State the Method of Direct Substitution for solving a nonlinear programming problem with equality constraints. List its two main limitations. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Method of Direct Substitution** is an optimization technique in which one or more variables are eliminated from the objective function by solving the equality constraints algebraically, reducing the constrained problem to an unconstrained one of lower dimension. The optimal solution is then obtained by setting the partial derivatives of the reduced objective function to zero.

**Limitations:**
1. The equality constraint must be **explicitly solvable** for one variable; this is not always possible for nonlinear or transcendental equations.
2. The method becomes **algebraically cumbersome and numerically unstable** when the difference $n - m$ is large.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Write the first-order necessary condition and second-order sufficient condition for a minimum of a twice continuously differentiable unconstrained function $F(\mathbf{x})$. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC):** A point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a stationary point of $F$ if
