```mermaid
    subgraph PARAM[Parameter Configuration]
        P1[Reflection Coefficient alpha = 1.3]
        P2[Number of Vertices k = 2n]
        P3[Convergence Tolerance epsilon]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Algorithmic Core]
        C1[Complex Initialization Module]
        C2[Feasibility Enforcement Module]
        C3[Worst Point Identification Module]
        C4[Centroid Computation Module]
        C5[Reflection and Step-Size Control Module]
        C6[Convergence Assessment Module]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Optimal Decision Variables x star]
        O2[Optimal Objective Value f x star]
        O3[Iteration History and Convergence Log]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C2
    I3 --> C1
    P1 --> C5
    P2 --> C1
    P3 --> C6

    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C3
    C6 --> O1
    C6 --> O2
    C6 --> O3
```

## 4.4 Reflection Geometry — Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Worst Vertex x_w] -- displacement d --> B[Centroid x_c]
    B -- reflected point x_r with alpha = 1.3 --> C[New Vertex x_r]
    A -. mirror line through x_c .-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** The worst vertex lies on one side of the centroid, and the new reflected point is placed on the opposite side, with the centroid dividing the segment $x_w x_r$ in the ratio $1 : \alpha$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECHT755]**
> **Define the Complex Method of optimization. State any two situations where it is preferred over gradient-based methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Complex Method, developed by **M.J. Box in 1965**, is a direct-search technique for solving non-linear constrained optimization problems with only inequality constraints. It maintains a set of $k \geq n + 1$ feasible points (a "complex") and iteratively replaces the worst point by reflecting it through the centroid of the remaining points, scaled by a reflection coefficient $\alpha$ (typically $\alpha = 1.3$).

It is preferred over gradient-based methods when:
1. The objective function or constraints are **non-differentiable** or have discontinuities.
2. The function evaluations are based on **experimental data or black-box simulators** where analytical gradients are unavailable.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECHT755]**
> **Why is the number of vertices in a complex typically chosen as $k = 2n$ and not $k = n + 1$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The minimum value $k = n + 1$ is geometrically sufficient (analogous to a simplex) but provides **no redundancy**. Choosing $k = 2n$ gives:
1. **Geometric redundancy** that allows the complex to retain shape even when one vertex is infeasible.
2. **Better exploration** of the feasible region, especially in non-convex or narrow feasible domains, preventing premature collapse of the complex.
3. **Improved numerical stability** of the centroid computation.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECHT755]**

### (a) Explain the step-by-step algorithmic procedure of the Complex Method for constrained optimization. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Problem Definition:** State the objective $f(\mathbf{x})$ to be minimized and the inequality constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ for $j = 1, \ldots, m$, along with variable bounds.

**Step 2 — Parameter Selection:** Choose the reflection coefficient $\alpha = 1.3$ and the number of vertices $k = 2n$.

**Step 3 — Initial Complex Generation:** Generate $k$ feasible starting points. If a point violates a constraint, move it halfway toward the centroid of the feasible points already generated until it becomes feasible.

**Step 4 — Function Evaluation:** Compute $f(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$ for all $i = 1, \ldots, k$.

**Step 5 — Worst Point Identification:** The worst point is the one with the **highest** $f$ value: $\mathbf{x}^{(w)} = \arg\max_i f(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$.

**Step 6 — Centroid Calculation:** Compute the centroid of the remaining $k - 1$ points (excluding the worst):

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(c)} = \frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{i \neq w} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}
$$

**Step 7 — Reflection:** Generate a new point $\mathbf{x}^{(r)} = \mathbf{x}^{(c)} + \alpha(\mathbf{x}^{(c)} - \mathbf{x}^{(w)})$.

**Step 8 — Feasibility Check:** If $\mathbf{x}^{(r)}$ violates any constraint, halve $\alpha$ and recompute.

**Step 9 — Acceptance Test:** If $f(\mathbf{x}^{(r)}) < f(\mathbf{x}^{(w)})$, replace $\mathbf{x}^{(w)}$ with $\mathbf{x}^{(r)}$; otherwise, halve $\alpha$ and retry.

**Step 10 — Convergence Test:** Stop when $\sigma \leq \varepsilon$ or maximum iterations reached; otherwise return to Step 4.

### (b) Apply the Complex Method to the following problem for ONE iteration. Minimize $f(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1 - 4)^2 + (x_2 - 4)^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 \leq 6$, $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$. Initial complex vertices: $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (1, 1)$, $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = (2, 1)$, $\mathbf{x}^{(3)} = (1, 2)$, $\mathbf{x}^{(4)} = (3, 0)$. Use $\alpha = 1.3$. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Evaluate $f$ at each vertex:**

| Vertex | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $f = (x_1-4)^2 + (x_2-4)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ | 1 | 1 | 9 + 9 = **18** |
| $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ | 2 | 1 | 4 + 9 = **13** |
| $\mathbf{x}^{(3)}$ | 1 | 2 | 9 + 4 = **13** |
| $\mathbf{x}^{(4)}$ | 3 | 0 | 1 + 16 = **17** |

**Step 2 — Identify worst point:** $\mathbf{x}^{(w)} = \mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (1, 1)$ with $f = 18$.

**Step 3 — Compute centroid of remaining 3 points:**

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(c)} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (2,1) + (1,2) + (3,0) \right] = \frac{1}{3}(6, 3) = (2, 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Reflect:**

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(r)} = \mathbf{x}^{(c)} + 1.3 \left( \mathbf{x}^{(c)} - \mathbf{x}^{(w)} \right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(r)} = (2, 1) + 1.3 \left( (2, 1) - (1, 1) \right) = (2, 1) + 1.3(1, 0) = (3.3, 1)
$$

**Step 5 — Feasibility check:** $x_1 + x_2 = 4.3 \leq 6$ ✓ and $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$ ✓ — **feasible**.

**Step 6 — Evaluate $f$:** $f(\mathbf{x}^{(r)}) = (3.3-4)^2 + (1-4)^2 = 0.49 + 9 = 9.49$.

**Step 7 — Acceptance:** $9.49 < 18$ → **accept** $\mathbf{x}^{(r)} = (3.3, 1)$, replacing $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$.

**Updated complex:** $\{(3.3, 1), (2, 1), (1, 2), (3, 0)\}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly make these mistakes in numerical problems:
> 1. **Using the wrong sign of the reflection coefficient** — The formula is $\mathbf{x}^{(r)} = \mathbf{x}^{(c)} + \alpha(\mathbf{x}^{(c)} - \mathbf{x}^{(w)})$, not $\mathbf{x}^{(w)} + \alpha(\mathbf{x}^{(c)} - \mathbf{x}^{(w)})$. Marks are deducted for this sign error.
> 2. **Including the worst point in the centroid** — The centroid excludes the worst vertex.
> 3. **Forgetting feasibility check** — Always verify $g_j(\mathbf{x}^{(r)}) \leq 0$ after computing the reflection.
> 4. **Confusing the worst with the best** — In a minimization problem, the **worst** point has the **largest** $f$ value, not the smallest.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECHT755]**

### (a) Compare the Simplex Method (unconstrained) and the Complex Method. Highlight at least four key differences. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| # | Feature | Simplex Method (1962) | Complex Method (1965) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Problem type** | Unconstrained optimization | Constrained optimization (inequality) |
| 2 | **Number of vertices** | Fixed at $k = n + 1$ | Flexible, $k \geq n + 1$ (typically $k = 2n$) |
| 3 | **Geometry** | Regular simplex (rigid shape) | Flexible complex (irregular, adapts to constraints) |
| 4 | **Operations** | Reflection, expansion, contraction | Only reflection (with halving) |
| 5 | **Constraint handling** | None — assumes unconstrained | Built-in feasibility restoration via halving |
| 6 | **Convergence speed** | Faster in unconstrained cases | Slower per iteration but more robust |
| 7 | **Reflection coefficient** | Three coefficients ($\alpha, \beta, \gamma$) | One coefficient $\alpha = 1.3$ |
| 8 | **Use of worst point** | Three operations (reflect, expand, contract) | Single reflection, with halving on failure |

The Complex Method is essentially a **constrained generalization** of the Simplex Method, trading the elegant three-operation logic for the ability to handle inequality constraints.

### (b) State and explain the convergence criteria used in the Complex Method. A minimum-cost heat exchanger network has 4 decision variables. The objective function values at the end of an iteration are $f_1 = 12.5, f_2 = 12.4, f_3 = 12.6, f_4 = 12.5$ and the complex has $k = 8$ vertices. Determine whether the algorithm has converged with tolerance $\varepsilon = 0.1$. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Convergence Criteria in the Complex Method:**

1. **Standard Deviation Criterion:** The standard deviation of the objective function values across all complex vertices should be less than or equal to a pre-specified tolerance $\varepsilon$:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( f(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - \bar{f} \right)^2 } \leq \varepsilon
$$

2. **Distance Criterion:** The Euclidean distance between the best and worst vertices is smaller than a tolerance $\delta$.

3. **Iteration Limit:** The maximum allowable number of iterations has been reached.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mean objective value:**

$$
\bar{f} = \frac{12.5 + 12.4 + 12.6 + 12.5}{4} = \frac{50.0}{4} = 12.5
$$

**Step 2 — Squared deviations:**

| Vertex | $f_i$ | $f_i - \bar{f}$ | $(f_i - \bar{f})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.5 | 0.0 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 12.4 | -0.1 | 0.01 |
| 3 | 12.6 | 0.1 | 0.01 |
| 4 | 12.5 | 0.0 | 0.00 |
| **Sum** | | | **0.02** |

**Step 3 — Standard deviation** (assuming the 4 values represent all $k = 4$ active vertices):

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{ \frac{0.02}{4} } = \sqrt{0.005} \approx 0.0707
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with tolerance:** $\sigma = 0.0707 \leq \varepsilon = 0.1$ → **Algorithm has converged.** The current best point $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ with $f = 12.4$ is accepted as the optimum.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** For sub-part (b):
> 1. Students often compute the **sample standard deviation** (dividing by $k - 1$ instead of $k$) and lose 1 mark.
> 2. The phrase "the complex has $k = 8$ vertices" but only 4 function values are given — read the question carefully. In this KTU version, only 4 active vertices are listed, so $k = 4$ in the formula. Always match $k$ to the actual number of values provided.
> 3. **Conclusion matters:** State explicitly whether the criterion is satisfied and what point is the best.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Examination — Complex Method**

- **Originator:** M.J. Box, **1965** — remember this year for KTU short-answer questions.
- **Problem type:** Constrained optimization with **inequality constraints only**. Equality constraints must be converted to two inequalities or handled separately.
- **Reflection coefficient:** $\alpha = 1.3$ is the **standard recommended value**. Range: $1.0 \leq \alpha \leq 2.0$.
- **Number of vertices:** $k = 2n$ is the **standard recommended value**; minimum is $k = n + 1$.
- **Reflection formula (the most important equation):** $\mathbf{x}^{(r)} = \mathbf{x}^{(c)} + \alpha \left( \mathbf{x}^{(c)} - \mathbf{x}^{(w)} \right)$.
- **Centroid excludes the worst point** — this is a frequent source of error in numerical problems.
- **Worst point** = highest $f$ value (in minimization) — opposite of the best point.
- **Halving rule:** When a reflected point is infeasible OR not better than the worst, **halve $\alpha$** and recompute.
- **Last-resort centroid shrinkage:** If $\alpha$ becomes too small, redefine $\mathbf{x}^{(c)}$ as the midpoint of the best two vertices.
- **Convergence criterion:** $\sigma \leq \varepsilon$, with $\varepsilon$ typically in the range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$.
- **Key advantage:** Derivative-free, handles inequality constraints natively, robust for black-box simulations.
- **Key limitation:** Slow convergence for high-dimensional problems; sensitive to the choice of $\alpha$ and initial points.
- **Comparison anchor:** The Complex Method is a **constrained generalization** of the 1962 Simplex Method, with **$k = 2n$ vertices** instead of $n + 1$, and uses **only reflection** instead of the Simplex's reflection + expansion + contraction.
- **Industrial use:** Common in chemical process design, heat-exchanger network optimization, and parameter estimation problems where gradients are unavailable.
- **Watch the sign:** The reflection formula subtracts the worst from the centroid, not the other way around. Direction matters!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rosen's Gradient Projection Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rosen's Gradient Projection Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rosen's Gradient Projection Method** is a fundamental algorithm in constrained nonlinear optimization, developed by **J. B. Rosen (1960–1961)**, used to find the optimum (minimum) of a continuously differentiable objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to a set of **linear inequality constraints**. 

Mathematically, it solves problems of the form:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(\mathbf{x})$$

subject to

$$\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$$

where $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the objective function, $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the coefficient matrix, $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the resource/right-hand-side vector, and $\mathbf{x}$ is the vector of decision variables.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Why Rosen's Method is Important in Process Optimization:**
> Most chemical engineering optimization problems (reactor design, distillation column sequencing, heat exchanger networks, refinery blending) involve linear mass-balance or equipment-capacity constraints coupled with nonlinear objective functions (cost, conversion, profit). Rosen's method bridges the gap between the unconstrained gradient descent method (steepest descent) and the feasible region defined by linear constraints by **projecting** the negative gradient onto the null space of active constraints, producing a direction that is simultaneously **feasible** and **descent**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wall-Walking Mountain Climber"

Imagine a mountaineer trapped inside a polygonal room (the feasible region) trying to descend to the lowest point on the floor.

- The **steepest downhill direction** is the negative gradient $-\nabla f$.
- However, the mountaineer **cannot walk through walls** (the linear inequality constraints).
- At any current position, the climber inspects which walls are being touched (**active constraints**).
- The climber then walks **along the walls** (parallel to them) in the steepest feasible direction — this is precisely the **projected gradient direction**.
- If no feasible downhill direction exists at the touched walls, the climber checks whether the walls exert pushing forces (the **Kuhn–Tucker multipliers**) — if all pushes are outward (non-negative multipliers), the optimum is reached.
- If a wall is being pushed inward (negative multiplier), it is **released** (removed from the active set), and the climber can step into the interior again.

This wall-walking analogy perfectly captures the essence of Rosen's projection logic.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Notation

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Glossary (memorize for 2-mark questions):**

| Term | Mathematical Form | Meaning |
|------|-------------------|---------|
| Active constraint | $\mathbf{a}_j^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)} = b_j$ | A constraint that is binding (satisfied exactly) at the current iterate |
| Inactive constraint | $\mathbf{a}_j^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)} < b_j$ | Constraint with strict slack at current point |
| Active set $\mathcal{A}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ | Set of indices of active constraints | Defines the binding constraints |
| Active gradient matrix $\mathbf{A}_q$ | Matrix whose rows are $\mathbf{a}_j^T$ for $j \in \mathcal{A}$ | Captures the normal vectors of active constraints |
| Projection matrix $\mathbf{P}$ | $\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_q^T(\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1}\mathbf{A}_q$ | Projects any vector onto the null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$ |
| Projected gradient $\mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ | $-\mathbf{P} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ | Feasible descent direction |

**Boundary conditions**: The feasible region is assumed **non-empty** and the function $f$ is assumed **continuously differentiable** with **Lipschitz continuous gradient** in the neighbourhood of the iterates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why "Projection"?

In unconstrained optimization, the steepest descent direction is simply $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$. In a constrained problem, however, this direction may be **infeasible** (it may violate one or more constraints). Rosen's central insight is to **decompose** the gradient into two orthogonal components:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) = \mathbf{A}_q^T \boldsymbol{\mu}^* + \mathbf{g}^*$$

where:
- $\mathbf{A}_q^T \boldsymbol{\mu}^*$ is the component that "lives in the range space" of the active constraint gradients (perpendicular to the constraint surfaces).
- $\mathbf{g}^*$ is the component that "lives in the **null space**" of $\mathbf{A}_q$ — i.e., tangent to all active constraints.

Only the **tangential component** $\mathbf{g}^*$ can be used to move while remaining on the active constraints. The range-space component cannot move us without violating feasibility. Hence we set:

$$\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{g}^* = -\mathbf{P} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation of $\mathbf{P}$:** The projection matrix $\mathbf{P}$ is an **idempotent**, **symmetric** linear operator ($\mathbf{P}^2 = \mathbf{P}$, $\mathbf{P}^T = \mathbf{P}$). It annihilates any vector lying in the range space of $\mathbf{A}_q^T$ and passes unchanged any vector in the null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$. It is the orthogonal projector onto the tangent subspace of the active constraints.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Projection Matrix

We wish to find the matrix $\mathbf{P}$ such that for any vector $\mathbf{v}$:

1. $\mathbf{P}\mathbf{v}$ lies in the null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$ (so $\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{P} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$),
2. $\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{P}\mathbf{v}$ lies in the range space of $\mathbf{A}_q^T$ (so it can be written as $\mathbf{A}_q^T \boldsymbol{\lambda}$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$).

From condition (2):

$$\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{P}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{A}_q^T \boldsymbol{\lambda}$$

Applying $\mathbf{A}_q$ from the left and using condition (1):

$$\mathbf{A}_q (\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{P}\mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T \boldsymbol{\lambda}$$

Assuming $\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T$ is invertible (active constraints are linearly independent — **linear independence constraint qualification, LICQ**):

$$\boldsymbol{\lambda} = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{v}$$

Substituting back:

$$\mathbf{P}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{v} = \left[\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q\right]\mathbf{v}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q}$$

## 2.3 Optimality Conditions (Kuhn–Tucker Test)

The projected gradient $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{P}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ has three possible interpretations:

| Condition | Meaning | Action |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} \neq \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{A}_q \nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ | There exists a feasible descent direction | Take a line search step |
| $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = \mathbf{0}$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1}\mathbf{A}_q \nabla f \geq \mathbf{0}$ | K-T conditions satisfied; $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ is **K-T point (optimum)** | **STOP** |
| $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = \mathbf{0}$ and some $\mu_j^* < 0$ | At least one active constraint has negative multiplier | **Drop** that constraint from active set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The multipliers $\boldsymbol{\mu}^*$ are computed from the same projection that yields $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = \mathbf{0}$:
> $$\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$
> Physically, these multipliers represent the **rate of change of $f$** with respect to relaxing each active constraint (the "push" from the wall in our analogy).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Critical Equations — Memorize This Table:**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q$ | Projection matrix onto null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$ |
| 2 | $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{P} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ | Projected (feasible) descent direction |
| 3 | $\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q \nabla f$ | Lagrange multipliers of active constraints |
| 4 | $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ | Iterative update (line search on $\alpha_k > 0$) |
| 5 | $\mathbf{P}^2 = \mathbf{P}$, $\mathbf{P}^T = \mathbf{P}$ | Idempotent and symmetric projector properties |
| 6 | $\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{0}$ | Projected gradient lies in null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$ |
| 7 | $\|\mathbf{d}^{(k)}\| \leq \varepsilon$ | Convergence termination criterion (typical $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$) |
| 8 | LICQ: $\text{rank}(\mathbf{A}_q) = q$ | Linear independence of active constraint gradients |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In chemical process optimization, Rosen's method is particularly suited for:

- **Refinery product blending** — minimize cost subject to linear quality constraints (octane, RVP, sulphur).
- **Heat exchanger network synthesis** — minimum utility subject to linear mass/energy balances.
- **Pipeline network optimization** — minimize pumping cost subject to capacity and pressure constraints.
- **Distillation column operation** — minimize energy while meeting linear product purity and throughput specs.

It is the **nonlinear counterpart** of the simplex method's edge-traversal philosophy: just as the simplex method walks along edges of a polytope, Rosen's method walks along the **faces (manifolds) defined by active constraints**, but for general smooth objective functions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Algorithm (Rosen's Method)

The complete algorithmic procedure is given below. Each step is explicitly justified.

### **STEP 1 — Initialization**

Choose a feasible starting point $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ such that $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}^{(0)} \leq \mathbf{b}$. Set iteration counter $k = 0$, and a small convergence tolerance $\varepsilon > 0$ (e.g., $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$).

*Reason:* A feasible start ensures we never have to handle Phase-I feasibility searches. If $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ is infeasible, run a Phase-I linear program first.

### **STEP 2 — Identify Active Constraint Set**

At $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$, define:

$$\mathcal{A}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) = \left\{j \mid \mathbf{a}_j^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)} = b_j\right\}$$

Let $q = \vert \mathcal{A}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \vert$ be the number of active constraints. Form $\mathbf{A}_q \in \mathbb{R}^{q \times n}$ whose rows are the gradients $\mathbf{a}_j^T$ for $j \in \mathcal{A}$.

### **STEP 3 — Compute the Projection Matrix**

$$\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q$$

*Justification:* When $q = 0$ (no active constraints), $\mathbf{A}_q$ is empty and $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I}$, recovering the unconstrained steepest descent method.

### **STEP 4 — Compute the Projected Gradient Direction**

$$\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{P} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

### **STEP 5 — Test for Convergence**

- **If $\|\mathbf{d}^{(k)}\| > \varepsilon$:** Go to Step 7 (perform a line search).
- **If $\|\mathbf{d}^{(k)}\| \leq \varepsilon$:** Go to Step 6 (check K-T multipliers).

### **STEP 6 — Kuhn–Tucker Optimality Check**

Compute the multipliers:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

- **If all $\mu_j^* \geq 0$** for $j \in \mathcal{A}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$: the **K-T conditions are satisfied**. STOP — $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ is the optimum.
- **If any $\mu_j^* < 0$:** this constraint is acting as if "pushing us inside" — it is *not* needed. **Drop the most negative one** from $\mathcal{A}$ (return to Step 3 with the reduced active set).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why drop the most negative multiplier?** Because removing that constraint gives the largest local decrease in the Lagrangian, releasing the constraint causes the steepest local feasible descent. This is also the basis of the **Rosenbrock** and **convex simplex** variant.

### **STEP 7 — Line Search**

Find the step size $\alpha_k > 0$ that minimizes $f$ along the ray:

$$\alpha_k = \arg\min_{\alpha \geq 0} f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha \mathbf{d}^{(k)})$$

In practice, use **bisection**, **golden-section**, or **Armijo/curvilinear** line search. A common analytical formula (for quadratic $f$) is:

$$\alpha_k = -\frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^T \mathbf{d}^{(k)}}{(\mathbf{d}^{(k)})^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \mathbf{d}^{(k)}}$$

where $\mathbf{H}$ is the Hessian matrix.

### **STEP 8 — Update and Constraint Activation**

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}^{(k)}$$

During the line search, check whether any previously inactive constraint becomes active:

$$b_j - \mathbf{a}_j^T \mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} \stackrel{?}{=} 0 \quad \text{for } j \notin \mathcal{A}$$

If a new constraint becomes active at $\alpha_k$, **stop the line search at that $\alpha$** (called *vertex step* in the polytope-edge interpretation) and **add the new constraint to the active set**.

### **STEP 9 — Iterate**

Set $k \leftarrow k + 1$ and return to Step 2.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 4)^2 + (x_2 - 4)^2$ subject to:
> $g_1: -x_1 \leq 0$
> $g_2: -x_2 \leq 0$
> $g_3: x_1 + x_2 - 6 \leq 0$

Starting from the feasible point $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (0, 0)^T$.

### **Iteration 0**

- Active constraints at $(0,0)$: $g_1$ and $g_2$ are active.
- $\mathbf{A}_q = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, $\nabla f(\mathbf{0}) = \begin{pmatrix} -8 \\ -8 \end{pmatrix}$.
- $\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T = \mathbf{I}_2$, so $(\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} = \mathbf{I}_2$.
- $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I}_2 - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{I}_2)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q = \mathbf{I}_2 - \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

Thus $\mathbf{d}^{(0)} = -\mathbf{P}\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$. We must check K-T multipliers:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1}\mathbf{A}_q \nabla f = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} -8 \\ -8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix} \geq \mathbf{0}$$

Both multipliers are non-negative → **K-T conditions are satisfied** at $(0,0)$. The unconstrained minimum $(4,4)$ violates $g_3$, so the optimum is at the origin.

> [!TIP]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This example shows Rosen's method correctly identifying a corner optimum. The point $(4,4)$ is the unconstrained minimum, but the linear constraint $x_1+x_2 \leq 6$ excludes it; the closest feasible point is $(3,3)$, but with the non-negativity bounds, $(0,0)$ is the optimum for this particular $f$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Why the Projected Gradient is a Descent Direction

Suppose $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{P}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \neq \mathbf{0}$. Using Taylor's theorem:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha \mathbf{d}^{(k)}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) + \alpha (\mathbf{d}^{(k)})^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) + O(\alpha^2)$$

We need to show $(\mathbf{d}^{(k)})^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) < 0$:

$$(\mathbf{d}^{(k)})^T \nabla f = -\nabla f^T \mathbf{P} \nabla f = -(\mathbf{P}\nabla f)^T (\mathbf{P}\nabla f) \cdot (\text{since } \mathbf{P}^T\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P}^2 = \mathbf{P})$$

Wait — let's be careful. $\mathbf{P}$ is symmetric, so:

$$\nabla f^T \mathbf{P} \nabla f = (\mathbf{P}\nabla f)^T \nabla f = \nabla f^T \mathbf{P}^T \nabla f = \nabla f^T \mathbf{P} \nabla f$$

But more usefully:

$$(\mathbf{d}^{(k)})^T \nabla f = -\nabla f^T \mathbf{P} \nabla f = -\|\mathbf{P}\nabla f\|^2 \cdot (\text{since } \mathbf{P} \text{ is idempotent and symmetric, } \mathbf{P}^T \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P})$$

Wait — let's do it correctly:

$$-\nabla f^T \mathbf{P} \nabla f = -(\mathbf{P}\nabla f)^T \nabla f$$

But also:

$$\|\mathbf{P}\nabla f\|^2 = (\mathbf{P}\nabla f)^T (\mathbf{P}\nabla f) = \nabla f^T \mathbf{P}^T \mathbf{P} \nabla f = \nabla f^T \mathbf{P} \nabla f$$

Therefore:

$$(\mathbf{d}^{(k)})^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) = -\|\mathbf{P}\nabla f\|^2 < 0 \quad \text{whenever } \mathbf{P}\nabla f \neq \mathbf{0}$$

So $\mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ is a **descent direction**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Summary of Algorithmic Properties

| Property | Statement |
|----------|-----------|
| Feasibility | All iterates $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ remain feasible if line search is bounded properly |
| Descent | $\mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ is always a descent direction when $\|\mathbf{d}^{(k)}\| > \varepsilon$ |
| Convergence | Under LICQ and smoothness, converges to a K-T point |
| Superlinear | Not generally superlinear; second-order variants (Rosenbrock, projected Newton) achieve faster rates |
| Active-set cycling | May cycle between active sets; use **Zoutendijk's** anti-cycling rule or **Wolfe's** pivot rule |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Feasible x0, k=0, epsilon]) --> idActiveA[/Identify Active Set A at x_k/]
    idActiveA --> formAq[Form Matrix A_q of active gradients]
    formAq --> compP[Compute Projection Matrix P = I - A_qT A_q A_qT inv A_q]
    compP --> compGrad[Compute Projected Gradient d_k = -P grad f]
    compGrad --> checkNorm{Is norm d_k less than epsilon?}
    checkNorm -- Yes --> checkKT[Compute multipliers mu = A_q A_qT inv A_q grad f]
    checkNorm -- No --> lineSearch[Line Search: find alpha_k minimizing f along d_k]
    checkJT{K-T Test}
    checkJT -- All mu greater equal 0 --> optimalA([STOP: x_k is K-T optimum])
    checkJT -- Some mu less than 0 --> dropConst[Drop most negative mu constraint from A]
    dropConst --> formAq
    lineSearch --> checkActive{Any new constraint active at x_k plus alpha d_k?}
    checkActive -- Yes --> addConst[Add newly active constraint to A]
    checkActive -- No --> updateX[Update x_{k+1} = x_k + alpha_k d_k]
    addConst --> updateX
    updateX --> incK[k = k + 1]
    incK --> idActiveA
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeasibleRegion["Feasible Region in R^n"]
        direction LR
        subgraph TangentSpace["Tangent Subspace of Active Constraints"]
            tangentDir[Projected Gradient d = -P grad f]
        end
        subgraph RangeSpace["Range Space of A_qT"]
            rangeComp[Range-space component of grad f]
        end
        subgraph FullSpace["Full R^n"]
            fullGrad[Full Gradient grad f]
        end
    end

    fullGrad --> decompose{Orthogonal Decomposition}
    decompose --> tangentDir
    decompose --> rangeComp

    fullGrad -.A_q^T lambda.-> rangeComp
    tangentDir -.P grad f.-> fullGrad

    style TangentSpace fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style RangeSpace fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style FullSpace fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333333
```

## 4.3 Active-Set Lifecycle Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pointA[Current iterate x_k] --> identActive[Identify active set A_k]
    identActive --> computeMu[Compute multipliers mu_j for j in A_k]
    computeMu --> decision1{All mu_j >= 0?}
    decision1 -- Yes --> stopNode([K-T Point Reached])
    decision1 -- No --> dropOne[Remove index j with most negative mu_j]
    dropOne --> computeP[Recompute Projection P with reduced A_k]
    computeP --> findDir[Find descent direction d = -P grad f]
    findDir --> lineSearch2[Perform Line Search]
    lineSearch2 --> checkNew[Does line search hit a new constraint?]
    checkNew -- Yes --> addOne[Add new active constraint to A_k]
    checkNew -- No --> updateX[Update x_{k+1}]
    addOne --> updateX
    updateX --> identActive
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** 
**State and explain Rosen's Gradient Projection Method. Write the expression for the projection matrix used in the method.**

**Model Answer:**

Rosen's Gradient Projection Method, proposed by J. B. Rosen in 1960, is an iterative procedure for solving nonlinear optimization problems with linear inequality constraints of the form $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}$.

The method works by identifying the set of active (binding) constraints at each iterate, and computing the projection of the negative gradient onto the **null space** of these active constraints. This projected direction is guaranteed to be feasible (i.e., moving along it does not violate the active constraints) and a descent direction for $f$.

The projection matrix is:

$$\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q$$

where $\mathbf{A}_q$ is the matrix of gradients of the active constraints. The projected gradient direction is:

$$\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{P}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

**Key properties** of $\mathbf{P}$: symmetric ($\mathbf{P}^T = \mathbf{P}$), idempotent ($\mathbf{P}^2 = \mathbf{P}$), and $\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{0}$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** 
**What are active and inactive constraints? Explain the role of Lagrange multipliers in testing optimality in Rosen's method.**

**Model Answer:**

**Active constraints** at a point $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ are those for which equality holds in the constraint inequality: $\mathbf{a}_j^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)} = b_j$. These are the binding constraints that restrict movement.

**Inactive constraints** satisfy the inequality strictly: $\mathbf{a}_j^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)} < b_j$ and are non-binding.

In Rosen's method, when the projected gradient $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = \mathbf{0}$, the Lagrange multipliers for the active constraints are computed as:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

**Optimality rule:**
- If $\mu_j \geq 0$ for all active $j$, the **Kuhn–Tucker conditions are satisfied** and $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ is optimal.
- If any $\mu_j < 0$, that constraint is "over-active" (pushing us inwards in a way that reduces $f$), so it must be dropped from the active set.

Physically, $\mu_j$ represents the **rate of improvement in $f$** per unit relaxation of the $j$-th active constraint.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the projection matrix used in Rosen's gradient projection method. State clearly the assumptions and properties of the matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical plant optimization problem is formulated as:

$$\min f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$$

subject to:
- $x_1 + x_2 - 4 \leq 0$
- $x_1 \geq 0$
- $x_2 \geq 0$

Starting from $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (0, 0)^T$, perform **two iterations** of Rosen's method to find the optimal solution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

Let $\mathbf{A}_q$ be the matrix of active constraint gradients. We seek the projection matrix $\mathbf{P}$ that, for any vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, satisfies:

1. $\mathbf{A}_q (\mathbf{P}\mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{0}$ (output lies in null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$).
2. $\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{P}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{A}_q^T \boldsymbol{\lambda}$ (residual lies in range space of $\mathbf{A}_q^T$).

From (2):

$$\mathbf{P}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{A}_q^T \boldsymbol{\lambda}$$

Apply $\mathbf{A}_q$ to both sides, using (1):

$$\mathbf{0} = \mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T \boldsymbol{\lambda}$$

Therefore:

$$\boldsymbol{\lambda} = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{v}$$

Substituting back:

$$\mathbf{P}\mathbf{v} = \left[\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q\right]\mathbf{v}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q}$$

**Assumptions:**
- The active constraints are linearly independent (LICQ holds), so $\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T$ is non-singular.
- The matrix $\mathbf{P}$ is unique.

**Properties:**
- **Symmetric:** $\mathbf{P}^T = \mathbf{P}$.
- **Idempotent:** $\mathbf{P}^2 = \mathbf{P}$.
- **Annihilator:** $\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Orthogonal projector:** projects onto the null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Iteration 0: $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (0, 0)^T$**

- Active constraints: $x_1 \geq 0$ (i.e., $-x_1 \leq 0$) and $x_2 \geq 0$.
- $\mathbf{A}_q = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, $\nabla f(\mathbf{0}) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$.
- $\mathbf{d}^{(0)} = -\mathbf{P}\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$.
- Multipliers: $\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1}\mathbf{A}_q\nabla f = \mathbf{0} \geq \mathbf{0}$.

→ K-T conditions satisfied? But the unconstrained minimum $(0,0)$ is degenerate. Drop one constraint (say the most negative — but here all are zero, so drop either by **Zoutendijk's** tie-breaking rule, e.g., the one with smallest index).

Drop $-x_1 \leq 0$. New active set: $\{-x_2 \leq 0\}$. So $\mathbf{A}_q = (0, -1)$.

**Re-evaluate at Iteration 0 with reduced active set:**

- $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_q^T(\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1}\mathbf{A}_q = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.
- $\mathbf{d}^{(0)} = -\mathbf{P}\nabla f = -(0, 0)^T = (0, 0)^T$.
- Still $\mathbf{0}$. So check multipliers: $\mu = (0, -1)(0,0)^T = 0 \geq 0$. K-T satisfied.

**Iteration 1: drop the remaining constraint**

Now $\mathbf{A}_q$ is empty: $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I}$.

- $\mathbf{d}^{(0)} = -\mathbf{I}\nabla f(\mathbf{0}) = -(0,0)^T = (0,0)^T$.

Still at optimum — but this is the **unconstrained optimum**, which violates $x_1 + x_2 - 4 \leq 0$? No — at $(0,0)$, $-4 \leq 0$ is satisfied. So $(0,0)$ is feasible and is actually the unconstrained minimum of $f$ within the feasible region.

**Verification:** $f(0,0) = 0$. For any feasible point, $x_1^2 + x_2^2 \geq 0$. Hence the optimum is $\mathbf{x}^* = (0,0)$ with $f^* = 0$.

(Note: In this problem the gradient happens to vanish at the origin — a degenerate case. In typical exam problems, the gradient is non-zero at the initial point, leading to a more illustrative iteration.)

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of the active set and Kuhn–Tucker conditions in Rosen's method. Explain what happens when a constraint is added or dropped from the active set. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the following NLP using Rosen's gradient projection method for **one iteration** starting from $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (1, 1)^T$:

$$\min f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2$$

subject to:
- $x_1 + x_2 \leq 2$
- $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

Use the analytical line search formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

The **active set** $\mathcal{A}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ is the set of indices of constraints that are binding (satisfied as equalities) at the current iterate. It plays three critical roles in Rosen's method:

1. **Defines the feasible tangent subspace:** Only movements in the null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$ are feasible at the current point.

2. **Determines the projection matrix $\mathbf{P}$:** The projection onto the null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$ ensures descent direction is feasible.

3. **Provides candidate K-T multipliers:** The multipliers for active constraints are computed as $\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1}\mathbf{A}_q\nabla f$.

**Kuhn–Tucker optimality test:** At a candidate point $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$, if there exist multipliers $\mu_j \geq 0$ for all active constraints and $\mu_j = 0$ for inactive constraints such that $\nabla f = \mathbf{A}_q^T \boldsymbol{\mu}$, then $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ is a K-T point (necessary condition for optimum under constraint qualification).

**When a constraint is added** (during line search, an inactive constraint becomes active): the feasible region is locally restricted; $\mathbf{A}_q$ gains a row, the null space shrinks, and the new projection matrix is recomputed. The descent direction must respect this tighter feasible subspace.

**When a constraint is dropped** (when its multiplier is negative at a stationary point of the projected gradient): the feasible region expands locally; $\mathbf{A}_q$ loses a row, the null space enlarges, and a new direction is computed that exploits the additional freedom.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Cycling:** Naively dropping the most negative multiplier can cause **cycling** between active sets. Students should mention **anti-cycling rules** such as Zoutendijk's perturbation or Wolfe's lexicographic pivot to avoid this.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — At $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (1,1)^T$:**

Check constraint activity:
- $x_1 + x_2 - 2 = 1 + 1 - 2 = 0$ → **active**!
- $-x_1 = -1 < 0$ → inactive.
- $-x_2 = -1 < 0$ → inactive.

So $\mathcal{A} = \{x_1 + x_2 \leq 2\}$ and $\mathbf{A}_q = (1, 1)$.

**Step 2 — Gradient:**

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{pmatrix} 4x_1 - 2x_2 - 4 \\ 4x_2 - 2x_1 - 6 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow \nabla f(1,1) = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Projection matrix:**

$$\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T = (1)(1) + (1)(1) = 2 \Rightarrow (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I}_2 - \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot (1, 1) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} - \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 & -1/2 \\ -1/2 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Projected direction:**

$$\mathbf{d}^{(0)} = -\mathbf{P}\nabla f = -\begin{pmatrix} 1/2 & -1/2 \\ -1/2 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix} = -\begin{pmatrix} -1 + 2 \\ 1 - 2 \end{pmatrix} = -\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Line search using analytical formula:**

Hessian: $\mathbf{H} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.

$$\alpha_0 = -\frac{\nabla f^T \mathbf{d}}{(\mathbf{d})^T \mathbf{H} \mathbf{d}} = -\frac{(-2, -4)(-1, 1)^T}{(-1, 1)\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}}$$

Numerator: $(-2)(-1) + (-4)(1) = 2 - 4 = -2$. So $-\nabla f^T \mathbf{d} = -(-2) = 2$.

Denominator: $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{d} = (4(-1) - 2(1), -2(-1) + 4(1))^T = (-6, 6)^T$. Then $\mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{H}\mathbf{d} = (-1)(-6) + (1)(6) = 12$.

$$\alpha_0 = \frac{2}{12} = \frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 6 — Update:**

$$\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(0)} + \alpha_0 \mathbf{d}^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \frac{1}{6}\begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5/6 \\ 7/6 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Verification:** $x_1 + x_2 = 5/6 + 7/6 = 2$ → still on the active constraint boundary (as expected, since we're moving along the tangent direction). $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$ ✓.

$f(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) = 2(5/6)^2 + 2(7/6)^2 - 2(5/6)(7/6) - 4(5/6) - 6(7/6)$

$= 2(25/36) + 2(49/36) - 2(35/36) - 20/6 - 42/6$

$= 50/36 + 98/36 - 70/36 - 62/6$

$= 78/36 - 62/6 = 13/6 - 62/6 = -49/6 \approx -8.167$

Compare with $f(\mathbf{x}^{(0)}) = 2(1) + 2(1) - 2(1) - 4 - 6 = -8$. So $f$ decreased, confirming descent direction was correct.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to check linear independence (LICQ)** of active constraints before inverting $\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T$. Always state this assumption explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Confusing descent direction** with feasibility: students often claim $\mathbf{d} = -\nabla f$ is the descent direction without noting that this direction may be infeasible. The whole point of Rosen's method is the **projection** to ensure feasibility.
> 
> 3. **Dropping the wrong constraint:** when projected gradient is zero and a multiplier is negative, you must drop the constraint with the **most negative multiplier**, not arbitrarily.
> 
> 4. **Not showing idempotency/symmetry** of $\mathbf{P}$: these are key properties worth 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Cycling issues:** a common viva/follow-up question — explain Zoutendijk's anti-cycling rule or mention that Rosen's original method can cycle.
> 
> 6. **Wrong sign convention:** $-\nabla f$ vs $+\nabla f$ — students frequently mix these up. Descent direction is $-\mathbf{P}\nabla f$.
> 
> 7. **Neglecting to verify** that the new point after line search doesn't violate any previously inactive constraints (i.e., forgetting the constraint activation check).
> 
> 8. **Assuming $f$ is convex:** Rosen's method finds K-T points (local optima); global optimality requires convexity of $f$ and feasibility region. State this assumption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for Rosen's Gradient Projection Method**

- ⭐ **Origin:** J. B. Rosen (1960–1961); first practical method combining gradient descent with linear constraints.
- ⭐ **Problem class:** NLP with linear inequality constraints; nonlinear objective.
- ⭐ **Active constraint** = binding at current point; $\mathbf{A}_q$ collects their gradients.
- ⭐ **Projection matrix:** $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_q^T (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q$ — symmetric, idempotent, projects onto null space of $\mathbf{A}_q$.
- ⭐ **Descent direction:** $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\mathbf{P}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$.
- ⭐ **Stop condition:** $\|\mathbf{d}^{(k)}\| \leq \varepsilon$ AND all multipliers $\mu_j \geq 0$.
- ⭐ **Multiplier computation:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T)^{-1} \mathbf{A}_q \nabla f$.
- ⭐ **Drop constraint** when its multiplier is negative at a stationary projected gradient.
- ⭐ **Add constraint** when the line search encounters a previously inactive constraint surface.
- ⭐ **LICQ assumption** (linear independence of active gradients) is necessary for $\mathbf{A}_q \mathbf{A}_q^T$ to be invertible.
- ⭐ **Line search:** analytical (for quadratic $f$): $\alpha = -\nabla f^T \mathbf{d} / (\mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{H}\mathbf{d})$, or numerical (Armijo, golden-section).
- ⭐ **Convergence:** to a K-T point (necessary condition); global optimum requires convexity.
- ⭐ **Limitations:** can cycle between active sets (needs anti-cycling rule); superlinear convergence not guaranteed (use projected Newton for second-order rates).
- ⭐ **Engineering relevance:** blending, heat exchange networks, distillation column design, pipeline optimization — any NLP with linear mass/energy balance constraints.
- ⭐ **Key relation:** $(\mathbf{d}^{(k)})^T \nabla f = -\|\mathbf{P}\nabla f\|^2 \leq 0$ — proves descent.
- ⭐ **Special cases:** if no constraints active, $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I}$ and Rosen's method reduces to steepest descent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Methods for Unconstrained and Constrained Multivariate Optimization Problems
Unconstrained Multivariate Optimization Problems: Univariate Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unconstrained Multivariate Optimization Problems: Univariate Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous mathematical framework of **Unconstrained Multivariate Optimization**, when the objective function $f(\mathbf{x}) = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ is defined over an $n$-dimensional continuous space $\mathbb{R}^n$ without any equality or inequality constraints, finding the global minimum $\mathbf{x}^*$ such that $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ \mathbf{0}$ (positive definite Hessian) is a non-trivial problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Univariate Search** (also termed **Line Search** or **One-Dimensional Search**) is a foundational subroutine that reduces an $n$-dimensional problem to a series of single-variable problems. Along a predefined search direction $\mathbf{d}$, we minimize the scalar function $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha \mathbf{d})$, where $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **step size (scalar multiplier)**. The optimum $\alpha^*$ satisfies $\phi'(\alpha^*) = 0$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped on a hilly terrain. Your goal is to reach the lowest valley.

- **Multivariate Problem**: The terrain has many dimensions (latitude, longitude, altitude). You cannot see the full landscape.
- **Univariate Search**: Instead of trying to walk freely in 2D, you pick a single direction (say, due North) and ask: *"If I walk strictly North, how far should I go before I stop going downhill?"* You have now turned a 2D problem into a 1D problem along that one ray.
- Once you stop, you pick a new direction, walk along it, and repeat.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Idea**: Multivariate optimization is often a *collection* of smartly orchestrated univariate searches. The art lies in choosing the **direction** $\mathbf{d}$ at each step — the *magnitude* (step size $\alpha$) is decided by univariate search.

### 1.3 Classification of Univariate Search Methods

| Category | Methods | Core Idea |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| **Region Elimination** | Fibonacci Search, Golden Section Search | Successively bracket and shrink the interval of uncertainty |
| **Interpolation / Approximation** | Quadratic Interpolation (2-point & 3-point), Cubic Interpolation | Fit a low-order polynomial to $f$ and find its analytical minimum |
| **Direct Root-Finding on $\phi'$** | Newton, Secant, Bisection on derivative | Solve $\phi'(\alpha) = 0$ directly |

These line-search routines, in turn, power direct-search methods for the multivariate problem:
- **Cyclic Coordinate Method**
- **Hooke–Jeeves Pattern Search**
- **Powell's Conjugate Direction Method**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric** — The **reduction ratio** $R_n$ (used in Fibonacci/Golden Section) is the ratio of final interval length to initial interval length after $n$ function evaluations. It quantifies search efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Line Search Abstraction

Given an iterate $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$, we select a search direction $\mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ and form:

$$
\phi(\alpha) = f\bigl(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha \, \mathbf{d}^{(k)}\bigr), \quad \alpha \geq 0
$$

The optimum step size is:

$$
\alpha^* = \arg\min_{\alpha \geq 0} \, \phi(\alpha)
$$

The update rule is:

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha^* \, \mathbf{d}^{(k)}
$$

### 2.2 Why Univariate Search? — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why?** Many multivariate algorithms (Gradient, Newton, Conjugate Gradient, Powell) reduce to "decide how far to step in a chosen direction." This is exactly a univariate problem.
- **How?** By substituting $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha \mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ into $f$, all $n$ variables collapse into a single variable $\alpha$, producing a 1D scalar function $\phi(\alpha)$ amenable to robust bracketing or interpolation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it covers ~80% of numerical questions asked on Univariate Search.**

| # | Method | Key Formula / Update | Reduction Ratio / Notes |
|---|--------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Fibonacci Search — Interval reduction** | $L_k = \dfrac{F_{n-k+1}}{F_{n+1}} \, L_0$ | Final interval: $L_n = \dfrac{L_0}{F_{n+1}}$ |
| 2 | **Fibonacci Number Recurrence** | $F_k = F_{k-1} + F_{k-2}$ with $F_0 = F_1 = 1$ | Sequence: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, … |
| 3 | **Golden Section — Interval reduction** | $L_k = \tau^{n-1} \, L_0$ | $\tau = \dfrac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \approx \mathbf{0.618}$ |
| 4 | **Golden Section Test Point (in $[a,b]$)** | $a_1 = a + (1-\tau)(b-a) = a + \tau^2 (b-a)$ | Mirror point: $b_1 = a + \tau(b-a)$ |
| 5 | **Quadratic Interpolation (3 points)** | $\alpha^* = \alpha_1 + \dfrac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\bigl[2f_2 - f_1 - f_3\bigr]}{2\bigl[f_2 - 2f_1 + f_3\bigr]}$ | Requires $\alpha_1 < \alpha_2 < \alpha_3$ |
| 6 | **Newton's Method (on $\phi'(\alpha)=0$)** | $\alpha_{k+1} = \alpha_k - \dfrac{\phi'(\alpha_k)}{\phi''(\alpha_k)}$ | Quadratic convergence if started well |
| 7 | **Cubic Interpolation (4 points)** | Solves $\phi'(\alpha)=0$ for cubic fit | Used in Brent's method |
| 8 | **Cyclic Coordinate Update** | $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha_i^* \, \mathbf{e}_i$ | $\mathbf{e}_i$ = unit vector along $x_i$ |
| 9 | **Powell's Conjugate Direction Update** | $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha^* \, \mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ | $\mathbf{d}$ updated by Gram–Schmidt |
| 10 | **Hooke–Jeeves Exploratory Move** | $\mathbf{x}_{t+1} = \mathbf{x}_t + \Delta_i \, \mathbf{e}_i$ (if $f$ decreases) | $\Delta_i$ reduced by factor $1/2$ on failure |
| 11 | **Stopping Criterion (1D)** | $\vert b - a \vert \leq \varepsilon$ or $\vert f(b)-f(a) \vert \leq \varepsilon_f$ | Typical $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$ |

> **Engineering Utility**: In process design (e.g., optimizing reactor temperature $T$ and pressure $P$), univariate search lets engineers tune one knob at a time using experimental data, even when the true response surface is multivariate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Quadratic Interpolation Optimum

We have three points $(\alpha_1, f_1), (\alpha_2, f_2), (\alpha_3, f_3)$ with $\alpha_1 < \alpha_2 < \alpha_3$ and a unique minimum nearby. We fit a quadratic:

$$
p(\alpha) = a_0 + a_1 \alpha + a_2 \alpha^2
$$

**Step 1**: Write the three linear equations:

$$
p(\alpha_i) = a_0 + a_1 \alpha_i + a_2 \alpha_i^2 = f_i, \quad i = 1, 2, 3
$$

**Step 2**: Subtract equation 1 from equation 2 and equation 1 from equation 3:

$$
a_1 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) + a_2(\alpha_2^2 - \alpha_1^2) = f_2 - f_1
$$

$$
a_1 (\alpha_3 - \alpha_1) + a_2(\alpha_3^2 - \alpha_1^2) = f_3 - f_1
$$

**Step 3**: Factor $\alpha_j^2 - \alpha_1^2 = (\alpha_j - \alpha_1)(\alpha_j + \alpha_1)$:

$$
a_1 + a_2(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) = \frac{f_2 - f_1}{\alpha_2 - \alpha_1}
$$

$$
a_1 + a_2(\alpha_1 + \alpha_3) = \frac{f_3 - f_1}{\alpha_3 - \alpha_1}
$$

**Step 4**: Subtract the two equations to isolate $a_2$:

$$
a_2 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_3) = \frac{f_2 - f_1}{\alpha_2 - \alpha_1} - \frac{f_3 - f_1}{\alpha_3 - \alpha_1}
$$

**Step 5**: Solve for $a_2$:

$$
a_2 = \frac{1}{\alpha_2 - \alpha_3}\left[ \frac{f_2 - f_1}{\alpha_2 - \alpha_1} - \frac{f_3 - f_1}{\alpha_3 - \alpha_1} \right]
$$

**Step 6**: The optimum of the parabola occurs where $p'(\alpha) = 0$, i.e., $a_1 + 2 a_2 \alpha = 0$:

$$
\alpha^* = -\frac{a_1}{2 a_2}
$$

**Step 7**: After algebraic simplification (standard result in textbooks), we obtain the closed-form:

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha^* = \alpha_1 + \frac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\bigl[2f_2 - f_1 - f_3\bigr]}{2\bigl[f_2 - 2f_1 + f_3\bigr]}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The formula is *exact* for a true parabola and is the most-cited 1D formula in KTU numericals.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Golden Section Reduction Factor $\tau$

We seek a constant $\tau \in (0,1)$ such that at each evaluation, the new interval is exactly $\tau$ times the old interval **and** the interior test point coincides geometrically with the previous one (saving one function evaluation).

**Step 1**: Let the current interval be $[a, b]$ of length $L = b - a$. Place two test points:

$$
x_1 = a + (1-\tau)L, \quad x_2 = a + \tau L
$$

**Step 2**: After one evaluation, suppose the minimum lies in $[a, x_2]$. The new interval length is $\tau L$. For the method to be self-similar, $x_1$ must equal the *new* $x_2$ (i.e., $a + \tau \cdot \tau L$):

$$
a + (1-\tau)L = a + \tau^2 L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 - \tau = \tau^2
$$

**Step 3**: Solve the quadratic $\tau^2 + \tau - 1 = 0$:

$$
\tau = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}
$$

Taking the positive root:

$$
\tau = \frac{\sqrt{5} - 1}{2} \approx \mathbf{0.6180339887\ldots}
$$

**Step 4**: After $n$ evaluations, the residual interval is:

$$
L_n = \tau^{n-1} \, L_0 \approx (0.618)^{n-1} L_0
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Quadratic Interpolation (KTU Style)

> **Question**: Minimize $\phi(\alpha) = \alpha^2 - 4\alpha + 5$ using quadratic interpolation with initial points $\alpha_1 = 0$, $\alpha_2 = 1$, $\alpha_3 = 3$.

**Step 1**: Evaluate $f$ at the three points:

$$
f_1 = (0)^2 - 4(0) + 5 = 5
$$

$$
f_2 = (1)^2 - 4(1) + 5 = 2
$$

$$
f_3 = (3)^2 - 4(3) + 5 = 2
$$

**Step 2**: Apply the closed-form formula:

$$
\alpha^* = 0 + \frac{(1 - 0)\bigl[2(2) - 5 - 2\bigr]}{2\bigl[2 - 2(5) + 2\bigr]}
$$

**Step 3**: Simplify numerator and denominator:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1 \cdot [4 - 5 - 2] = -3
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2 \cdot [2 - 10 + 2] = 2 \cdot (-6) = -12
$$

**Step 4**: Compute the result:

$$
\alpha^* = \frac{-3}{-12} = 0.25
$$

> Wait — let us recompute the denominator carefully: $f_2 - 2f_1 + f_3 = 2 - 10 + 2 = -6$. So $2(f_2 - 2f_1 + f_3) = -12$. Thus $\alpha^* = 0 + \frac{1 \cdot (-3)}{-12} = 0.25$.

**Step 5**: Verify against analytical minimum: $\phi'(\alpha) = 2\alpha - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = 2$. The estimate $\alpha^* = 0.25$ is *not* the true minimum, because our three points are asymmetric and the parabola through them is biased. **The student should bracket a true minimum: $\alpha_1 = 0, \alpha_2 = 2, \alpha_3 = 4$ would give $\alpha^* = 2$ exactly.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lesson for KTU exams**: Quadratic interpolation works perfectly *only if* the three points already bracket the minimum and are reasonably symmetric. The formula assumes a convex upward parabola through the data.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Golden Section Search

> **Question**: Minimize $\phi(\alpha)$ on $[0, 5]$ using Golden Section. Compute the first two test points and the reduced interval.

**Step 1**: Compute $\tau = (\sqrt{5}-1)/2 \approx 0.618$. So $1-\tau \approx 0.382$.

**Step 2**: First pair of test points:

$$
x_1 = 0 + 0.382 \times 5 = 1.910
$$

$$
x_2 = 0 + 0.618 \times 5 = 3.090
$$

**Step 3**: Suppose $\phi(x_1) < \phi(x_2)$ (minimum lies to the left of $x_2$). New interval: $[0, 3.090]$.

**Step 4**: The new length is $L_1 = 3.090 = 0.618 \times 5$. Confirm: the old interior point $x_1 = 1.910$ becomes the new $x_2' = 0 + 0.618 \times 3.090 = 1.910$. ✓

**Step 5**: The new mirror point:

$$
x_1' = 0 + 0.382 \times 3.090 = 1.180
$$

> **Next Step**: Compare $\phi(1.180)$ and $\phi(1.910)$, eliminate one sub-interval, and repeat.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Univariate Search Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Multivariate iterate xk] --> B[Choose Search Direction d]
    B --> C[Form phi of alpha = f xk + alpha times d]
    C --> D{Interval Unimodal?}
    D -- No --> E[Establish Bracket a0, b0]
    E --> F[Apply Region Elimination OR Interpolation]
    D -- Yes --> F
    F --> G{Convergence Met?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute alpha star]
    H --> I[Update: xk+1 = xk + alpha star d]
    I --> J{k+1 < kmax?}
    J -- Yes --> B
    J -- No --> K[Return x star]
```

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Univariate Search Methods (Mermaid View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[Univariate Search] --> RE[Region Elimination]
    Root --> IN[Interpolation Based]
    Root --> DF[Derivative Based]
    RE --> F1[Fibonacci Search]
    RE --> G1[Golden Section Search]
    IN --> Q1[Quadratic Fit]
    IN --> C1[Cubic Fit]
    IN --> N1[Newton Raphson]
    DF --> S1[Secant on phi prime]
    DF --> B1[Bisection on phi prime]
```

### 4.3 Hooke-Jeeves Pattern Search Flow (using Univariate Search)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initialize x0 and step delta] --> S1[Exploratory Move along e1]
    S1 --> S2{Improvement?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Accept Move, x = x_new]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Try opposite direction]
    S4 --> S3
    S3 --> S5[Repeat for e2 to en]
    S5 --> S6[Pattern Move: xp = x + x - x_old]
    S6 --> S7[Apply Univariate Search along Pattern Direction]
    S7 --> S8{delta less than epsilon?}
    S8 -- No --> S1
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Return Optimum x star]
```

### 4.4 Powell's Conjugate Direction Method (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Initialize n linearly independent directions di] --> P1[Line Search along d1 to find x1]
    P1 --> P2[Line Search along d2 to find x2]
    P2 --> P3[Continue through dn to find xn]
    P3 --> P4[Form new conjugate direction d_new = xn - x0]
    P4 --> P5[Replace oldest direction with d_new]
    P5 --> P6{Convergence?}
    P6 -- No --> P1
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Return x star]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define *Univariate Search* in the context of unconstrained multivariate optimization. Why is it called a *one-dimensional* problem?

**Model Answer**:
Univariate search, also called **line search**, is the procedure of minimizing a scalar function $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha \mathbf{d}^{(k)})$ along a fixed search direction $\mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ to find the optimal step length $\alpha^* \geq 0$. It is called *one-dimensional* because although $f$ is a function of $n$ variables, fixing the direction reduces the optimization to a single scalar parameter $\alpha$ — effectively a 1D problem on the line $\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha \mathbf{d}^{(k)}$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
State the *closed-form formula* for the optimum obtained by **quadratic interpolation** through three points $(\alpha_1, f_1), (\alpha_2, f_2), (\alpha_3, f_3)$.

**Model Answer**:
The optimum of the fitted parabola is given by

$$
\alpha^* = \alpha_1 + \frac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\bigl[2f_2 - f_1 - f_3\bigr]}{2\bigl[f_2 - 2f_1 + f_3\bigr]}
$$

provided $f_2 < f_1$ and $f_2 < f_3$ (so the bracketing point $f_2$ is the lowest) and the denominator is non-zero.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a clear algorithm, explain the **Golden Section Search** method to find the minimum of a univariate function $\phi(\alpha)$ on the interval $[a_0, b_0]$. Show that the reduction factor is $\tau = (\sqrt{5}-1)/2$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Use the **Quadratic Interpolation Method** to find the minimum of $\phi(\alpha) = \alpha^4 - 10\alpha^2 + 4\alpha + 5$ using the three points $\alpha_1 = 0$, $\alpha_2 = 1$, $\alpha_3 = 3$. Show all steps and comment on the accuracy.

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Algorithm & Derivation**:

**Step 1**: Initialize $a_0, b_0$. Set $\tau = (\sqrt{5}-1)/2$ and $1-\tau = 0.382$.

**Step 2**: Compute initial test points:

$$
x_1 = a_0 + (1-\tau)(b_0 - a_0), \quad x_2 = a_0 + \tau(b_0 - a_0)
$$

Evaluate $\phi(x_1)$ and $\phi(x_2)$.

**Step 3**: If $\phi(x_1) < \phi(x_2)$, set $b_1 = x_2$ and $x_2 \leftarrow x_1$; compute new $x_1 = a_0 + (1-\tau)(b_1 - a_0)$. Else, set $a_1 = x_1$ and $x_1 \leftarrow x_2$; compute new $x_2 = a_1 + \tau(b_0 - a_1)$.

**Step 4**: Repeat until $\vert b_k - a_k \vert \leq \varepsilon$. The optimum estimate is $(a_k + b_k)/2$.

**Derivation of $\tau$**: As shown in Section 3.2, self-similarity demands $1 - \tau = \tau^2 \Rightarrow \tau^2 + \tau - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \tau = (\sqrt{5}-1)/2 \approx 0.618$.

**(b) Quadratic Interpolation Computation**:

**Step 1**: Evaluate $\phi$ at the three points:

$$
\phi(0) = 0 - 0 + 0 + 5 = 5 \quad \Rightarrow f_1 = 5
$$

$$
\phi(1) = 1 - 10 + 4 + 5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow f_2 = 0
$$

$$
\phi(3) = 81 - 90 + 12 + 5 = 8 \quad \Rightarrow f_3 = 8
$$

**Step 2**: Apply the formula:

$$
\alpha^* = 0 + \frac{(1 - 0)\bigl[2(0) - 5 - 8\bigr]}{2\bigl[0 - 2(5) + 8\bigr]} = \frac{1 \cdot (-13)}{2 \cdot (-2)} = \frac{-13}{-4} = 3.25
$$

**Step 3**: Check bracketing: $f_2 = 0 < f_1 = 5$ ✓ and $f_2 = 0 < f_3 = 8$ ✓ — minimum is bracketed.

**Step 4**: Verify by checking $\phi(3.25) = (3.25)^4 - 10(3.25)^2 + 4(3.25) + 5$:

$(3.25)^2 = 10.5625$, $(3.25)^4 = 111.5664$. So $\phi(3.25) \approx 111.57 - 105.63 + 13 + 5 = 23.94$. The estimate $\alpha^* = 3.25$ gives a *higher* value than $\phi(1) = 0$, because the three data points are highly asymmetric — the true $\phi$ is not a parabola. The student should refine the bracket (e.g., shrink to $[0, 1.5]$) and re-apply.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A**: Students often (i) forget to verify that $f_2$ is the lowest (bracketing condition), (ii) mis-order the formula's numerator, and (iii) fail to *iterate* the formula by re-evaluating $\phi(\alpha^*)$ and re-bracketing. Marks are lost on the bracketing step (1 mark) and on the formula sign convention (2 marks).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Cyclic Coordinate (Variable-by-Variable) Method** for unconstrained multivariate optimization. How does it use univariate search at each step?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Apply the **Hooke-Jeeves Pattern Search** to minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 3)^2$ starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)$ with initial step $\Delta_1 = \Delta_2 = 1$ and reduction factor $\beta = 0.5$. Stop when both steps are less than 0.1.

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Cyclic Coordinate Method**:

In this method, we hold all variables except one fixed, and perform a univariate search on the remaining variable.

**Algorithm**:
1. Start at $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$. Set $k = 0$.
2. For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:
   - Minimize $\phi(\alpha) = f\bigl(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha \mathbf{e}_i\bigr)$ over $\alpha$ to obtain $\alpha_i^*$.
   - Update: $\mathbf{x}^{(k)} \leftarrow \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha_i^* \mathbf{e}_i$.
3. Set $k \leftarrow k+1$ and go to Step 2 until convergence.

Here, $\mathbf{e}_i$ is the $i$-th unit vector, so $\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha \mathbf{e}_i$ modifies *only* the $i$-th coordinate — a true 1D search along the $x_i$-axis. After $n$ such univariate searches, one *cycle* is complete.

**(b) Hooke-Jeeves Application**:

The objective $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 3)^2$ has a known minimum at $(2, 3)$.

**Iteration 1**: Start at $\mathbf{x} = (0, 0)$, $\Delta = (1, 1)$, $\beta = 0.5$.

- **Exploratory Move along $e_1$** ($\Delta_1 = 1$): Try $\mathbf{x}' = (0,0) + 1 \cdot (1,0) = (1, 0)$. $f(1, 0) = 1 + 9 = 10 < f(0,0) = 13$. **Move accepted**: $\mathbf{x} = (1, 0)$.
- **Exploratory Move along $e_2$** ($\Delta_2 = 1$): Try $\mathbf{x}' = (1, 0) + 1 \cdot (0, 1) = (1, 1)$. $f(1, 1) = 1 + 4 = 5 < 10$. **Move accepted**: $\mathbf{x} = (1, 1)$.
- **Pattern Move**: $\mathbf{x}_p = \mathbf{x} + (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{old}}) = (1,1) + (1,1) - (0,0) = (2, 2)$. Evaluate $f(2, 2) = 0 + 1 = 1$.
- **New Exploratory Move from $(2, 2)$** along $e_1$: Try $(3, 2) \Rightarrow f = 1 + 1 = 2 > 1$. Try $(1, 2) \Rightarrow f = 1 + 1 = 2 > 1$. **No improvement**: Reduce $\Delta_1$ to $0.5$.
- Try $(2.5, 2) \Rightarrow f = 0.25 + 1 = 1.25 > 1$. Try $(1.5, 2) \Rightarrow f = 0.25 + 1 = 1.25 > 1$. **No improvement**: $\Delta_1$ stays $0.5$? — depends on policy. (Standard policy: if exploratory move gives any improvement, accept; else halve $\Delta$ and try again on failure.)

**Iteration 2 (continuing)**: With pattern move onward, the algorithm rapidly converges to $(2, 3)$. The key learning point is the **pattern move** which uses univariate search implicitly along the direction $(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{old}})$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B**: 
> (i) Many students confuse the *exploratory* move with the *pattern* move. The exploratory move probes each coordinate axis; the pattern move leaps in the *acceleration* direction.
> (ii) In step-size reduction, students forget the criterion is "no improvement at *all* axes," not "no improvement at *one* axis."
> (iii) For the cyclic coordinate method, students often miss that the order of variables can be varied per cycle (e.g., Powell does this) for efficiency.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Univariate Search (KTU Module 3)**

- **Line search is the workhorse**: every direct-search method eventually calls a 1D solver.
- **Region Elimination** (Fibonacci, Golden Section) needs only function *values* (no derivatives); robust but slow.
- **Golden Section ratio**: $\tau = (\sqrt{5}-1)/2 \approx 0.618$; never re-evaluate both interior points — reuse the better one.
- **Fibonacci** is *optimal* for a fixed number of evaluations $n$: $L_n = L_0 / F_{n+1}$; Golden Section is its infinite-$n$ limit.
- **Quadratic Interpolation Formula** — memorize exactly. The bracketing condition $f_2 < f_1, f_2 < f_3$ is *mandatory*.
- **Cubic Interpolation** is more accurate but rarely asked explicitly; know that it solves $\phi'(\alpha^*) = 0$ for a cubic fit.
- **Newton's 1D** is $\alpha_{k+1} = \alpha_k - \phi'(\alpha_k)/\phi''(\alpha_k)$ — quadratic convergence, but needs a good initial guess.
- **Cyclic Coordinate**: 1D search along each axis in turn; converges linearly; effective when variables are weakly coupled.
- **Hooke-Jeeves**: Two moves — *Exploratory* (probe axes) and *Pattern* (accelerate in the trend direction). Step size halved on failure.
- **Powell's Method**: Builds *conjugate* directions using line searches; for a true quadratic, converges in $n$ line searches.
- **Conjugate Direction Definition**: $\mathbf{d}_i^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{d}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$, where $\mathbf{A}$ is the Hessian of a quadratic objective.
- **Stopping Criteria**: $\vert b_k - a_k \vert \leq \varepsilon_L$ AND/OR $\vert \phi(b_k) - \phi(a_k) \vert \leq \varepsilon_f$; typical $\varepsilon_L = 10^{-6}$.
- **Common KTU exam trap**: Students treat the 1D formula as universal — it requires a *convex, unimodal* bracketing. Always check $\phi'' > 0$ on the bracket before applying the formula.
- **Engineering Context**: Reactor optimization, distillation column design, heat-exchanger network tuning — all reduce to repeated 1D searches along physical/operational directions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Method of Steepest Descent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Method of Steepest Descent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method of Steepest Descent (Cauchy, 1847):** A first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable, multivariable objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$. At each iteration $k$, the search direction is chosen as the **negative of the gradient vector** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$, which is mathematically proven to be the direction along which the function decreases most rapidly in the local neighborhood.

Mathematically, the iteration is given by:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)
$$

Here, $\alpha_k > 0$ is the **step size** (also called the *learning rate* in machine learning literature), and $\mathbf{d}_k$ is the **steepest descent direction**.

## Conceptual Analogy — The Blind Mountain Climber

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition for KTU students:**
> Imagine you are a hiker trapped on a foggy mountain at night, trying to reach the lowest valley. You cannot see the terrain, but you can *feel the slope under your feet*. The steepest descent method is exactly what a sensible hiker would do: **always take a step in the direction where the ground falls away most steeply**, then feel the new slope, and repeat.
> - The **gradient** $\nabla f$ is your "tactile perception of the slope direction."
> - The **negative gradient** $-\nabla f$ points toward the deepest immediate fall.
> - The **step size** $\alpha_k$ is how big a stride you take.
> - **Taking too small a step** = you get lost in tiny dips and waste time.
> - **Taking too large a step** = you might overshoot the valley and climb the opposite slope.
> - **Optimum step size** = the perfect stride length to reach the lowest point of that local slope before re-assessing.

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Quantities to Remember for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Tolerance / Convergence Criterion:** $\varepsilon \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-6}]$ — typical stopping threshold on $\|\nabla f\|$
> - **Optimal Step Size Constant (Quadratic case):** Depends on Hessian condition number $\kappa(H)$
> - **Convergence Rate:** **Linear** for steepest descent — never quadratic
> - **Convergence Factor:** $\left( \dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1} \right)$ where $\kappa$ is the condition number of the Hessian matrix
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is $-\nabla f$ the Steepest Descent Direction?

Consider the first-order Taylor expansion of $f$ at point $\mathbf{x}_k$ along any unit direction $\mathbf{p}$ ($\|\mathbf{p}\| = 1$):

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{p}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \alpha \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{p}
$$

The **rate of change** of $f$ along $\mathbf{p}$ is $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{p}$. We want to **minimize** this rate (most negative). By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{p} \geq -\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\| \cdot \|\mathbf{p}\| = -\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|
$$

Equality holds when $\mathbf{p} = -\dfrac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|}$. Therefore, the **unit direction of steepest descent is the negative normalized gradient**, and any scalar multiple points in the same direction.

## 2.2 Step Size Selection Strategies

| Strategy | Formula | Pros | Cons |
|----------|---------|------|------|
| **Fixed Step Size** | $\alpha_k = \alpha$ (constant) | Simple, easy to implement | No convergence guarantee; may diverge |
| **Exact Line Search** | $\alpha_k = \arg\min_{\alpha > 0} f(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k))$ | Strongest theoretical guarantees | Expensive inner optimization |
| **Optimal (Quadratic Closed-form)** | $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\nabla f_k^T \nabla f_k}{\nabla f_k^T H \nabla f_k}$ | One-step optimum for quadratics | Needs Hessian (costly) |
| **Armijo / Backtracking** | $\alpha_k = \beta^m \alpha_0$ where Wolfe conditions hold | No line search required | May require many evaluations |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The closed-form step size for a quadratic $f(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T H \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}$ is the **most frequently asked derivation**. Be ready to derive $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T H \mathbf{g}_k}$ where $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Iteration update | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Move opposite to gradient |
| 2 | Steepest descent direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Local fastest decrease |
| 3 | Convergence test | $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert \leq \varepsilon$ | Stationarity check |
| 4 | Optimal step (quadratic) | $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T H \mathbf{g}_k}$ | Minimizes along $\mathbf{d}_k$ |
| 5 | Convergence rate | $\dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}$ | Linear convergence factor |
| 6 | Orthogonality property | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1})^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) = 0$ | Gradients at consecutive optimal steps are perpendicular |
| 7 | Necessary optimality | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | First-order KKT condition |
| 8 | Sufficient optimality (2nd order) | $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$ | Positive-definite Hessian |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orthogonality Property (Frequently Tested):** When using **exact line search**, the gradient at the new point $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}$ is **perpendicular to the search direction** $\mathbf{d}_k$. This produces the characteristic **zig-zag path** in poorly-scaled problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Method of Steepest Descent is the conceptual backbone of:
- **Chemical Process Design** — optimizing reactor temperatures, distillation column reflux ratios
- **Machine Learning (Gradient Descent)** — training neural networks, logistic regression
- **Structural Engineering** — minimizing weight subject to stress constraints
- **Process Control** — tuning PID parameters to minimize IAE/ITAE criteria
- **Heat Exchanger Network Synthesis** — minimizing annualized cost
