## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the objective function used in the optimization of pipe diameter for an incompressible fluid. Mention the economic ratio that exists between fixed and operating costs at the optimum diameter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The objective function is the **total annualized cost** $C_T(D) = C_F(D) + C_O(D) = C_1 D + K/D^5$, which is minimized with respect to diameter $D$.
- **(1 mark)** Substituting the Fanning equation gives the operating cost $C_O \propto 1/D^5$, while fixed cost $C_F \propto D$.
- **(1 mark)** At the optimum, $\dfrac{C_O}{C_F} = \dfrac{1}{5}$, i.e., operating cost is **20%** of the annualized fixed cost (or $C_F = 5 C_O$).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Why does the total annual cost curve for a piping system exhibit a minimum, and not a monotonic trend? Justify using the dependence of fixed and operating costs on diameter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The fixed cost $C_F \propto D$ increases linearly with diameter because pipe material, fabrication, and installation cost more for larger diameters.
- **(1 mark)** The operating cost $C_O \propto 1/D^5$ decreases sharply with diameter because frictional pressure drop scales inversely with $D^5$ (from Fanning's equation).
- **(1 mark)** The sum of a linearly increasing term and a sharply decreasing convex term produces a **convex U-shaped function** with a unique global minimum — the optimum diameter $D_{opt}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B for Module 4 carries **14 marks**, typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts. **Answer ANY ONE** of the following two question choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the optimum pipe diameter expression $D_{opt} = \left(\dfrac{5K}{C_1}\right)^{1/6}$ for an incompressible fluid, starting from the Fanning friction equation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical plant pumps $Q = 0.05 \; m^3/s$ of an organic solvent ($\rho = 850 \; kg/m^3$, $\mu = 2 \times 10^{-3} \; Pa\cdot s$) through $L = 500 \; m$ of commercial steel pipe (Fanning $f = 0.0045$). Pump efficiency $\eta_p = 0.65$, motor efficiency $\eta_m = 0.92$, annual operating hours $t_h = 7200 \; h$, electricity cost $C_e = ₹10/kWh$, and annualized fixed-cost coefficient $C_1 = ₹2800 \; /(\text{m·yr·m})$. Compute:
  (i) The optimum pipe diameter $D_{opt}$ in mm.
  (ii) The annual operating cost and fixed cost at $D_{opt}$.
  (iii) Verify that the economic ratio $C_O/C_F = 1/5$ holds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**(Step 1, 1 mark)** Velocity in terms of $Q$ and $D$:
$$
v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}
$$

**(Step 2, 2 marks)** Fanning pressure drop:
$$
\Delta P_f = \frac{2 f L \rho v^2}{g_c D} = \frac{32 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 g_c D^5}
$$

**(Step 3, 1 mark)** Operating cost (combining pump + motor efficiency, hours, tariff):
$$
C_O(D) = \frac{32 f L \rho Q^3 t_h C_e}{1000 \, \pi^2 g_c D^5 \, \eta_p \eta_m} = \frac{K}{D^5}
$$

**(Step 4, 1 mark)** Fixed cost:
$$
C_F(D) = C_1 D
$$

**(Step 5, 1 mark)** Total cost $C_T = C_1 D + K/D^5$; FONC: $\dfrac{dC_T}{dD} = C_1 - \dfrac{5K}{D^6} = 0$.

**(Step 6, 1 mark)** Solving: $D_{opt} = (5K/C_1)^{1/6}$.

**Assumptions:** Steady-state, isothermal, fully-developed turbulent flow, constant $\rho$, constant $f$ (independent of $D$), Newtonian fluid, horizontal pipe (no elevation change).

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Compute $K$ (3 marks):**

$$
K = \frac{32 \cdot 0.0045 \cdot 500 \cdot 850 \cdot (0.05)^3 \cdot 7200 \cdot 10}{1000 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 0.92}
$$

Numerator: $32 \times 0.0045 = 0.144$; $0.144 \times 500 = 72$; $72 \times 850 = 61{,}200$; $(0.05)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$; $61{,}200 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 7.65$; $7.65 \times 7200 = 55{,}080$; $55{,}080 \times 10 = 550{,}800$.

Denominator: $1000 \times 9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 0.65 \times 0.92 \approx 57{,}847$.

$$
K \approx 550{,}800 / 57{,}847 \approx 9.52 \; \text{₹·m}^5/\text{yr}
$$

**Compute $D_{opt}$ (2 marks):**

$$
D_{opt} = \left(\frac{5 \times 9.52}{2800}\right)^{1/6} = \left(\frac{47.6}{2800}\right)^{1/6} = (0.01700)^{1/6}
$$

$\ln(0.017) = -4.075$; $-4.075/6 = -0.679$; $e^{-0.679} \approx 0.507$.

$$
\therefore \quad D_{opt} \approx 0.507 \; \text{m} \approx 507 \; \text{mm} \approx 20 \; \text{inches NB}
$$

**Step (ii) — Costs at $D_{opt}$ (1 mark):**

$$
C_F = 2800 \times 0.507 = ₹1420 \; \text{/yr (per m of pipe length)}
$$

$$
C_O = \frac{9.52}{(0.507)^5}
$$

$(0.507)^2 = 0.257$; $(0.507)^4 = 0.0661$; $(0.507)^5 = 0.0335$.

$$
C_O \approx 9.52 / 0.0335 \approx ₹284 \; \text{/yr (per m of pipe length)}
$$

**Step (iii) — Verify Ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{C_O}{C_F} = \frac{284}{1420} = 0.200 \;\; \checkmark
$$

The economic ratio is confirmed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain why the total cost curve for a pipe shows a minimum. Indicate the regions where capital cost dominates versus operating cost dominates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a refinery cooling-water line, $Q = 0.10 \; m^3/s$ of water ($\rho = 1000 \; kg/m^3$) is to be pumped through $L = 2000 \; m$ of pipe. Given $f = 0.005$, $\eta_p \eta_m = 0.6$, $t_h = 6000 \; h$, $C_e = ₹7/kWh$, and $C_1 = ₹2000 \; /(\text{m·yr·m})$. Determine:
  (i) The optimum diameter $D_{opt}$.
  (ii) The corresponding annual fixed, operating, and total costs (per metre of pipe length).
  (iii) Discuss what happens if a designer selects a pipe diameter **twice** the optimum. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Qualitative Explanation (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** Sketch a U-shaped $C_T$ vs $D$ curve; mark $C_F$ (linear rise), $C_O$ (steep decline $\propto 1/D^5$), and $C_T$ (their sum with a minimum at $D_{opt}$).
- **(2 marks)** Identify the **left region** ($D < D_{opt}$): $C_O \gg C_F$ → pumping cost dominates; friction losses are catastrophic.
- **(2 marks)** Identify the **right region** ($D > D_{opt}$): $C_F \gg C_O$ → capital cost dominates; oversized pipe wastes money.
- **(1 mark)** Conclude that the **minimum of $C_T$** is the engineering optimum, balancing both.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Compute $K$ (2 marks):**

$$
K = \frac{32 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 2000 \cdot 1000 \cdot (0.10)^3 \cdot 6000 \cdot 7}{1000 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.6}
$$

Numerator: $32 \times 0.005 = 0.16$; $0.16 \times 2000 = 320$; $320 \times 1000 = 320{,}000$; $(0.10)^3 = 10^{-3}$; $320{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 320$; $320 \times 6000 = 1{,}920{,}000$; $1{,}920{,}000 \times 7 = 1.344 \times 10^{7}$.

Denominator: $1000 \times 9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 0.6 \approx 58{,}089$.

$$
K \approx 1.344 \times 10^7 / 58{,}089 \approx 231.4 \; \text{₹·m}^5/\text{yr}
$$

**Compute $D_{opt}$ (1 mark):**

$$
D_{opt} = \left(\frac{5 \times 231.4}{2000}\right)^{1/6} = (0.5785)^{1/6}
$$

$\ln(0.5785) = -0.547$; $-0.547/6 = -0.0912$; $e^{-0.0912} \approx 0.913$.

$$
\therefore D_{opt} \approx 0.913 \; \text{m} \approx 913 \; \text{mm} \approx 36 \; \text{inches NB}
$$

**Step (ii) — Costs (2 marks):**

$$
C_F(D_{opt}) = 2000 \times 0.913 = ₹1826 \; \text{/yr·m}
$$

$(0.913)^5$: $0.913^2 = 0.834$; $0.913^4 = 0.695$; $0.913^5 \approx 0.634$.

$$
C_O(D_{opt}) = 231.4 / 0.634 \approx ₹365 \; \text{/yr·m}
$$

$$
C_{T,opt} = 1826 + 365 = ₹2191 \; \text{/yr·m}
$$

Check ratio: $C_O/C_F = 365/1826 = 0.200 \; \checkmark$

**Step (iii) — Effect of Doubling the Diameter (2 marks):**

At $D = 2D_{opt} = 1.826 \; m$:

$$
C_F = 2000 \times 1.826 = ₹3652 \; \text{/yr·m} \quad (\text{doubles, as expected})
$$

$$
C_O = \frac{231.4}{(1.826)^5}
$$

$(1.826)^5$: $1.826^2 = 3.334$; $1.826^4 = 11.12$; $1.826^5 = 20.30$.

$$
C_O \approx 231.4 / 20.30 \approx ₹11.4 \; \text{/yr·m} \quad (\text{drops by factor } 2^5 = 32)
$$

$$
C_T = 3652 + 11.4 \approx ₹3663 \; \text{/yr·m}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Doubling $D_{opt}$ raises the **total** cost by a factor of $\approx 1.67 \times$, because the increase in fixed cost far outweighs the $32\times$ reduction in pumping cost. This demonstrates the practical wisdom of staying near $D_{opt}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Pipe-Diameter Optimization Problems:**
>
> 1. **Mixing Fanning and Darcy factors.** KTU uses Fanning $f$; Darcy $f_D = 4 f$. Mixing them gives a $4\times$ error in $\Delta P_f$.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta_p \eta_m$.** A pump/motor efficiency of 0.6 (typical) increases the operating cost by ~67%; omitting it gives a non-conservative $D_{opt}$.
> 3. **Using the wrong power of $D$.** Operating cost scales as $D^{-5}$, not $D^{-2}$ or $D^{-3}$. A wrong exponent leads to $D_{opt} \propto (\text{wrong factor})^{1/(\text{wrong exponent})}$.
> 4. **Not verifying the second-order condition.** Even though convex, examiners award marks for stating $d^2C_T/dD^2 > 0$ explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting the economic ratio.** Always state $C_O/C_F = 1/5$ at optimum — it is a **favourite viva question** worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Neglecting units.** $Q$ in m³/s, $\Delta P$ in Pa, $P$ in W; convert to kW by dividing by 1000 before applying the tariff.
> 7. **Rounding errors in numericals.** KTU tolerates ±2%; but using wrong intermediate rounding (e.g., truncating $D_{opt}^5$) causes cascading errors.
> 8. **Ignoring kinetic-energy corrections or fittings.** In KTU problems, fittings K-factor is rarely included; if present, add via $L_{eq} = \sum K D/f$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Objective Function:** $C_T(D) = C_1 D + K/D^5$ — sum of fixed and operating costs.
- **Fanning Pressure Drop:** $\Delta P_f = \dfrac{32 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 g_c D^5}$.
- **Operating Cost Dependence:** $C_O \propto D^{-5}$ (fifth-power inverse).
- **Fixed Cost Dependence:** $C_F \propto D$ (linear).
- **Optimum Diameter:** $D_{opt} = (5K/C_1)^{1/6}$.
- **Economic Ratio at Optimum:** $C_O/C_F = 1/5$ ⇒ operating cost = **20%** of fixed cost.
- **Second-Order Check:** $\dfrac{d^2 C_T}{dD^2} = \dfrac{30K}{D^7} > 0$ ⇒ confirmed minimum.
- **Assumptions:** Incompressible fluid, isothermal, steady-state, fully-developed turbulent flow, Newtonian, constant $f$, horizontal pipe (no $\Delta z$).
- **Limitation:** Resulting $D_{opt}$ is often rounded up to the next **standard nominal bore (NB)** size; the corresponding cost penalty is small (<5%) if the next-larger standard size is chosen.
- **Extensions (Higher-Semester Preview):** For **compressible fluids** (gas pipelines), $\rho$ varies along the pipe, requiring numerical integration of the Fanning equation coupled with the optimization — handled in advanced design courses.
- **Industrial Practice:** Always overlay the continuous $D_{opt}$ result with **standard schedule tables** (ANSI B36.10 / B36.19) before finalizing procurement.
- **Cognitive Level Tested:** Apply → Analyze → Evaluate (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy).
- **Typical Marks Distribution in KTU 2024 ESE:** Part A (3 marks) → 1 question; Part B (14 marks) → 1 question with internal choice — total exposure per module ≈ 14–17 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization of Heat Exchanger Network
Optimal Reflux Ratio for a Staged Distillation Column
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Optimal Reflux Ratio for a Staged Distillation Column # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In a staged distillation column, the **reflux ratio (R)** is the ratio of liquid returned to the column as reflux to the distillate product withdrawn: $$R = \frac{L}{D}$$ The **Optimal Reflux Ratio (R_opt)** is the value of R that minimizes the **Total Annualized Cost (TAC)** of the column, which is the sum of the **Capital Cost** (column shell, trays, condenser, reboiler) and the **Operating Cost** (primarily utility costs — steam to reboiler and cooling water to condenser) over the operating lifetime of the plant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The optimal reflux ratio is the reflux ratio at which the derivative of the total cost (fixed + operating) with respect to R equals zero. It lies between the minimum reflux ratio (R_min) and total reflux condition, and is typically in the range **1.2 R_min ≤ R_opt ≤ 1.5 R_min**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Compromise Tap" Imagine you are filling a swimming pool using two hoses — one for water (capital = bigger pool/tank) and one for pumping energy (operating cost). - If you set R **too low** (close to R_min), the column needs **an enormous number of trays** (very tall column) — capital cost is huge, but steam bills are small. - If you set R **too high**, the column needs very few trays (cheap, short column), but the reboiler must vaporize and re-condense a huge recycle stream every second — operating (energy) cost is astronomical. - The **Optimal Reflux Ratio** is the "sweet spot" on the control tap — a tall-enough column (not too tall) running at a reflux not too wasteful. Your annual bill (capital + energy) is at its lowest. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | |---|---|---| | Minimum Reflux Ratio | R_min | Found by Underwood equation | | Optimal Reflux Ratio | R_opt | **1.2 – 1.5 × R_min** | | Minimum Number of Stages | N_min | Found by Fenske equation | | Total Reflux Condition | R → ∞ | All condensate returned, D = 0 | | Energy Cost Factor (annualized) | C_v ($/yr) | Function of utility price | | Capital Recovery Factor | CRF | Converts installed cost → annual cost | > [!NOTE] > For KTU 2024 ESE, students are expected to know that **R_opt is a design optimization variable, not an operating variable**. Once R_opt is selected, the column is built; the operating engineer then cannot freely change R without re-optimizing. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Cost Trade-Off — "Why" and "How" The design problem in distillation has two competing cost components: **A. Capital Cost (C_cap)** — dominated by: - Column shell height ∝ **Number of stages (N)** - Number of trays, reboiler, condenser heat-exchanger area - As R increases → N decreases sharply → C_cap **decreases** **B. Operating Cost (C_op)** — dominated by: - Reboiler steam duty Q_B ∝ (R + 1) × D - Condenser cooling water ∝ (R + 1) × D - As R increases → Q_B increases linearly → C_op **increases** ### Trade-off Curve $$C_{total}(R) = C_{cap}(R) + C_{op}(R)$$ - At R = R_min → N → ∞ → C_cap → ∞ - At R → ∞ → N → N_min → C_cap minimized, but C_op → ∞ - Therefore, C_total(R) has a **single minimum** — this is R_opt. ## 2.2 Mathematical Condition for R_opt The necessary condition for the optimum (assuming smooth, differentiable cost functions): $$\frac{dC_{total}(R)}{dR} = \frac{dC_{cap}(R)}{dR} + \frac{dC_{op}(R)}{dR} = 0$$ Equivalently, the **slope of capital cost decrease equals the slope of operating cost increase** at the optimum: $$\left| \frac{dC_{cap}}{dR} \right|_{R_{opt}} = \left| \frac{dC_{op}}{dR} \right|_{R_{opt}}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Name | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[ \left( \frac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \right)\left( \frac{1 - x_W}{x_W} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | **Fenske Equation** | Minimum stages at total reflux | | 2 | $\sum_{i} \frac{q_i \, z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1$ | **Underwood Equation** | Finds θ, then R_min from $\sum \frac{q_i x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = R_{min} + 1$ | | 3 | $Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668})$ where $X = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1}$, $Y = \frac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1}$ | **Gilliland Correlation** | Number of stages for any R | | 4 | $Y = 1 - \exp\!\left[ \frac{(1 + 54.4X)(X - 1)}{(11 + 117.2X)\sqrt{X}} \right]$ | **Eduljee Correlation** | Improved Gilliland fit | | 5 | $R_{min} = 1.4 \, R_{min, Underwood}$ (heuristic shortcut) | **Heuristic R_min** | Quick estimate | | 6 | $R_{opt} \approx 1.2 \, R_{min}$ to $1.5 \, R_{min}$ | **Erbar–Maddox Rule** | First-pass design optimum | | 7 | $Q_B = D(R + 1)\lambda$ | **Reboiler Duty** | Operating cost driver | | 8 | $\text{TAC} = \text{CRF} \cdot C_{capital} + C_{operating}$ | **Total Annualized Cost** | Optimization objective | | 9 | $\text{CRF} = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | **Capital Recovery Factor** | Converts CAPEX → annual | | 10 | $C_{cap} = a + bN^{0.8}$ (typical cost correlation) | **Cost vs. Stages** | Empirical CAPEX model | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, students often confuse R_min (theoretical, from Underwood) with R_opt (economic). Always state clearly: **R_min is the thermodynamic lower limit; R_opt is the economic optimum, and R_opt > R_min.** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Petroleum refining**: Crude and vacuum distillation columns are designed near R_opt to balance huge throughput energy costs against column shell cost. - **Cryogenic air separation**: Oxygen/nitrogen columns use R_opt analysis because reboiler duties are enormous. - **Pharmaceutical / fine chemicals**: Often R_opt is shifted upward (1.5–2.0 × R_min) because column flexibility and product purity safety margins matter more than minimum energy. - **CO₂ capture & LNG plants**: Modern pinch-analysis overlays the TAC curve on the column grand composite curve to find R_opt. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Cost as a Function of R We model the problem with the following realistic engineering assumptions: - The column has a fixed feed (F, z_F) and fixed product specifications (x_D, x_W). - The number of stages N(R) decreases monotonically as R increases. - Reboiler duty is linear in R: $Q_B = D(R+1)\lambda$. ### Step 1: Capital Cost Model We use the **six-tenths rule** for cost scaling (a standard heuristic in KTU chemical engineering design): $$C_{cap}(R) = C_0 \left( \frac{N(R)}{N_{min}} \right)^{0.6} \cdot A_{shell}(R) + C_{tray} \cdot N(R) + C_{HX}(Q_C, Q_B)$$ For simplification in the KTU analytical context, assume: $$C_{cap}(R) \approx a + b \cdot N(R)$$ where $a$ is fixed cost (site prep, piping, instrumentation) and $b$ is per-tray installed cost. ### Step 2: Operating Cost Model $$C_{op}(R) = 8760 \cdot c_{steam} \cdot Q_B(R) + 8760 \cdot c_{cw} \cdot Q_C(R)$$ Using $Q_B = D(R+1)\lambda$ and $Q_C \approx Q_B$ (energy balance), and combining constants: $$C_{op}(R) = k(R + 1)$$ where $k = 8760 \cdot D \cdot \lambda \cdot (c_{steam} + c_{cw})$ is a positive constant. ### Step 3: Total Cost $$C_{total}(R) = a + b \cdot N(R) + k(R + 1)$$ ### Step 4: N(R) via Gilliland We need an analytical N(R). Using the **Erbar–Maddox form**, the optimal R usually satisfies a fixed ratio. A common closed-form approximation (used in KTU problems) is: $$N(R) = N_{min} + \frac{c}{R - R_{min}}$$ where c is a positive empirical constant (this form is consistent with the asymptotic behavior $N \to \infty$ as $R \to R_{min}$ and $N \to N_{min}$ as $R \to \infty$). ### Step 5: Apply First-Order Optimality $$C_{total}(R) = a + b\left( N_{min} + \frac{c}{R - R_{min}} \right) + k(R + 1)$$ Differentiate with respect to R and set to zero: $$\frac{dC_{total}}{dR} = b \cdot \left( -\frac{c}{(R - R_{min})^2} \right) + k = 0$$ Solving: $$\frac{bc}{(R_{opt} - R_{min})^2} = k$$ $$(R_{opt} - R_{min})^2 = \frac{bc}{k}$$ $$\boxed{R_{opt} = R_{min} + \sqrt{\frac{bc}{k}}}$$ **Interpretation of result:** - If capital is expensive (large b) or the N(R) curvature is steep (large c), $R_{opt}$ shifts upward (taller reflux for shorter column). - If energy is expensive (large k), $R_{opt}$ shifts downward (toward R_min, accept more trays to save energy). ### Step 6: Verify Second-Order Condition $$\frac{d^2 C_{total}}{dR^2} = b \cdot \frac{2c}{(R - R_{min})^3} > 0 \quad \text{for } R > R_{min}$$ ✅ This is **positive** — confirming a **minimum** (not a maximum), so the optimum is well-defined. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Erbar–Maddox Correlation for R_opt Empirically (Erbar & Maddox, 1961), the R that minimizes TAC for hydrocarbon systems correlates with feed composition and relative volatility. The KTU-version of the design heuristic: $$\frac{R_{min}}{R_{opt}} = f(\alpha, x_F)$$ For typical systems the rule-of-thumb holds: $$R_{opt} \approx 1.2 \, R_{min} \quad \text{(low } \alpha \text{, high } x_F\text{)}$$ $$R_{opt} \approx 1.5 \, R_{min} \quad \text{(high } \alpha \text{, low } x_F\text{)}$$ ### Step-by-Step Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Given:** A binary distillation column separates benzene from toluene. - Feed: z_F = 0.50 (mole fraction benzene) - Distillate: x_D = 0.95 - Bottoms: x_W = 0.05 - Relative volatility α = 2.47 - D = 100 kmol/h **Step (a): Find R_min using Underwood (saturated liquid feed, q = 1).** The Underwood θ-equation: $$\frac{z_F}{\alpha - \theta} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{0.50}{2.47 - \theta} = 1$$ Solving: $$2.47 - \theta = 0.50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 1.97$$ R_min equation: $$R_{min} + 1 = \frac{\alpha \cdot x_D}{\alpha - \theta} + \frac{(1 - x_D)}{(1 - \theta)}$$ $$R_{min} + 1 = \frac{2.47 \times 0.95}{2.47 - 1.97} + \frac{0.05}{1 - 1.97}$$ $$R_{min} + 1 = \frac{2.3465}{0.50} + \frac{0.05}{-0.97} = 4.693 - 0.0515 = 4.642$$ $$\boxed{R_{min} = 3.642}$$ **Step (b): Choose R_opt via Erbar–Maddox heuristic.** For α = 2.47 and x_F = 0.50: $$R_{opt} \approx 1.3 \times R_{min} = 1.3 \times 3.642 = 4.735$$ **Step (c): Verify by finding N at both R values using Fenske + Gilliland.** Fenske: $$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[ \left( \frac{0.95}{0.05} \right)\left( \frac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \right]}{\ln(2.47)} = \frac{\ln(361)}{\ln(2.47)} = \frac{5.886}{0.9046} = 6.51$$ Take $N_{min} \approx 7$ stages. Gilliland at R = R_min = 3.642: - $X = (R - R_{min})/(R+1) = 0/4.642 = 0$ - $Y = 0.75(1 - 0^{0.5668}) = 0.75$ - $N - N_{min}/(N+1) = 0.75$ → $N \to \infty$ ✅ (as expected) Gilliland at R_opt = 4.735: - $X = (4.735 - 3.642)/(4.735 + 1) = 1.093/5.735 = 0.1906$ - $Y = 0.75(1 - 0.1906^{0.5668}) = 0.75(1 - 0.385) = 0.75 \times 0.615 = 0.461$ - Solve $0.461 = (N - 7)/(N + 1)$: - $0.461N + 0.461 = N - 7$ - $0.539N = 7.461$ - $N = 13.84 \approx 14$ stages **Step (d): Compute Annualized Costs.** Assume: tray cost b = ₹50,000/stage·yr (annualized), steam cost k = ₹20,000/(kmol/h reflux)·yr, D·λ factor absorbed. $$C_{cap}(R_{opt}) = a + b \cdot N = a + 50{,}000 \times 14 = a + 7.0 \times 10^5$$ $$C_{op}(R_{opt}) = k(R_{opt} + 1) = 20{,}000 \times 5.735 = 1.147 \times 10^6$$ $$C_{total}(R_{opt}) = a + 1.847 \times 10^6 \text{ ₹/yr}$$ Compare with R = 2 R_min = 7.284 (very high reflux): - $X = (7.284 - 3.642)/(7.284 + 1) = 3.642/8.284 = 0.4397$ - $Y = 0.75(1 - 0.4397^{0.5668}) = 0.75(1 - 0.612) = 0.291$ - $N \approx 7 + 0.291(N+1)$ → $0.709N = 7.291$ → $N = 10.3 \approx 11$ stages - $C_{cap} = a + 50{,}000 \times 11 = a + 5.5 \times 10^5$ - $C_{op} = 20{,}000 \times 8.284 = 1.657 \times 10^6$ - $C_{total} = a + 2.207 \times 10^6$ ₹/yr ❌ **higher** (as expected, R too high) **Conclusion:** $R_{opt} \approx 4.74$ minimizes TAC for this system. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Table | Parameter change | Effect on C_cap | Effect on C_op | Effect on R_opt | |---|---|---|---| | Energy price ↑ | negligible | ↑↑ | ↓ (moves toward R_min) | | Steel price ↑ | ↑ | negligible | ↑ (moves toward R_min) | | α ↑ (easier separation) | ↓ | ↓ (less reflux needed) | both R_min and R_opt ↓ | | Higher x_D required | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | | Longer plant life (higher CRF denominator effect) | fixed CAPEX spread out | unchanged | R_opt ↓ slightly | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Total Cost vs. Reflux Ratio Trade-off ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph costCurves["Annualized Cost versus Reflux Ratio"] direction LR nodeA["R = R_min
Tall Column
N -> infinity"] --> nodeB["R = R_opt
Minimum TAC
1.2 to 1.5 R_min"] nodeB --> nodeC["R = infinity
Total Reflux
N = N_min"] end subgraph capCost["Capital Cost C_cap"] capH["Very High at R_min"] --> capM["Decreasing"] --> capL["Minimum at Total Reflux"] end subgraph opCost["Operating Cost C_op"] opL["Low at R_min"] --> opM["Rising Linearly"] --> opH["Very High at Total Reflux"] end subgraph tacCurve["Total Annualized Cost"] tacPeak["Infinite at R_min"] --> tacMin["Optimum at R_opt"] --> tacRise["Rises Again at High R"] end nodeB --> tacMin capM --> tacMin opM --> tacMin style nodeA fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000 style nodeB fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#008800 style nodeC fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#003399 style tacMin fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#aa8800 style capH fill:#ffd6d6 style capL fill:#d4f4d4 style opL fill:#d4f4d4 style opH fill:#ffd6d6 style tacPeak fill:#ffd6d6 style tacRise fill:#ffd6d6 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Optimization Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD specBlock["Feed Specification
F, zF, q"] --> calRmin["Compute R_min
via Underwood"] specBlock --> calNmin["Compute N_min
via Fenske"] calRmin --> gilliland["Gilliland Loop
Sweep R from R_min to 3 R_min"] calNmin --> gilliland gilliland --> nAtR["N at each R"] nAtR --> capModel["C_cap equals a plus b times N"] specBlock --> opModel["C_op equals k times R plus 1"] capModel --> tacSum["TAC equals C_cap plus C_op"] opModel --> tacSum tacSum --> optimumSearch["Find R where dTAC by dR equals zero"] optimumSearch --> roptOutput["Output R_opt and N_opt and TAC_min"] roptOutput --> designReport["Engineering Design Report"] optimumSearch -->|Recheck Sensitivity| calRmin style specBlock fill:#e6f0ff style calRmin fill:#fff4cc style calNmin fill:#fff4cc style gilliland fill:#ffe0cc style tacSum fill:#d4f4d4 style roptOutput fill:#ffd6a8,stroke:#aa5500 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Description (Mermaid Fallback — N vs. R Curve) The number of theoretical stages N(R) is a **hyperbolic decay curve** that asymptotes to: - N → ∞ as R → R_min (vertical asymptote on the left) - N → N_min as R → ∞ (horizontal asymptote on the right, equal to Fenske result) Combined with the **linearly rising C_op** and the **monotonically decreasing C_cap**, the **TAC curve** looks like a U-shape (or more precisely, a steep descent from infinity, a clear minimum, then a slow rise) — the minimum of which is R_opt. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "optimal reflux ratio" in the context of a staged distillation column. Why is it always greater than the minimum reflux ratio?* **Model Answer:** The optimal reflux ratio (R_opt) is the value of R that minimizes the **Total Annualized Cost (TAC)** of the distillation column, which is the sum of the annualized capital cost (column shell, trays, reboiler, condenser) and the operating cost (steam, cooling water, electricity). It is always greater than R_min because: - At R = R_min, the number of stages required is **infinite**, making the capital cost infinite. - To obtain a **finite and economic** design, one must operate at R > R_min. - As R increases from R_min, capital cost decreases (fewer stages) but operating cost increases (more reboiling). The minimum of the sum gives R_opt. - Empirically, $R_{opt} \approx (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) R_{min}$ (Erbar–Maddox rule). *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State the Underwood equation for minimum reflux and the Fenske equation for minimum stages. How are these used to find the optimal reflux ratio?* **Model Answer:** - **Underwood equation** (for R_min, with θ between α_light and α_heavy): $$\sum_{i} \frac{q_i z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1, \quad \text{then} \quad R_{min} = \sum_i \frac{q_i x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$$ - **Fenske equation** (for N_min, total reflux): $$N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_{D, LK}}{x_{D, HK}} \right) \left( \frac{x_{W, HK}}{x_{W, LK}} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$ **Use in finding R_opt:** R_min (Underwood) and N_min (Fenske) define the two asymptotic endpoints of the operating range. The Gilliland correlation then gives N at any R between R_min and infinity. Computing the TAC for several values of R and finding the minimum gives R_opt. *(3 marks: 1 mark Underwood, 1 mark Fenske, 1 mark application)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **A binary distillation column is to be designed for the following specifications:** - Feed: 50/50 mol% benzene–toluene, saturated liquid (q = 1), F = 1000 kmol/h - Distillate: x_D = 0.95 benzene - Bottoms: x_W = 0.05 benzene - Relative volatility α = 2.47 - Annualized tray cost: b = ₹60,000 per stage - Energy cost coefficient: k = ₹50,000 per unit of (R+1) - Fixed capital: a = ₹2 × 10⁵ **(a)** Find the minimum reflux ratio (R_min) using the Underwood equation and the minimum number of stages (N_min) using the Fenske equation. (7 marks) **(b)** Assuming the empirical relation $N(R) = N_{min} + \frac{15}{R - R_{min}}$ for this system, determine the optimal reflux ratio (R_opt) and the corresponding number of stages. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part A **Part (a) Solution:** *Step 1: Underwood θ* (q = 1, single light key): $$\frac{z_F}{\alpha - \theta} = 1 \Rightarrow 0.50 = 2.47 - \theta \Rightarrow \theta = 1.97$$ *Step 2: R_min:* $$R_{min} + 1 = \frac{\alpha x_D}{\alpha - \theta} + \frac{(1 - x_D)}{1 - \theta} = \frac{2.47 \times 0.95}{0.50} + \frac{0.05}{-0.97}$$ $$R_{min} + 1 = 4.693 - 0.0515 = 4.6415$$ $$\boxed{R_{min} = 3.6415}$$ *Step 3: Fenske N_min:* $$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[ \left( \frac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \left( \frac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \right]}{\ln(2.47)} = \frac{\ln(361)}{0.9046} = \frac{5.886}{0.9046}$$ $$\boxed{N_{min} = 6.51 \approx 7 \text{ stages}}$$ *(7 marks: 2 Underwood, 2 R_min, 3 Fenske)* --- **Part (b) Solution:** *Step 1: Total cost function:* $$C_{total}(R) = a + b\left( N_{min} + \frac{15}{R - R_{min}} \right) + k(R + 1)$$ $$C_{total}(R) = a + 60000 \times 7 + \frac{60000 \times 15}{R - 3.6415} + 50000(R + 1)$$ *Step 2: Differentiate and set to zero:* $$\frac{dC_{total}}{dR} = -\frac{9.0 \times 10^5}{(R - 3.6415)^2} + 50000 = 0$$ $$(R_{opt} - 3.6415)^2 = \frac{9.0 \times 10^5}{50000} = 18$$ $$R_{opt} - 3.6415 = \sqrt{18} = 4.2426$$ $$\boxed{R_{opt} = 7.884 \approx 7.88}$$ *Step 3: Number of stages at R_opt:* $$N(R_{opt}) = 7 + \frac{15}{7.884 - 3.6415} = 7 + \frac{15}{4.2426} = 7 + 3.536$$ $$\boxed{N_{opt} \approx 10.5 \approx 11 \text{ stages}}$$ *Step 4: Second-derivative check:* $$\frac{d^2 C_{total}}{dR^2} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^6}{(R - 3.6415)^3} > 0 \quad \text{for } R > R_{min} \quad ✅$$ *Step 5: Cross-check with Erbar–Maddox heuristic:* $$R_{opt} / R_{min} = 7.884 / 3.6415 = 2.165$$ This is higher than the typical 1.2–1.5 range — indicating that the empirical $N(R)$ model in this problem is steeper than Erbar–Maddox assumes (this is acceptable; problem-specific data overrides general heuristics). *(7 marks: 2 cost formulation, 3 differentiation and solution, 1 second-derivative check, 1 heuristic comparison)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the variation of **(i) capital cost, (ii) operating cost, and (iii) total annualized cost** as functions of the reflux ratio R for a binary distillation column. Mark the location of R_min, R_opt, and total reflux on the diagram. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the expression for the optimal reflux ratio starting from the total cost function $C_{total}(R) = a + bN_{min} + \dfrac{bc}{R - R_{min}} + k(R + 1)$. Clearly state the assumption on $N(R)$ and verify the second-order condition for a minimum. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part B **Part (a) Solution:** The three curves behave as follows (refer to the Mermaid block-diagram in Section 4): | Curve | Behavior | |---|---| | **C_cap(R)** | Monotonically decreasing; → ∞ as R → R_min; → minimum at total reflux | | **C_op(R)** | Monotonically increasing (linear in R); minimum at R_min, → ∞ as R → ∞ | | **C_total(R)** | U-shaped; → ∞ at both R_min and total reflux; **minimum at R_opt** | The intersection of the **slopes** of C_cap and C_op (in absolute value) occurs at R_opt, which lies between R_min (vertical asymptote) and total reflux. Marking: - R_min — left vertical asymptote of C_cap - R_opt — minimum of C_total, where slopes cancel - R → ∞ — right end (total reflux, N = N_min, minimum C_cap) *(7 marks: 2 marks per curve, 1 mark for marking critical points)* --- **Part (b) Solution:** *Assumption:* $$N(R) = N_{min} + \frac{c}{R - R_{min}}, \quad c > 0$$ This satisfies the asymptotic constraints: $N \to \infty$ as $R \to R_{min}^+$ and $N \to N_{min}$ as $R \to \infty$. *Step 1: Total cost:* $$C_{total}(R) = a + b N_{min} + \frac{bc}{R - R_{min}} + k(R + 1)$$ where a, b, c, k > 0 are constants (a = fixed cost, b = per-tray cost, c = curvature of N(R), k = energy cost per unit reflux). *Step 2: First-order necessary condition:* $$\frac{dC_{total}}{dR} = -\frac{bc}{(R - R_{min})^2} + k = 0$$ *Step 3: Solve for R_opt:* $$(R - R_{min})^2 = \frac{bc}{k}$$ $$R_{opt} = R_{min} + \sqrt{\frac{bc}{k}}$$ *Step 4: Second-order condition:* $$\frac{d^2 C_{total}}{dR^2} = \frac{2bc}{(R - R_{min})^3}$$ For $R > R_{min}$, the denominator is positive, so $\frac{d^2 C_{total}}{dR^2} > 0$. ✅ This confirms that the stationary point is a **local minimum** of $C_{total}$. *Step 5: Interpretation:* - $R_{opt} - R_{min} = \sqrt{bc/k}$ - Increase capital cost (b↑) or steeper N(R) curve (c↑) → R_opt moves up. - Increase energy cost (k↑) → R_opt moves down (toward R_min). *(7 marks: 1 assumption, 2 cost expression, 2 derivative and solution, 2 second-derivative check and interpretation)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing R_min with R_opt**: Marks are deducted when students use R_min as the design value. Always state R_opt > R_min explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting to verify the second-order condition**: A first-order derivative = 0 is a necessary, not sufficient, condition. Always show d²C/dR² > 0. > 3. **Using the wrong Underwood θ range**: θ must lie between α_heavy_key and α_light_key, and the Underwood equation is solved iteratively for multicomponent feeds. > 4. **Mixing Gilliland X and Y definitions**: $X = (R - R_{min})/(R+1)$ and $Y = (N - N_{min})/(N+1)$. Reverse definitions are a common error. > 5. **Ignoring annualized vs. installed cost**: TAC requires CAPEX × CRF; students often compare total CAPEX with annual OPEX, which is dimensionally inconsistent. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **R_min** = thermodynamic lower limit (Underwood), where $N \to \infty$. - **N_min** = minimum stages at total reflux (Fenske). - **R_opt** = economic optimum where $d(C_{cap} + C_{op})/dR = 0$. - **Empirical rule**: $R_{opt} \approx (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) R_{min}$ (Erbar–Maddox). - **Gilliland correlation** bridges the gap between (R_min, ∞) and (∞, N_min) — both X and Y must be correctly defined. - **TAC** (Total Annualized Cost) = CRF × CAPEX + Annual OPEX — this is the **objective function** for R_opt. - **Second-order check** $d^2 C/dR^2 > 0$ must always be verified. - **Capital cost dominates at low R** (tall column), **operating cost dominates at high R** (energy waste). - **Sensitivity matters**: rising energy prices push R_opt down; rising steel prices push R_opt up. - For exam: always show **all five steps** — (1) cost model, (2) cost derivative, (3) set to zero, (4) solve, (5) second-derivative check.

topics.title: Optimal Design of an Ammonia Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimal Design of an Ammonia Reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Design of an Ammonia Reactor** is the systematic application of constrained and unconstrained optimization techniques to a heterogeneous catalytic, exothermic, equilibrium-limited reaction system (N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃, ΔH°₂₉₈ ≈ **−91.8 kJ/mol**) in order to determine the operating temperature profile, pressure, space velocity, feed ratio, and reactor configuration that maximize a chosen economic objective function — typically **profit per unit time** or **minimum reactor volume for a specified conversion**.

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with NEP 2020 / Outcome-Based Education), this is a **multi-variable, non-linear, constrained optimization** problem in which:

- **Decision Variables (u):** Reactor temperature T(x), operating pressure P, inlet H₂/N₂ ratio θ, catalyst distribution, number of adiabatic stages Nₛ.
- **Objective Function (Φ):** Φ = (Revenue from NH₃) − (Cost of feed) − (Cost of compression) − (Cost of utilities) − (Capital cost of reactor).
- **Equality Constraints:** Component material balances, energy balance, kinetic rate expression, equilibrium relation.
- **Inequality Constraints:** T_min ≤ T(x) ≤ T_max, P_min ≤ P ≤ P_max, x_NH₃ ≤ x_eq(T).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Mountain Climber's Dilemma:**
> Imagine climbing a mountain whose summit represents the **equilibrium conversion** (the theoretical maximum yield of ammonia). Two opposing forces act on the climber:
> - **Kinetic force (push forward):** Higher temperature → faster reaction rate → more NH₃ per unit time.
> - **Thermodynamic force (pull back):** Higher temperature → lower equilibrium constant → lower ultimate conversion (Le Chatelier's principle on an exothermic reaction).
>
> The **optimal path** is to start near the summit rapidly (high T for fast kinetics when conversion is low) and then deliberately cool the reacting gas as conversion climbs (move down the mountain along the **locus of maximum rate**) so that the equilibrium ceiling keeps rising. This is precisely the philosophy of the **interstage-cooled multibed adiabatic reactor** used in every commercial Haber–Bosch plant.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Design Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **ΔH°ᵣₓₙ (NH₃ synthesis)** | **−91.8 kJ/mol NH₃** | Strongly exothermic — drives T-profile design |
| **Δn (moles of gas change)** | **−2 per 2 mol NH₃** | Volume reduction → high P favours equilibrium |
| **Catalyst (Fe, promoted)** | **Fe – Al₂O₃ – K₂O – CaO** | Operating window ~ **400–500 °C** |
| **Commercial pressure range** | **150 – 300 atm** | Compression cost vs. conversion trade-off |
| **Optimum H₂ : N₂ feed ratio** | **2.8 – 3.2 : 1** | Slightly sub-stoichiometric to handle H₂ slip |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Chemistry

The synthesis of ammonia follows the heterogeneous gas–solid reaction:

$$\text{1/2 N}_2 + \text{3/2 H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 \quad \Delta H^\circ = -45.9\ \text{kJ/mol NH}_3$$

or stoichiometrically:

$$\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$$

The reaction is:
- **Exothermic** (heat is liberated — favours low T at equilibrium).
- **Mole-reducing** (4 mol → 2 mol — favours high P at equilibrium).
- **Catalyst-dependent** (kinetically inaccessible below ~400 °C even with promoted iron).

### 2.2 The Temkin–Pyzhev Rate Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The rate of ammonia formation per unit mass of promoted iron catalyst is conventionally expressed by the **Temkin–Pyzhev** kinetic model (the standard reference kinetic form used in KTU textbooks and Fogler's *Elements of Chemical Reaction Engineering*):

$$r_{NH_3} = k_1 \, e^{-E_1/RT}\frac{P_{N_2}^{0.5}\, P_{H_2}^{1.5}}{P_{NH_3}} \;-\; k_2 \, e^{-E_2/RT}\frac{P_{NH_3}}{P_{H_2}^{1.5}}$$

A simplified, more compact form equivalent to setting the bracketed expression of partial pressures equal to the departure from equilibrium:

$$r_{NH_3} = k(T)\left[K_{eq}(T)\,\frac{P_{N_2}^{0.5}\, P_{H_2}^{1.5}}{P_{NH_3}} \;-\; \frac{P_{NH_3}}{P_{N_2}^{0.5}\, P_{H_2}^{1.5}}\right]$$

where the temperature dependence splits cleanly into:

$$k(T) = k_0\, e^{-E/RT}, \qquad K_{eq}(T) = K_0\, e^{\Delta H^\circ / RT}$$

For an **exothermic** reaction, ΔH° < 0, so K_eq **decreases** exponentially as T rises.

### 2.3 Equilibrium Conversion (Why T and P Matter)

From the van't Hoff integration (assuming ΔH° approximately constant over the range of interest):

$$\ln K_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \text{constant}$$

The equilibrium mole fraction of NH₃ at total pressure Pₜ and feed ratio y_{H₂,0}/y_{N₂,0} is obtained from:

$$K_{eq}(T) = \frac{P_{NH_3}^\star}{(P_{N_2}^\star)^{0.5}(P_{H_2}^\star)^{1.5}}$$

### 2.4 The Optimization Problem — Structured Logic

The optimum is found by the chain of reasoning:

1. **Step 1 — PFR/CSTR model:** Write component mole balance:
   $$\frac{dF_i}{dW} = \nu_i \, r_{NH_3}, \qquad F_i(W=0) = F_{i,0}$$
   Equivalently, in terms of conversion X of nitrogen:
   $$\frac{dX}{dW} = \frac{r_{NH_3}}{F_{N_2,0}}$$

2. **Step 2 — Locus of Maximum Rate:** For a **given** conversion X, the temperature T*(X) that maximizes r_{NH₃} satisfies:
   $$\left.\frac{\partial r_{NH_3}}{\partial T}\right|_{X,\,P} = 0$$
   This defines the **optimum temperature locus** T*(X, P).

3. **Step 3 — Reactor Sizing:** The minimum catalyst mass required to reach conversion X_f along this locus is:
   $$W_{\min} = F_{N_2,0} \int_0^{X_f} \frac{dX}{r_{NH_3}\bigl[T^\star(X,\,P),\,X,\,P\bigr]}$$

4. **Step 4 — Economic Optimization over P:** Compute the annual profit:
   $$\Phi(P) = C_{NH_3}\,\dot n_{NH_3,\,out}(P) - C_{feed}\,\dot n_{feed}(P) - C_{comp}(P) - C_{cat}\,\frac{W(P)}{\tau}$$
   Find P* that maximizes Φ(P) by setting dΦ/dP = 0.

5. **Step 5 — Configuration Selection:** Compare PFR with continuous intercooling vs. multiple adiabatic CSTR-in-series with interstage heat exchange; select the configuration with maximum Φ.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Block — Memorize before exam day.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_{NH_3} = k_1 e^{-E_1/RT}\,P_{N_2}^{0.5}P_{H_2}^{1.5}/P_{NH_3} - k_2 e^{-E_2/RT}\,P_{NH_3}/P_{H_2}^{1.5}$ | Temkin–Pyzhev rate | kmol kg-cat⁻¹ h⁻¹ |
| 2 | $K_{eq}(T) = K_0\,e^{\Delta H^\circ / RT}$ | van't Hoff relation | dimensionless |
| 3 | $d\ln K_{eq}/dT = \Delta H^\circ/RT^2$ | Differential form | K⁻¹ |
| 4 | $dX/dW = -r_A / F_{A0}$ | PFR (heterogeneous) | kg-cat⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\partial r_{NH_3}/\partial T\,\vert_{X,P} = 0$ | Optimum temperature locus | — |
| 6 | $W_{\min} = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^{X_f}\dfrac{dX}{r_{NH_3}[T^\star(X),X]}$ | Min. catalyst mass | kg-cat |
| 7 | $\Phi = \sum c_i \dot n_i - C_{cap}\cdot W$ | Economic objective | ₹/h or $/yr |
| 8 | $P^\star$ : $d\Phi/dP = 0$ | Economic optimum pressure | atm or bar |
| 9 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K_{eq}$ | Thermodynamic ceiling | kJ/mol |
| 10 | $y_{NH_3}^\star = f(P,T,\theta)$ from $\sum y_i = 1$, $K_{eq}$ relation | Equilibrium composition | mole fraction |

> **Pitfall Avoided:** All absolute-value or norm symbols in the table above use plain text or LaTeX `\vert`, **never** a pipe `|` character that would break the markdown parser.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Haber–Bosch plants** (capacity 1,000 – 3,000 MTPD) worldwide employ the very principles derived here — interstage cooling with **2 – 4 adiabatic catalyst beds**, operating in the window **400 – 500 °C** and **150 – 300 atm**.
- **Ammonia synthesis loop design** is a canonical **process integration** problem combining reaction engineering, equilibrium thermodynamics, and economic optimization.
- The methodology generalizes to **methanol synthesis, Fischer–Tropsch, and SO₂ oxidation** — every exothermic, equilibrium-limited, catalytic reaction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Locus of Maximum Rate (T* – X Curve)

**Statement to Prove:** For a reversible exothermic reaction, the rate r_{NH₃}(T, X, P) at fixed X and P has a unique maximum at some temperature T*(X, P) < T_eq.

**Starting Point — Generalised Rate Form:**

Write the rate as the difference between a forward and a reverse term, each with a different exponential temperature dependence:

$$r(T) = \underbrace{A_f e^{-E_f/RT} f_F(X,P)}_{\text{forward}} - \underbrace{A_r e^{-E_r/RT} f_R(X,P)}_{\text{reverse}}$$

where, for the ammonia reaction, E_r > E_f (the reverse activation energy exceeds the forward one because E_r − E_f = −ΔH° > 0 for an exothermic step).

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to T at fixed X, P:**

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial T} = \frac{A_f E_f}{RT^2} e^{-E_f/RT} f_F - \frac{A_r E_r}{RT^2} e^{-E_r/RT} f_R$$

**Step 2 — Set ∂r/∂T = 0 to find extremum:**

$$\frac{A_f E_f}{RT^2} e^{-E_f/RT} f_F = \frac{A_r E_r}{RT^2} e^{-E_r/RT} f_R$$

Cancel the common factor 1/(RT²):

$$A_f E_f\, e^{-E_f/RT} f_F = A_r E_r\, e^{-E_r/RT} f_R$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate T:**

$$\frac{e^{-E_f/RT}}{e^{-E_r/RT}} = \frac{A_r E_r f_R}{A_f E_f f_F}$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$\frac{E_r - E_f}{RT} = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_r E_r f_R}{A_f E_f f_F}\right)$$

Since E_r − E_f = −ΔH° > 0 for exothermic, we obtain:

$$\boxed{T^\star(X, P) = \frac{-\Delta H^\circ / R}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_r E_r f_R(X,P)}{A_f E_f f_F(X,P)}\right)}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**

- As X increases, f_R(X, P) grows (more NH₃ present — reverse term stronger) and f_F(X, P) shrinks (less N₂, H₂ — forward term weaker).
- The logarithm therefore **decreases** as X increases.
- Hence **T\* decreases with X** — the optimum temperature profile in a PFR is a **monotonically decreasing** curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the mathematical basis for the famous "**descending temperature program**" used in industrial converters — start hot (~500 °C) for fast kinetics in the first bed; cool between beds to ~430 °C in the last bed to push equilibrium up.

**Step 5 — Verify second-derivative condition (maximum not minimum):**

$$\frac{\partial^2 r}{\partial T^2}\bigg|_{T=T^\star} < 0$$

A sign analysis of the forward-reverse terms confirms that because the reverse term has the larger exponent E_r, raising T further accelerates the reverse term faster than the forward term — so T* is indeed a **maximum** of r(T).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equilibrium Mole Fraction of NH₃

**Given:** Stoichiometric feed (H₂/N₂ = 3:1, no inerts), total pressure Pₜ, temperature T.

**Step 1 — Define extent of reaction per mole of N₂ fed:**

For 1 mole N₂ + 3 moles H₂ initially:
- N₂ remaining: (1 − ξ)
- H₂ remaining: (1 − 3ξ)
- NH₃ formed: 2ξ
- Total moles: 4 − 2ξ

The mole fractions at extent ξ:

$$y_{N_2} = \frac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}, \quad y_{H_2} = \frac{3(1-\xi)}{4-2\xi}, \quad y_{NH_3} = \frac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}$$

**Step 2 — Express partial pressures:**

$$P_i = y_i \, P_t$$

**Step 3 — Apply the equilibrium expression:**

For N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃:

$$K_{eq}(T) = \frac{P_{NH_3}^2}{P_{N_2}\, P_{H_2}^3} = \frac{(y_{NH_3} P_t)^2}{(y_{N_2}\, P_t)(y_{H_2}\, P_t)^3} = \frac{1}{P_t^2}\,\frac{y_{NH_3}^2}{y_{N_2}\, y_{H_2}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute mole fractions:**

$$K_{eq}(T) = \frac{1}{P_t^2}\,\frac{\left(\dfrac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}\right)^{\!2}}{\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}\right)\left(\dfrac{3(1-\xi)}{4-2\xi}\right)^{\!3}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the algebra:**

Group the (4 − 2ξ) terms:

$$\left(\frac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}\right)^{\!2} = \frac{4\xi^2}{(4-2\xi)^2}$$

$$\left(\frac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}\right)\!\left(\frac{3(1-\xi)}{4-2\xi}\right)^{\!3} = \frac{27(1-\xi)^4}{(4-2\xi)^4}$$

Therefore:

$$K_{eq}(T) = \frac{1}{P_t^2}\cdot\frac{4\xi^2/(4-2\xi)^2}{27(1-\xi)^4/(4-2\xi)^4} = \frac{1}{P_t^2}\cdot\frac{4\xi^2 \cdot (4-2\xi)^2}{27(1-\xi)^4}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{K_{eq}(T)\, P_t^2 \;=\; \frac{4\,\xi^2\,(4-2\xi)^2}{27\,(1-\xi)^4}}$$

Equilibrium conversion ξ* is obtained by solving this implicit equation for ξ at any given (T, P_t). The qualitative behaviour:

- **As T ↑** → K_eq ↓ → ξ* ↓ (lower equilibrium conversion).
- **As P_t ↑** → ξ* ↑ (higher equilibrium conversion).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Catalyst Mass for Optimum Temperature Profile

**Given:** PFR with catalyst mass W, feed F_{N₂,0}, optimum temperature T*(X, P).

**Step 1 — Mole balance on N₂:**

$$\frac{dX}{dW} = \frac{r_{NH_3}(T,X,P)}{F_{N_2,0}}$$

**Step 2 — When T = T*(X, P), the rate r_{NH₃} is at its maximum value r_{NH₃}^{max}(X, P).**

Integrate from inlet (W = 0, X = 0) to outlet (W = W, X = X_f):

$$W_{\min} = F_{N_2,0}\int_{0}^{X_f}\frac{dX}{r_{NH_3}^{\max}(X,\,P)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Temkin–Pyzhev form evaluated at T*(X, P):**

$$r_{NH_3}^{\max}(X,P) = k_1(T^\star)\,P_{N_2}^{0.5}P_{H_2}^{1.5}/P_{NH_3} - k_2(T^\star)\,P_{NH_3}/P_{H_2}^{1.5}$$

Numerical integration (or graphical area under 1/r vs. X) gives W_min(P) for each candidate pressure.

**Step 4 — Capital cost component:**

$$C_{cat}(P) = c_{cat}\,W_{\min}(P) \cdot \text{(annualisation factor)}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Economic Optimum Pressure

**Step 1 — Profit function per unit time:**

$$\Phi(P) = a_1\,\dot n_{NH_3,\,out}(P) - a_2\,\dot n_{feed}(P) - a_3\,P\,\dot V_{feed} - a_4\,W_{\min}(P)$$

where a₁ = selling price of NH₃, a₂ = cost of feed, a₃ = unit compression work cost, a₄ = annualised catalyst cost.

**Step 2 — Output flow of NH₃ as function of P:**

For a given conversion X_f, the equilibrium conversion X_eq(P, T) increases with P; the actual X_f is bounded by X_eq. In practice, X_f is fixed by plant specification, and we have:

$$\dot n_{NH_3,\,out} = 2\,F_{N_2,0}\,X_f \quad \text{(independent of P)}$$

So the *revenue term is constant*, and the *cost terms depend on P* through compression work and catalyst mass.

**Step 3 — Differentiate Φ with respect to P:**

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dP} = -\frac{d}{dP}\!\left[a_3 P \dot V_{feed}\right] - a_4\frac{dW_{\min}}{dP} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for P*:**

$$\boxed{a_3\,\dot V_{feed} + a_4\,\frac{dW_{\min}}{dP}\bigg|_{P^\star} = 0}$$

At P*, the marginal saving in catalyst cost exactly balances the marginal cost of additional compression. Typical numerical values from KTU-endorsed texts place P* in the **150 – 250 atm** window.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Variables vs. Constraints

| Decision Variable | Symbol | Typical Range | Primary Constraint | Effect on Φ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inlet temperature | T₀ | 400 – 500 °C | Catalyst sintering ≤ 520 °C | ↑ T₀ ⇒ ↑ rate, ↓ equilibrium |
| Exit temperature | T_f | 380 – 450 °C | T_f ≥ T_ignition (Fe cat.) | ↑ T_f ⇒ ↑ W (bigger reactor) |
| Operating pressure | P | 100 – 300 atm | Compressor cost vs. yield | ↑ P ⇒ ↑ ξ*, ↑ compression cost |
| H₂ : N₂ ratio | θ | 2.5 – 3.5 | H₂ slip & recycle cost | θ ≈ 3 optimum for most economics |
| Inerts (Ar + CH₄) | y_I | ≤ 5 % | Purge cost | ↑ y_I ⇒ ↓ ξ* (dilution) |
| Number of beds | N_s | 2 – 4 | Capital vs. approach to T*(X) | ↑ N_s ⇒ smaller ΔT approach |
| Space velocity | Sᵥ | 5,000 – 30,000 h⁻¹ | Pressure drop, contact time | ↑ Sᵥ ⇒ ↓ X_f |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ammonia Reactor Optimization Framework

The diagram below represents the **process information flow** of the optimization algorithm. Each node is a functional module; arrows represent data/parameter exchange.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[problem1: Problem Definition] --> B[problem2: Specify Objective Phi]
    B --> C[problem3: Decision Variables u T P theta Ns]
    C --> D[problem4: Reaction Stoichiometry 12N2 plus 32H2 to NH3]
    D --> E[problem5: Kinetics Temkin Pyzhev Rate]
    E --> F[problem6: Loop Over Candidate Pressures]
    F --> G[problem7: Solve Locus of Max Rate T star of X]
    G --> H[problem8: Compute Equilibrium xi star of T P]
    H --> I[problem9: Compute W min of P via Integration]
    I --> J[problem10: Evaluate Capital Cost Ccat of P]
    J --> K[problem11: Evaluate Compression Cost Ccomp of P]
    K --> L[problem12: Compute Profit Phi of P]
    L --> M{decision: Pstar Found?}
    M -- no --> F
    M -- yes --> N[problem13: Compare Reactor Configurations PFR vs Multi Bed]
    N --> O[problem14: Select Optimum Configuration and Phi max]
    O --> P[problem15: Output Operating Window Tstar X Pstar Ns]
```

### 4.2 Process-Level Block Diagram — Multi-Bed Ammonia Converter with Interstage Cooling

The diagram below is a **process topology** representation of an industrial Haber–Bosch converter showing the recycle loop, the catalytic converter train, the heat-exchanger network, and the purge stream.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed1[Feed Gas 75 percent H2 plus 25 percent N2] --> comp1[Compressor 150 to 300 atm]
    comp1 --> mix1[Mixer with Recycle]
    recycle[Recycle Stream unreacted H2 N2] --> mix1
    mix1 --> he1[Feed Effluent Exchanger Preheat]
    he1 --> conv1[Catalytic Bed 1 Adiabatic T drops 500 to 470 C]
    conv1 --> icool1[Interstage Cooler 1]
    icool1 --> conv2[Catalytic Bed 2 T drops 460 to 430 C]
    conv2 --> icool2[Interstage Cooler 2]
    icool2 --> conv3[Catalytic Bed 3 T drops 430 to 410 C]
    conv3 --> he1
    he1 --> cooler1[Product Cooler]
    cooler1 --> sep1[High Pressure Ammonia Separator]
    sep1 --> prod1[Liquid NH3 Product]
    sep1 --> split1[Purge Stream]
    split1 --> recycle
```

### 4.3 Information Flow for the Locus-of-Maximum-Rate Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Given P and Theta] --> inputX[For each X from 0 to Xf]
    inputX --> evalRate[Evaluate r NH3 of T X P]
    evalRate --> sweepT[Sweep T over Tmin to Tmax]
    sweepT --> findTstar[Find Tstar X by d r NH3 over d T equals 0]
    findTstar --> record[Store Tstar and r NH3 max]
    record --> nextX{All X sampled?}
    nextX -- no --> inputX
    nextX -- yes --> integrate[Integrate 1 over r NH3 max to get Wmin of P]
    integrate --> outputA[Output Tstar of X curve and Wmin of P]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each rectangular node is a functional block; arrows indicate the **direction of computation or material flow**. In the first diagram, the loop from "P* Found?" back to "Loop Over Candidate Pressures" implements the iterative search for the economic optimum pressure.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **"Why is a descending temperature profile adopted along the length of an industrial ammonia converter? Justify with reference to rate and equilibrium considerations."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The ammonia synthesis reaction is **exothermic and reversible** (ΔH° = −91.8 kJ/mol). As conversion X increases along the reactor:

1. **Kinetics:** The forward reaction rate depends on the residual N₂ and H₂ partial pressures; it decreases as X rises.
2. **Equilibrium:** The equilibrium conversion X_eq(T) decreases as T increases — at high T the ceiling on X_eq is too low.
3. **Optimum:** At each X there exists a unique temperature T*(X) that maximises the net rate r_{NH₃}(T, X, P). Because the reverse term grows faster than the forward term as T rises, **T\* is a monotonically decreasing function of X**.

Hence, to **always operate at the maximum rate**, the reactor must run at high T early (where the forward driving force is large) and progressively **cool** the reacting gas (interstage cooling between adiabatic catalyst beds). This gives the "**descending temperature program**" universally used in commercial converters.

> **Marking key (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for stating the exothermic nature + equilibrium shift; 1 mark for kinetic argument; 1 mark for the locus concept and descending T.

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **"For an ammonia converter operating at fixed conversion and feed ratio, the profit function is observed to have a maximum at a finite pressure rather than at the highest allowable pressure. Explain."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Profit = (Revenue from NH₃) − (Feed cost) − (Compression cost) − (Capital cost of catalyst).

1. **Revenue and feed cost** are nearly independent of P (because X_f is fixed).
2. **Compression cost** rises **linearly** (or faster) with P — work of compression W ∝ ln(P₂/P₁) for ideal gas, and the volumetric flow at the compressor inlet is large.
3. **Catalyst cost** falls as P rises because higher equilibrium conversion permits a smaller W_min(P) (catalyst mass) for a fixed X_f.

The **sum of compression cost (rising with P) and catalyst cost (falling with P)** has a **minimum at some P***. Equivalently, profit has a **maximum at P***. Beyond P*, additional compression cost outweighs catalyst savings; below P*, the reactor becomes prohibitively large. Typical P* in industry is **150 – 250 atm**.

> **Marking key (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for compression-cost curve; 1 mark for capital-cost curve; 1 mark for the trade-off argument.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the **locus of maximum rate** for the ammonia synthesis reaction starting from the Temkin–Pyzhev rate expression. Show explicitly that T* is a monotonically decreasing function of conversion X.
>
> **Part (b) [7 marks]:** A stoichiometric feed (H₂ : N₂ = 3 : 1, no inerts) enters a PFR ammonia reactor at 200 atm and T = 480 °C. Using the equilibrium relation
> $$K_{eq}(T) = \frac{1}{P_t^{2}}\cdot\frac{4\xi^{2}(4-2\xi)^{2}}{27(1-\xi)^{4}}$$
> compute the **equilibrium conversion ξ\*** at 480 °C given K_eq(480 °C) = 1.45 × 10⁻⁵ atm⁻². Comment on the engineering implication.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Full Derivation:**

Start from the Temkin–Pyzhev form:

$$r_{NH_3} = k_1\,e^{-E_1/RT}\,\frac{P_{N_2}^{0.5}P_{H_2}^{1.5}}{P_{NH_3}} - k_2\,e^{-E_2/RT}\,\frac{P_{NH_3}}{P_{H_2}^{1.5}}$$

Define the forward driving force g_F(X,P) and reverse driving force g_R(X,P):

$$r = k_{1,0}\,e^{-E_1/RT}\,g_F(X,P) - k_{2,0}\,e^{-E_2/RT}\,g_R(X,P)$$

Differentiate r with respect to T at constant X, P:

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial T} = \frac{k_{1,0}E_1}{RT^{2}}\,e^{-E_1/RT}\,g_F - \frac{k_{2,0}E_2}{RT^{2}}\,e^{-E_2/RT}\,g_R = 0$$

Cancel 1/(RT²):

$$k_{1,0}E_1\,e^{-E_1/RT}\,g_F = k_{2,0}E_2\,e^{-E_2/RT}\,g_R$$

Rearrange:

$$e^{(E_2-E_1)/RT} = \frac{k_{2,0}E_2\,g_R}{k_{1,0}E_1\,g_F}$$

Since E₂ − E₁ = E_r − E_f = −ΔH° > 0 for exothermic:

$$\frac{-\Delta H^\circ}{RT^\star} = \ln\!\left(\frac{k_{2,0}E_2\,g_R(X,P)}{k_{1,0}E_1\,g_F(X,P)}\right)$$

Hence:

$$T^\star(X,P) = \frac{-\Delta H^\circ/R}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{2,0}E_2\,g_R(X,P)}{k_{1,0}E_1\,g_F(X,P)}\right)}$$

As X increases, g_R (proportional to P_{NH₃}) rises and g_F (proportional to P_{N₂}^{0.5}P_{H₂}^{1.5}) falls; the argument of the logarithm decreases, so T\* falls. **Hence T*(X) is monotonically decreasing** in X. ∎

> **Marking key:** 2 marks for setting up r and differentiating; 2 marks for algebraic rearrangement; 2 marks for isolating T*; 1 mark for the monotonic-decreasing conclusion.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: P_t = 200 atm, K_eq = 1.45 × 10⁻⁵ atm⁻².

Substitute into the equilibrium equation:

$$1.45\times 10^{-5}\times (200)^{2} = \frac{4\xi^{2}(4-2\xi)^{2}}{27(1-\xi)^{4}}$$

Compute the left-hand side:

$$LHS = 1.45\times 10^{-5}\times 4\times 10^{4} = 0.58$$

So:

$$0.58 = \frac{4\xi^{2}(4-2\xi)^{2}}{27(1-\xi)^{4}}$$

Solve by trial:

- Try ξ = 0.20: numerator = 4(0.04)(3.6)² = 4(0.04)(12.96) = 2.0736; denominator = 27(0.8)⁴ = 27(0.4096) = 11.0592; ratio = 0.1875 → too low.
- Try ξ = 0.30: numerator = 4(0.09)(3.4)² = 4(0.09)(11.56) = 4.1616; denominator = 27(0.7)⁴ = 27(0.2401) = 6.4827; ratio = 0.6420 → slightly high.
- Try ξ = 0.29: numerator = 4(0.0841)(3.42)² = 4(0.0841)(11.6964) ≈ 3.9344; denominator = 27(0.71)⁴ = 27(0.2541) = 6.8607; ratio = 0.5735 → very close to 0.58.
- Try ξ = 0.293: ratio ≈ 0.585 → essentially matches.

**Answer: ξ\* ≈ 0.29 (≈ 29 % equilibrium conversion at 480 °C and 200 atm).**

**Engineering implication:** Even at the high pressure of 200 atm, the equilibrium conversion is **only ~29 %** at 480 °C. This means the reactor must operate with a **large recycle of unreacted H₂ and N₂** (typical recycle ratio 4 – 6), justifying the use of **multiple catalyst beds with interstage cooling** to push the actual conversion closer to the equilibrium limit while following the descending T*(X) locus.

> **Marking key:** 3 marks for setting up the numerical equation; 2 marks for trial-and-error iteration; 2 marks for the engineering commentary.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Discuss the **influence of operating pressure** on the design and performance of an industrial ammonia reactor. Show mathematically why an economic optimum pressure exists.
>
> **Part (b) [7 marks]:** An ammonia plant produces 1,000 MTPD of liquid NH₃. The synthesis loop operates at 200 atm with a recycle ratio R = 5 (recycle flow / fresh feed flow). The single-pass conversion is 22 %. The feed is stoichiometric H₂ : N₂ = 3 : 1. Compute the **fresh feed flow rate** in kmol/h and the **catalyst volume** required if the average rate of ammonia formation over the reactor is 12 kmol m⁻³ h⁻¹.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Pressure Influence and Economic Optimum:**

The equilibrium constant expression in terms of total pressure is:

$$K_{eq}(T) = \frac{1}{P_t^{2}}\cdot\frac{4\xi^{2}(4-2\xi)^{2}}{27(1-\xi)^{4}}$$

As P_t increases at fixed T, K_eq is constant; therefore the LHS is constant and the RHS must also stay constant. Since 1/P_t² grows as P_t falls, the term in ξ must compensate by rising, which means **ξ\* rises with P_t**.

Economically, profit is:

$$\Phi(P) = (\text{Revenue}) - (\text{Feed cost}) - (\text{Compression cost}) - (\text{Catalyst cost})$$

Revenue ≈ constant (fixed production); feed cost ≈ constant. Compression cost C_comp(P) = c_w·W_comp ∝ P_t ln(P_t/P_atm); catalyst cost C_cat(P) ∝ W_min(P), and W_min(P) decreases as P rises.

$$C_{total}(P) = c_w P_t \ln(P_t/P_{atm}) + \frac{c_{cat}\,F_{N_2,0}\,X_f}{\bar r(P)}$$

Differentiate:

$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dP_t} = c_w\!\left[\ln(P_t/P_{atm}) + 1\right] + c_{cat}\,F_{N_2,0}\,X_f\,\frac{d}{dP_t}\!\left(\frac{1}{\bar r}\right) = 0$$

This yields a unique finite P* — the economic optimum pressure. ∎

> **Marking key:** 3 marks for pressure–conversion relation; 2 marks for profit decomposition; 2 marks for setting dC/dP = 0.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Production rate: 1,000 MTPD liquid NH₃.
- Molar mass of NH₃ = 17 kg/kmol.
- Recycle ratio R = 5.
- Single-pass conversion X = 0.22.
- Stoichiometric feed: H₂ : N₂ = 3 : 1.

**Step 1 — Molar production rate of NH₃:**

$$\dot n_{NH_3,\,prod} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000\ \text{kg/day}}{17\ \text{kg/kmol}\times 24\ \text{h/day}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{408} = 2451\ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Fresh feed flow rate:**

In a recycle loop, the **net** NH₃ produced equals 2 × (N₂ reacted per pass) per mole of fresh N₂. The N₂ in the fresh feed is converted over many passes, but with recycle ratio R = 5, the fresh feed composition reaches the reactor with five times the fresh flow.

Define:
- F_fresh = fresh feed flow (kmol/h, total).
- Stoichiometric ⇒ fresh N₂ = F_fresh/4, fresh H₂ = 3 F_fresh/4.
- Recycle flow = 5 × F_fresh.

Single-pass conversion X = 0.22 of N₂ in the reactor inlet. The reactor inlet is fresh + recycle = 6 F_fresh.

N₂ in reactor inlet = (1/4) × F_fresh + (N₂ in recycle). In steady state, the recycle composition is unchanged from reactor outlet, so N₂ entering per pass = F_N₂,0(reactor) = F_fresh/4 (from stoichiometry conservation at steady state with R = 5).

N₂ converted per pass = 0.22 × (F_fresh/4). NH₃ produced per pass = 2 × 0.22 × (F_fresh/4) = 0.11 × F_fresh.

In steady state, the net NH₃ produced per pass equals the fresh-feed N₂ × conversion that "escapes" the recycle loop. Equivalently, NH₃ removed from the loop per cycle = 2 × (N₂ reacted per pass per unit fresh feed). Over many cycles, the net rate out equals the per-pass production rate since one pass corresponds to one turnover of fresh feed (for recycle ratio 5, fresh feed effectively takes 6 cycles to fully react).

A simpler approach: **Net NH₃ production per unit fresh feed** = 2 × X_fresh, where X_fresh is the overall conversion of fresh N₂. For recycle ratio R, X_fresh = 1 − 1/(R+1) for complete mixing, so X_fresh = 1 − 1/6 = 0.833 (overall).

But per single pass, conversion is 22 %. Hence:

$$\dot n_{NH_3,\,prod} = \frac{\text{N₂ reacted per pass}}{\text{time per pass}} = 2\times 0.22\times \dot n_{N_2,\,reactor\,in}$$

and the **fresh-feed N₂ supply rate** is:

$$\dot n_{N_2,\,fresh} = \dot n_{N_2,\,reactor\,in}/(1 + R)\cdot(\text{steady-state dilution factor})$$

Working cleanly:

In the recycle loop at steady state, all the N₂ that ever enters eventually leaves as NH₃ (or in purge). With negligible purge, the rate of NH₃ production equals **2 × fresh N₂ feed rate**:

$$\dot n_{NH_3,\,prod} = 2\,\dot n_{N_2,\,fresh}$$

Therefore:

$$\dot n_{N_2,\,fresh} = \frac{2451}{2} = 1225.5\ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$\dot n_{H_2,\,fresh} = 3\times 1225.5 = 3676.5\ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot n_{fresh} = \dot n_{N_2,\,fresh} + \dot n_{H_2,\,fresh} = 4902\ \text{kmol/h}}$$

**Step 3 — Catalyst volume:**

Reactor inlet flow = fresh + recycle = 6 × F_fresh. N₂ in reactor inlet = (1/4) × 6 × F_fresh = 1.5 × F_fresh.

Single-pass conversion = 22 % ⇒ N₂ reacted per pass = 0.22 × 1.5 × F_fresh = 0.33 × F_fresh.

NH₃ produced per pass = 2 × 0.33 × F_fresh = 0.66 × F_fresh = 0.66 × 4902 = 3235 kmol/h.

Wait — but the **net** NH₃ production must equal 2451 kmol/h (what is actually removed). The "per-pass" production is greater because of recycle: the same molecules of N₂ react many times. With single-pass conversion 22 %, each N₂ molecule takes ~1/0.22 ≈ 4.55 passes to react fully. The net production rate is what matters for the volume calculation.

Catalyst volume from net production and average rate:

$$V_{cat} = \frac{\dot n_{NH_3,\,net}}{\bar r_{NH_3}} = \frac{2451\ \text{kmol/h}}{12\ \text{kmol/m}^{3}\,\text{h}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{cat} \approx 204\ \text{m}^{3}}$$

> **Marking key:** 3 marks for fresh-feed calculation (correct stoichiometry & recycle logic); 2 marks for catalyst volume from net production; 2 marks for engineering context.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of ΔH°** — the derivation of the descending T*(X) locus requires ΔH° < 0; writing ΔH° > 0 leads to an *increasing* T* and immediate loss of 2 – 3 marks.
> 2. **Ignoring the recycle loop** — in numerical problems with "recycle ratio," students must relate the *fresh feed* flow to the *reactor inlet* flow via (1 + R); conflating the two is the most common 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the catalyst cost vs. compression cost trade-off** — when asked "why is there an optimum pressure?", simply stating "higher P gives higher conversion" earns 1 mark; the economic argument (compression cost rising with P) is mandatory for full credit.
> 4. **Confusing forward/reverse activation energies** — recall E_r − E_f = −ΔH° > 0 for exothermic; writing the inequality backwards invalidates the maximum-rate proof.
> 5. **Unit mismatches** — in the Temkin–Pyzhev equation, partial pressures must be in **atm** (or consistent units matching the empirical k₁, k₂ values provided).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Optimal Design of an Ammonia Reactor**

- **Reaction:** N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃; **exothermic** (ΔH° ≈ −92 kJ/mol); **mole-reducing** (Δn = −2).
- **Catalyst:** Promoted iron (Fe – Al₂O₃ – K₂O – CaO); active window **400 – 500 °C**; minimum ignition temperature ≈ 400 °C.
- **Temkin–Pyzhev rate:** $r = k_1 e^{-E_1/RT}\,P_{N_2}^{0.5}P_{H_2}^{1.5}/P_{NH_3} - k_2 e^{-E_2/RT}\,P_{NH_3}/P_{H_2}^{1.5}$.
- **K_eq relation:** $d\ln K_{eq}/dT = \Delta H^\circ/RT^2 < 0$ ⇒ K_eq **decreases** with T (exothermic).
- **Optimum Temperature Locus:** T*(X, P) defined by $\partial r / \partial T \vert_{X,P} = 0$; **monotonically decreasing in X**.
- **Industrial Implementation:** Multiple **adiabatic catalyst beds** (2 – 4) with **interstage cooling** approximate the descending T*(X) curve.
- **Economic Optimum Pressure P*:** Trade-off between compression cost (rising with P) and catalyst cost (falling with P); typical P* ≈ **150 – 250 atm**.
- **Optimum H₂ : N₂ Ratio:** ≈ **2.8 – 3.2** (slight H₂ excess for kinetics + easier separation).
- **Inert Penalty:** Ar + CH₄ dilute the reactants → lower X_eq; mitigated by purge stream.
- **Reactor Type:** PFR with continuous intercooling is the theoretical optimum; in practice, **staged adiabatic PFRs with intercoolers** approximate it best.
- **Spatial Velocity (Sᵥ):** Higher Sᵥ ⇒ lower single-pass conversion ⇒ smaller reactor but larger recycle.
- **Key Equations to Memorise:**
  - $T^\star(X,P) = \dfrac{-\Delta H^\circ/R}{\ln\!\bigl(k_{2,0}E_2\,g_R/(k_{1,0}E_1\,g_F)\bigr)}$
  - $W_{\min} = F_{N_2,0}\!\displaystyle\int_0^{X_f}\dfrac{dX}{r_{NH_3}^{\max}(X,P)}$
  - $K_{eq}\,P_t^{2} = 4\xi^{2}(4-2\xi)^{2}/\bigl[27(1-\xi)^{4}\bigr]$
  - $\Phi(P)$ optimum: $\dfrac{d\Phi}{dP} = 0$ balances compression vs. catalyst cost.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Real converters operate within ~30 °C of the descending T*(X) locus; deviation penalises rate by ~ 50 % per 30 °C.
- **Common Confusions to Avoid:**
  - Higher T always helps kinetics **only at low X**; at high X, lower T helps approach the rising equilibrium ceiling.
  - Increasing P always helps equilibrium conversion; it does **not** help the rate expression's order beyond what K_eq already encodes.
  - The recycle ratio R is **dimensionless** (recycle flow / fresh feed flow), not the recycle stream's purity ratio.
- **Industrial Examples to Cite:** Haldor Topsøe radial-flow converters; ICI quench converter; Kellogg horizontal intercooled converter — all implement the T*(X)-locus principle with mechanical variation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving Chemical Engineering Optimization Problems in Excel/Python/MATLAB/Scilab or Any Optimization Software (Assignments/Microproject Only)
Chemical Product Design and Optimization: Introduction to the Current Product Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemical Product Design and Optimization: Introduction to the Current Product Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chemical Product Design** is the systematic, multidisciplinary engineering activity of conceiving, formulating, and developing chemical-based products that deliver desired performance, functionality, and customer value. It integrates chemistry, transport phenomena, thermodynamics, economics, and consumer science.

The **Current (Traditional) Product Design Process**, in the context of Process Optimization, refers to the historically established methodology in the chemical industry where the *product specification is fixed* by the customer or marketing division, and the engineer's task is reduced to **Process Design** — i.e., selecting and optimizing a manufacturing route to produce that pre-defined product at the lowest cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "Current" product design process is essentially a *reactive*, *top-down*, *specification-driven* approach. The shift toward *proactive* *need-driven* design is the modern Chemical Product Design paradigm taught in PECHT755 Module 4.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cook vs. Chef" Analogy**
> - **Traditional Product Design (Cook):** A customer says, *"Make me a vanilla cake."* The engineer/process designer (cook) optimizes the recipe — oven temperature, mixing speed, baking time — to deliver a perfect vanilla cake at minimum cost. The product is **fixed**.
> - **Modern Product Design (Chef):** The chef first asks, *"What do you want? A dessert? A birthday celebration? A low-calorie snack?"* Then *invents* a new product (e.g., a yogurt-mousse with strawberry swirl) to match the *unmet need*. The product is **invented**.

The traditional approach is analogous to **optimizing a known route on Google Maps**, whereas modern product design is **deciding the destination itself** before plotting the route.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Product Lifecycle Time (Industrial Average):** 1–5 years for chemicals, **5–15 years** for pharmaceuticals.
- **Stages of Design:** Typically **5** distinct stages.
- **R&D Spend Benchmark:** Pharmaceutical industry invests **15–20%** of revenue in R&D; commodity chemicals only **0.5–2%**.

## 1.4 KTU Course Outcome Alignment

| CO Code | Course Outcome (Truncated) | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) |
|---------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| **CO4** | Design chemical products using structured methodology | Apply / Analyze |
| **CO5** | Optimize product formulations and processes | Evaluate / Create |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Current" (Traditional) Product Design Process — Decomposition

The traditional chemical industry workflow can be decomposed into **four sequential logical stages**:

1. **Market Specification Stage (Marketing-Driven)**
   - Marketing/sales force surveys the market.
   - A *product specification sheet* is generated: purity, color, particle size, melting point, toxicity limits, packaging, etc.
   - The specifications are handed to R&D/engineering as a "design brief."

2. **Process Synthesis Stage**
   - Chemical engineers select a *reaction pathway* (thermodynamically and kinetically feasible).
   - Choose raw materials, catalysts, solvents, and operating conditions (T, P, conversion).

3. **Process Optimization & Detailed Engineering Stage**
   - Apply *unit operation* design: reactors, separators, heat exchangers.
   - Economic optimization: minimize **Total Annual Cost (TAC)**.
   - Safety, environmental, and control systems are integrated.

4. **Plant Construction, Commissioning, and Operation**
   - Translate design into hardware.
   - Operate, troubleshoot, and incrementally debottleneck.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Limitation of the Current Approach:** The engineer has **no control** over the *product itself* — only over the *process*. Innovation is therefore confined to incremental manufacturing efficiency, **not** to new value creation.

## 2.2 Why a "New" Approach is Needed

The traditional model fails for:
- **High-value differentiated products** (cosmetics, paints, pharmaceuticals, food) where *function* and *consumer experience* dominate over purity.
- **Fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG)** where the *product form* is the value proposition.
- **Customized chemicals** (specialty/intermediate chemicals) where the customer needs *performance*, not molecules.

## 2.3 The Need-Driven Product Design Paradigm (Cussler–Moggridge)

The modern approach (introduced by **Cussler & Moggridge**) restructures design into **5 stages**:

| Stage | Name | Output |
|-------|------|--------|
| 1 | **Needs** | Customer need translated into a chemical engineering specification |
| 2 | **Ideas** | Multiple candidate product concepts generated |
| 3 | **Selection** | Best concept chosen using screening matrices |
| 4 | **Manufacture** | Process designed to make the chosen product |
| 5 | **Sales** | Product launched with iteration feedback |

> [!NOTE]
> The **last three stages** of modern design (Selection, Manufacture, Sales) overlap with the **traditional process design** — but the **first two stages (Needs, Ideas)** are the new, value-creating engine.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Equation | Units | KTU Relevance |
|---------------|----------------------|-------|----------------|
| $\text{TAC}$ | Total Annualized Cost $= C_{\text{cap}} \cdot f + C_{\text{op}}$ | $\$/\text{yr}$ | Process optimization |
| $f$ | Capital recovery factor $= \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $\text{yr}^{-1}$ | Economic evaluation |
| $C_{\text{cap}}$ | Total capital investment | $\$$ | Stage 4 (Manufacture) |
| $C_{\text{op}}$ | Annual operating cost | $\$/\text{yr}$ | Stage 4 (Manufacture) |
| $\text{NPV}$ | Net Present Value $= \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | $\$$ | Project viability |
| $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ | Overall process efficiency | dimensionless | Process design |
| $N_{\text{stag}}$ | Number of screening criteria | dimensionless | Stage 3 (Selection) |
| $W_i$ | Weight of screening criterion $i$ | dimensionless | Stage 3 (Selection) |
| $S_j$ | Score of candidate $j$ on criterion $i$ | $0$–$10$ | Stage 3 (Selection) |
| $\text{Score}_{\text{total},j}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \cdot S_{i,j}$ | dimensionless | Weighted decision matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Math Safety Rule:** In markdown tables, the pipe character `|` inside equations (such as absolute value notation) **breaks the table parser**. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ in any tabular environment.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharma:** Designing a sustained-release tablet (product) vs. designing the reactor for the active ingredient (process).
- **Paints & Coatings:** Balancing pigment volume concentration, binder chemistry, and rheology — pure *product* design.
- **Food Industry:** Designing a low-fat ice cream with desired mouthfeel — a *product* problem solved through *emulsion* engineering.
- **Detergents:** Choosing surfactant blends for hard water — a *formulation* optimization problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Total Annual Cost (TAC) Function for Process Selection

The **Total Annualized Cost** is the cornerstone of any process optimization decision. It must be derived carefully because it forms the basis for screening economic feasibility of any manufacturing route.

**Step 1 — Define Capital Recovery:**
The capital cost $C_{\text{cap}}$ is a one-time investment at $t = 0$. To annualize it over a project lifetime of $n$ years at an interest rate $i$, we use the capital recovery factor $f$:

$$
f = \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Annualized Capital Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{cap, annual}} = f \cdot C_{\text{cap}}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Operating Cost:**
The annual operating cost $C_{\text{op}}$ includes raw materials, utilities, labor, maintenance, and consumables. It is treated as a uniform annual cash flow:

$$
C_{\text{op, annual}} = C_{\text{op}}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine into Total Annual Cost:**

$$
\text{TAC} = f \cdot C_{\text{cap}} + C_{\text{op}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
The engineer minimizes TAC by trading off **higher capital** (cheaper to operate) against **lower capital** (more expensive to operate). The optimum lies where:

$$
\frac{\partial \, \text{TAC}}{\partial X_k} = 0
$$

where $X_k$ represents any design variable (e.g., reactor volume, heat exchanger area, conversion).

**Step 6 — Worked Example:**
Consider two process alternatives A and B:
