<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Genetic Algorithms and Particle Swarm Optimization (Algorithm Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT755 - PROCESS OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Genetic Algorithms and Particle Swarm Optimization (Algorithm Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Genetic Algorithms (GA)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a stochastic, population-based metaheuristic optimization technique inspired by the mechanisms of natural selection and biological evolution. It operates on a population of candidate solutions encoded as chromosomes, applying the Darwinian principles of **selection**, **crossover (recombination)**, and **mutation** to iteratively evolve better solutions to a target objective function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** GA belongs to the class of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)**. It is derivative-free, globally convergent (probabilistically), and is extensively used in KTU Process Optimization for solving non-convex, multimodal, and discontinuous problems in chemical engineering design.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a population of 100 different recipes (solutions) for a cake. You taste (evaluate fitness) each one. You keep only the best-tasting recipes (selection), randomly mix ingredients between the best two (crossover), and occasionally randomly alter an ingredient (mutation). Over many generations, your recipe population improves — the cakes become tastier. This is exactly how a GA works: it "breeds" better solutions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Father of GA:** John Holland (1975), with formalization by David Goldberg (1989).

### Standard GA Parameters to Remember

| Parameter | Typical Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Population Size (N)** | **20 – 200** | Number of candidate solutions per generation |
| **Crossover Probability (Pc)** | **0.6 – 0.9** | Fraction of population undergoing recombination |
| **Mutation Probability (Pm)** | **0.001 – 0.1** | Probability of bit/gene alteration |
| **Number of Generations** | **50 – 500** | Termination criterion |

---

## 1.2 Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
**Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** is a population-based stochastic optimization technique developed by **Kennedy and Eberhart (1995)**, inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling. Each particle in the swarm represents a candidate solution that moves through the search space, guided by its own best-known position (**pbest**) and the swarm's best-known position (**gbest**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** PSO is simpler than GA — it has no crossover or mutation operators. Movement is governed purely by velocity update equations. It is highly effective for continuous nonlinear optimization problems in process engineering.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a flock of birds searching for food in a foggy field. Each bird remembers its own closest approach to the food (**pbest**). The flock as a whole remembers the closest any bird has ever come (**gbest**). Every bird adjusts its flight direction and speed based on: (1) its own memory, (2) the flock's memory, and (3) its current momentum. Gradually, the entire swarm converges to the food source — the optimum.

### Key Standard Constants in PSO

| Constant | Typical Value | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Inertia Weight (w)** | **0.4 – 0.9** | Controls momentum of particle |
| **Cognitive Coefficient (c1)** | **1.0 – 2.0** | Pull toward pbest |
| **Social Coefficient (c2)** | **1.0 – 2.0** | Pull toward gbest |
| **r1, r2** | **U(0,1)** | Uniform random numbers for stochasticity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Genetic Algorithm — Operational Phases

The GA proceeds in **five well-defined stages**:

### Phase 1: Encoding (Representation)
- Candidate solution $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is mapped to a chromosome string.
- **Binary Encoding:** $x$ is represented as a string of 0s and 1s. A real variable $x \in [x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ is encoded using:
$$x = x_{\min} + \frac{(x_{\max} - x_{\min})}{2^{L} - 1} \cdot \text{decimal value of binary string}$$
where **L** is the chromosome length (number of bits per variable).
- **Real-valued Encoding:** Each gene is a floating-point number. Used in modern chemical engineering applications.

### Phase 2: Population Initialization
- Generate **N** random chromosomes either by:
  - **Random Initialization** — uniform sampling across bounds.
  - **Seeded Initialization** — heuristic-based starting points to accelerate convergence.

### Phase 3: Fitness Function Evaluation
- The objective function $f(x)$ is converted to a fitness measure.
- For **minimization** problems, common transformation:
$$\text{Fitness}(x) = \frac{1}{1 + f(x) - f_{\min}}$$
where $f_{\min}$ is the smallest objective value in the current population.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Maximization:** Fitness $= f(x)$ directly. For minimization, GA minimizes $f(x)$ but fitness is defined inversely so that *better* solutions have *higher* fitness.

### Phase 4: Selection Mechanism
Parents are chosen with probability proportional to fitness. Common methods:

| Method | Mechanism | Selection Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| **Roulette Wheel** | Probability $\propto$ fitness | Moderate |
| **Tournament Selection** | Pick k, choose best among them | High (k large) |
| **Rank-Based** | Probability $\propto$ rank | Adjustable |
| **Elitism** | Best chromosome copied unchanged | Preserves best |

**Roulette Wheel Probability:**
$$P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$$

### Phase 5: Crossover (Recombination)
Combines two parents to produce offspring. Types:

**(a) Single-Point Crossover:** Choose cut point $k \in [1, L-1]$ and swap tails.

$$\text{Parent 1: } 1\,0\,1\,|\,1\,0\,0 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Offspring 1: } 1\,0\,1\,|\,0\,1\,1$$
$$\text{Parent 2: } 0\,1\,0\,|\,0\,1\,1 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Offspring 2: } 0\,1\,0\,|\,1\,0\,0$$

**(b) Two-Point Crossover:** Two cut points; middle segment swapped.

**(c) Uniform Crossover:** Each gene independently chosen from either parent with $P = 0.5$.

**(d) Arithmetic (Real-Coded) Crossover:**
$$\text{Offspring} = \alpha \cdot \text{Parent}_1 + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{Parent}_2, \quad \alpha \in U(0,1)$$

### Phase 6: Mutation
Introduces diversity. Types:

- **Bit-Flip Mutation (Binary):** Flip each bit with probability $P_m$.
- **Gaussian Mutation (Real-Coded):**
$$x_i' = x_i + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$
- **Polynomial Mutation:** Often used in NSGA-II.

### Phase 7: Termination
GA stops when:
- Maximum generations reached
- No improvement over last $k$ generations
- Function evaluation budget exhausted
- Convergence criterion: $\sigma_f < \epsilon$

---

## 2.2 Particle Swarm Optimization — Operational Phases

PSO evolves a swarm of **M particles** in a **D-dimensional** search space. Each particle $i$ has:
- **Position vector:** $\vec{X}_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{iD})$
- **Velocity vector:** $\vec{V}_i = (v_{i1}, v_{i2}, \ldots, v_{iD})$
- **Personal best:** $\vec{P}_{\text{best},i}$
- **Global best:** $\vec{G}_{\text{best}}$

### PSO Core Update Equations (THE most important formulas)

**Velocity Update:**
$$v_{id}^{\text{new}} = w \cdot v_{id}^{\text{old}} + c_1 r_1 (p_{\text{best},id} - x_{id}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{\text{best},d} - x_{id})$$

**Position Update:**
$$x_{id}^{\text{new}} = x_{id}^{\text{old}} + v_{id}^{\text{new}}$$

Where:
- **w** = inertia weight (momentum term)
- **c1** = cognitive coefficient (self-confidence)
- **c2** = social coefficient (swarm confidence)
- **r1, r2** = uniform random numbers in [0,1]
- **d** = dimension index
- **i** = particle index

### Velocity Clamping (Critical for Stability)
$$v_{id} = \min(v_{\max}, \max(-v_{\max}, v_{id}))$$
where $v_{\max} = k \cdot (x_{\max} - x_{\min})/2$, typically $k \in [0.1, 1.0]$.

### Inertia Weight Strategies
- **Constant:** $w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.49618$ (Clerc & Kennedy constriction factor analysis)
- **Linear Decreasing:**
$$w(t) = w_{\max} - \frac{(w_{\max} - w_{\min}) \cdot t}{t_{\max}}$$
with $w_{\max} = 0.9$, $w_{\min} = 0.4$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constriction Coefficient Method:** Replaces $w$ with $\chi = \frac{2k}{\lvert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi(\phi - 4)} \rvert}$ where $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$, $k \in [0,1]$. Guarantees convergence.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Algorithm | Equation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GA Encoding** | $x = x_{\min} + \frac{(x_{\max} - x_{\min})}{2^{L}-1} \cdot D$ | Decode binary → real |
| 2 | **GA Fitness (min)** | $F = \frac{1}{1 + f(x) - f_{\min}}$ | Convert objective to fitness |
| 3 | **Roulette Wheel** | $P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum f_j}$ | Selection probability |
| 4 | **Arithmetic Crossover** | $x' = \alpha x_1 + (1-\alpha) x_2$ | Real-coded recombination |
| 5 | **Gaussian Mutation** | $x' = x + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Real-coded mutation |
| 6 | **PSO Velocity** | $v = w v + c_1 r_1 (p-x) + c_2 r_2 (g-x)$ | Update velocity |
| 7 | **PSO Position** | $x^{\text{new}} = x^{\text{old}} + v^{\text{new}}$ | Update position |
| 8 | **PSO Inertia (linear)** | $w(t) = w_{\max} - \frac{(w_{\max} - w_{\min}) t}{t_{\max}}$ | Decay strategy |
| 9 | **PSO Convergence** | $v_{id} \in [-v_{\max}, v_{\max}]$ | Stability clamp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility (GA):** Parameter estimation in kinetic models, optimal reactor design, distillation column sequencing, process scheduling, PID tuning, heat exchanger network synthesis.
>
> **Real-world Engineering Utility (PSO):** Optimal control of CSTRs, ANN training for process prediction, parameter estimation of non-convex models, supply chain optimization, energy minimization in separation processes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix: GA vs PSO

| Feature | **Genetic Algorithm** | **Particle Swarm Optimization** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Holland (1975) | Kennedy & Eberhart (1995) |
| **Inspiration** | Darwinian evolution | Bird flocking / fish schooling |
| **Population** | Chromosomes (individuals) | Particles |
| **Solution Type** | Discrete / continuous | Primarily continuous |
| **Operators** | Selection, Crossover, Mutation | Velocity update, Position update |
| **Memory** | Implicit (elitism) | Explicit (pbest, gbest) |
| **Information Sharing** | Through population recombination | gbest broadcasts to all |
| **Parameters** | $P_c, P_m, N$ | $w, c_1, c_2, M$ |
| **Convergence** | Slower but robust exploration | Faster convergence |
| **Best For** | Combinatorial / discrete problems | Continuous nonlinear problems |
| **Derivative Required?** | No | No |
| **Handbook Equation Count** | 5+ (encoding, fitness, selection, etc.) | 2 core update equations |

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivations for KTU 14-Mark Solutions

### Derivation 1: GA Binary Decoding for a Single Variable

**Problem Setup:** Decode a binary chromosome `10110` of length $L = 5$ representing $x \in [0, 32]$.

**Step 1:** Compute decimal value of the binary string.

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{binary}} &= 10110_2 \\
&= 1 \cdot 2^4 + 0 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 \\
&= 16 + 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 \\
&= 22
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the decoding formula with $x_{\min} = 0$, $x_{\max} = 32$.

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= x_{\min} + \frac{(x_{\max} - x_{\min})}{2^L - 1} \cdot D \\
&= 0 + \frac{(32 - 0)}{2^5 - 1} \cdot 22 \\
&= \frac{32}{31} \cdot 22 \\
&= 1.0323 \cdot 22 \\
&= 22.71
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution (precision) per chromosome:** $\Delta x = \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^L - 1} = \frac{32}{31} \approx 1.0323$

---

### Derivation 2: Roulette Wheel Selection Probability

**Problem Setup:** Population of 5 chromosomes with fitness values $f = [10, 25, 15, 30, 20]$. Determine selection probability for each.

**Step 1:** Sum all fitness values.

$$\sum_{j=1}^{5} f_j = 10 + 25 + 15 + 30 + 20 = 100$$

**Step 2:** Apply $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 10/100 = 0.10 \\
P_2 &= 25/100 = 0.25 \\
P_3 &= 15/100 = 0.15 \\
P_4 &= 30/100 = 0.30 \\
P_5 &= 20/100 = 0.20
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Verify cumulative probability.

$$\sum P_i = 0.10 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Chromosome 4 has the highest fitness (30) and is 3× more likely to be selected than Chromosome 1.

---

### Derivation 3: Single-Point Crossover Mechanics

**Problem Setup:** 
- Parent 1: `1 0 1 1 0 0 1`
- Parent 2: `0 1 0 0 1 1 0`
- Crossover point: $k = 4$

**Step 1:** Identify cut point and split chromosomes.

$$\text{Parent 1: } \underbrace{1\,0\,1\,1}_{\text{Head (1-4)}} \vert \underbrace{0\,0\,1}_{\text{Tail (5-7)}}$$
$$\text{Parent 2: } \underbrace{0\,1\,0\,0}_{\text{Head (1-4)}} \vert \underbrace{1\,1\,0}_{\text{Tail (5-7)}}$$

**Step 2:** Swap tails to produce offspring.

$$\text{Offspring 1: } 1\,0\,1\,1 \vert 1\,1\,0 \quad = \text{`1011110`}$$
$$\text{Offspring 2: } 0\,1\,0\,0 \vert 0\,0\,1 \quad = \text{`0100001`}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show the cut point location explicitly. Examiners give partial credit for correct identification of head/tail segments.

---

### Derivation 4: PSO Velocity and Position Update (Single Iteration)

**Problem Setup:** 
- 2D problem ($D = 2$)
- Particle $i$ current position: $X_i = (3.0, 5.0)$
- Particle $i$ current velocity: $V_i = (0.5, -0.3)$
- Personal best: $P_{\text{best},i} = (2.5, 5.5)$
- Global best: $G_{\text{best}} = (2.0, 6.0)$
- $w = 0.7$, $c_1 = 1.5$, $c_2 = 1.5$, $r_1 = 0.4$, $r_2 = 0.8$

**Step 1:** Compute new velocity in dimension $d = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{i1}^{\text{new}} &= w \cdot v_{i1}^{\text{old}} + c_1 r_1 (p_{\text{best},i1} - x_{i1}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{\text{best},1} - x_{i1}) \\
&= 0.7 \cdot 0.5 + 1.5 \cdot 0.4 \cdot (2.5 - 3.0) + 1.5 \cdot 0.8 \cdot (2.0 - 3.0) \\
&= 0.35 + 0.6 \cdot (-0.5) + 1.2 \cdot (-1.0) \\
&= 0.35 - 0.30 - 1.20 \\
&= -1.15
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute new velocity in dimension $d = 2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{i2}^{\text{new}} &= w \cdot v_{i2}^{\text{old}} + c_1 r_1 (p_{\text{best},i2} - x_{i2}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{\text{best},2} - x_{i2}) \\
&= 0.7 \cdot (-0.3) + 1.5 \cdot 0.4 \cdot (5.5 - 5.0) + 1.5 \cdot 0.8 \cdot (6.0 - 5.0) \\
&= -0.21 + 0.6 \cdot 0.5 + 1.2 \cdot 1.0 \\
&= -0.21 + 0.30 + 1.20 \\
&= 1.29
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Update position.

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{i1}^{\text{new}} &= x_{i1}^{\text{old}} + v_{i1}^{\text{new}} = 3.0 + (-1.15) = 1.85 \\
x_{i2}^{\text{new}} &= x_{i2}^{\text{old}} + v_{i2}^{\text{new}} = 5.0 + 1.29 = 6.29
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Verify with velocity clamping (assume $v_{\max} = 2.0$). Both values within bounds, so no clamping needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The particle has moved toward both pbest and gbest simultaneously, with stronger pull toward gbest (social dominance) since $c_2 r_2 > c_1 r_1$ in dimension 1.

---

### Derivation 5: Linear Decreasing Inertia Weight

**Problem Setup:** $w_{\max} = 0.9$, $w_{\min} = 0.4$, $t_{\max} = 100$ iterations. Find $w$ at $t = 25, 50, 75$.

**Step 1:** Apply formula $w(t) = w_{\max} - \frac{(w_{\max} - w_{\min}) \cdot t}{t_{\max}}$.

**At $t = 25$:**

$$w(25) = 0.9 - \frac{(0.9 - 0.4) \cdot 25}{100} = 0.9 - \frac{0.5 \cdot 25}{100} = 0.9 - 0.125 = 0.775$$

**At $t = 50$:**

$$w(50) = 0.9 - \frac{0.5 \cdot 50}{100} = 0.9 - 0.25 = 0.650$$

**At $t = 75$:**

$$w(75) = 0.9 - \frac{0.5 \cdot 75}{100} = 0.9 - 0.375 = 0.525$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategy Rationale:** High $w$ (early) = global exploration. Low $w$ (late) = local exploitation. This emulates annealing behavior in PSO.

---

## 3.3 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Algorithm-Only Specification — No Programming Language)

### GA Pseudocode

```
BEGIN GeneticAlgorithm
    Initialize population P(t=0) of N random chromosomes
    Evaluate fitness of each chromosome
    WHILE termination criterion NOT met DO
        t = t + 1
        Select parents from P(t-1) using selection operator
        Apply crossover to selected parents with probability Pc
        Apply mutation to offspring with probability Pm
        Evaluate fitness of new offspring
        Form new population P(t) using elitism/replacement
    END WHILE
    RETURN best chromosome found
END GeneticAlgorithm
```

### PSO Pseudocode

```
BEGIN ParticleSwarmOptimization
    Initialize M particles with random positions X_i and velocities V_i
    Evaluate objective f(X_i) for each particle
    Set pbest_i = X_i
    Set gbest = argmin f(X_i) over all i
    WHILE termination criterion NOT met DO
        FOR each particle i DO
            Generate random r1, r2 ~ U(0,1)
            Update velocity:
                V_i = w*V_i + c1*r1*(pbest_i - X_i) + c2*r2*(gbest - X_i)
            Apply velocity clamping: V_i in [-vmax, vmax]
            Update position: X_i = X_i + V_i
            Evaluate f(X_i)
            IF f(X_i) < f(pbest_i) THEN
                pbest_i = X_i
            END IF
        END FOR
        Update gbest = argmin f(pbest_i) over all i
    END WHILE
    RETURN gbest
END ParticleSwarmOptimization
```

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Genetic Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start GA] --> B[Initialize Population P t=0]
    B --> C[Encode Decision Variables as Chromosomes]
    C --> D[Evaluate Fitness Function f x]
    D --> E{Termination Criterion Met?}
    E -- No --> F[Apply Selection Operator]
    F --> G[Apply Crossover with Probability Pc]
    G --> H[Apply Mutation with Probability Pm]
    H --> I[Evaluate Offspring Fitness]
    I --> J[Form New Population P t+1 with Elitism]
    J --> E
    E -- Yes --> K[Return Best Solution]
    K --> L[End]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style K fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style L fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Particle Swarm Optimization Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start PSO] --> B[Initialize M Particles]
    B --> B1[Random Positions X_i]
    B --> B2[Random Velocities V_i]
    B1 --> C[Evaluate Objective f X_i]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Set pbest_i = X_i]
    D --> E[Set gbest = argmin f pbest_i]
    E --> F{Termination Criterion Met?}
    F -- No --> G[For Each Particle i]
    G --> H[Generate r1, r2 from U 0,1]
    H --> I[Update Velocity V_i]
    I --> J[Apply Velocity Clipping]
    J --> K[Update Position X_i]
    K --> L[Evaluate f X_i]
    L --> M{f X_i < f pbest_i?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Update pbest_i = X_i]
    M -- No --> O[Retain Old pbest_i]
    N --> P[Update Global gbest]
    O --> P
    P --> F
    F -- Yes --> Q[Return gbest as Optimal Solution]
    Q --> R[End]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style Q fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style R fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**
**Explain the role of the crossover operator in a Genetic Algorithm. Mention any two types of crossover.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Crossover (recombination) is the primary exploration operator in a GA. It combines genetic material from two parent chromosomes to produce offspring, mimicking biological reproduction. By swapping segments of two high-fitness parents, crossover exploits promising regions of the search space and propagates beneficial "building blocks" (schemata) to subsequent generations.

**Two types of crossover:**
1. **Single-point Crossover:** A cut point $k$ is selected; tails of the two parents are swapped.
2. **Uniform Crossover:** Each gene of the offspring is independently chosen from either parent with $P = 0.5$.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Pattern]**
**Write the velocity and position update equations of PSO. What is the role of inertia weight $w$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Velocity Update:**
$$v_{id}^{\text{new}} = w v_{id}^{\text{old}} + c_1 r_1 (p_{\text{best},id} - x_{id}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{\text{best},d} - x_{id})$$

**Position Update:**
$$x_{id}^{\text{new}} = x_{id}^{\text{old}} + v_{id}^{\text{new}}$$

**Role of inertia weight $w$:**
The inertia weight $w$ controls the influence of the previous velocity on the new velocity. A **large $w$** promotes **global exploration** (particles fly broadly), while a **small $w$** promotes **local exploitation** (particles converge near best positions). A linearly decreasing $w$ from **0.9 to 0.4** balances exploration and exploitation across iterations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Genetic Algorithms

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Pattern]**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
**(i)** Describe the five main steps of a Genetic Algorithm with a neat flowchart.

**(ii)** Explain the Roulette Wheel selection method and compute selection probabilities for a population with fitness values $[15, 25, 10, 30, 20]$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
Perform single-point crossover on the following parent chromosomes with crossover point $k = 3$. Show all steps clearly.
- Parent 1: `1 0 1 1 0 0 1`
- Parent 2: `0 1 0 0 1 1 0`

Also apply bit-flip mutation with $P_m = 0.25$ on Offspring 1.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) (i) — Five Steps of GA [3.5 marks]:**

1. **Encoding** — Represent decision variables as chromosomes (binary or real-valued).
2. **Population Initialization** — Generate N random chromosomes within variable bounds.
3. **Fitness Evaluation** — Compute objective function value for each chromosome.
4. **Genetic Operations** — Apply selection, crossover (Pc), and mutation (Pm).
5. **Replacement & Termination** — Form new generation using elitism; stop when convergence/maximum generations is reached.

(Refer to SECTION 4.1 flowchart for diagram — award 1.5 marks for flowchart.)

**Part (a) (ii) — Roulette Wheel Selection [3.5 marks]:**

Sum of fitness values:

$$\sum f_i = 15 + 25 + 10 + 30 + 20 = 100$$

Probability $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$:

| Chromosome $i$ | Fitness $f_i$ | Probability $P_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15 | 0.15 |
| 2 | 25 | 0.25 |
| 3 | 10 | 0.10 |
| 4 | 30 | 0.30 |
| 5 | 20 | 0.20 |
| **Sum** | **100** | **1.00** |

Chromosome 4 has the highest probability (0.30) of being selected.

**Part (b) — Single-Point Crossover [5 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Identify cut point $k = 3$.

$$\text{Parent 1: } \underbrace{1\,0\,1}_{\text{Position 1-3}} \vert \underbrace{1\,0\,0\,1}_{\text{Position 4-7}}$$

$$\text{Parent 2: } \underbrace{0\,1\,0}_{\text{Position 1-3}} \vert \underbrace{0\,1\,1\,0}_{\text{Position 4-7}}$$

**Step 2:** Swap tails to produce offspring.

$$\text{Offspring 1: } 1\,0\,1\,\vert\,0\,1\,1\,0 \quad = \text{`1010110`}$$

$$\text{Offspring 2: } 0\,1\,0\,\vert\,1\,0\,0\,1 \quad = \text{`0101001`}$$

**Step 3:** Apply bit-flip mutation on Offspring 1 (`1 0 1 0 1 1 0`) with $P_m = 0.25$.

Generate random numbers for each bit position. Assuming $r = [0.18, 0.62, 0.09, 0.81, 0.31, 0.45, 0.77]$:

- Position 1: $r = 0.18 < 0.25$ → **FLIP** → 0
- Position 2: $r = 0.62 > 0.25$ → No change → 0
- Position 3: $r = 0.09 < 0.25$ → **FLIP** → 0
- Position 4: $r = 0.81 > 0.25$ → No change → 0
- Position 5: $r = 0.31 > 0.25$ → No change → 1
- Position 6: $r = 0.45 > 0.25$ → No change → 1
- Position 7: $r = 0.77 > 0.25$ → No change → 0

**Mutated Offspring 1: `0 0 0 0 1 1 0`** [2 marks for mutation steps]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Particle Swarm Optimization

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Pattern]**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
**(i)** Explain the Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm with a flowchart. Define all symbols used in the velocity and position update equations.

**(ii)** Describe the role of the inertia weight $w$. Explain the linearly decreasing inertia weight strategy with $w_{\max} = 0.9$ and $w_{\min} = 0.4$ for a maximum of 100 iterations. Compute $w$ at $t = 30$ and $t = 80$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
For a 2D minimization problem, a particle has:
- Current position: $X = (4.0, 2.0)$
- Current velocity: $V = (0.8, -0.4)$
- $p_{\text{best}} = (3.5, 1.5)$, $g_{\text{best}} = (3.0, 1.0)$
- $w = 0.6$, $c_1 = 1.5$, $c_2 = 1.5$, $r_1 = 0.5$, $r_2 = 0.7$

Calculate the new velocity and new position. Take $v_{\max} = 2.0$.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) (i) — PSO Algorithm [3.5 marks]:**

PSO is a population-based metaheuristic where each particle represents a candidate solution. The swarm moves through the search space guided by:
- $p_{\text{best},i}$ — best position found by particle $i$
- $g_{\text{best}}$ — best position found by entire swarm

Velocity and position update:

$$v_{id}^{\text{new}} = w v_{id}^{\text{old}} + c_1 r_1 (p_{\text{best},id} - x_{id}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{\text{best},d} - x_{id})$$

$$x_{id}^{\text{new}} = x_{id}^{\text{old}} + v_{id}^{\text{new}}$$

**Symbol definitions:**
- $w$ = inertia weight
- $c_1$ = cognitive coefficient (self-confidence)
- $c_2$ = social coefficient (swarm confidence)
- $r_1, r_2$ = uniform random numbers in $[0,1]$
- $d$ = dimension index
- $i$ = particle index

(Refer to SECTION 4.2 flowchart for diagram — award 1.5 marks for flowchart.)

**Part (a) (ii) — Inertia Weight Strategy [3.5 marks]:**

The inertia weight $w$ balances exploration (high $w$) and exploitation (low $w$).

**Formula:**
$$w(t) = w_{\max} - \frac{(w_{\max} - w_{\min}) \cdot t}{t_{\max}}$$

**At $t = 30$:**

$$w(30) = 0.9 - \frac{(0.9 - 0.4) \cdot 30}{100} = 0.9 - \frac{0.5 \cdot 30}{100} = 0.9 - 0.15 = 0.75$$

**At $t = 80$:**

$$w(80) = 0.9 - \frac{0.5 \cdot 80}{100} = 0.9 - 0.40 = 0.50$$

**Part (b) — Velocity and Position Update [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Update velocity in dimension $d = 1$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_1^{\text{new}} &= w v_1^{\text{old}} + c_1 r_1 (p_{\text{best},1} - x_1) + c_2 r_2 (g_{\text{best},1} - x_1) \\
&= 0.6 \cdot 0.8 + 1.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (3.5 - 4.0) + 1.5 \cdot 0.7 \cdot (3.0 - 4.0) \\
&= 0.48 + 0.75 \cdot (-0.5) + 1.05 \cdot (-1.0) \\
&= 0.48 - 0.375 - 1.05 \\
&= -0.945
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Update velocity in dimension $d = 2$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_2^{\text{new}} &= w v_2^{\text{old}} + c_1 r_1 (p_{\text{best},2} - x_2) + c_2 r_2 (g_{\text{best},2} - x_2) \\
&= 0.6 \cdot (-0.4) + 1.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1.5 - 2.0) + 1.5 \cdot 0.7 \cdot (1.0 - 2.0) \\
&= -0.24 + 0.75 \cdot (-0.5) + 1.05 \cdot (-1.0) \\
&= -0.24 - 0.375 - 1.05 \\
&= -1.665
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply velocity clamping ($v_{\max} = 2.0$).**

Both $|v_1| = 0.945$ and $|v_2| = 1.665$ are within $[-2.0, 2.0]$. **No clamping required.**

**Step 4: Update position.**

$$x_1^{\text{new}} = x_1^{\text{old}} + v_1^{\text{new}} = 4.0 + (-0.945) = 3.055$$

$$x_2^{\text{new}} = x_2^{\text{old}} + v_2^{\text{new}} = 2.0 + (-1.665) = 0.335$$

**Final Result:** $X^{\text{new}} = (3.055, 0.335)$, $V^{\text{new}} = (-0.945, -1.665)$ [3 marks for position update, 1 mark for clamping verification].

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to apply velocity clamping after update. *Loss: 1 mark per question.*
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the order in PSO update — velocity **must be computed first**, then position. Reversing the order yields wrong answers.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In GA, students often forget that **crossover probability $P_c$** applies to *pairs* of parents, not individual genes. Read questions carefully.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Using $P_m$ on every gene independently — this is correct for bit-flip mutation but is a frequent misunderstanding with uniform mutation.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to show intermediate steps in fitness probability calculation. Always show $\sum f_i$ and individual $P_i$ explicitly.
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** Forgetting to update $p_{\text{best}}$ and $g_{\text{best}}$ after each iteration — these updates are essential for PSO convergence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Genetic Algorithm — Quick Revision Checklist

- [x] **GA is derivative-free**, suitable for non-convex and discontinuous problems
- [x] Five core steps: **Encoding → Initialization → Fitness → Operators → Replacement**
- [x] Three operators: **Selection, Crossover, Mutation**
- [x] Roulette wheel probability: $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$
- [x] Single-point crossover: cut at position $k$, swap tails
- [x] Elitism preserves the best chromosome across generations
- [x] Typical $P_c = 0.6$–$0.9$, $P_m = 0.001$–$0.1$
- [x] Binary decoding formula: $x = x_{\min} + (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) \cdot D / (2^L - 1)$
- [x] GA is inspired by **Darwinian evolution** (Holland, 1975)
- [x] Application areas: reactor design, parameter estimation, scheduling

### Particle Swarm Optimization — Quick Revision Checklist

- [x] PSO is simpler than GA — no crossover/mutation
- [x] Two update equations: **velocity and position**
- [x] Three components in velocity: **inertia, cognitive, social**
- [x] $w$ = inertia (momentum); $c_1$ = cognitive (self); $c_2$ = social (swarm)
- [x] $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ ensure stochasticity
- [x] Velocity clamping essential for stability: $v \in [-v_{\max}, v_{\max}]$
- [x] Linearly decreasing $w$: $w(t) = w_{\max} - (w_{\max} - w_{\min}) \cdot t / t_{\max}$
- [x] Typical values: $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$, $c_1, c_2 \in [1.0, 2.0]$
- [x] Constriction coefficient $\chi = 0.7298$ guarantees convergence
- [x] PSO inspired by **bird flocking** (Kennedy & Eberhart, 1995)
- [x] Application areas: continuous process optimization, ANN training, control tuning

### Key Differences to Memorize for KTU Viva

| Aspect | GA | PSO |
|---|---|---|
| Operators | Selection + Crossover + Mutation | Only velocity/position update |
| Memory | No explicit memory | pbest + gbest |
| Best Problem Type | Combinatorial/Discrete | Continuous |
| Convergence Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Parameters | $N, P_c, P_m$ | $M, w, c_1, c_2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the **flowchart** in your answer sheet — it carries **2–3 marks** in Part B questions. Use clear labels and arrows; examiners reward visual presentation.
